name: TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA

topics.title: Quality - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Quality - Definition ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is "Quality"? **Quality** is a multi-dimensional construct that has no single universally accepted definition. In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Six Sigma**, it represents the totality of characteristics of an entity (product, service, or process) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. > [!NOTE] > **ISO 8402 / ISO 9000 Definition (KTU Approved Standard):** > *"Quality is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs."* In simpler terms — Quality is not just "perfection." It is about **meeting customer requirements consistently** and **reducing variation** in every process. ### 1.2 Classical Definitions from Quality Gurus (KTU High-Yield) | Guru | Year | Definition (Verbatim Emphasis) | Core Idea | |---|---|---|---| | **Walter Shewhart** | 1939 | *"Quality as conformance to specifications, achieved through statistical control."* | Subjective + Objective blend | | **W. Edwards Deming** | 1982 | *"A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability at low cost, suited to the market."* | **Variability Reduction** | | **Joseph M. Juran** | 1974 | *"Fitness for Use."* | **Customer-Oriented** | | **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979 | *"Conformance to Requirements."* | **Zero Defects Mindset** | | **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956 | *"Total Quality — the customer’s quality experience as determined by the combined effect of marketing, engineering, manufacturing, and service."* | **Organization-wide** | | **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1985 | *"Quality is the totality of features and characteristics that satisfy customer needs."* | **Customer-Centric** | | **Genichi Taguchi** | 1979 | *"Quality is the loss a product causes to society after being shipped."* | **Loss Function (Cost of Poor Quality)** | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Experience" > [!TIP] > **Analogy: Imagine eating at your favourite restaurant.** > - The food tastes great (Performance). > - It arrives hot within 15 minutes (Reliability). > - The chef used fresh, hygienic ingredients (Conformance). > - You receive a warm smile and courteous service (Serviceability). > - The bill matches the menu exactly (Conformance to specification). > - You return the next week → consistency (Dependability). > > **All these elements together = Quality.** This is precisely why Juran called it **"Fitness for Use"** — the *entire experience* must satisfy the customer's needs, not just one feature. ### 1.4 Why So Many Definitions? The plurality of definitions arises because quality is viewed from **three different lenses**: 1. **Transcendental View** — Quality is innate, absolute, recognizable excellence (subjective). 2. **Product-Based View** — Quality is a function of measurable product attributes. 3. **User-Based View** — Quality is in the eyes of the beholder (subjective, customer-defined). 4. **Manufacturing-Based View** — Quality is conformance to engineering specifications (objective). 5. **Value-Based View** — Quality is the ratio of usefulness to price (cost-vs-performance trade-off). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favourite Line:** "Quality is **dynamic**, not static — it changes with customer needs, technology, and competition." --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Quality Characteristics (The Q-Q-Q Framework) Quality in a manufactured product or service is evaluated through **three primary characteristics** as established in KTU Module 1 syllabus: 1. **Quality of Design** — The set of specifications (engineering tolerances, dimensions, features) the product *should* meet. 2. **Quality of Conformance** — The degree to which the actual product matches the design specifications. 3. **Quality of Performance** — How well the product performs in the field after being delivered to the customer. > [!NOTE] > The gap between **Quality of Design** and **Quality of Performance** is often called the **"Quality Gap"** — a recurring KTU 2-mark concept. ### 2.2 Dimensions of Product Quality (David Garvin's 8 Dimensions) This is a **mandatory KTU syllabus item**. Garvin (1987) identified **eight dimensions** used to define and measure quality: 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., mileage of a car). 2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that supplement basic functioning (e.g., GPS in a car). 3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over a specified time. 4. **Conformance** — Degree to which product meets established standards. 5. **Durability** — Length of product life before deterioration. 6. **Serviceability** — Ease and speed of repair/maintenance. 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound). 8. **Perceived Quality** — Image, brand reputation (subjective judgment). ### 2.3 Dimensions of Service Quality (Parasuraman–Zeithaml–Berry, 1988) Since TQM applies to **services** too, KTU expects the **SERVQUAL** dimensions: 1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance. 2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably. 3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers promptly. 4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees → inspires trust. 5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Definition-Related Metrics) > [!TIP] > Since "Quality - Definition" is primarily conceptual, the following **quality measurement formulas** are essential when *quantifying* the definitions above — they are heavily tested in Module 1 questions. | Metric | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---| | **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | Used in **Six Sigma** as the universal defect-rate metric | | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Good Units at First Attempt}}{\text{Total Units Entered}}$ | Expressed as a **decimal or %** | | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Product of all stage yields | | **Process Sigma Level ($\sigma$)** | Read from Six Sigma Normal Distribution Table | $\sigma = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; 66{,}807$ DPMO | | **Six Sigma Benchmark** | $\text{Six Sigma} \equiv 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$ | World-class quality standard | | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | **Taguchi Loss Function** generalisation | | **Taguchi Loss Function** | $L(x) = k(x - T)^{2}$ | $T$ = target, $x$ = actual value, $k$ = constant | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Walk-Through: Deriving Six Sigma Quality Level Since "Six Sigma" is the foundational benchmark behind every Quality Definition in KTU Module 1, the derivation of its DPMO value is **mandatory reading**. **Step 1 — Define the Six Sigma Tolerance Window** In a Six Sigma process, the specification limits are set at $\pm 6\sigma$ from the process mean, where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the process. $$\text{Tolerance Limits} = \mu \pm 6\sigma$$ **Step 2 — Calculate Area Under Normal Curve Within $\pm 6\sigma$** For a standard normal variable $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, the probability of being inside the $\pm 6\sigma$ region is: $$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 6) = \Phi(6) - \Phi(-6)$$ Where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution. **Step 3 — Apply Symmetry of the Normal Distribution** Since the normal distribution is symmetric around zero: $$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 6) = 2 \cdot \Phi(6) - 1$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Tabulated Value** From standard normal tables: $$\Phi(6) \approx 0.9999999980$$ Therefore: $$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 6) = 2 \times 0.9999999980 - 1 = 0.9999999960$$ **Step 5 — Convert Probability to Defect Rate** The probability of a part being *outside* specifications (i.e., a defect) is: $$P(\text{Defect}) = 1 - 0.9999999960 = 0.0000000040$$ **Step 6 — Translate to DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** $$\text{DPMO} = 0.0000000040 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 4.0 \text{ DPMO}$$ **Step 7 — Apply the Long-Term 1.5σ Shift** In real manufacturing, the process mean drifts by **$1.5\sigma$** over time. Accounting for this shift, the effective defect rate becomes: $$\text{Six Sigma DPMO} \approx 3.4 \text{ DPMO}$$ $$\boxed{\therefore \text{Six Sigma Quality} \equiv 3.4 \text{ Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)}}$$ ### 3.2 Comparison Matrix — How Each Guru Defines Quality | Guru | Customer-Focused? | Statistics-Based? | Cost-Emphasis? | KTU Frequency | |---|---|---|---|---| | Deming | Medium | **High** | High | ★★★★ | | Juran | **High** | Medium | Medium | ★★★★★ | | Crosby | Medium | Low | **High** (Prevention) | ★★★★ | | Feigenbaum | **High** | Medium | Medium | ★★★ | | Ishikawa | **High** | Medium | Low | ★★★★ | | Taguchi | Low | **High** | **High** (Loss Function) | ★★★★ | | Shewhart | Low | **Very High** | Low | ★★★ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Evolution of Quality Definitions (Mermaid Timeline Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["A1 - 1939
SHEWHART"] --> nodeB["A2 - 1956
FEIGENBAUM"] nodeB --> nodeC["A3 - 1974
JURAN"] nodeC --> nodeD["A4 - 1979
CROSBY"] nodeD --> nodeE["A5 - 1979
TAGUCHI"] nodeE --> nodeF["A6 - 1982
DEMING"] nodeF --> nodeG["A7 - 1985
ISHIKAWA"] nodeG --> nodeH["A8 - 1987
GARVIN 8 DIMENSIONS"] nodeH --> nodeI["A9 - 1988
SERVQUAL MODEL"] nodeI --> nodeJ["A10 - MODERN ERA
SIX SIGMA + ISO 9000"] nodeJ --> nodeK["A11 - TQM ERA
TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT"] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Garvin's 8 Quality Dimensions ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeQ["CUSTOMER
JUDGMENT"] --> nodeP["PRODUCT"] nodeP --> node1["PERFORMANCE"] nodeP --> node2["FEATURES"] nodeP --> node3["RELIABILITY"] nodeP --> node4["CONFORMANCE"] nodeP --> node5["DURABILITY"] nodeP --> node6["SERVICEABILITY"] nodeP --> node7["AESTHETICS"] nodeP --> node8["PERCEIVED QUALITY"] node1 --> nodeOUT["OVERALL
QUALITY"] node2 --> nodeOUT node3 --> nodeOUT node4 --> nodeOUT node5 --> nodeOUT node6 --> nodeOUT node7 --> nodeOUT node8 --> nodeOUT nodeOUT --> nodeDEC["PURCHASE
DECISION"] ``` ### 4.3 The Quality Hierarchy — Definition to Delivery ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeDEF["QUALITY
DEFINITION"] --> nodeD1["QUALITY OF
DESIGN"] nodeDEF --> nodeD2["QUALITY OF
CONFORMANCE"] nodeDEF --> nodeD3["QUALITY OF
PERFORMANCE"] nodeD1 --> nodeGAP["QUALITY
GAP ANALYSIS"] nodeD2 --> nodeGAP nodeD3 --> nodeGAP nodeGAP --> nodeTQM["TQM
IMPLEMENTATION"] nodeTQM --> nodeCUST["CUSTOMER
SATISFACTION"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define Quality according to **Joseph Juran**. Explain the term **"Fitness for Use"** with a suitable example. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** Joseph Juran, in 1974, defined Quality as **"Fitness for Use."** This definition emphasizes that a product or service must be suitable for the *intended purpose* of the customer. Quality is judged from the **customer's perspective**, not the manufacturer's. **Example:** A smartphone with an excellent camera but poor battery life is *not* "fit for use" for a traveller who needs long battery backup. Hence, Juran's definition stresses end-user satisfaction across all relevant features. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Fitness for Use" with "Fitness for Purpose." The correct term is **"Fitness for Use."** Writing "purpose" instead of "use" may cost **0.5–1 mark**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** List any **four dimensions of product quality** as given by **David Garvin** with one-line explanations. **[3 Marks]** **Model Answer:** David Garvin (1987) identified eight dimensions of product quality. Four key dimensions are: 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics of the product (e.g., mileage, speed). 2. **Reliability** — Probability that the product will function without failure over a specified period. 3. **Conformance** — Degree to which the product meets pre-established engineering specifications and standards. 4. **Durability** — Length of useful life of the product before it physically deteriorates or requires replacement. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question 1 (a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Define Quality. Explain the various **definitions given by Quality Gurus** (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi) with their key emphasis. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definition of Quality:** Quality is a multidimensional concept referring to the totality of characteristics of a product or service that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (ISO 8402). **Guru Definitions:** | Guru | Year | Definition | Key Emphasis | |---|---|---|---| | **Deming** | 1982 | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability at low cost suited to the market | **Variability reduction** and **statistical thinking** | | **Juran** | 1974 | Fitness for Use | **Customer satisfaction** across features | | **Crosby** | 1979 | Conformance to Requirements | **Zero defects** and **prevention over inspection** | | **Feigenbaum** | 1956 | Total Quality — quality is the customer's experience across all functions | **Organization-wide** responsibility | | **Ishikawa** | 1985 | Quality is the totality of features satisfying customer needs | **Customer-centricity** and **company-wide quality control** | | **Taguchi** | 1979 | Quality is the loss a product causes to society after shipment | **Loss Function** $L = k(x - T)^2$ and **social cost** | **Conclusion:** While each guru emphasised a different facet (customer, conformance, cost, variation), they all converge on one truth — **quality must be defined from the customer's viewpoint, delivered consistently, and improved continuously.** --- #### **Question 1 (b). [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Explain **David Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality** and **Parasuraman's Five Dimensions of Service Quality (SERVQUAL)** with examples. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Garvin's 8 Dimensions of Product Quality:** 1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., speed of a sports car). 2. **Features** — Supplementary characteristics (e.g., sunroof, GPS). 3. **Reliability** — Failure-free operation over time. 4. **Conformance** — Meeting engineering specifications. 5. **Durability** — Product life span. 6. **Serviceability** — Ease of repair. 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, finish). 8. **Perceived Quality** — Brand image, reputation. **SERVQUAL — Five Dimensions of Service Quality (Parasuraman et al., 1988):** 1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, staff appearance (e.g., clean hospital). 2. **Reliability** — Dependable promised service (e.g., on-time courier delivery). 3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help promptly (e.g., 24×7 customer helpline). 4. **Assurance** — Knowledge & courtesy inspire trust (e.g., certified doctor). 5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualised attention (e.g., remembering a regular customer's order). **Comparison:** Product quality is **tangible and measurable**; service quality is **intangible, perishable, and inconsistent** — hence SERVQUAL bridges the perception gap. --- #### **Question 2 (a) [Internal Choice]. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** Define **Six Sigma**. Derive the relationship between **Process Sigma Level** and **DPMO** with a step-by-step mathematical derivation. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Six Sigma is a **disciplined, data-driven methodology** that aims to eliminate defects in any process by reducing variation so that the process operates within $\pm 6\sigma$ of its mean, yielding no more than **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. **Derivation:** *(as shown in Section 3.1 above)* For a process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the tolerance limits are: $$L = \mu \pm 6\sigma$$ Converting to standard normal $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$: $$P(\vert Z \vert \leq 6) = 2\Phi(6) - 1$$ Substituting $\Phi(6) = 0.9999999980$: $$P(\text{within}) = 0.9999999960$$ $$P(\text{defect}) = 1 - 0.9999999960 = 4 \times 10^{-9}$$ $$\text{DPMO} = 4 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^{6} = 4.0 \text{ DPMO}$$ After applying the **1.5σ shift** (process mean drift in long-term operation): $$\boxed{\text{Six Sigma DPMO} \approx 3.4 \text{ DPMO}}$$ --- #### **Question 2 (b) [Internal Choice]. [KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Explain **Quality of Design, Quality of Conformance, and Quality of Performance** with a real-world example. How are these three aspects related to the concept of **Total Quality Management**? **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **1. Quality of Design (QoD):** The *engineered* specifications the product *should* meet. It is determined during the design phase. Example: A car designed to give 20 km/l mileage, 1200cc engine, 5-star safety rating. **2. Quality of Conformance (QoC):** The extent to which the actual manufactured product matches the design specifications. Example: Out of 1,000 cars produced, how many actually deliver 20 km/l as designed? **3. Quality of Performance (QoP):** How the product performs *in the hands of the customer* under real-world conditions. Example: Does the car still deliver 20 km/l after 5 years on Indian roads? **Relationship with TQM:** - **TQM** integrates all three — by **involving all functions** (marketing, design, production, service) in the **continuous improvement** of each dimension. - The **Quality Gap** (difference between QoD and QoP) is the target for TQM initiatives. - TQM uses **Kaizen, Six Sigma, and PDCA cycles** to shrink this gap to near zero. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. Writing *"Quality means excellence"* without citing a specific guru → loses **2 marks** in 7-mark questions. > 2. Confusing **Quality of Design** with **Quality of Conformance** — they are *not* the same. > 3. Forgetting to apply the **1.5σ shift** in Six Sigma derivation → answer shows 4 DPMO instead of 3.4 DPMO → loses **1 mark**. > 4. Citing *"Philip Crosby said fitness for use"* — this is JURAN's definition, NOT Crosby's → **factually incorrect**, loses **2 marks**. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Quality has no single definition** — it is contextual, dynamic, and customer-driven. - ✅ **Juran = Fitness for Use** *(most quoted)*; **Crosby = Conformance to Requirements**; **Deming = Uniformity & Dependability**; **Taguchi = Loss to Society**. - ✅ **Garvin's 8 Dimensions** are the universal framework for product quality. - ✅ **SERVQUAL's 5 Dimensions** apply specifically to services. - ✅ **Three Q's** = Quality of Design, Quality of Conformance, Quality of Performance. - ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO** after applying the **1.5σ shift** (this is the famous benchmark). - ✅ **Taguchi Loss Function** $L = k(x - T)^2$ — quality loss is *quadratic* with deviation from target. - ✅ **COPQ** has four components: Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. - ✅ KTU often asks **"Compare Juran and Crosby"** — know the *similarity* (both customer-focused) and *difference* (fitness vs. conformance). ---

topics.title: Quality Quantified
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Quantified

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Quantified** refers to the systematic, mathematical translation of the abstract concept of *Quality* into measurable, controllable metrics (numbers). It is the bridge between the philosophical view of quality and the engineering/economic reality of process improvement. Under the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with **NEP 2020**), *Quality Quantified* is the foundational lens through which subjective attributes (e.g., "good car," "fast service") are converted into objective Key Performance Indicators (KPIs), defect rates, and financial impacts.

In formal Total Quality Management (TQM) terminology, **Quality Quantification** rests on three pillars:

1. **Conformance to Requirements** (Philip Crosby) — A product or service must meet agreed specifications; if it doesn't, there is a *quality gap*.
2. **Fitness for Use** (Joseph Juran) — Quality is judged by the *customer's ability to use* the product/service without failure in its intended environment.
3. **Customer-Driven Value** (Modern TQM) — Quality is ultimately the *degree to which a product meets customer needs*, and this must be measured through Voice of Customer (VOC) tools.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Thermometer Analogy:** Saying a patient "feels sick" is a qualitative observation. Taking their temperature and getting **101 °F** is *quantification*. In the same way, "the product is of good quality" is qualitative; saying "**the process produces 3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO), equating to a Six Sigma capability**" is *Quality Quantified*.

**Real-world analogy — The Restaurant:**
- **Qualitative Quality:** "The biryani was awesome."
- **Quantified Quality:** "Out of 1,000 orders this month, 992 were served within 15 minutes (Yield = 99.2%), customer complaints = 8 (DPMO = 8,000 for service-speed CTQ), rework cost = ₹12,000 (Internal Failure Cost), and warranty/dissatisfaction refund cost = ₹5,000 (External Failure Cost)."

This conversion transforms emotion into **data**, which is the *only* language management and Six Sigma understand.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in TQM

The following **standard metrics** are universally used in KTU-evaluated TQM problems:

- **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) — Unit: *defects / million opportunities*.
- **DPO** (Defects Per Opportunity) — Unit: *defects / opportunity*.
- **DPU** (Defects Per Unit) — Unit: *defects / unit*.
- **Yield (Y)** — Unit: *% (or decimal between 0 and 1)*.
- **RTY** (Rolled Throughput Yield) — Unit: *%*.
- **Sigma Level (σ)** — Standard values used: **3σ, 4σ, 5σ, 6σ** with benchmark DPMOs of **66,807; 6,210; 233; 3.4** respectively (with 1.5σ shift).
- **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality) — Unit: *currency (₹, $, €)*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of "Quality Quantified"

The topic is logically divided into **four sub-pillars** as per the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1 – Quality Quantified):

#### Pillar A — Quality Vocabulary
- **Quality:** The totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000).
- **Grade:** A category or rank given to entities having the same functional use but different technical characteristics (e.g., economy vs. premium).
- **Customer Satisfaction:** The customer's perception that a product/service has met or exceeded their expectations.
- **Customer Value:** The ratio of *perceived benefits* to *perceived costs* (Value = Benefits / Cost).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality ≠ Grade.** A *Toyota* and a *Rolls-Royce* can both be defect-free (high quality) but be of different grades (luxury vs. economy). KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

#### Pillar B — Dimensions of Quality (Garvin's 8 Dimensions)

| Dimension | Meaning | Quantification Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Performance | Primary operating characteristics | Test scores, throughput |
| Features | "Extra" characteristics beyond basic | Number of features |
| Reliability | Probability of failure-free operation | Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) |
| Conformance | Degree to which specs are met | First Pass Yield (FPY) |
| Durability | Length of useful life | Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) |
| Serviceability | Speed & ease of repair | Average Repair Time |
| Aesthetics | Sensory characteristics | Customer survey (1–10 scale) |
| Perceived Quality | Reputation, brand image | Net Promoter Score (NPS) |

#### Pillar C — Cost of Quality (COPQ)

Total Cost of Quality = Prevention Cost (P) + Appraisal Cost (A) + Internal Failure Cost (IF) + External Failure Cost (EF).

- **Prevention Cost:** Money spent to *prevent* defects (training, quality planning).
- **Appraisal Cost:** Money spent to *detect* defects (inspection, testing).
- **Internal Failure Cost:** Cost of defects found *before* delivery (rework, scrap).
- **External Failure Cost:** Cost of defects found *after* delivery (warranty, recalls, lost goodwill).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Juran's Optimization Principle:** There exists an *Optimum Cost of Quality* where the **sum of (P + A + IF + EF)** is at its minimum. Increasing Prevention costs initially raises total COPQ but eventually drops failure costs dramatically — yielding a **U-shaped curve**.

#### Pillar D — Six Sigma Foundation Metrics

This is the *quantitative heart* of the topic and the most heavily tested in KTU 2024 ESE papers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in ~70% of Module 1 numericals.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $DPO = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$ | Defects per Opportunity | defects/opportunity |
| 2 | $DPMO = DPO \times 1{,}000{,}000$ | Defects per Million Opportunities | defects/10⁶ opportunities |
| 3 | $DPU = \dfrac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}}$ | Defects per Unit | defects/unit |
| 4 | $Y = 1 - DPO$ | First Pass Yield | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 5 | $RTY = FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times \ldots \times FPY_n$ | Rolled Throughput Yield | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 6 | $Normal\ Yield = e^{-DPMO / 1{,}000{,}000}$ | Yield assuming normal distribution | decimal (0 to 1) |
| 7 | $COPQ_{Total} = P + A + IF + EF$ | Cost of Poor Quality | ₹ / $ |
| 8 | $Customer\ Value = \dfrac{Benefits}{Cost}$ | Value Proposition | dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $First\ Time\ Right\ (FTR) = \dfrac{\text{Units completed without rework}}{\text{Total units started}}$ | FTR metric | decimal |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Mechanical/Civil):** Used to set tolerance levels; DPMO drives acceptance sampling plans (ANSI/ASQ Z1.4).
- **Software Industry:** DPMO is applied per *code lines* or *test cases* to certify processes at CMMi Level 5.
- **Service Industry (Banking, Hospitals):** Quantifies "errors per million transactions," directly linking to regulatory compliance.
- **Cost Engineering:** COPQ is a direct line item in any Six Sigma project's *Business Case* submitted to KTU viva panels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Defects to DPMO

The cornerstone of "Quality Quantified" is the **DPMO equation**. We derive it from first principles so that every algebraic transition is explicit.

**Step 1 — Define the Opportunity.**
An *opportunity* is any chance for a defect to occur in one unit. If a car has 50 components that could fail, then **Opportunities per Unit = 50**.

**Step 2 — Compute Total Opportunities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Opportunities} &= (\text{Number of Units}) \times (\text{Opportunities per Unit}) \\[6pt]
\text{Total Opportunities} &= N \times O
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Defects Per Opportunity (DPO).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPO &= \frac{\text{Total Number of Defects observed}}{\text{Total Opportunities}} \\[6pt]
DPO &= \frac{D}{N \times O}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to DPMO (Scale by 1,000,000).**
Since $DPO$ is usually a tiny decimal (e.g., 0.000621), we scale it to a more intuitive number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= DPO \times 1{,}000{,}000 \\[6pt]
DPMO &= \frac{D}{N \times O} \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Yield.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - DPO \\[6pt]
Y_{\%} &= (1 - DPO) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Standard)

> **Problem:** A mobile phone assembly line produced **8,500 phones** in a week. Each phone has **15 critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics**. Inspectors found **310 total defects** during final inspection. Compute DPU, DPO, DPMO, and Yield.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad N &= 8{,}500, \quad O = 15, \quad D = 310 \\[6pt]
DPU &= \frac{D}{N} = \frac{310}{8{,}500} = 0.03647 \text{ defects/unit} \\[6pt]
DPO &= \frac{D}{N \times O} = \frac{310}{8{,}500 \times 15} = \frac{310}{127{,}500} = 0.002431 \\[6pt]
DPMO &= 0.002431 \times 1{,}000{,}000 = 2{,}431 \text{ defects per million opportunities} \\[6pt]
Yield &= 1 - 0.002431 = 0.997569 = 99.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sigma Level Lookup:** A DPMO of 2,431 corresponds to approximately **4.4σ** (interpolating from the standard Six Sigma conversion table).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sigma Level vs DPMO (Short-Term, with 1.5σ Shift)

| Sigma Level | DPMO (with 1.5σ shift) | Yield (%) | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 308,537 | 69.15% | Average Indian passport office |
| 3σ | 66,807 | 93.32% | Average Indian restaurant billing |
| 4σ | 6,210 | 99.38% | Best-in-class airline baggage handling |
| 5σ | 233 | 99.977% | Best-in-class hospital surgery |
| 6σ | 3.4 | 99.99966% | World-class (Motorola, GE) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **1.5σ shift** is a Motorola convention (adopted universally) that accounts for long-term process drift. It is **always used in KTU numericals unless explicitly stated otherwise**.

### 3.4 Derivation: Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

When a product passes through **n sequential processes**, the cumulative yield is the *product* (not sum) of individual first-pass yields. We prove this:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Output of Process 1} &= N \times FPY_1 \\[6pt]
\text{Output of Process 2} &= (N \times FPY_1) \times FPY_2 = N \times FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Output of Process n} &= N \times FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times \ldots \times FPY_n \\[6pt]
RTY &= \frac{\text{Final Good Units}}{\text{Initial Units}} = FPY_1 \times FPY_2 \times \ldots \times FPY_n
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiplication, not addition?** Yields are *probabilities of independence*. If each process has a 95% chance of success, the chance of *both* succeeding is $0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.9025$ (90.25%), not 190%.

**Numerical Example:**
A product goes through 4 processes with FPYs of 95%, 97%, 92%, and 99%. Compute RTY.

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= 0.95 \times 0.97 \times 0.92 \times 0.99 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.95 \times 0.97 = 0.9215 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.9215 \times 0.92 = 0.8478 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.8478 \times 0.99 = 0.8393 = 83.93\%
\end{aligned}
$$

So out of 1,000 input units, only **839.3** pass all 4 processes without rework.

### 3.5 Derivation: Normal Yield (Poisson Approximation)

For high-quality processes (DPMO in thousands), the *normal* yield approximation is used:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } \lambda &= \text{DPMO} / 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ is the probability of defect} \\[6pt]
\text{Then } Y_{normal} &= e^{-\lambda} \\[6pt]
Y_{normal} &= e^{-DPMO / 1{,}000{,}000}
\end{aligned}
$$

This follows from the Poisson distribution's limit, a standard Six Sigma assumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Quality is Quantified

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Needs VOC] --> B[CTQ Characteristics Identification]
    B --> C[Defect Opportunity Mapping]
    C --> D[Data Collection from Process]
    D --> E[Defect Counting D]
    E --> F[DPO Computation]
    F --> G[DPMO Computation]
    G --> H[Sigma Level Lookup Table]
    H --> I[Yield Calculation]
    I --> J[COPQ Cost Assignment]
    J --> K[Six Sigma Project Charter]
    K --> L[Continuous Improvement DMAIC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read:** The diagram represents the *information flow* of Quality Quantification. Voice of Customer (VOC) feeds into CTQ identification, which drives defect data collection, which feeds the mathematical models of DPMO → Sigma → Yield → Cost → Improvement Loop.

### 4.2 Cost of Quality Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COPQ[Cost of Poor Quality]
        direction TB
        P[Prevention Cost] --> Pdesc[Training, QPlanning, Design Reviews]
        A2[Appraisal Cost] --> Adesc[Inspection, Testing, Calibration]
        IF[Internal Failure Cost] --> IFdesc[Scrap, Rework, Reinspection]
        EF[External Failure Cost] --> EFdesc[Warranty, Recall, Litigation, Lost Goodwill]
    end
    COPQ --> Sum[Total COPQ = P + A + IF + EF]
    Sum --> Optimum[Juran Optimum Cost Curve U-Shape]
    Optimum --> Decision[Invest More in Prevention]
```

### 4.3 DPMO Computation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Input N, O, D] --> S2[Compute TotalOpportunities = N x O]
    S2 --> S3[Compute DPO = D divided by TotalOpportunities]
    S3 --> S4[Compute DPMO = DPO x 1000000]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Yield = 1 minus DPO]
    S5 --> S6[Lookup Sigma from Conversion Table]
    S6 --> S7[Output: DPMO, Yield, Sigma]
```

### 4.4 Garvin's 8 Dimensions — Hierarchical Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q[Quality Quantified] --> D1[Performance]
    Q --> D2[Features]
    Q --> D3[Reliability]
    Q --> D4[Conformance]
    Q --> D5[Durability]
    Q --> D6[Serviceability]
    Q --> D7[Aesthetics]
    Q --> D8[Perceived Quality]
    D1 --> M1[Bench Test Score]
    D3 --> M3[MTBF Hours]
    D4 --> M4[FPY Percent]
    D8 --> M8[Net Promoter Score]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between Quality and Grade with a suitable example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Quality** is the *degree to which a product meets specifications* and is *defect-free*. (1 mark)
- **Grade** is the *category or rank* assigned to products having the *same functional use* but *different feature sets or quality levels*. (1 mark)
- **Example:** Two cars — a Maruti Alto and a Maruti Swift — are both defect-free (high quality) but the Swift is a *higher grade* due to better features, engine, and aesthetics. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define DPMO. A process produces 250 defects across 5,000 units with 8 opportunities per unit. Calculate the DPMO.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition:** DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities) is the number of defects per million opportunities for a process, indicating process capability. (1 mark)
- **Calculation:** $DPO = \dfrac{250}{5000 \times 8} = 0.00625$. (1 mark)
- **DPMO** $= 0.00625 \times 10^6 = 6{,}250$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the *eight dimensions of quality* given by David Garvin with relevant examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**
1. **Performance** — Primary operating characteristics (e.g., laptop processor speed = 3.5 GHz). (1 mark)
2. **Features** — Additional attributes beyond basics (e.g., touchscreen, fingerprint sensor). (1 mark)
3. **Reliability** — Probability of failure-free operation over time (e.g., MTBF = 50,000 hours). (1 mark)
4. **Conformance** — Adherence to design specifications (e.g., tolerance ±0.01 mm). (1 mark)
5. **Durability** — Total useful life before replacement (e.g., washing machine life = 10 years). (0.5 mark)
6. **Serviceability** — Speed & ease of repair (e.g., average laptop repair time = 2 hours). (0.5 mark)
7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal (look, feel, sound) (e.g., smartphone finish). (0.5 mark)
8. **Perceived Quality** — Indirect measures such as brand image (e.g., Apple vs. generic brand). (0.5 mark)

Total = 7 marks.

**(b)** A loan processing department has *5 sequential sub-processes*. The first-pass yields of these processes are 0.94, 0.88, 0.96, 0.91, and 0.98. If 2,000 loan applications are received, calculate: **(i)** the Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY), and **(ii)** the expected number of applications that pass all 5 processes without rework. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RTY &= 0.94 \times 0.88 \times 0.96 \times 0.91 \times 0.98 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.94 \times 0.88 = 0.8272 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.8272 \times 0.96 = 0.7941 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.7941 \times 0.91 = 0.7226 \\[6pt]
RTY &= 0.7226 \times 0.98 = 0.7082 = 70.82\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(3 marks for RTY calculation)

**Number of applications passing without rework:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{good} &= 2000 \times 0.7082 = 1416.4 \approx 1416 \text{ applications}
\end{aligned}
$$

(2 marks)

**Interpretation:** Despite each process being 90%+ efficient, only ~71% of applications go through end-to-end without rework — *this is why RTY is the true measure of process quality.* (2 marks)

Total = 7 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of *Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)* with a clear categorization of cost components. Sketch the Juran optimum cost curve. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

COPQ is the total cost incurred because of poor quality — money that would *not have been spent* in a perfect process.

**Categories:** (4 marks)

| Category | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Prevention Cost (P) | Proactive | Quality training, design reviews, supplier capability audits |
| Appraisal Cost (A) | Detection | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, calibration |
| Internal Failure (IF) | Reactive (pre-delivery) | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime |
| External Failure (EF) | Reactive (post-delivery) | Warranty claims, recalls, legal liability, lost goodwill |

**Juran's Optimum Cost Curve:** (3 marks)
- The Total COPQ curve is **U-shaped** (or sometimes called "iceberg model").
- Initially, increasing Prevention costs *raises* total COPQ (because money is being spent before any savings materialize).
- Beyond a point, Prevention cost investments *reduce* Internal and External failure costs dramatically, causing total COPQ to fall.
- The **optimum** is at the lowest point of the U-curve — this is the recommended investment level in prevention activities.

> Cross-sectional description: $COPQ_{Total}(P) = P + A + IF(P) + EF(P)$, where $IF$ and $EF$ are decreasing functions of $P$, while $P$ itself is increasing linearly.

---

**(b)** A printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing line produces 12,000 boards/month. Each board has 20 critical solder points (opportunities). In a sample audit, 480 defects were found. **(i)** Calculate DPU, DPO, DPMO, and Yield. **(ii)** Using the Six Sigma conversion table (with 1.5σ shift), determine the process sigma level. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 12{,}000, \quad O = 20, \quad D = 480 \\[6pt]
DPU &= \frac{480}{12{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ defects/unit} \\[6pt]
DPO &= \frac{480}{12{,}000 \times 20} = \frac{480}{240{,}000} = 0.002 \\[6pt]
DPMO &= 0.002 \times 10^6 = 2{,}000 \text{ defects/million opportunities} \\[6pt]
Yield &= 1 - 0.002 = 0.998 = 99.80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

(4 marks for (i))

**(ii) Sigma Level Lookup:** From the standard table:
- 4σ = 6,210 DPMO
- 4.5σ ≈ 1,350 DPMO (interpolated: 6σ = 3.4, 5σ = 233)
- For 2,000 DPMO, the sigma level is approximately **4.4σ** (using linear interpolation between 4σ and 4.5σ). (3 marks)

$$
\sigma \approx 4 + (6210 - 2000) / (6210 - 1350) \times 0.5 = 4 + (4210/4860) \times 0.5 \approx 4 + 0.43 = 4.43\sigma
$$

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks (real KTU valuation patterns):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DPU with DPO:** DPU uses *units* in the denominator; DPO uses *units × opportunities*. Mixing these loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift:** Most sigma conversion tables in textbooks have *both* short-term and long-term columns. KTU always expects the **1.5σ shifted (long-term)** values unless the question explicitly says otherwise.
> 3. **Adding yields instead of multiplying in RTY problems:** A classic error. RTY = *product*, not *sum*.
> 4. **Missing the "Quality vs Grade" distinction** in 3-mark questions — examiners allocate 1 mark for each definition and 1 mark for the example.
> 5. **Not converting percentages to decimals** before multiplication in RTY — yielding answers like $0.94 \times 0.88 = 82.72\%$ instead of $0.8272 = 82.72\%$. Always show both forms.
> 6. **Unit Errors in COPQ problems:** Mixing ₹ and $ within a single calculation — a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quality** = Conformance to requirements + Fitness for use. **Grade** = Category/rank — they are *not* synonyms.
- **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality — *the 8 P-words extended with S, A, P*.
- **DPMO** is the *single most-tested metric* in KTU Module 1 numericals.
- **RTY** = product of all stage yields; always ≤ minimum individual yield.
- **COPQ = P + A + IF + EF**, with Prevention being the *only* category that is purely voluntary — all others are forced.
- **Juran's Optimum Cost Curve** is U-shaped; invest in Prevention until failure costs start dropping faster than prevention cost rises.
- **Six Sigma benchmarks (with 1.5σ shift):** 3σ = 66,807 DPMO; 4σ = 6,210; 5σ = 233; 6σ = 3.4.
- **Customer Value = Benefits / Cost** — a dimensionless ratio used in VOC analysis.
- **Yield approximations:** $Y = 1 - DPO$ (simple) and $Y = e^{-DPMO/10^6}$ (Poisson/normal — used for very low defect rates).
- Always carry **units** in intermediate steps for full marks in 14-mark problems.
- For multi-stage process problems, default to **RTY** (not FPY) when the question asks for *overall* effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensions of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Dimensions of Quality # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (David A. Garvin, 1987 — Harvard Business Review)** > *Dimensions of Quality* are the **multiple, discrete attributes** that collectively define and measure the construct of "quality" in a product or service. Since "quality" is an abstract and multidimensional concept, it cannot be captured by a single metric; it must be decomposed into measurable components that customers use to evaluate offerings. The seminal framework was introduced by **Professor David A. Garvin** in his landmark 1987 paper *"Competing on the Eight Dimensions of Quality,"* published in the *Harvard Business Review*. This paper is considered the **foundational text** of modern quality management theory and is prescribed reading in the KTU 2024 TQM syllabus (Module 1). Garvin's 8 Dimensions apply primarily to **manufactured (tangible) products**, while a parallel framework called **SERVQUAL** (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988) provides **5 Dimensions of Service Quality**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Buying a Smartphone** > > Imagine you walk into a store to buy a smartphone. You don't just ask "Is this a good phone?" You subconsciously evaluate it on **many** criteria: > - *Does the battery last long?* → **Performance** > - *Does it have a good camera, fingerprint sensor, 5G?* → **Features** > - *Will it crash or hang in 6 months?* → **Reliability** > - *Is it built exactly to advertised specs?* → **Conformance** > - *How many years will it last?* → **Durability** > - *Is there a nearby service center?* → **Serviceability** > - *Does it look beautiful, feel premium?* → **Aesthetics** > - *Is the brand trusted?* → **Perceived Quality** > > **Each of these is a Dimension of Quality.** A great product must score well across ALL of them. ## 1.3 Why Dimensions Matter in TQM > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 1** > Understanding quality dimensions is the *prerequisite* for: > 1. Designing quality products (Quality Function Deployment / QFD) > 2. Measuring customer satisfaction (Customer Satisfaction Index / CSI) > 3. Benchmarking competitors > 4. Building Six Sigma project CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) trees The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that managers who treat quality as a **single number** (e.g., defect rate) miss the **strategic and experiential** aspects of quality. Garvin's framework operationalizes the **"fitness for use"** concept (Juran) and the **"conformance to requirements"** concept (Crosby) into a usable map. ## 1.4 Two Parallel Schools of Thought | Aspect | Garvin's Framework (1987) | SERVQUAL Framework (1988) | |---|---|---| | **Domain** | Products (tangible goods) | Services (intangible offerings) | | **Number of Dimensions** | **8 dimensions** | **5 dimensions** | | **Origin** | Harvard Business Review | Journal of Marketing | | **Proponents** | David A. Garvin | Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry | | **Measurement Approach** | Categorical/attribute-based | Gap-based (Expectations − Perceptions) | | **KTU Relevance** | High — primary syllabus topic | High — service industries module | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality — Detailed Analysis ### **Dimension 1: Performance** - **Definition:** The primary operating characteristics of a product — the core functional capabilities the product was designed to deliver. - **Examples:** - Car → top speed, mileage, acceleration (0–100 km/h) - Mobile phone → processor speed, RAM, battery hours - Air conditioner → cooling capacity (BTU/hr) - **Key Insight:** Always measurable on a **quantitative scale** (e.g., **0–100 km/h in 7.2 seconds**). This is the most "objective" dimension. ### **Dimension 2: Features** - **Definition:** The secondary, supplementary characteristics that add value beyond primary performance — the "bells and whistles." - **Examples:** - Car → GPS, sunroof, parking sensors, voice command - Mobile phone → wireless charging, water resistance (IP68) - **Key Insight:** Features are **subjective add-ons** and evolve rapidly with technology. They differentiate products in mature markets. ### **Dimension 3: Reliability** - **Definition:** The probability that a product will perform its intended function **failure-free** over a specified time period under stated conditions. - **Engineering Metric:** **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** — measured in hours. - **Examples:** - Hard disk → MTBF of 1,000,000 hours - Aircraft engine → MTBF of 50,000 flight hours - **Key Insight:** Reliability is **measurable in the field** and directly affects warranty costs. ### **Dimension 4: Conformance** - **Definition:** The degree to which a product's design and operating characteristics meet **pre-established standards and specifications**. - **Examples:** - ISO 9001 standard compliance - Tolerance limits in manufacturing (e.g., shaft diameter = 25 mm ± 0.01 mm) - Six Sigma 3.4 DPMO target - **Key Insight:** This is **Crosby's "conformance to requirements"** dimension. Measured via **defect rates** and **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**. ### **Dimension 5: Durability** - **Definition:** The measure of a product's **useful life** before it deteriorates or physically breaks down to the point of replacement. - **Examples:** - Light bulb → 25,000 hours - Refrigerator → 15 years - Mobile phone → 3–5 years of useful life - **Key Insight:** Durability combines **technical life** with **economic life** (when repair costs exceed replacement costs). ### **Dimension 6: Serviceability** - **Definition:** The speed, ease, courtesy, and competence with which a product is **restored to working order** after a failure. - **Examples:** - Average Time To Repair (MTTR) - Availability of spare parts - Skill of service technicians - Service center network density - **Key Insight:** Even highly reliable products can score low on quality if serviceability is poor. ### **Dimension 7: Aesthetics** - **Definition:** The subjective, sensory dimension — how a product **looks, feels, sounds, smells, or tastes**. - **Examples:** - Industrial design (Apple vs. generic phone) - Color, finish, ergonomic grip - Door-closing sound of a luxury car - **Key Insight:** This is the **most subjective** dimension and the hardest to engineer. Driven by industrial design teams. ### **Dimension 8: Perceived Quality** - **Definition:** The customer's **subjective perception** of a product's overall quality, often based on **brand reputation, advertising, and word-of-mouth** — not on objective testing. - **Examples:** - "Made in Germany" perception for cars - Apple brand premium - Rolex vs. generic watch - **Key Insight:** Sometimes **objective** quality (based on testing) and **perceived** quality diverge dramatically. Perceived quality is heavily influenced by **marketing, branding, and past experiences**. --- ## 2.2 SERVQUAL's Five Dimensions of Service Quality Since the KTU 2024 syllabus covers the shift from product to service economies, the **SERVQUAL model** is mandatory study. It defines service quality as the **gap between customer expectations and perceptions**. ### **The 5 SERVQUAL Dimensions:** | # | Dimension | Definition | Practical Examples | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, communication materials | Clean hospital, uniformed staff, modern equipment | | 2 | **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately** | On-time delivery, accurate billing, error-free service | | 3 | **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide **prompt service** | Quick wait time, 24/7 helpline, fast complaint resolution | | 4 | **Assurance** | Employee **knowledge and courtesy** that inspire trust and confidence | Certified technicians, transparent processes, money-back guarantee | | 5 | **Empathy** | Caring, **individualized attention** provided to customers | Personalized service, remembering customer preferences, senior-citizen priority | --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Memorization Table for KTU University Exam** | # | Concept / Framework | Key Formulas or Core Elements | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Service Quality (SERVQUAL) | $SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (E_i - P_i)$ | Measures expectation–perception gap | | 2 | Overall Gap (Parasuraman) | $Gap_5 = f(Gap_1, Gap_2, Gap_3, Gap_4)$ | Sum of earlier gaps | | 3 | Garvin Dimensions | **8 dimensions** (mnemonic: **P-F-R-C-D-S-A-P** — "Powerful Factors Reveal Customer's Demanded Service, Aesthetics, Perception") | Product quality mapping | | 4 | SERVQUAL Dimensions | **5 dimensions** (mnemonic: **T-R-R-A-E** — "The Right Response And Empathy") | Service quality mapping | | 5 | Reliability Metric | $MTBF = \frac{Total\ Operating\ Time}{Number\ of\ Failures}$ | Dimension 3 measurement | | 6 | Serviceability Metric | $MTTR = \frac{Total\ Downtime}{Number\ of\ Repairs}$ | Dimension 6 measurement | | 7 | Six Sigma Conformance | $DPMO \leq 3.4$ defects per million opportunities | Dimension 4 target | | 8 | Quality of Design vs. Conformance | $Quality = Q_{Design} + Q_{Conformance}$ (Garvin's two-tier view) | Engineering vs. manufacturing | > [!WARNING] > **Critical LaTeX Note:** When writing absolute value in tables, always use $\vert$ or $\mid$ — never the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ — to prevent markdown table breakage. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Dominant Quality Dimensions | |---|---| | **Automotive (e.g., Tata, Toyota)** | Performance, Reliability, Durability, Safety | | **Software (e.g., Microsoft, Google)** | Features, Reliability, Conformance, Serviceability | | **Healthcare (e.g., Apollo Hospitals)** | SERVQUAL — Tangibles, Assurance, Empathy | | **Banking (e.g., SBI, HDFC)** | SERVQUAL — Responsiveness, Reliability, Empathy | | **Luxury Goods (e.g., Apple, Rolex)** | Aesthetics, Perceived Quality, Features | KTU examiners love linking theory to **Indian industry examples** (Tata Nano, Maruti Suzuki, ISRO missions, Apollo Hospitals, Indian Railways). Use these in your answers to score extra marks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the SERVQUAL Gap Model The SERVQUAL framework is fundamentally built on **five gaps**. We will derive the **service quality equation** step by step — this is a **guaranteed KTU 14-mark question** if framed numerically. ### **Step 1: Define the Basic Service Quality Score for Dimension $i$** For each of the 5 SERVQUAL dimensions, a customer has an **Expectation (E)** and a **Perception (P)** of actual service received. $$ SQ_i = E_i - P_i $$ Where: - $E_i$ = Customer's expectation score for dimension $i$ (rated 1–7 on Likert scale) - $P_i$ = Customer's perception score for dimension $i$ (rated 1–7 on Likert scale) ### **Step 2: Interpret the Gap** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } SQ_i &> 0 \quad \text{(Expectation exceeds Perception)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Service quality GAP exists — customer is disappointed.} \\ \text{If } SQ_i &= 0 \quad \text{(Expectation equals Perception)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Service meets expectations — minimum acceptable quality.} \\ \text{If } SQ_i &< 0 \quad \text{(Perception exceeds Expectation)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Service exceeds expectations — delight zone.} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 3: Aggregate Across All Five Dimensions** The **Overall Service Quality Score (OSQ)** is the sum of the absolute gaps across all 5 dimensions: $$ OSQ = \sum_{i=1}^{5} SQ_i = (E_1 - P_1) + (E_2 - P_2) + (E_3 - P_3) + (E_4 - P_4) + (E_5 - P_5) $$ ### **Step 4: Normalize to a 0–100 Scale** For management reporting, the SERVQUAL score is often normalized: $$ OSQ_{normalized} = \frac{OSQ}{5 \times 6} \times 100 $$ The denominator $5 \times 6 = 30$ comes from **5 dimensions × maximum 6-point gap** (since Likert scale is 1–7). ### **Step 5: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)** > A hospital surveyed 100 patients on 5 SERVQUAL dimensions. Compute the overall service quality gap. | Dimension (i) | Expectation $E_i$ (out of 7) | Perception $P_i$ (out of 7) | Gap $SQ_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1. Tangibles | 6.5 | 4.2 | +2.3 | | 2. Reliability | 6.8 | 5.0 | +1.8 | | 3. Responsiveness | 6.2 | 3.5 | +2.7 | | 4. Assurance | 6.4 | 5.8 | +0.6 | | 5. Empathy | 6.0 | 4.0 | +2.0 | | **Total OSQ** | | | **+9.4** | $$ \begin{aligned} OSQ &= 2.3 + 1.8 + 2.7 + 0.6 + 2.0 \\ &= 9.4 \text{ points (overall gap)} \\[4pt] OSQ_{normalized} &= \frac{9.4}{30} \times 100 \\ &= 31.33\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The hospital has a **31.33% service quality deficit**. The worst dimension is **Responsiveness (2.7)** — patients wait too long. The best (least bad) is **Assurance (0.6)**. The hospital should focus first on **Responsiveness improvement programs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always end a numerical SERVQUAL answer with a **prioritization recommendation** — which dimension to fix first. This shows **Apply (Level 3 of Bloom's Taxonomy)** thinking, worth 1–2 bonus marks. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Garvin vs. SERVQUAL Across KTU 2024 Course Outcomes | KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Bloom's Level | Topic Coverage | Sample Question | |---|---|---|---| | **CO1**: Recall TQM principles | Remember (L1) | List the 8 Garvin dimensions | "List Garvin's 8 dimensions" (3 marks) | | **CO2**: Explain quality frameworks | Understand (L2) | Compare Garvin vs. SERVQUAL | "Differentiate product and service quality dimensions" (7 marks) | | **CO3**: Apply quality tools | Apply (L3) | Compute SERVQUAL gap for a case | Solve the hospital example (7 marks) | | **CO4**: Analyze trade-offs | Analyze (L4) | Which dimension matters most in an industry? | "Justify which 3 dimensions matter most for ISRO" (7 marks) | ### **Quality of Design vs. Quality of Conformance (Garvin's Extension)** Garvin later expanded his framework by distinguishing: $$ Quality_{total} = Quality_{design} + Quality_{conformance} $$ - **Quality of Design:** The intentional engineering specifications chosen during product development. - **Quality of Conformance:** The degree to which the produced product **matches the design specifications**. > A product can have **excellent design** but **poor conformance** (e.g., Apple designs a great iPhone, but if 20% of units have manufacturing defects, conformance is poor). TQM addresses both ends. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Garvin's Eight Dimensions — Mermaid Concept Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Quality of a Product] --> B[Garvin 8 Dimensions] B --> C1[Performance
Primary function] B --> C2[Features
Extra capabilities] B --> C3[Reliability
Failure-free operation] B --> C4[Conformance
Meets standards] B --> C5[Durability
Useful life span] B --> C6[Serviceability
Ease of repair] B --> C7[Aesthetics
Look and feel] B --> C8[Perceived Quality
Brand image] C1 --> D1[Measurable: mph, GB, hrs] C2 --> D2[Subjective add-ons] C3 --> D3[MTBF metric] C4 --> D4[DPMO, ISO, Six Sigma] C5 --> D5[Years, cycles, hours] C6 --> D6[MTTR, service network] C7 --> D7[Industrial design team] C8 --> D8[Marketing and brand] ``` ## 4.2 SERVQUAL Five-Dimension Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Q[Service Quality SQ] --> A[Tangibles
Physical evidence] Q --> B[Reliability
Dependable delivery] Q --> C[Responsiveness
Prompt service] Q --> D[Assurance
Trust and competence] Q --> E[Empathy
Personalized care] A --> F[Customer
Expectations E] B --> F C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Customer
Perceptions P] G --> H[Gap SQ = E minus P] H --> I{Is Gap positive?} I -->|Yes| J[Service Deficiency
Improve] I -->|No| K[Service Excellence
Maintain] ``` ## 4.3 KTU Module 1 Quality Dimensions — Master Map ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Module 1: Quality Dimensions] --> N[David Garvin 1987] M --> O[Parasuraman SERVQUAL 1988] N --> P1[8 Product Dimensions] O --> P2[5 Service Dimensions] P1 --> Q1[Performance] P1 --> Q2[Features] P1 --> Q3[Reliability] P1 --> Q4[Conformance] P1 --> Q5[Durability] P1 --> Q6[Serviceability] P1 --> Q7[Aesthetics] P1 --> Q8[Perceived Quality] P2 --> R1[Tangibles] P2 --> R2[Reliability] P2 --> R3[Responsiveness] P2 --> R4[Assurance] P2 --> R5[Empathy] Q1 --> S[Applied in
Manufacturing] Q8 --> S R1 --> T[Applied in
Service Industry] R5 --> T ``` ## 4.4 Service Quality Gap Formation Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Customer Expectations E] --> B[Service Provider] A2[Management Perceptions of Expectations] --> B B --> C[Service Specification] C --> D[Service Delivery] D --> E[External Communication to Customer] E --> F[Customer Perceptions P] F --> G[Gap 5: E minus P] G --> H{Action} H --> I[Close Gap via
Quality Improvement] H --> J[Maintain via
Quality Control] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **Q:** Define *Quality* according to Philip Crosby and list any **two** of Garvin's eight dimensions of quality with one example each. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Crosby's Definition:** *"Quality is conformance to requirements (or specifications)."* According to Philip Crosby, quality is not "goodness" but simply the **degree to which a product meets its pre-defined standards**. The cost of non-conformance (CONC) is the true measure of quality. **Two dimensions of Garvin (any two, with example):** 1. **Reliability** — The probability that a product will perform its function without failure over a specific period. *Example:* A laptop's MTBF of 50,000 hours means it is expected to operate without failure for that duration. 2. **Conformance** — The degree to which a product meets pre-established engineering specifications. *Example:* A ball bearing manufactured to a tolerance of 25 mm ± 0.01 mm demonstrates high conformance if every unit falls within that range. *(Alternative valid dimensions: Performance, Features, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality — each scored equally.)* --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Q:** Differentiate between **Quality of Design** and **Quality of Conformance** as proposed by David Garvin. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Quality of Design | Quality of Conformance | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | The set of **engineering specifications and features** intentionally chosen during the product development stage. | The degree to which the **manufactured product matches** those design specifications. | | **Stage of occurrence** | Decided in **product planning / R&D** phase. | Achieved (or failed) in the **production / manufacturing** phase. | | **Responsibility** | Design engineers, R&D team. | Production workers, quality inspectors. | | **Example** | Deciding that an AC will cool 1.5-ton in a 150 sq.ft. room. | Ensuring that **every** AC unit produced actually delivers 1.5-ton cooling. | | **Improvement method** | Market research, QFD (House of Quality). | SPC, Six Sigma, statistical process control. | | **Cost impact** | High — locked in early. | Continuous — variable cost of conformance. | > Garvin's central insight: **Total Quality = Quality of Design + Quality of Conformance.** Both must be excellent. --- ## 📝 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries 14 marks, with **internal choice** (you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B). Each part-question is 7 marks. Aim for 5–6 pages of structured answer. --- ### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **Q:** *(a)* Explain in detail **Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Product Quality** with one industrial example for each dimension. *(7 marks)* > > **Q:** *(b)* A restaurant chain surveyed 200 customers using the SERVQUAL model. The average scores on a 1–7 Likert scale are as follows: > > | Dimension | Expectation $E_i$ | Perception $P_i$ | > |---|---|---| > | Tangibles | 6.4 | 4.1 | > | Reliability | 6.7 | 5.2 | > | Responsiveness | 6.0 | 3.0 | > | Assurance | 6.5 | 5.5 | > | Empathy | 5.8 | 3.8 | > > Compute the overall service quality gap and recommend the **top priority area for improvement**. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution (a) — Garvin's 8 Dimensions (7 marks)** **(Award 0.75–1 mark per dimension for clear definition + 0.25 mark for example. Total 8 × 0.875 = 7 marks.)** 1. **Performance** — The primary operating characteristics of a product. *Example:* The mileage of a Maruti Swift Dzire is 24 km/litre (ARAI certified). 2. **Features** — Secondary characteristics that supplement the basic functioning. *Example:* The Maruti Swift comes with a touchscreen infotainment system, push-button start, and reverse parking camera as additional features. 3. **Reliability** — The probability of failure-free operation over time. *Example:* Tata Power solar inverters are rated with an MTBF of 100,000 hours. 4. **Conformance** — Degree to which the product meets pre-set standards. *Example:* A pharmaceutical tablet that must contain exactly 500 mg ± 5 mg of active ingredient. 5. **Durability** — Length of useful life of a product. *Example:* A Voltas AC is engineered for 10–12 years of useful life. 6. **Serviceability** — Ease and speed of repair. *Example:* Apple iPhones have a serviceability score defined by the average wait time at Apple Authorized Service Centers (typically 2–4 days). 7. **Aesthetics** — Sensory appeal — look, feel, sound. *Example:* The matte finish and minimalist design of OnePlus phones, or the "thud" sound of a Mercedes door closing. 8. **Perceived Quality** — Customer's subjective impression of quality, often based on brand. *Example:* The Rolex brand commands a 10× price premium over a generic watch of similar mechanical quality, purely due to perceived quality. **Conclude with:** *"These eight dimensions, when mapped together, allow managers to diagnose quality gaps in their products and prioritize improvement initiatives."* (1 mark) #### **Model Solution (b) — SERVQUAL Numerical (7 marks)** **Step 1: Compute per-dimension gap (1 mark)** | Dimension | $E_i$ | $P_i$ | $SQ_i = E_i - P_i$ | |---|---|---|---| | Tangibles | 6.4 | 4.1 | +2.3 | | Reliability | 6.7 | 5.2 | +1.5 | | Responsiveness | 6.0 | 3.0 | **+3.0** | | Assurance | 6.5 | 5.5 | +1.0 | | Empathy | 5.8 | 3.8 | +2.0 | **Step 2: Sum to compute Overall Service Quality (OSQ) (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} OSQ &= 2.3 + 1.5 + 3.0 + 1.0 + 2.0 \\ &= 9.8 \text{ points (raw gap)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Normalize to percentage (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} OSQ_{normalized} &= \frac{9.8}{5 \times 6} \times 100 \\ &= \frac{9.8}{30} \times 100 \\ &= 32.67\% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Interpretation (2 marks)** The restaurant has a **32.67% service quality deficit** — significant. All five dimensions show positive gaps, indicating that **service is below customer expectations across the board**. The customers are **systematically disappointed** in every aspect of service. **Step 5: Recommendation (2 marks)** The **top priority is Responsiveness (gap = 3.0)** — the largest deficit. Customers are most dissatisfied with the **speed and willingness** of staff to respond. Recommended actions: - Reduce average waiting time for tables and orders. - Train staff on faster order-taking and problem resolution. - Install a real-time queue management system. - Hire additional waiters during peak hours. Secondary priority is **Tangibles (gap = 2.3)** — refurbish interiors, upgrade lighting, ensure uniformed staff. The **best-performing dimension is Assurance (gap = 1.0)**, which should be maintained as a strength. --- ### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Q:** *(a)* Describe the **SERVQUAL model** of service quality. List its five dimensions and explain any **two** in detail with examples from the **Indian banking or healthcare industry**. *(7 marks)* > > **Q:** *(b)* Critically compare **Garvin's product quality dimensions** with the **SERVQUAL service quality dimensions**. Why is a single set of dimensions insufficient to capture both product and service quality? *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution (a) — SERVQUAL Model (7 marks)** **Introduction (1.5 marks):** > [!NOTE] > The **SERVQUAL model** (Service Quality Gap Model) was developed by **Valarie A. Zeithaml, A. Parasuraman, and Leonard L. Berry** in **1988** in their seminal paper *"SERVQUAL: A Multiple-Item Scale for Measuring Consumer Perceptions of Service Quality"* (Journal of Marketing). > > It is based on the premise that service quality is measured by the **gap between customer expectations (E) and customer perceptions (P)** of the actual service received. **Mathematical Form (1 mark):** $$ SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{5} (E_i - P_i) \quad \text{for } i \in \{\text{Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy}\} $$ **List of 5 Dimensions (1 mark):** 1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance. 2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. 3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. 4. **Assurance** — Employees' knowledge and courtesy that inspire trust and confidence. 5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers. **Detailed Explanation of Two Dimensions (3.5 marks total — 1.75 each):** **Dimension 1: Reliability (1.75 marks)** - **Definition:** The ability to deliver the promised service **consistently, dependably, and accurately** every single time. - **Indian Banking Example:** HDFC Bank's promise of "**4-hour home loan disbursement**" is a Reliability commitment. If 95% of applications are disbursed within 4 hours, the bank's Reliability score is high. If delays become frequent, the SERVQUAL Reliability gap widens. - **Indian Healthcare Example:** Apollo Hospitals' diagnostic labs promise "**blood test reports in 6 hours**." Consistent delivery of this report is a Reliability metric — failure causes patient dissatisfaction. **Dimension 2: Empathy (1.75 marks)** - **Definition:** The provision of **caring, individualized attention** to each customer, recognizing their unique needs. - **Indian Banking Example:** Senior-citizen priority counters at SBI, personalized relationship managers for high-net-worth customers at ICICI, doorstep banking for elderly/disabled customers. - **Indian Healthcare Example:** Tata Memorial Hospital assigning dedicated nurse navigators to each cancer patient, personal follow-up calls after discharge, and home-care visits for terminal patients. #### **Model Solution (b) — Critical Comparison (7 marks)** **Comprehensive Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Basis of Comparison | Garvin's 8 Dimensions | SERVQUAL 5 Dimensions | |---|---|---| | **Domain** | Manufactured, tangible products | Intangible services | | **Number** | 8 dimensions | 5 dimensions | | **Measurement** | Mostly objective + some subjective | Entirely gap-based (subjective Likert) | | **Originator** | David A. Garvin (HBR, 1987) | Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry (JM, 1988) | | **Tangible focus** | Aesthetics, Performance, Features | Tangibles | | **Time element** | Durability, Reliability | Reliability (focused on timeliness) | | **Customer interaction** | Perceived Quality (one-shot impression) | Empathy, Responsiveness, Assurance (ongoing) | | **Application** | Six Sigma, QFD, benchmarking | Customer satisfaction surveys, service auditing | **Why a single framework is insufficient (4 marks):** 1. **Tangibility difference** (1 mark): Products are physical and can be inspected, tested, and measured objectively (e.g., a car's mileage). Services are intangible — you cannot "hold" a hospital's surgery — so SERVQUAL uses **perception-based** measurement, while Garvin uses **attribute-based** measurement. 2. **Customer interaction intensity** (1 mark): Services are produced and consumed **simultaneously** (a haircut, a bank transaction), requiring empathy and responsiveness dimensions. Products are produced first, then sold later, so Garvin's framework focuses on **pre-sale** attributes. 3. **Storage and inventory** (1 mark): Products can be stored and inspected at any point; services **cannot be stored**. Quality defects in services are **real-time and irrecoverable**, requiring SERVQUAL's gap model to capture the **moment-of-truth** experience. 4. **Customer participation** (1 mark): In services, the customer is a **co-producer** (e.g., a patient must describe symptoms accurately to a doctor). Garvin's framework assumes the customer is a **passive recipient**. Empathy and Assurance in SERVQUAL account for this co-production. **Concluding statement (1 mark):** > *"Garvin's framework is a 'supply-side' engineering lens; SERVQUAL is a 'demand-side' customer perception lens. KTU 2024 recognizes that modern economies need BOTH lenses, as India transitions from a product-based to a service-based economy."* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid these to gain 3–5 extra marks)** > > 1. **Memorizing without examples** — KTU examiners award marks for *industry-specific examples* (Tata, Maruti, Apollo, ISRO, SBI). Bare definitions without examples = lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Confusing Reliability (Garvin) with Reliability (SERVQUAL)** — In Garvin, Reliability is **MTBF of a product**. In SERVQUAL, Reliability is **dependability of service delivery**. They share a word but mean different things. Always specify the framework. > 3. **Skipping normalization in numerical problems** — If you compute only the raw SERVQUAL gap and forget to normalize to a percentage, you lose 1 mark. > 4. **Not recommending action** — A SERVQUAL calculation without a "**top priority dimension and improvement plan**" loses 1.5 marks. Always end with **Apply (L3 Bloom)** thinking. > 5. **Writing Garvin dimensions in any order** — While no strict order is required, examiners prefer the **canonical order (Performance → Features → Reliability → Conformance → Durability → Serviceability → Aesthetics → Perceived Quality)**. Deviation without reason may cause examiner confusion. > 6. **Forgetting Quality of Design vs. Conformance** — A 14-mark answer that omits Garvin's **two-tier view** (Design + Conformance) will likely lose 1.5 marks. Examiners consider it a *favorite* short-note question. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read 5 minutes before the exam)** - [x] **Garvin (1987) = 8 Product Dimensions** — Mnemonic: **"P-F-R-C-D-S-A-P"** (Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality). - [x] **SERVQUAL (1988) = 5 Service Dimensions** — Mnemonic: **"T-R-R-A-E"** (Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy). - [x] **Reliability (Garvin)** = **MTBF** (product failure probability). - [x] **Serviceability (Garvin)** = **MTTR** (repair time). - [x] **Conformance** is measured by **DPMO (≤ 3.4 in Six Sigma)**. - [x] **Aesthetics** is the **most subjective** Garvin dimension. - [x] **Perceived Quality** is influenced by **brand and marketing** — it can diverge from objective quality. - [x] **SERVQUAL formula:** $SQ_i = E_i - P_i$; positive gap = customer dissatisfaction. - [x] **Normalize SERVQUAL** by dividing by 30 (5 dimensions × 6-point max gap) and multiplying by 100. - [x] **Garvin's two-tier view:** Total Quality = **Quality of Design** + **Quality of Conformance**. - [x] **Garvin = product-side / supply-side / engineering lens**; **SERVQUAL = service-side / demand-side / customer-perception lens**. - [x] Always give an **Indian industry example** — Tata, Maruti, ISRO, Apollo, SBI, HDFC, Reliance Jio. - [x] End numerical SERVQUAL answers with a **prioritization recommendation** (Apply-level thinking). - [x] The KTU 2024 examiner LOVES framing 14-mark questions as *"Explain with industrial examples"* — always include 2–3 examples per dimension. - [x] **Kano Model** is a related (but distinct) framework — sometimes asked in Module 1 — that classifies quality attributes into **Must-be, One-dimensional, and Attractive** categories. Not part of Garvin/SERVQUAL but appears in KTU paper 2 questions. --- **End of Module 1 — Dimensions of Quality Notes** *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) for Course PEIET631 — Total Quality Management and Six Sigma*

topics.title: TQM - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** TQM - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM)

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to embed **quality** in every aspect of the business — from product design and supplier selection to manufacturing, delivery, and after-sales service — by involving **all employees** (top management, middle management, supervisors, and shop-floor workers) in a culture of continuous improvement and customer satisfaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Board-Standard Definition:**
> *TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, fact-based, people-driven, and continuously improving management system that integrates all functions of an organization to deliver superior value to both internal and external customers.*

The three core words of the term carry specific meaning in KTU syllabus:

- **Total** → Involves *every* person, *every* department, *every* process, *every* level of the organization (top to bottom, supplier to customer).
- **Quality** → The degree to which a product or service meets or exceeds customer *expectations* (not just specifications) — defined by the customer, not the producer.
- **Management** → A *system*, not a slogan; it requires leadership commitment, planning, measurement, control, and continuous improvement (often modeled on the **PDCA Cycle – Plan-Do-Check-Act** popularized by W. Edwards Deming).

---

### 1.2 Definitions by Quality Gurus (Board-Favorite Content)

KTU examiners frequently test definitions given by pioneers. Memorize these precisely:

| Guru | Definition (Verbatim / Standard Academic Form) |
|------|---------------------------------------------|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | *"A system of interdependent activities that involve everyone in the organization — management and workforce — in a continuous effort to improve quality at every level."* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | *"A set of quality activities that constitute the universal sequence of: Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement."* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | *"A preventive style of management wherein quality is defined as conformance to requirements, and the system of quality is prevention, not inspection."* |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | *"A company-wide system for producing goods and services that satisfy customer requirements at the lowest possible cost, involving every employee."* |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | *"An effective system for integrating the quality development, quality maintenance, and quality improvement efforts of the various groups in an organization so as to enable production and service at the most economical levels which allow for full customer satisfaction."* |
| **ISO 8402 (Legacy Standard)** | *"A management approach of an organization, centered on quality, based on the participation of all its members and aiming at long-term success through customer satisfaction and benefits to all members of the organization and to society."* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva / Short Notes:** *"De-Ju-Cro-Ishi-Feige"* — Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum. Any one of these five may appear as a 3-mark Part A question.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Five-Star Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a **five-star restaurant** where the head chef decides to make *every dish* perfect, not just the gourmet items on the menu.

- **"Total"** → The head chef, the sous chef, the line cooks, the dishwashers, the servers, and *even the gardener* who supplies herbs are responsible for quality. No one is "off the hook."
- **"Quality"** → A dish is "quality" not when it follows the recipe (specification), but when the *guest comes back tomorrow* and orders the same dish again (customer satisfaction).
- **"Management"** → The head chef installs a system: every day, after service, the team reviews what went wrong (the burnt sauce, the late order), writes it down (data/facts), and changes the process for tomorrow (continuous improvement — PDCA).

Just as in a kitchen, TQM in a factory or service firm means: **quality is not inspected in at the end — it is built in from the beginning, by every person, every day.**

---

### 1.4 Why TQM Matters — The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Strategic Pillars of TQM (High-Yield for KTU):**
