> 1. **Customer Focus** — Internal & external customers define quality.
> 2. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — There is no final destination; quality is a journey.
> 3. **People Involvement** — Every employee, from CEO to operator, is a stakeholder in quality.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy — "Why TQM Exists"

TQM emerged in post-WWII Japan (1950s) when Japanese industrialists — facing destroyed infrastructure and a reputation for cheap, defective goods — invited American statisticians **Deming, Juran, and Feigenbaum** to teach them how to rebuild quality from the ground up. The Japanese applied these teachings with religious discipline, and within two decades, "Made in Japan" transformed from a joke into a global benchmark of excellence.

The fundamental philosophical shifts TQM demands from traditional management are:

1. **From Inspection → Prevention** (Crosby's core message): Quality cannot be "tested into" a product after production. Defects must be prevented at the source by improving the process.
2. **From Departmental Silos → Cross-Functional Teams**: Quality is not the Quality Department's job alone. Engineering, Production, Marketing, HR, Finance — all share responsibility.
3. **From Internal Standards → External Customer Satisfaction**: Quality is not "meeting specifications drawn up internally" — it is "meeting or exceeding what the customer actually needs."
4. **From Blame-the-Worker → Fix-the-System (Deming's 85/15 Rule)**: Deming estimated that **~85% of quality problems** are attributable to *systems and processes*, and only ~15% to *individual workers*. Therefore, management owns the system.
5. **From Static → Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)**: Standards are baselines, not ceilings. Every process can be improved every day.

---

### 2.2 The TQM Framework — Eight Building Blocks

KTU examiners often ask students to "explain the elements/principles of TQM." The following eight-element framework is the most frequently tested in KTU 2024 Scheme papers:

| # | Element | Core Idea | KTU Examiner's Cue |
|---|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Customer Focus** | Identify internal & external customers; measure their satisfaction (e.g., CSAT, NPS). | "Customer is the final inspector." |
| 2 | **Total Employee Involvement / Participation** | Quality is everyone's job. Use quality circles, suggestion schemes. | "From boardroom to shop-floor." |
| 3 | **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** | Small, incremental improvements daily; PDCA cycle. | "There is no finish line." |
| 4 | **Process-Centered Approach** | Manage the process, not the people. If the process is right, results follow. | "Work on the system, not in it." |
| 5 | **Integrated System** | All departments must function as one unified system with shared quality goals. | "One company, one plan." |
| 6 | **Strategic & Systematic Approach** | Quality goals must align with the organization's strategic vision and mission. | "Quality must be in the strategic plan." |
| 7 | **Fact-Based Decision Making** | Use data, statistics, SPC charts — not intuition or guesswork. | "In God we trust; all others bring data." |
| 8 | **Communication** | Effective two-way communication at all levels during times of change. | "Silence is the enemy of quality." |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Mnemonic:** *"Cu-E-C-P-I-S-F-Co"* (Customer, Employees, Continuous Improvement, Process, Integrated, Strategic, Fact-based, Communication).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" — TQM Conceptual Metrics

Although TQM is qualitative, the following measurable ratios and indices are **commonly tested** in KTU papers (especially as 7-mark or 14-mark questions involving numerical illustrations):

$$
\text{Defect Rate (DPMO)} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Number of Units Produced}} \times 1{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\text{First Pass Yield (FPY)} = \frac{\text{Units Passing Inspection First Time}}{\text{Total Units Inspected}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Customer Satisfaction Index (CSI)} = \frac{\text{Average Customer Rating}}{\text{Maximum Possible Rating}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

$$
\text{Sigma Level (\sigma)} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \text{Defects per Million Opportunities (DPMO)}
$$

| Sigma Level | DPMO | Yield (%) | Real-World Analogy |
|:-----------:|:----:|:---------:|:-------------------|
| **2σ** | 308,537 | 69.15% | Roughly average world company |
| **3σ** | 66,807 | 93.32% | Typical Indian service industry benchmark |
| **4σ** | 6,210 | 99.38% | Good — typical "world-class" claim |
| **5σ** | 233 | 99.977% | Excellent — Six Sigma aspirational |
| **6σ** | 3.4 | 99.99966% | World-class — Motorola / GE benchmark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *DPMO* (defects per *million opportunities*) with *defect rate*. Always check the denominator: **DPMO = (Defects ÷ Opportunities for one defect) × 1,000,000.** This is the bridge between *TQM* (philosophy) and *Six Sigma* (the metric-heavy extension covered later in the syllabus).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of TQM

- **Manufacturing:** Reduction of rework, scrap, and warranty claims (e.g., Toyota Production System = living TQM).
- **Software Engineering:** CMMI (Capability Maturity Model Integration) is a TQM-aligned process-improvement framework.
- **Construction:** ISO 9001-based TQM systems reduce rework and site accidents.
- **Healthcare:** TQM reduces patient readmission rates and surgical errors (e.g., Virginia Mason Medical Center, USA).
- **Education:** Outcome-Based Education (OBE) — which is the basis of KTU's own 2024 Scheme — is itself an application of TQM philosophy to academic delivery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: How the "Total" in TQM is Quantified

The conceptual claim of TQM — "every employee impacts quality" — can be illustrated quantitatively using the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** model. This derivation shows how TQM's "prevention-based" approach mathematically outperforms the traditional "inspection-based" approach.

**Step 1 — Define the four cost components of quality (Feigenbaum's model):**

$$
COPQ_{\text{Total}} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{Prevention}}$ = Cost of activities to *prevent* defects (training, process design, supplier capability development).
- $C_{\text{Appraisal}}$ = Cost of *inspecting* for defects (testing, audits, QC labs).
- $C_{\text{Internal Failure}}$ = Cost of defects found *before* shipment (rework, scrap, downtime).
- $C_{\text{External Failure}}$ = Cost of defects found *after* shipment (warranty, returns, lawsuits, lost reputation).

**Step 2 — Express total quality cost as a function of prevention investment ($x$):**

Empirically (Juran's cost model), as prevention investment $x$ increases:

$$
C_{\text{Prevention}}(x) = a \cdot x \quad (\text{linear increase})
$$

$$
C_{\text{Appraisal}}(x) \approx \text{constant or slowly rising}
$$

$$
C_{\text{Internal Failure}}(x) = \frac{b}{x} \quad (\text{hyperbolic decline})
$$

$$
C_{\text{External Failure}}(x) = \frac{c}{x^2} \quad (\text{steep hyperbolic decline})
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the costs to obtain the total quality cost curve (the "Quality Cost Curve"):**

$$
COPQ_{\text{Total}}(x) = a \cdot x + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + \frac{b}{x} + \frac{c}{x^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the optimum by differentiating with respect to $x$ and setting to zero:**

$$
\frac{d\,COPQ_{\text{Total}}}{dx} = a - \frac{b}{x^2} - \frac{2c}{x^3} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to identify the economically optimal prevention investment:**

$$
a = \frac{b}{x^2} + \frac{2c}{x^3}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the result in plain engineering language:**

> The *optimal* prevention investment is the value of $x$ at which a marginal rupee spent on prevention eliminates exactly one rupee of failure cost. Beyond this point, additional prevention spending is wasteful; below it, the firm is over-spending on failure.

This is the **economic justification** of TQM — it is not charity or "feel-good" management; it is a **mathematically demonstrable cost-minimization strategy**.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of TQM Guru Philosophies

| Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby | Ishikawa | Feigenbaum |
|-----------|--------|-------|--------|----------|------------|
| **Country** | USA | USA | USA | Japan | USA |
| **Era** | 1950s+ | 1950s+ | 1970s+ | 1960s+ | 1950s+ |
| **Core Focus** | System / Variation | Planning / Control | Prevention / Conformance | Company-wide / Tools | Total Quality Control |
| **Famous Tool** | PDCA / 14 Points | Quality Trilogy | "Quality is Free" | Fishbone / QC Circles | Quality Costs |
| **View of Defects** | Result of system variation | Result of poor planning | Result of non-conformance | Result of process issues | Result of poor coordination |
| **View of Workers** | 85% system, 15% worker | Need to be trained in quality | Must be motivated to "do it right first time" | Every worker is a quality engineer | All functions share responsibility |
| **Quality Definition** | Predictable uniformity | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Customer satisfaction at lowest cost | Customer satisfaction at lowest cost |
| **KTU Typical Question** | "Explain Deming's 14 Points" | "Explain Juran's Quality Trilogy" | "Explain Crosby's 14 Steps" | "Explain Ishikawa's QC Circles" | "Explain Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control" |

---

### 3.3 The Evolution of Quality Management — Analytical Timeline

| Period | Era | Dominant Philosophy | Key Figure(s) |
|--------|-----|--------------------|--------------|
| **Pre-1920** | Craft Inspection | One craftsman inspected his own work | Guild system |
| **1920–1940** | Quality Control (QC) | Statistical sampling inspection | Shewhart (Control Charts) |
| **1940–1960** | Statistical Quality Control (SQC) | Statistical methods applied to process control | Deming, Juran |
| **1960–1980** | Total Quality Control (TQC) | Quality is a company-wide responsibility | Ishikawa, Feigenbaum |
| **1980–Present** | **Total Quality Management (TQM)** | Customer-driven, continuous improvement, people-powered | All gurus + Japanese practitioners |
| **1986–Present** | Six Sigma | Data-driven DMAIC methodology | Motorola, Jack Welch (GE) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If asked *"How is TQM different from TQC?"*, the standard answer is: **TQC** (Ishikawa / Feigenbaum) emphasized *company-wide quality control* but was primarily a *manufacturing-floor* discipline; **TQM** broadened this to include *all functions* (marketing, HR, finance, R&D) and added the *strategic and customer-satisfaction* dimensions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TQM Framework — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Leadership and Top Management Commitment]) --> nodeB[Define Vision, Mission, and Quality Policy]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Internal and External Customers]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Map Core Processes and Value Chain]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Establish Quality Metrics and KPIs]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Train All Employees on Quality Tools]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Deploy PDCA Cycle Across Processes]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Empower Quality Circles and Suggestion Schemes]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Measure Outcomes via SPC and Audits]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{Standards Met?}
    nodeJ -- No --> nodeK[Root Cause Analysis with Fishbone and 5 Why]
    nodeK --> nodeG
    nodeJ -- Yes --> nodeL[Standardize and Document Best Practice]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Continuous Improvement Kaizen Loop]
    nodeM --> nodeG
    nodeM --> nodeN([Customer Satisfaction and Organizational Excellence])
```

### 4.2 The PDCA (Deming) Cycle — Foundation of Continuous Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[PLAN: Identify problem and plan a change] --> nodeD[DO: Implement the change on a small scale]
    nodeD --> nodeC[CHECK: Measure and analyze results]
    nodeC --> nodeA[ACT: If successful, standardize; if not, revise plan]
    nodeA --> nodeP
```

### 4.3 TQM Guru Influence Map — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot([TQM - Total Quality Management])
    nodeRoot --> nodeUSA[American School]
    nodeRoot --> nodeJPN[Japanese School]
    nodeUSA --> nodeDem[Deming: System and Variation]
    nodeUSA --> nodeJur[Juran: Quality Trilogy]
    nodeUSA --> nodeCro[Crosby: Prevention and Conformance]
    nodeUSA --> nodeFei[Feigenbaum: Total Quality Control]
    nodeJPN --> nodeIsh[Ishikawa: Company Wide Quality and QC Circles]
    nodeJPN --> nodeTag[Taguchi: Loss Function and Robust Design]
    nodeDem --> nodePDCA[PDCA Cycle]
    nodeJur --> nodeTrilogy[Quality Planning, Control, Improvement]
    nodeCro --> nodeZDM[Zero Defects Mindset]
    nodeIsh --> nodeQC[Quality Circles and Fishbone Tool]
    nodePDCA --> nodeTQMcore[Core TQM Engine]
    nodeTrilogy --> nodeTQMcore
    nodeZDM --> nodeTQMcore
    nodeQC --> nodeTQMcore
    nodeTQMcore --> nodeSix[Six Sigma Extension DMAIC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Cue:** When a question asks you to "draw the PDCA cycle" or "explain TQM as a continuous loop," the diagram above is the *minimum acceptable* schematic. Adding the **customer at the top** and the **improvement feedback arrow** elevates a 7-mark answer to a 14-mark answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Total Quality Management (TQM). Mention any two quality gurus who contributed to its development.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** *Total Quality Management (TQM) is a comprehensive, customer-focused management philosophy that seeks to continuously improve the quality of products, services, and processes by involving all members of the organization — from top management to shop-floor workers — in a structured system of planning, control, and improvement, with the ultimate aim of achieving long-term customer satisfaction and organizational excellence.*
>
> **Two Gurus (1 mark):** *W. Edwards Deming (PDCA cycle, 14 Points for Management) and Joseph M. Juran (Quality Trilogy: Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement).* (Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum are equally acceptable.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any six elements / principles of Total Quality Management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

> 1. Customer Focus (Internal and External)
> 2. Total Employee Involvement
> 3. Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)
> 4. Process-Centered Approach
> 5. Integrated System
> 6. Strategic and Systematic Approach
> 7. Fact-Based Decision Making
> 8. Effective Communication

*(Any six in correct wording carry full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** *Define Total Quality Management in detail. Explain the eight key elements / principles of TQM.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the contributions of any three Quality Gurus (Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum) to the development of TQM, with reference to their core philosophies and famous tools.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
TQM is a holistic, organization-wide management philosophy and system that integrates all functions — design, production, marketing, service, finance, HR — to focus continuously on meeting or exceeding customer expectations (both internal and external), by involving every employee in a culture of prevention, fact-based decision-making, and continuous process improvement.

**Eight Elements (5 marks — ½ to 1 mark each, depth proportional to marks):**

1. **Customer Focus:** Identify and satisfy internal and external customers. Measure satisfaction regularly using surveys, CSAT, NPS, complaints data.
2. **Total Employee Involvement:** All employees participate through quality circles, suggestion schemes, cross-functional teams. Empowerment + training.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen):** PDCA cycle applied daily to all processes. Small, incremental improvements compound over time.
4. **Process-Centered Approach:** Manage the process (system), not the people. Use process maps, SIPOC, FMEA to standardize and stabilize.
5. **Integrated System:** All departments must function as one unified system with a shared quality vision. Eliminate silos.
6. **Strategic & Systematic Approach:** Quality is a strategic objective embedded in the organization's mission, not a bolt-on program.
7. **Fact-Based Decision Making:** Use data — SPC charts, Pareto analysis, hypothesis testing — to drive decisions, not intuition.
8. **Communication:** Maintain effective two-way communication during periods of change; share quality metrics transparently.

*(A well-labeled diagram of the TQM framework will fetch bonus marks under KTU valuation rules.)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison (≈2 marks per guru; final ≈1 mark for the comparative synthesis):**

| Dimension | Deming | Juran | Crosby |
|-----------|--------|-------|--------|
| **Core Philosophy** | Quality is a system; reduce variation in processes. | Quality has three components: planning, control, improvement. | Quality is conformance to requirements; prevention, not inspection. |
| **Famous Tool/Framework** | PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel); 14 Points for Management | Juran's Quality Trilogy; Pareto Principle (80/20) | 14 Steps to Quality Improvement; Zero Defects; "Quality is Free" book |
| **Quality Definition** | Predictable uniformity and reliability at lowest cost. | Fitness for use (actual vs. intended use). | Conformance to requirements (specifications). |
| **View of Workers** | Workers are part of the system; 85% of problems are systemic, 15% are worker-related. | Workers need training in quality management tools. | Workers must be motivated and recognized for doing things right the first time. |
| **View of Management's Role** | Top management must drive the transformation with constancy of purpose. | Middle management must lead the quality trilogy project by project. | Management must establish the commitment to zero defects from the top. |
| **Primary Target** | System-wide statistical process control. | Project-by-project breakthrough improvement. | Cost reduction via prevention culture. |

**Comparative Synthesis (1 mark):**
> While Deming focused on *statistical process control and systemic variation*, Juran emphasized *managerial planning and project-based improvement*, and Crosby championed a *cultural and motivational shift toward prevention and zero defects*. Together, these three pillars — *system, planning, and culture* — formed the foundation upon which modern TQM was built.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) model. Show graphically how total quality cost varies with prevention investment, and derive the condition for the economically optimal level of prevention spending.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Define TQM. Discuss the evolution of quality management from Inspection → QC → SQC → TQC → TQM → Six Sigma with a clear timeline and key contributors.* **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**COPQ Components (3 marks):**

COPQ has four components:

1. **Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring — e.g., quality training, supplier capability development, process design, preventive equipment maintenance.
2. **Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Costs of *detecting* defects — e.g., incoming inspection, in-process testing, lab testing, quality audits.
3. **Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Costs of defects found *before* the customer receives them — e.g., rework, scrap, re-testing, downtime, concession.
4. **External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Costs of defects found *after* the customer receives them — e.g., warranty claims, returns, repairs, lawsuits, lost goodwill, customer churn.

$$
COPQ_{\text{Total}} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

**Quality Cost Curve (2 marks):**

As prevention investment ($x$) increases:
- $C_P$ increases linearly.
- $C_A$ remains roughly constant or rises slightly.
- $C_{IF}$ decreases sharply (hyperbolic decline).
- $C_{EF}$ decreases very sharply (steep hyperbolic decline).

The **total COPQ curve** is **U-shaped**: it is high when $x \to 0$ (no prevention, all failures) and high when $x \to \infty$ (over-investment in prevention). The **minimum** of the curve corresponds to the *optimal* prevention investment.

**Derivation of the Optimum (2 marks):**

$$
COPQ(x) = a x + \frac{b}{x} + \frac{c}{x^2} + C_A
$$

$$
\frac{d\,COPQ}{dx} = a - \frac{b}{x^2} - \frac{2c}{x^3} = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow\quad a = \frac{b}{x^2} + \frac{2c}{x^3}
$$

This is the **economically optimal prevention investment condition** — beyond it, every additional rupee in prevention saves less than one rupee in failure cost; below it, the firm is losing money by under-investing in prevention.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**TQM Definition (1 mark):** *TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, fact-based, people-driven, and continuously improving management system involving all members of the organization in achieving long-term customer satisfaction and organizational excellence.*

**Evolutionary Timeline (6 marks — 1 mark per era):**

1. **Pre-1920 — Craft Inspection:** Individual craftsmen inspected their own work; quality was the maker's personal pride.
2. **1920–1940 — Quality Control (QC):** Introduction of mass production created the need for *separate* inspection departments. Walter Shewhart (Bell Labs, 1924) developed the **statistical control chart**.
3. **1940–1960 — Statistical Quality Control (SQC):** During WWII, statistical methods were applied to munitions and military supplies. W. Edwards Deming and Joseph Juran taught SQC to Japanese engineers (1950s).
4. **1960–1980 — Total Quality Control (TQC):** Kaoru Ishikawa and Armand Feigenbaum broadened QC to *all* functions and *all* employees. Ishikawa introduced **Quality Circles** and the **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** in Japan.
5. **1980–Present — Total Quality Management (TQM):** Western companies (under competitive pressure from Japan) adopted and named the philosophy "TQM." Added emphasis on customer satisfaction, strategic alignment, and leadership commitment.
6. **1986–Present — Six Sigma:** Motorola (Mikel Harry, Bill Smith) launched a data-driven DMAIC extension of TQM. General Electric under Jack Welch (1995) made it globally famous. Goal: **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO).**

**Concluding Statement (included in marks):**
> *TQM is the philosophical foundation; Six Sigma is the metric-driven, project-based methodology that operationalizes TQM using DMAIC and the DMAIC toolset. Together, they form the modern quality-management ecosystem taught in this course.*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in TQM Definition Questions:**
> 1. **Writing "TQM means total quality" — ❌ No marks.** You must write a *full definitional sentence* including customer focus, people involvement, continuous improvement, and system thinking.
> 2. **Confusing TQM with TQC.** Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you use the terms interchangeably. TQC = company-wide QC (Ishikawa, 1960s); TQM = strategic, customer-centric, organization-wide (1980s onwards).
> 3. **Quoting only Deming and Juran and "forgetting" Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum.** KTU papers since 2020 have shown at least one question per paper referencing Crosby's "Quality is Free" or Ishikawa's QC Circles.
> 4. **Writing the elements in random order with no explanation.** Listing "Customer focus, Teamwork, Kaizen" without *defining each* in one line yields only 1 out of 3 marks in Part A.
> 5. **Mixing up DPMO and DPPM.** Six Sigma's benchmark is **3.4 DPMO = 3.4 defects per million *opportunities***, not per million units. State this clearly.
> 6. **Skipping the derivation in COPQ questions.** Even if the answer is "the curve is U-shaped," you *must* show the derivative = 0 step for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **✅ Rapid-Revision Checklist — TQM Definition**
>
> - [ ] TQM = **Total** (all people, all functions) + **Quality** (customer-defined) + **Management** (system, not slogan).
> - [ ] Three Pillars: **Customer Focus**, **Continuous Improvement**, **People Involvement**.
> - [ ] Five Gurus: **Deming, Juran, Crosby, Ishikawa, Feigenbaum** — know at least one signature contribution of each.
> - [ ] Deming's **PDCA Cycle** (Plan–Do–Check–Act) = engine of continuous improvement.
> - [ ] Deming's **85/15 Rule**: ~85% of quality problems are systemic; only ~15% are worker-related.
> - [ ] Juran's **Quality Trilogy**: Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement.
> - [ ] Crosby's motto: **"Quality is conformance to requirements; prevention, not inspection."** Aim: **Zero Defects**.
> - [ ] Ishikawa's contributions: **Quality Circles** (small-group worker problem-solving teams) and the **Fishbone Diagram** (cause-and-effect analysis tool).
> - [ ] Feigenbaum coined **"Total Quality Control"** in 1956 — the direct precursor of TQM.
> - [ ] Eight Elements of TQM: Customer Focus, Total Employee Involvement, Continuous Improvement, Process-Centered Approach, Integrated System, Strategic Approach, Fact-Based Decisions, Communication.
> - [ ] Evolution: **Inspection → QC → SQC → TQC → TQM → Six Sigma**.
> - [ ] COPQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$; total cost curve is U-shaped in prevention investment; optimum when $\frac{d\,COPQ}{dx} = 0$.
> - [ ] Six Sigma benchmark = **3.4 DPMO** at **6σ** level.
> - [ ] TQM is *philosophy*; Six Sigma is *methodology* (DMAIC) — never use the words interchangeably.
> - [ ] KTU 2024 Scheme values **diagrammed answers** — always include a labeled block diagram for 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gurus of TQM - Deming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Gurus of TQM - Deming # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## W. Edwards Deming — The Father of Statistical Quality Control > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > **W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)** was an American engineer, statistician, and management consultant whose work laid the foundation of modern **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. He is globally recognized as the intellectual architect of the post-war **Japanese industrial revolution**, and his philosophy emphasizes **statistical process control, systems thinking, and management responsibility** for quality rather than worker blame. --- ### 📘 Background Snapshot | Attribute | Detail | |---|---| | **Full Name** | William Edwards Deming | | **Birth–Death** | 14 October 1900 – 20 December 1993 | | **Nationality** | American | | **Primary Fields** | Statistics, Operations Research, Management Theory | | **Key Works** | *Out of the Crisis* (1982), *The New Economics* (1993) | | **Japanese Recognition** | The **Deming Prize** (established 1951) is Japan's most prestigious award for quality, awarded annually in his honor | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Deming taught top Japanese engineers in **1950** at the invitation of Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE). His 14 Points became the spiritual backbone of Japan's industrial rebirth. --- ### 🍎 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition — "The Hospital Ward" Imagine a hospital ward where patients are not recovering as expected. A traditional manager would blame the **nurses** (front-line workers). Deming would instead walk in and ask: 1. *Who designed the shift roster?* → **System** 2. *Were the right medicines stocked in the supply chain?* → **Process** 3. *Is the building's ventilation system causing infections?* → **Environment** 4. *Are the doctors trained in the latest protocols?* → **Knowledge** > The nurses are only the *last link* in a long chain. **94% of problems belong to the system; only 6% to the worker.** This is the Deming worldview. Think of an organization as a **river**. Workers drink the water (operate within the system) that management pours (designs the system). Most quality problems are *upstream* in management's hands. --- ### 🎯 The Three Pillars of Deming's Thinking > [!TIP] > **Memorize this triad — it appears repeatedly in KTU exams:** > 1. **Appreciation for a System** — understanding the whole, not just parts > 2. **Knowledge of Variation** — common cause vs. special cause > 3. **Theory of Knowledge** — learning through prediction and observation (PDSA) > > *Plus one operational pillar: Psychology — understanding human motivation.* # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) Deming synthesized his entire philosophy into **four interrelated domains**. A manager must understand ALL FOUR to transform an organization. ### 🌐 The Four Components of SoPK | # | Component | Core Question Addressed | Key Insight | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Appreciation for a System** | "How do parts interact to achieve the aim?" | Optimize the whole, not maximize individual components | | 2 | **Knowledge of Variation** | "Why does the process behave differently?" | Distinguish common-cause vs special-cause variation | | 3 | **Theory of Knowledge** | "How do we learn what works?" | Predictions tested through PDSA experimentation | | 4 | **Psychology** | "What motivates people?" | Intrinsic motivation drives quality; fear destroys it | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** Any exam question asking *"Explain Deming's philosophy"* or *"Gurus of TQM"* must reference the **System of Profound Knowledge** as the cornerstone answer. --- ## 2.2 Deming's 14 Points for Management (The Operating Code) Deming's 14 Points, published in his book *Out of the Crisis* (1982), are a **code of conduct for transforming management**. They are not a checklist — they form an interconnected web. ### 📋 The Complete 14 Points (Memorize for KTU Part B) 1. **Create constancy of purpose** for improvement of product and service. 2. **Adopt the new philosophy** — we are in a new economic age. 3. **Cease dependence on inspection** to achieve quality; build quality into the product. 4. **End the practice of awarding business on price alone**; minimize total cost. 5. **Improve constantly** every process for planning, production, and service. 6. **Institute training on the job** for all employees. 7. **Institute leadership** — supervision should help people do a better job. 8. **Drive out fear** so everyone can work effectively. 9. **Break down barriers between departments** — team work across silos. 10. **Eliminate slogans and exhortations** asking for zero defects or new levels of productivity. 11. **Eliminate numerical quotas** and management by objective (MBO) without methods. 12. **Remove barriers** that rob the hourly worker of his right to pride of workmanship. 13. **Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement**. 14. **Put everybody to work** to accomplish the transformation. --- ## 2.3 Deming's Seven Deadly Diseases (Obstacles to Transformation) These are **management ailments** that prevent quality transformation: | # | Disease | Modern Manifestation | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Lack of constancy of purpose** | Short-term profit obsession | | 2 | **Emphasis on short-term profits** | Quarterly earnings pressure | | 3 | **Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review** | Ranking employees numerically | | 4 | **Mobility of management** (job hopping) | CEOs staying only 2–3 years | | 5 | **Running a company on visible figures alone** | Ignoring qualitative metrics | | 6 | **Excessive medical costs** | Absorbed in product price | | 7 | **Excessive costs of liability** | Litigation-driven product costs | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Deming's 14 Points** with **Seven Deadly Diseases**. They are different — Points are *positive prescriptions*, Diseases are *negative pathologies*. --- ## 2.4 Statistical Quality Control — Deming's Mathematical Foundation Deming championed the use of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts to monitor variation. The cornerstone formula is the **3-Sigma Control Limit**: ### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet | Formula | Expression | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** | $\text{UCL} = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$ | Process mean + 3 standard deviations | | **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** | $\text{LCL} = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ | Process mean − 3 standard deviations | | **Center Line** | $\bar{X}$ | Process mean (average of sample means) | | **Control Limits (Range)** | $\text{LCL} = D_4 \bar{R}, \quad \text{UCL} = D_3 \bar{R}$ | Using R-chart with tabulated factors $D_3$, $D_4$ | | **Process Capability (Cp)** | $C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Compares process spread to specification width | | **Deming's 94/6 Rule** | $\approx 94\%$ system, $\approx 6\%$ worker | Causes of quality problems | | **Six Sigma Logic** | $3.4 \text{ DPMO}$ | 3.4 defects per million opportunities | > [!NOTE] > **Important:** Never write absolute values like `|x|` inside a markdown table — use `\vert x \vert` to keep the table parsable. ### 🌎 Real-World Utility - **Toyota Production System** — uses Deming's statistical thinking + Japanese craftsmanship - **Healthcare** — Mayo Clinic adopted SoPK to reduce surgical errors - **IT/Software** — Six Sigma (Motorola, GE) is the direct descendant of Deming's philosophy - **Government** — US Federal agencies use Baldrige Criteria, which trace roots to Deming # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Process Capability Index (Cp) — Deming's Logic The Process Capability Index measures how well a process fits inside its specification limits. Deming's philosophy mandates designing the process such that natural variation ($\pm 3\sigma$) falls well within customer specifications. ### 🧮 Derivation Walk-Through We start with the assumption that a stable process follows a **normal distribution** centered at the process mean $\mu$, with standard deviation $\sigma$. The natural tolerance of a process (per Deming's SPC philosophy) is: $$ \text{Natural Tolerance} = \mu \pm 3\sigma $$ This means the process spreads over a total width of: $$ W_{\text{process}} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma $$ Now, the specification width set by customers or engineers is: $$ W_{\text{spec}} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL} $$ The **Process Capability Index** is defined as the ratio of the allowable specification width to the actual natural process spread: $$ C_p = \frac{W_{\text{spec}}}{W_{\text{process}}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision Rule:** A value of $C_p \ge 1.0$ means the process *can* fit inside specifications. Deming advocated $C_p \ge 1.33$ for robust manufacturing. ### Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A shaft diameter must lie between USL = 10.05 mm and LSL = 9.95 mm. The process mean is $\bar{X} = 10.00$ mm with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. Compute $C_p$. **Step 1 — Specification width:** $$ W_{\text{spec}} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL} = 10.05 - 9.95 = 0.10 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Process natural spread:** $$ W_{\text{process}} = 6\sigma = 6 \times 0.015 = 0.09 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Capability index:** $$ C_p = \frac{W_{\text{spec}}}{W_{\text{process}}} = \frac{0.10}{0.09} \approx 1.11 $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** $$ C_p = 1.11 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Process is capable but not robust; aim for } C_p \ge 1.33 $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Control Limits for the X-bar Chart The X-bar chart monitors the **average of subgroups** of size $n$ taken from the process. **Step 1 — Population parameters:** We assume subgroup means $\bar{X}_i$ are normally distributed around the grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ with standard deviation $\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ given by: $$ \sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ **Step 2 — 3-Sigma Control Limits:** Using Deming's statistical reasoning — points outside $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ occur with probability only $0.27\%$ and indicate special-cause variation: $$ \text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ $$ \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - \frac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitution using the Average Range $\bar{R}$:** When $\sigma$ is unknown, the unbiased estimator is: $$ \hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} $$ where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant based on subgroup size $n$. Substituting: $$ \text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + \frac{3\bar{R}}{d_2 \sqrt{n}} $$ Letting $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, we obtain the standard textbook form: $$ \text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} $$ $$ \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to write control limits, write BOTH the X-bar chart AND the R-chart limits to score full marks. ### 🧠 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tool | Situation | Deming's Recommended Tool | Why? | |---|---|---| | Monitoring process mean shifts | **X-bar chart** | Detects location changes | | Monitoring process variability | **R-chart** | Detects spread changes | | Designing new process | **PDSA cycle** | Tests learning hypothesis | | Counting defects per unit | **c-chart or u-chart** | Poisson-based attribute data | | Diagnosing chronic waste | **Ishikawa + Pareto** | Identifies root causes | | System redesign | **Flow diagram + SoPK** | Holistic transformation | --- ## 3.3 The PDSA Cycle — Deming's Learning Engine Deming (popularizing Walter Shewhart) defined the iterative learning cycle: $$ \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Do} \rightarrow \text{Study} \rightarrow \text{Act} $$ 1. **Plan** — Formulate a hypothesis and design an experiment 2. **Do** — Execute on small scale; record data 3. **Study** — Compare actual results with predictions 4. **Act** — If successful, standardize; if not, revise hypothesis and loop back > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Writing "PDCA" instead of **PDSA**. Deming specifically used **Study**, not "Check", because learning is the deeper purpose — not mere verification. --- ## 3.4 Deming's Chain Reaction — The Economic Case for Quality Deming visualized quality improvement as a self-reinforcing loop: $$ \text{Improve Quality} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lower Costs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Increase Productivity} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Secure Market} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stay in Business} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Provide Jobs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Improve Quality} $$ This cycle is **circular**: each link feeds the next. Higher quality *reduces* rework, warranty, and inspection costs — directly contradicting the old belief that "quality costs more." # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK) — Knowledge Graph ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["Appreciation for a System
Optimize the whole interconnected chain"] nodeB["Knowledge of Variation
Common cause vs Special cause"] nodeC["Theory of Knowledge
Predictions tested via PDSA"] nodeD["Psychology
Intrinsic motivation and human nature"] nodeE["TRANSFORMATION
Leadership and Quality Culture"] nodeF["Organization Outcomes
Lower cost, Higher productivity"] nodeA --> nodeE nodeB --> nodeE nodeC --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeA -.interacts with.-> nodeB nodeB -.interacts with.-> nodeC nodeC -.interacts with.-> nodeD nodeD -.interacts with.-> nodeA ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide:** The four lenses (A, B, C, D) feed into leadership **Transformation**, which yields **Organizational Outcomes**. The dotted edges show that the four domains are mutually reinforcing — mastery in one strengthens the others. --- ## 4.2 The PDSA Cycle — Iterative Learning Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR stepP["PLAN
Define objective
Hypothesize
Design test"] stepD["DO
Execute small scale
Collect data
Document observations"] stepS["STUDY
Compare results
with predictions
Identify learnings"] stepA["ACT
Standardize if successful
Revise hypothesis if not"] stepP --> stepD stepD --> stepS stepS --> stepA stepA --> stepP stepA -.Successful.-> stepOut["Standardize and
implement widely"] stepA -.Failed.-> stepP ``` --- ## 4.3 Deming's 14 Points — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB pillar1["PILLAR 1: FOUNDATION
Point 1: Constancy of Purpose
Point 2: Adopt the New Philosophy"] pillar2["PILLAR 2: PROCESS EXCELLENCE
Point 3: Cease Dependence on Inspection
Point 4: End Price-Based Purchasing
Point 5: Improve Constantly"] pillar3["PILLAR 3: PEOPLE DEVELOPMENT
Point 6: Institute Training
Point 13: Institute Education
Point 14: Put Everybody to Work"] pillar4["PILLAR 4: LEADERSHIP CULTURE
Point 7: Institute Leadership
Point 8: Drive Out Fear
Point 10: Eliminate Slogans
Point 11: Eliminate Quotas
Point 12: Remove Pride Barriers"] pillar5["PILLAR 5: ORGANIZATIONAL UNITY
Point 9: Break Down Departmental Barriers"] pillar1 --> outcomeX["TRANSFORMED ORGANIZATION
Lower Cost + Higher Productivity
Long-term Survival"] pillar2 --> outcomeX pillar3 --> outcomeX pillar4 --> outcomeX pillar5 --> outcomeX ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked to "explain" the 14 Points in an exam, organizing them into 4–5 pillars (as above) earns higher marks than listing them as 14 bullet points. --- ## 4.4 Deming's Chain Reaction — Quality-Cost-Profit Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR p1["Improve
Quality"] --> p2["Lower
Costs"] p2 --> p3["Increase
Productivity"] p3 --> p4["Capture
Market Share"] p4 --> p5["Stay in
Business"] p5 --> p6["Provide
Jobs"] p6 --> p1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Note:** This is a positive feedback loop — each round makes the system stronger. Investing in quality does NOT raise costs; it reduces them. --- ## 4.5 Variation — Common Cause vs Special Cause ```mermaid flowchart TB obs["OBSERVED DATA
Points on chart"] sys["System is STABLE
Only Common Cause Variation
Within UCL and LCL
INFERENCE: Modify the system"] spec["System is UNSTABLE
Special Cause Variation Present
Points outside control limits
INFERENCE: Remove the special cause"] obs --> chk{"Are points
within 3-sigma
control limits?"} chk -- YES --> sys chk -- NO --> spec ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📚 KTU Past Year Style Questions --- ### **PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)** #### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — 3 Marks **State any three contributions of W. Edwards Deming to the field of Total Quality Management.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Deming's 14 Points for Management** — A roadmap of practices for transforming business effectiveness and achieving quality leadership. *(1 mark)* 2. **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** — Four interrelated domains (System, Variation, Knowledge, Psychology) that form the foundation for organizational transformation. *(1 mark)* 3. **PDSA (Plan-Do-Study-Act) Cycle** — An iterative learning cycle for testing improvements in a scientific manner. *(1 mark)* *(Acceptable alternatives: Deming Prize, Statistical Process Control in Japanese industry, Seven Deadly Diseases, Chain Reaction of Quality)* --- #### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — 3 Marks **Explain the Deming Wheel (PDSA Cycle) with its four phases.** **Model Answer:** The **PDSA Cycle**, also known as the **Deming Wheel** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative learning process: *(1 mark)* 1. **Plan** — Identify the goal, formulate a hypothesis, and plan the data collection. *(0.5 mark)* 2. **Do** — Execute the plan on a small scale and record observations systematically. *(0.5 mark)* 3. **Study** — Analyze data, compare actual results with predictions, derive learnings. *(0.5 mark)* 4. **Act** — If the change works, standardize it organization-wide; if not, revise the plan and loop back. *(0.5 mark)* > The cycle is **continuous** and embodies Deming's belief that learning is empirical and never-ending. --- ### **PART B — Descriptive (14 Marks, Internal Choice)** #### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail Deming's **System of Profound Knowledge** and its four components. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe Deming's **14 Points for Management** and explain how they collectively support Total Quality Management. *(7 marks)* --- ### 📝 Model Answer for Option A — Part (a) **Deming's System of Profound Knowledge (7 Marks)** The **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** is Deming's magnum opus — a framework that integrates statistical thinking, systems theory, and human psychology into a unified management philosophy. Deming believed that *transformation* of any organization requires mastery of four interrelated knowledge domains. *(1 mark for introduction)* **1. Appreciation for a System** *(1.5 marks)* A system is a network of interdependent components working together toward a common **aim**. Managers must understand: - The **optimization** of the whole takes precedence over maximizing parts. - Inter-departmental cooperation replaces sub-optimization. - Components without a shared aim produce conflict. > *Example:* A hospital's aim is patient recovery — every department (labs, surgery, pharmacy) must align with this aim. **2. Knowledge of Variation** *(1.5 marks)* All processes exhibit variation. Deming classified variation into: - **Common-cause variation** — inherent to the system; requires system redesign. - **Special-cause variation** — assignable to specific events; requires local action. > Tampering (reacting to common-cause as if it were special-cause) **worsens** the process. **3. Theory of Knowledge** *(1.5 marks)* Knowledge comes from **prediction tested by experience**. The PDSA cycle operationalizes this. Without theory, observation is meaningless; without observation, theory is unverified. **4. Psychology** *(1.5 marks)* People are motivated by: - **Intrinsic satisfaction** (pride of workmanship, learning) - **Fear destroys** performance — thus Point 8 (drive out fear). External rewards alone (bonuses, threats) cannot sustain long-term quality. > *Conclusion:* Mastery of all four domains is **non-negotiable** for any leader aspiring to transform quality. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### 📝 Model Answer for Option A — Part (b) **Deming's 14 Points for Management (7 Marks)** Deming's 14 Points, articulated in *Out of the Crisis* (1982), are **not a checklist** but an interconnected web of management philosophy. They are divided into four pillars: *(0.5 mark for context)* **Pillar 1 — Constancy and Vision:** *(1.5 marks)* - Point 1: **Create constancy of purpose** toward improvement — long-term commitment to innovation, research, and education. - Point 2: **Adopt the new philosophy** — accept that the world has changed. **Pillar 2 — Process and Systems:** *(2 marks)* - Point 3: **Cease dependence on inspection** — build quality in. - Point 4: **End price-only purchasing** — minimize total cost. - Point 5: **Improve constantly every process**. **Pillar 3 — People and Learning:** *(2 marks)* - Point 6: Institute **on-the-job training**. - Point 8: **Drive out fear**. - Point 9: **Break down silos** between departments. - Point 10: **Eliminate slogans** like "zero defects" without a method. - Point 11: **Eliminate numerical quotas** and management-by-objective. - Point 12: **Remove barriers** to pride of workmanship. - Point 13: **Education and self-improvement** programme. - Point 14: **Engage everyone** in the transformation. **Pillar 4 — Leadership:** *(1 mark)* - Point 7: **Institute leadership** — supervisors as coaches, not judges. > **Conclusion:** Together, these 14 Points dismantle the old system of fear-based, inspection-driven management and replace it with a **system of cooperation, learning, and continuous improvement**. *(0.5 mark)* --- #### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **(a)** What are the **Seven Deadly Diseases** identified by Deming? Explain any five in detail. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain Deming's **Chain Reaction** with a neat diagram. Discuss how it links quality improvement to economic performance. *(7 marks)* --- ### 📝 Model Answer for Option B — Part (a) **Seven Deadly Diseases (7 Marks)** Deming identified **seven management diseases** that obstruct quality transformation: *(0.5 mark for introduction)* **1. Lack of constancy of purpose** *(1 mark)* Planning only for short-term profits leads to erosion of long-term quality and customer trust. **2. Emphasis on short-term profits** *(1 mark)* Pressure for quarterly earnings forces managers to cut corners on quality, training, and R&D. **3. Evaluation by performance, merit rating, or annual review** *(1 mark)* Numerical ranking destroys teamwork, creates fear, and discourages collaboration. **4. Mobility of management** *(1 mark)* Frequent job-hopping prevents managers from seeing the long-term consequences of their decisions. **5. Running a company on visible figures alone** *(1 mark)* Quality is multi-dimensional — over-reliance on financial metrics hides systemic issues. **6. Excessive medical costs** *(1 mark)* Rising healthcare premiums inflate product cost and reduce competitiveness. **7. Excessive costs of liability** *(1 mark)* Litigation-driven product design and insurance burden inflate prices. > *Conclusion:* These diseases are **management-induced** and can only be cured by leadership commitment to transformation. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### 📝 Model Answer for Option B — Part (b) **Deming's Chain Reaction (7 Marks)** Deming proposed a **self-reinforcing chain** that demonstrates quality is the engine of profitability, not its enemy: *(1 mark for introduction)* $$ \text{Quality Improvement} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Lower Costs} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Higher Productivity} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Market Capture} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Survival} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{More Jobs} $$ **Step-by-step logic:** *(5 marks)* 1. **Quality Up** — Better design + fewer defects + lower rework 2. **Costs Down** — Lower warranty, lower scrap, lower inspection overhead 3. **Productivity Up** — Less rework, smoother flow, higher throughput 4. **Market Captured** — Better reputation → customer loyalty 5. **Stay in Business** — Long-term profitability secured 6. **Provide Jobs** — Sustained employment > **Key Insight:** This contradicts the old assumption that quality is expensive. Deming proves quality is the **cheapest** path to survival. *(1 mark)* **Diagram (reproduce in exam):** ``` [Improve Quality] → [Lower Costs] → [Higher Productivity] ↑ ↓ [Provide Jobs] ← [Stay in Business] ← [Capture Market] ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. Confusing **Deming's 14 Points** with **Crosby's 14 Steps** or **Juran's 10 Steps** — they are different. > 2. Writing **"PDCA"** instead of **"PDSA"** — partial mark deduction. > 3. Listing 14 Points as flat bullets without showing **interconnection** or **pillars**. > 4. Forgetting to mention **System of Profound Knowledge** in any "Deming" question. > 5. Mis-attributing the **Deming Prize** to Juran — the prize is named after Deming, awarded by JUSE. > 6. Omitting the **94/6 rule** when discussing why workers are usually not the cause of defects. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Deming (1900–1993)** — American statistician, the architect of modern TQM, revered in Japan. - **System of Profound Knowledge (SoPK)** has 4 components: **System, Variation, Knowledge, Psychology** — master all four. - **Deming's 14 Points** can be grouped into **5 pillars**: Constancy, Process, People, Leadership, Unity. - **PDSA Cycle** = Plan → Do → Study → Act — the engine of continuous improvement. - **Seven Deadly Diseases** = pathologies of management; not of workers. - **Deming's 94/6 Rule** = roughly 94% of problems are systemic, ~6% are worker-related. - **Deming Chain Reaction** proves quality improvement → cost reduction → market capture → survival. - **Control Limits**: $\text{UCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$; $\text{LCL} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$. - **Process Capability**: $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL}) / (6\sigma)$; aim for $C_p \ge 1.33$. - **The Deming Prize** was instituted by **JUSE in 1951** — predates the Baldrige Award. - Deming insisted **workers are not the enemy** — management owns the system. - Always distinguish **common-cause variation** (system) from **special-cause variation** (event). - **PDSA** (not PDCA) — Deming used "Study" to emphasize learning over mere checking. - Deming's philosophy is **profound, not procedural** — transformation requires deep belief change. - For **KTU 14-mark questions**, organize answers into **subsections with headings** for clarity.

topics.title: Juran
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Juran # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Joseph M. Juran — The Quality Guru **Joseph Moses Juran (1904–2008)** was a Romanian-born American engineer, lecturer, author, and management consultant who is universally regarded as one of the founding fathers of modern **Total Quality Management (TQM)**. He is often referred to as the "Father of Quality Management" and was the first to introduce the human side of quality — focusing on the role of **top management** in driving quality, rather than confining quality to the factory floor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus (Quality Gurus):** Juran is examined alongside Deming, Crosby, Feigenbaum, and Ishikawa. Students must know his **Trilogy**, **Pareto Principle**, **Quality Spiral**, **Fitness for Use**, and the **10 Steps to Quality Improvement**. ### Juran's Definition of Quality Juran defined quality in two complementary dimensions: 1. **Quality consists of those product features which meet the needs of customers** and thereby provide product **satisfaction**. 2. **Quality consists of freedom from deficiencies** — freedom from errors, rework, and defects that detract from product usability. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition (Board Exam Ready):** > "Quality is **Fitness for Use (FFU)**." — *Juran, 1951* > FFU encompasses all product features that satisfy consumer needs and freedom from deficiencies. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Analogy:** > Imagine you go to a fine-dining restaurant. **Quality** here means: (1) The food *tastes good*, *looks appetizing*, and *arrives hot* (features that satisfy your needs), AND (2) there are *no* hair strands, *no* stale ingredients, and *no* wrong orders (freedom from defects). > > Juran says a company is responsible for BOTH dimensions. Most companies obsess only over (2) — removing defects — and forget (1) — delighting the customer. Juran was the first guru to say: **"Quality is not just about catching defects; it is about designing the product to be useful."** This is the essence of *Fitness for Use*. ### Key Biographical Highlights - **Born:** December 23, 1904, in Brăila, Romania. - **Education:** B.S. in Electrical Engineering from the University of Minnesota (1924). - **Career Started:** Worked at **Western Electric's Hawthorne Works** (the same Hawthorne plant where the Hawthorne Effect was later studied) in the Inspection Department. - **Authored:** The **Juran's Quality Control Handbook (1951)** — the single most influential reference work in the quality field, still in print. - **Founded:** The **Juran Institute** in 1979, dedicated to research and training in quality. - **Died:** February 28, 2008, aged 103, in Rye, New York. > [!NOTE] > Juran was the first to introduce the **Pareto Principle (80/20 rule)** into the field of quality. He also invented the concept of the **Quality Spiral** (Juran Spiral). ### Juran's Contributions at a Glance | Contribution | Year | Significance | |---|---|---| | Juran's Quality Control Handbook | **1951** | First comprehensive reference on quality management | | Fitness for Use | **1951** | Customer-driven definition of quality | | Pareto Principle applied to Quality | **1950s** | 80% of problems come from 20% of causes | | Quality Spiral (Juran Spiral) | **1962** | Quality is a continuous, evolutionary process | | Juran's Trilogy | **1986** | Planning, Control, Improvement (Quality's three managerial processes) | | Juran Institute | **1979** | Global quality consulting and training body | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Juran's Quality Trilogy (Juran Trilogy) > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE MOST HIGH-YIELD TOPIC FOR KTU EXAMS.** > Juran stated that **Quality Management** consists of three universal managerial processes — applicable to *any* industry, *any* country, *any* culture. The trilogy is structured as a **sequential, interlinked cycle**: ### (a) Quality Planning (QP) - **Purpose:** Identify *who* the customers are, determine their needs, develop product features that respond to those needs, and design processes capable of producing those features. - **Steps inside QP:** 1. Establish quality goals. 2. Identify customers (internal and external). 3. Determine customer needs (Voice of Customer — VOC). 4. Develop product features that meet those needs. 5. Develop process capabilities to produce the product features. 6. Establish process controls and transfer to operations. - **Output:** A product that **can** meet customer needs under operating conditions. ### (b) Quality Control (QC) - **Purpose:** Evaluate actual quality performance, compare it against quality goals, and act on the difference. - **Steps inside QC (The QC Loop):** 1. Evaluate actual quality performance. 2. Compare actual performance to quality goals. 3. Act on the difference. - **Output:** Stability of operations — performance matches plan. ### (c) Quality Improvement (QI) - **Purpose:** Establish an infrastructure (councils, teams, measures) and a disciplined roadmap to *raise* quality performance to **breakthrough levels** — beyond what was originally planned. - **Steps inside QI:** 1. Establish infrastructure for improvement. 2. Identify the specific improvement projects (using Pareto). 3. Establish project teams with clear charters. 4. Provide resources, training, and motivation. 5. Establish measures of progress. - **Output:** New, higher quality standards — a **breakthrough**. > [!NOTE] > **The Big Insight — Juran's distinction between QC and QI:** > - **Quality Control** = holding the *gain* (preventing regression to the old, lower standard). > - **Quality Improvement** = making the *breakthrough* (raising the standard to a new, higher level). > Together, they ensure quality **never stops improving**. --- ## 2.2 The Pareto Principle (The 80/20 Rule) Vilfredo Pareto, an Italian economist, observed in 1906 that **80% of Italy's wealth was owned by 20% of the population**. Juran adapted this insight to quality in the 1950s, observing that **roughly 80% of quality problems are caused by only 20% of the underlying causes** (the "vital few"). ### Mathematical Form of Pareto Analysis For a given set of defects, complaints, or problems, the cumulative contribution of categories follows an exponential-like distribution: $$P_{\text{cum}}(k) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{N}$$ Where: - $P_{\text{cum}}(k)$ = cumulative proportion of problems contributed by the first $k$ categories - $f_i$ = frequency (count) of problems in category $i$ - $N$ = total number of problems across all categories - Categories are arranged in **descending order** of $f_i$ before summation The "**vital few**" are those categories that account for the **first 80%** of cumulative problems; the remaining 20% (cumulatively) constitute the "**trivial many**" or "useful many." > [!NOTE] > **Critical Insight:** The exact ratio (80/20) is a heuristic — actual splits may be 70/30, 90/10, etc. The point is *concentration of causes*, not the exact numbers. --- ## 2.3 The Juran Quality Spiral (Product Life Cycle Spiral) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequently asked as a 7-mark question in KTU exams.** Draw the spiral and label each stage. Quality is not a one-time event. Juran argued that quality must be considered across the **entire product life cycle**, repeated for every new generation of the product. Each new cycle (generation) results in a higher quality standard and corresponds to a **larger diameter** in the spiral. ### The Stages of the Quality Spiral (in order): 1. **Market Research** — Identify customer needs. 2. **Product Development** — Design product to meet needs. 3. **Process Development** — Design process to make the product. 4. **Purchasing** — Procure materials of required quality. 5. **Manufacture / Production** — Produce the product. 6. **Inspection & Testing** — Verify conformance. 7. **Sales & Distribution** — Deliver to customer. 8. **Customer Use & Feedback** — Customer evaluates fitness for use; feedback flows back to **stage 1**, restarting the spiral. ### Key Takeaway Each revolution of the spiral represents **one product generation**. As generations evolve, the spiral widens (quality improves), reflecting the cumulative effect of quality planning and improvement. --- ## 2.4 Juran's 10 Steps for Quality Improvement (Universal Improvement Sequence) > [!HIGH-YIELD] > **Direct 7-mark question topic.** Memorize the list and be able to describe the logic. | Step # | Action | |---|---| | **1** | Build awareness of the need and opportunity for improvement. | | **2** | Set goals for improvement. | | **3** | Organize to reach the goals (establish a quality council, identify problems, choose projects, appoint teams). | | **4** | Provide training. | | **5** | Carry out projects to solve problems. | | **6** | Report progress. | | **7** | Give recognition. | | **8** | Communicate results. | | **9** | Keep score. | | **10** | Maintain momentum by making annual improvement part of the company's systems and processes. | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below contains every equation, rule, and metric you will need for a Juran problem on the KTU exam. | Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Pareto Cumulative Share | $P_{\text{cum}}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}{N}$ | Fraction of total problems from top $k$ causes | Dimensionless; $0 \le P_{\text{cum}} \le 1$ | | Defect Rate (incoming) | $D_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{defective}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Proportion of defective units | Dimensionless or **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) | | First Pass Yield | $Y_{\text{FPY}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{good on first attempt}}}{N_{\text{total entered}}$ | Efficiency of the process | $0 \le Y_{\text{FPY}} \le 1$ | | Rolled Throughput Yield | $Y_{\text{RTY}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Cumulative yield across $n$ process steps | $0 \le Y_{\text{RTY}} \le 1$ | | Quality Cost Ratio (Juran) | $\dfrac{\text{Failure cost}}{\text{Prevention cost}}$ | Indicator of quality system health | Optimal $\le 1$ | | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{failure}} + C_{\text{appraisal}}$ | Internal + external failure + appraisal cost | Currency (INR, USD, etc.) | | Optimal Quality Cost | $\dfrac{dC_{\text{total}}}{dQ} = 0$ | Find minimum of $C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{failure}}$ | Used in economic models of quality | > [!NOTE] > For LaTeX in tables, all vertical bars (absolute value) must use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown. The above formulas already use parentheses, which are safe. --- ## 2.6 Juran's View on the Cost of Quality (Quality Cost Curve) Juran was the first to formalize the concept of the **"Cost of Poor Quality" (COPQ)**. He identified the trade-off between the cost of preventing defects (which rises with quality effort) and the cost of failures (which falls as quality improves). The **optimal level of quality** is where the **total quality cost** is minimized. $$C_{\text{total}}(Q) = C_{\text{prevention}}(Q) + C_{\text{appraisal}}(Q) + C_{\text{failure}}(Q)$$ Where: - $C_{\text{prevention}}$: Cost of preventing defects (training, planning, design) — *increases* with $Q$. - $C_{\text{appraisal}}$: Cost of inspection, testing, audits — *relatively constant* in the optimum range. - $C_{\text{failure}}$: Cost of scrap, rework, warranty, lost customers — *decreases* as $Q$ improves. > [!TIP] > **Why this matters in TQM:** Traditional managers try to *cut prevention/appraisal cost*, increasing failure cost. Juran showed that **investment in prevention has the highest ROI** because every rupee saved on prevention may cost ten rupees in failure. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Example: Applying the Pareto Principle > [!EXAMPLE] > **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):** > A manufacturing firm has recorded 250 customer complaints in the last quarter, classified into six categories. Using the data below, (a) construct a Pareto chart, (b) identify the vital few and useful many, and (c) recommend the firm's priority action. | Category | A | B | C | D | E | F | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Number of Complaints ($f_i$) | 110 | 65 | 35 | 20 | 12 | 8 | ### Step 1 — Compute the total number of complaints The total number of complaints is the sum of all category frequencies: $$ \begin{aligned} N &= \sum_{i=1}^{6} f_i \\ &= f_A + f_B + f_C + f_D + f_E + f_F \\ &= 110 + 65 + 35 + 20 + 12 + 8 \\ &= 250 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Sort categories in descending order of frequency Categories are already in descending order: **A > B > C > D > E > F**. ### Step 3 — Compute cumulative frequency for each rank position $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Cum}_A &= 110 \\ \text{Cum}_B &= 110 + 65 = 175 \\ \text{Cum}_C &= 175 + 35 = 210 \\ \text{Cum}_D &= 210 + 20 = 230 \\ \text{Cum}_E &= 230 + 12 = 242 \\ \text{Cum}_F &= 242 + 8 = 250 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert cumulative counts to cumulative percentage $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{cum}}(A) &= \frac{110}{250} \times 100\% = 44.0\% \\ P_{\text{cum}}(B) &= \frac{175}{250} \times 100\% = 70.0\% \\ P_{\text{cum}}(C) &= \frac{210}{250} \times 100\% = 84.0\% \\ P_{\text{cum}}(D) &= \frac{230}{250} \times 100\% = 92.0\% \\ P_{\text{cum}}(E) &= \frac{242}{250} \times 100\% = 96.8\% \\ P_{\text{cum}}(F) &= \frac{250}{250} \times 100\% = 100.0\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Identify the vital few The threshold is **80%**. Categories A, B, and C together account for **84%** of complaints. Therefore, **A, B, and C are the "vital few"**. Categories D, E, and F (the remaining 16%) are the "useful many" / "trivial many." ### Step 6 — Strategic recommendation The firm should focus its quality improvement resources — root-cause analysis, corrective actions, 5-Why, Ishikawa diagrams — on categories A, B, and C. Solving these three categories alone will eliminate ~84% of complaints, a far higher return on effort than treating all six categories equally. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quality Cost Optimization Consider a process with the following quality cost structure (in Lakhs of INR per month): | Quality Level ($Q$, in % defect-free) | Prevention Cost | Appraisal Cost | Failure Cost | Total Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | 95% | 0.5 | 1.0 | 8.5 | 10.0 | | 97% | 1.0 | 1.2 | 5.8 | 8.0 | | **99%** | **2.0** | **1.5** | **2.5** | **6.0** | | 99.5% | 3.0 | 1.8 | 1.7 | 6.5 | | 99.9% | 5.0 | 2.5 | 1.0 | 8.5 | ### Derivation of the optimum The optimum is found by inspecting where the total cost is minimum: $$ \begin{aligned} C_{\text{total}}(95\%) &= 0.5 + 1.0 + 8.5 = 10.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\ C_{\text{total}}(97\%) &= 1.0 + 1.2 + 5.8 = 8.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\ C_{\text{total}}(99\%) &= 2.0 + 1.5 + 2.5 = 6.0 \text{ Lakhs} \quad \text{(minimum)} \\ C_{\text{total}}(99.5\%) &= 3.0 + 1.8 + 1.7 = 6.5 \text{ Lakhs} \\ C_{\text{total}}(99.9\%) &= 5.0 + 2.5 + 1.0 = 8.5 \text{ Lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ The **optimum is at 99% defect-free**, where the marginal increase in prevention cost equals the marginal reduction in failure cost. Going beyond this (e.g., to 99.9%) actually *raises* total cost. > [!NOTE] > **Key managerial insight:** "Higher quality is always costlier" is a *myth*. Going from 95% to 99% *saves* the firm 4 Lakhs/month. The cost curve is **U-shaped**, not monotonically increasing. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Juran vs. Deming vs. Crosby | Dimension | **Juran** | **Deming** | **Crosby** | |---|---|---|---| | Definition of Quality | Fitness for Use | A predictable degree of uniformity | Conformance to requirements | | Primary Focus | **Top Management** & Customers | **System** and Statistical Knowledge | **Zero Defects**, Prevention | | Roadmap | Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve) | 14 Points, PDCA, System of Profound Knowledge | 14 Steps, Quality Vaccine | | Improvement Method | **Pareto + Projects** | Statistical Process Control (SPC) | **Zero Defects** culture | | Cost Approach | Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | Cost of poor quality (intramural) | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) | | Origin | Industry / Engineering | Statistics / Operations Research | Practitioner / Line worker | | Best Quote | "Quality is fitness for use." | "In God we trust; all others bring data." | "Quality is free." | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Juran's Quality Trilogy — Master Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Quality Planning
Identify customers and
their needs, develop
product and process] B[Quality Control
Evaluate actual performance,
compare to goals, act
on the difference] C[Quality Improvement
Establish infrastructure,
identify projects, drive
breakthrough performance] A ==> B B ==> C C ==> A ``` > [!NOTE] > The double-lined arrow (==>) denotes a **sequentially managed** transition, not a casual connection. Each phase produces the inputs for the next, but the cycle as a whole is continuous and never "ends." --- ## 4.2 Juran's Quality Spiral — Product Life Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[1. Market Research
Identify customer needs] S2[2. Product Development
Design features for FFU] S3[3. Process Development
Design capable process] S4[4. Purchasing
Procure quality materials] S5[5. Production
Manufacture the product] S6[6. Inspection and Testing
Verify conformance] S7[7. Sales and Distribution
Deliver to customer] S8[8. Customer Use
Customer evaluates FFU] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 S6 --> S7 S7 --> S8 S8 -.Feedback to next generation.-> S1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The dashed arrow from S8 back to S1 represents the **feedback loop** that drives the next product generation. The "spiral" emerges because the next generation is *outward* (wider) than the previous one — quality keeps advancing. --- ## 4.3 Pareto Analysis Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1: Collect data
on defects/complaints] P2[Step 2: Categorize
the data by type] P3[Step 3: Sort categories
in descending order] P4[Step 4: Compute cumulative
frequency and percentage] P5[Step 5: Plot the
Pareto chart] P6[Step 6: Identify vital few
that contribute ~80 percent] P7[Step 7: Direct improvement
effort to the vital few] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 ``` --- ## 4.4 The Quality Cost Optimization Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR QC1[Prevention Cost
Rises with quality effort] QC2[Appraisal Cost
Relatively flat] QC3[Failure Cost
Falls as quality improves] QC4[Total Cost
U-shaped curve
Minimum at optimum Q] QC1 --- QC2 QC2 --- QC3 QC3 --- QC4 ``` > [!NOTE] > The total cost is the **sum** of the three components. The optimum point on the X-axis (Quality Level) is where the *upward* trend of prevention/appraisal is exactly balanced by the *downward* trend of failure cost. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Q1. Define "Fitness for Use" as coined by Joseph Juran.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** "Fitness for Use" is Juran's definition of quality, introduced in his *Quality Control Handbook* (1951). It states that quality means a product's features must (i) **satisfy customer needs** and (ii) be **free from deficiencies**. In other words, a quality product is one that is *fit for its intended purpose* from the perspective of the end user. This is a customer-focused definition, in contrast to a purely specification-based or conformance-based definition. > **Marking Scheme (3):** Definition (1), Two dimensions (1), Customer-focus point (1). --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Q2. State Juran's Quality Trilogy. Briefly explain any one component.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Juran's Quality Trilogy consists of three universal managerial processes: **Quality Planning, Quality Control, and Quality Improvement**. - *Quality Planning:* Identify customers, determine their needs, develop products and processes to meet those needs, and transfer the plan to operations. - *Quality Control:* Evaluate actual performance, compare it to the quality goals, and act on the difference. (Optional second part.) - *Quality Improvement:* Establish an infrastructure (council, teams, measures) to drive breakthrough performance beyond the original goals. > **Marking Scheme (3):** Naming all three (1), Explanation of one in detail (2). --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1 > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a) Explain Juran's Quality Trilogy in detail, with a description of each of the three managerial processes. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** In 1986, Joseph M. Juran, in his book *The Planning and Control of Quality*, defined quality management as consisting of three universal managerial processes — the Quality Trilogy. This trilogy applies to all industries, all cultures, and all levels of management. **1. Quality Planning (QP) — (2 marks):** Quality Planning is the activity of developing products and processes required to meet customer needs. Its key steps are: - Establish quality goals and identify the customers (both internal and external). - Determine the customers' needs (the "Voice of Customer" or VOC). - Translate those needs into product features (engineering specifications). - Develop the process that is capable of producing those features. - Establish process controls and transfer the plan to operating forces. **2. Quality Control (QC) — (2 marks):** Quality Control is the operational process of holding quality gains. The QC loop consists of: - Evaluate the actual quality performance (using statistical and operational measures). - Compare actual performance with quality goals. - Act on the difference — if performance deviates from goals, take corrective action. The purpose of QC is to ensure that the product *as produced* meets the quality goals established during planning. **3. Quality Improvement (QI) — (2 marks):** Quality Improvement is the management of change. Juran argued that simply controlling quality is not enough; companies must continually *raise* their quality standards. QI requires: - Establishing an infrastructure — a Quality Council, project sponsors, trained teams. - Identifying improvement projects, usually via the Pareto analysis. - Providing resources, training, and motivation to project teams. - Establishing measures to track progress. The output of QI is a *breakthrough* — a new, higher performance standard that the QC process then holds. --- **(b) Describe Juran's 10-step process for Quality Improvement. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** Juran proposed a universal 10-step roadmap for quality improvement that is applicable to any organization. These steps establish a structured pathway from awareness to sustainable results. **The 10 Steps (6 marks — ½ to 1 mark each, summarized):** 1. **Build awareness** — Educate managers and workers that quality can and must be improved. 2. **Set goals** — Establish clear, measurable goals for the improvement program. 3. **Organize to reach goals** — Create a Quality Council, appoint facilitators, and choose projects. 4. **Provide training** — Train all levels of the organization in quality tools and concepts. 5. **Carry out projects** — Use statistical methods, Pareto analysis, and root-cause tools to solve problems. 6. **Report progress** — Communicate project status to all stakeholders. 7. **Give recognition** — Acknowledge the efforts of teams and individuals. 8. **Communicate results** — Share lessons learned across the organization. 9. **Keep score** — Maintain scorecards, dashboards, and KPIs to track improvement. 10. **Maintain momentum** — Institutionalize annual improvement plans and embed quality into company systems. > **Marking Scheme (7):** Introduction (1), Ten steps with brief justification (5), Conclusion (1). --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) Define the Pareto Principle. Using a suitable example, explain how it is applied to quality management. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The **Pareto Principle**, as applied by Juran to quality, states that a *small number of causes* (the "vital few") are responsible for *most* of the quality problems (the "trivial many"). Typically, this takes the form 80% of problems come from 20% of causes, though the exact ratio is not fixed. **Example Application (5 marks):** *Scenario:* A switchgear manufacturing company receives 500 customer warranty claims in a year, classified by failure type: | Failure Type | Number of Claims | |---|---| | Coil burn-out | 240 | | Contact welding | 110 | | Mechanical jamming | 80 | | Auxiliary contact failure | 50 | | Terminal loosening | 20 | *Step 1: Total claims* = 500. *Step 2: Sort* — order is already descending: coil burn-out, contact welding, mechanical jamming, auxiliary contact failure, terminal loosening. *Step 3: Cumulative %* — 48%, 70%, 86%, 96%, 100%. *Step 4: Identify vital few* — Coil burn-out and contact welding together account for **70%** of claims. Adding mechanical jamming brings the cumulative to **86%**, well past the 80% threshold. Hence, these three categories form the "vital few." *Step 5: Action* — The firm's improvement team uses Ishikawa (fishbone) diagrams, 5-Why analysis, and design-of-experiments (DOE) to address the root causes of coil burn-out and contact welding. Solving these two issues alone will eliminate 70% of warranty claims — a far higher return on effort than addressing all five categories equally. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Juran's adaptation of the Pareto Principle revolutionized quality management by allowing managers to focus scarce resources on the *most impactful* problems, rather than dispersing effort evenly. This remains a cornerstone of modern TQM and Six Sigma project selection. --- **(b) Explain Juran's concept of the Quality Spiral. How does it differ from a static quality cycle? (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept (3 marks):** Juran introduced the **Quality Spiral** in 1962 to depict the fact that quality is not a one-time event but an **ongoing, evolutionary process** spanning the entire product life cycle.
