The spiral consists of eight interconnected stages: 1. Market Research 2. Product Development 3. Process Development 4. Purchasing 5. Production 6. Inspection & Testing 7. Sales & Distribution 8. Customer Use & Feedback After stage 8, customer feedback loops back to stage 1, but to a *new* generation of the product at a *higher* quality level. This is the "spiral" — each revolution is wider than the previous one, symbolizing rising quality. **Differences from a static quality cycle (3 marks):** | Aspect | Static Quality Cycle | Juran's Quality Spiral | |---|---|---| | Number of cycles | One fixed loop | Repeats, generation after generation | | Quality level | Constant | **Continuously increasing** | | Customer involvement | Limited (input only at start) | Continuous (VOC + post-sale feedback) | | Time dimension | Atemporal | Evolutionary, with successive product generations | | Outcome | Product launch | **Continuous innovation and quality improvement** | **Conclusion (1 mark):** The Quality Spiral captures the dynamic, customer-driven, and generational nature of quality. It is a precursor to modern concepts like the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle**, **continuous improvement (Kaizen)**, and the **Voice of Customer** process. By drawing the spiral, Juran taught managers that quality is never "achieved" — it is an endless journey. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students lose marks on Juran questions:** > 1. **Confusing "Fitness for Use" with "Conformance to Specifications."** Juran's definition is *customer-driven* (FFU), not specification-driven. Crosby uses the latter, not Juran. (–1 mark) > 2. **Listing the Trilogy as three independent concepts** instead of explaining that they form a *cycle* — QP feeds QC, QC holds gains, QI raises the bar. (–1 mark) > 3. **Treating the Quality Spiral as a circular diagram** (which would imply static quality) instead of a *spiral* that widens with each product generation. (–1 mark) > 4. **Pareto Principle misstated as 80/20 always.** It is a heuristic — actual ratios vary. The principle is *concentration of causes*. (–1 mark) > 5. **Omitting the "trilogy breaks down" argument** that QP is everyone's job, but QC and QI need special infrastructure (Quality Council) — a Juran-specific insight. (–1 mark) > 6. **Forgetting Juran's emphasis on top management.** Unlike Deming, Juran specifically targeted *upper management* as the driver of quality — the worker follows management's lead. (–1 mark) --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **30-second exam revision list — read this just before entering the exam hall.** - **Juran = "Father of Quality Management";** Deming = "Father of Statistical Quality Control"; Ishikawa = "Father of Quality Circles"; Crosby = "Zero Defects." - **Definition of Quality:** **Fitness for Use (FFU)** — two dimensions: features meeting needs + freedom from deficiencies. - **Juran's Trilogy:** **Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement** (universal, sequential, cyclic). - QP = design to meet needs - QC = hold the gain (loop: evaluate → compare → act) - QI = make the breakthrough (Pareto, projects, infrastructure) - **Pareto Principle (80/20):** Vital few (top ~20% of causes) drive ~80% of problems. Used to **prioritize** improvement projects. - **Quality Spiral (8 stages):** Market Research → Product Dev → Process Dev → Purchasing → Production → Inspection → Sales → Customer Use → (loops back, wider, to next generation). - **10 Steps to Quality Improvement:** Awareness, Goals, Organize, Train, Projects, Report, Recognize, Communicate, Score, Maintain momentum. - **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** Prevention + Appraisal + Failure. The total cost is **U-shaped**; the optimum is where failure cost savings = prevention cost added. - **Top Management Focus:** Juran uniquely stressed that *upper management* is responsible for quality, not the worker. - **First to do what:** - First comprehensive quality handbook (1951). - First to apply Pareto to quality. - First to coin "Fitness for Use." - First to publish the Trilogy as a unified model. - **Date to remember:** Juran's Quality Control Handbook — **1951**; Trilogy — **1986**. - **Juran Institute:** Founded **1979** for global quality training and consulting. - **Workplace tip:** When asked to *compare* gurus, always use a comparison matrix (Juran vs. Deming vs. Crosby) — it earns easy marks and demonstrates depth. --- > **End of Notes — Juran | PEIET631 Module 1** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) — Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Shewhart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shewhart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Who is Shewhart? — The Father of Statistical Quality Control

**Walter Andrew Shewhart (1891–1967)** was an American physicist, engineer, and statistician who is universally recognized as the **father of Statistical Quality Control (SQC)** and modern **Statistical Process Control (SPC)**. He spent most of his career at **Bell Telephone Laboratories**, where in **1924** he introduced the concept of the **control chart**, fundamentally transforming how industries think about quality and variation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Shewhart's contributions form the *theoretical backbone* of Total Quality Management (TQM) and Six Sigma. Every modern quality framework — from Deming's PDCA cycle to Six Sigma DMAIC — is a direct intellectual descendant of Shewhart's work.

### Formal KTU Definition

> **Shewhart's Doctrine of Statistical Quality Control** is the systematic application of statistical methods — particularly the *Shewhart Control Chart* and the *Shewhart Cycle (PDCA)* — to monitor, control, and improve industrial processes by distinguishing between **common cause variation** (inherent to the process) and **special cause variation** (attributable to external, identifiable factors).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermometer of a Process"

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. Your **speedometer** continuously monitors your speed. You have set a **target speed** of 80 km/h. Now, occasionally the speed wobbles between 78 and 82 km/h — that is **normal, expected variation** (common cause). But if suddenly the speed drops to 50 km/h, you immediately know something is wrong (a special cause — perhaps a flat tire or steep slope).

**Shewhart's control chart is essentially the "speedometer" of any industrial process** — it tells the operator when the process is behaving normally and when an external "flat tire" event has occurred. Without it, the operator is essentially driving blind.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from Shewhart:** *A state of statistical control is a state in which the variations in a process are solely due to common causes — i.e., the process is predictable within statistical bounds.*

---

## 1.3 Three Pillars of Shewhart's Legacy

Shewhart's contribution to modern quality management rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **The Shewhart Cycle (PDCA)** — A four-step iterative learning loop for continuous improvement.
2. **The Control Chart** — A graphical statistical tool to monitor process variation over time.
3. **The Concept of Statistical Control** — The idea that any process contains two distinct types of variation.

These three pillars are mathematically and conceptually interdependent, and together they constitute the bedrock upon which **Six Sigma, TQM, and ISO 9000** have been built.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Shewhart 3-Sigma Limits** are the most widely used control limits in industrial SPC. The numerical constants are **±3σ** (where σ is the standard deviation of the process variable). For a normal distribution, this captures approximately **99.73%** of common cause variation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Shewhart Cycle (PDCA) — Plan, Do, Check, Act

The **Shewhart Cycle**, later popularized by **W. Edwards Deming** (and often called the *Deming Cycle* or *PDCA*), is a four-stage iterative method for the continuous improvement of products and processes. It is the engine that drives every modern quality system.

### The Four Stages Explained

- **P — PLAN:** Identify the problem, analyze current state, hypothesize root causes, and design a planned improvement or intervention. This stage involves data collection, voice of customer (VOC) analysis, and target setting.
- **D — DO:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot run). Document observations, deviations, and unexpected outcomes meticulously. This is essentially a controlled experiment.
- **C — CHECK:** Compare the *observed results* against the *planned objectives* using statistical tools (control charts, hypothesis tests, Pareto analysis). Determine whether the intervention worked.
- **A — ACT:** If successful, *standardize* the change and roll it out across the organization. If unsuccessful, *abandon or revise* the plan and re-enter the PLAN stage. Either way, the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Shewhart Cycle" AND "Deming Cycle"?** Shewhart first described the cycle in 1939. Deming later refined and popularized it in Japan in the 1950s. KTU textbooks commonly refer to both names interchangeably, with PDCA being the universally accepted acronym.

## 2.2 The Shewhart Control Chart — Anatomy and Construction

A **control chart** is a time-series plot of a process characteristic with three horizontal reference lines:

| Line | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Center Line** | CL | The process mean (average historical value) |
| **Upper Control Limit** | UCL | Threshold above which the process is "out of control" |
| **Lower Control Limit** | LCL | Threshold below which the process is "out of control" |

By convention (and Shewhart's recommendation), the control limits are set at **±3σ** from the center line. Points falling within these limits represent **common cause variation**. Points outside the limits signal **special cause variation** requiring investigation.

### Two Families of Control Charts

Shewhart classified control charts into two broad families based on the type of data:

1. **Variable Control Charts** — for *continuous* data (measurements like length, weight, temperature):
   - **$\bar{X}$-chart (X-bar chart):** Monitors the process mean.
   - **R-chart (Range chart):** Monitors process variability (range within subgroups).
   - **S-chart (Standard deviation chart):** Modern alternative to R-chart for larger subgroups.

2. **Attribute Control Charts** — for *discrete* data (counts or classifications):
   - **p-chart:** Proportion of defective items.
   - **np-chart:** Number of defective items.
   - **c-chart:** Number of defects per unit.
   - **u-chart:** Defects per unit when sample size varies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Use **$\bar{X}$-R charts** when subgroup size $n \leq 10$. Use **$\bar{X}$-S charts** when subgroup size $n > 10$. Use **attribute charts** when measurement is impractical and only pass/fail or defect counts are available.

---

## 2.3 Common Cause vs. Special Cause Variation

Shewhart's most profound insight was that variation in any process is of **two distinct types**, and confusing them leads to two managerial errors:

| Type of Variation | Also Called | Nature | Manager's Correct Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Common Cause** | Chance cause, random variation, noise | Inherent to the process; affects every outcome | Improve the **system** itself |
| **Special Cause** | Assignable cause, signal | Due to identifiable external factors | Remove the **specific** cause |

> [!WARNING]
> **Deming's Hammer — The Two Deadly Sins:**
> - Treating common cause variation as special cause → tampering (over-adjustment) → inflates variation.
> - Treating special cause variation as common cause → neglect → lost opportunity for improvement.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Shewhart Control Chart Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for solving Shewhart control chart problems in KTU examinations. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table syntax.

| # | Chart Type | Center Line (CL) | Upper Control Limit (UCL) | Lower Control Limit (LCL) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\bar{X}$-chart | $\bar{\bar{X}}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ |
| 2 | R-chart | $\bar{R}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ |
| 3 | S-chart | $\bar{S}$ | $B_4 \bar{S}$ | $B_3 \bar{S}$ |
| 4 | p-chart | $\bar{p}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}}$ |
| 5 | np-chart | $n\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ | $n\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}$ |
| 6 | c-chart | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{c} + 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{c} - 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ |
| 7 | u-chart | $\bar{u}$ | $\bar{u} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ | $\bar{u} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{u}}{n}}$ |

### Definition of Constants Used

- **$\bar{\bar{X}}$** — Grand mean (average of all subgroup means).
- **$\bar{R}$** — Average range across all subgroups.
- **$\bar{S}$** — Average standard deviation across all subgroups.
- **$\bar{p}$** — Average proportion defective = (Total defectives) / (Total items inspected).
- **$\bar{c}$** — Average number of defects per unit.
- **$\bar{u}$** — Average defects per unit = (Total defects) / (Total units).

### Tabulated Control Chart Constants (selected values)

| Subgroup Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $B_3$ | $B_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 0 | 2.568 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 0 | 2.266 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 0 | 2.089 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 0.030 | 1.970 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 0.118 | 1.882 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 0.185 | 1.815 | 2.847 |
| 9 | 0.337 | 0.184 | 1.816 | 0.239 | 1.761 | 2.970 |
| 10 | 0.308 | 0.223 | 1.777 | 0.284 | 1.716 | 3.078 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world manufacturing (automotive, semiconductor, pharmaceutical), Shewhart charts are the *primary diagnostic tool* for SPC. Six Sigma's **DMAIC methodology** uses these charts in the **Measure** and **Control** phases. ISO 9001:2015 also implicitly requires statistical process monitoring for any process whose outputs cannot be fully verified by subsequent inspection.

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Procedure to Construct a Shewhart Control Chart

1. **Collect data** in rational subgroups (typically 20–25 subgroups, each of size $n = 4$ or $5$).
2. **Compute subgroup statistics** — mean ($\bar{X}_i$), range ($R_i$), or standard deviation ($S_i$).
3. **Compute grand statistics** — $\bar{\bar{X}}$, $\bar{R}$, $\bar{S}$, etc.
4. **Calculate control limits** using the formulas in the table above.
5. **Plot the points** with the CL, UCL, and LCL as horizontal reference lines.
6. **Interpret** — Investigate any points outside the limits or non-random patterns (runs, trends).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — Constructing an $\bar{X}$-R Chart

> **Problem (KTU Typical 14-mark style):**
> The following data represent the measurements (in mm) of a critical dimension taken from a manufacturing process. Subgroup size $n = 5$, and 6 subgroups have been collected. Construct $\bar{X}$ and R control charts and comment on process stability.
>
> | Subgroup $i$ | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ | $X_5$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 50 | 52 | 51 | 49 | 48 |
> | 2 | 51 | 50 | 49 | 52 | 53 |
> | 3 | 48 | 49 | 50 | 51 | 52 |
> | 4 | 50 | 51 | 52 | 50 | 49 |
> | 5 | 47 | 48 | 50 | 52 | 53 |
> | 6 | 52 | 53 | 51 | 49 | 50 |

### Step 1: Compute Subgroup Means ($\bar{X}_i$) and Ranges ($R_i$)

For each subgroup, the mean is the arithmetic average and the range is (max − min).

**Subgroup 1:** Values {50, 52, 51, 49, 48}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_1 &= \frac{50 + 52 + 51 + 49 + 48}{5} \\
&= \frac{250}{5} \\
&= 50.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_1 = \max - \min = 52 - 48 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Subgroup 2:** Values {51, 50, 49, 52, 53}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_2 &= \frac{51 + 50 + 49 + 52 + 53}{5} \\
&= \frac{255}{5} \\
&= 51.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_2 = 53 - 49 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Subgroup 3:** Values {48, 49, 50, 51, 52}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_3 &= \frac{48 + 49 + 50 + 51 + 52}{5} \\
&= \frac{250}{5} \\
&= 50.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_3 = 52 - 48 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Subgroup 4:** Values {50, 51, 52, 50, 49}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_4 &= \frac{50 + 51 + 52 + 50 + 49}{5} \\
&= \frac{252}{5} \\
&= 50.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_4 = 52 - 49 = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**Subgroup 5:** Values {47, 48, 50, 52, 53}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_5 &= \frac{47 + 48 + 50 + 52 + 53}{5} \\
&= \frac{250}{5} \\
&= 50.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_5 = 53 - 47 = 6 \text{ mm}$$

**Subgroup 6:** Values {52, 53, 51, 49, 50}

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_6 &= \frac{52 + 53 + 51 + 49 + 50}{5} \\
&= \frac{255}{5} \\
&= 51.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$R_6 = 53 - 49 = 4 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Compute Grand Mean ($\bar{\bar{X}}$) and Average Range ($\bar{R}$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{\bar{X}} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} \bar{X}_i}{6} \\
&= \frac{50.00 + 51.00 + 50.00 + 50.40 + 50.00 + 51.00}{6} \\
&= \frac{302.40}{6} \\
&= 50.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{R} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{6} R_i}{6} \\
&= \frac{4 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 6 + 4}{6} \\
&= \frac{25}{6} \\
&= 4.17 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Determine the Control Chart Constants

From the constants table for subgroup size $n = 5$:

$$A_2 = 0.577, \quad D_3 = 0, \quad D_4 = 2.114$$

### Step 4: Calculate Control Limits for $\bar{X}$-chart

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.40 \text{ mm} \\
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} \\
&= 50.40 + (0.577)(4.17) \\
&= 50.40 + 2.406 \\
&= 52.81 \text{ mm} \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} \\
&= 50.40 - 2.406 \\
&= 47.99 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Control Limits for R-chart

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CL}_R &= \bar{R} = 4.17 \text{ mm} \\
\text{UCL}_R &= D_4 \cdot \bar{R} \\
&= 2.114 \times 4.17 \\
&= 8.82 \text{ mm} \\
\text{LCL}_R &= D_3 \cdot \bar{R} \\
&= 0 \times 4.17 \\
&= 0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

All six subgroup means (50.00, 51.00, 50.00, 50.40, 50.00, 51.00) lie between LCL = 47.99 and UCL = 52.81 — **no $\bar{X}$ point is out of control**. All six ranges (4, 4, 4, 3, 6, 4) lie between LCL = 0 and UCL = 8.82 — **no R point is out of control**.

> **Conclusion:** The process is **in statistical control**. However, with only 6 subgroups, the analysis is preliminary; in practice, a minimum of 20–25 subgroups is recommended before drawing strong inferences (per the Central Limit Theorem and standard SPC practice).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — p-chart for Attribute Data

> **Problem:** A manufacturing line produces 500 units per day. The number of defectives observed on 7 consecutive days is: 12, 18, 15, 22, 14, 20, 16. Construct a p-chart for proportion defective and determine whether the process is in control.

### Step 1: Compute the Proportion Defective for Each Day

For each day, $p_i = (\text{Number of defectives}) / n$, where $n = 500$ is constant.

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= 12/500 = 0.024 \\
p_2 &= 18/500 = 0.036 \\
p_3 &= 15/500 = 0.030 \\
p_4 &= 22/500 = 0.044 \\
p_5 &= 14/500 = 0.028 \\
p_6 &= 20/500 = 0.040 \\
p_7 &= 16/500 = 0.032
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Average Proportion Defective ($\bar{p}$)

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{p} &= \frac{\sum n p_i}{\sum n} = \frac{\text{Total defectives}}{\text{Total inspected}} \\
&= \frac{12 + 18 + 15 + 22 + 14 + 20 + 16}{7 \times 500} \\
&= \frac{117}{3500} \\
&= 0.03343
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Standard Error of p

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_p &= \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1 - \bar{p})}{n}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{(0.03343)(0.96657)}{500}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{0.03231}{500}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.0000646} \\
&= 0.00804
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute Control Limits

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CL}_p &= \bar{p} = 0.03343 \\
\text{UCL}_p &= \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p \\
&= 0.03343 + 3(0.00804) \\
&= 0.03343 + 0.02412 \\
&= 0.05755 \\
\text{LCL}_p &= \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p \\
&= 0.03343 - 0.02412 \\
&= 0.00931
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

All seven daily proportions (0.024, 0.036, 0.030, 0.044, 0.028, 0.040, 0.032) lie within the limits [0.00931, 0.05755]. **The process is in statistical control.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Why 3-Sigma?

Shewhart's choice of **3-sigma limits** was not arbitrary. It represents an economic balance between two types of error:

- **Type I Error (False Alarm):** Concluding the process is out of control when it is actually in control. For a normal distribution, the probability of a point falling beyond ±3σ is **0.27%** (≈ 1 in 370 points).
- **Type II Error (Missed Signal):** Failing to detect an actual shift in the process.

The **Average Run Length (ARL)** for a 3-sigma control chart when the process is in control is:

$$\text{ARL}_0 = \frac{1}{0.0027} \approx 370.4$$

This means that, on average, one false alarm occurs every ~370 plotted points — economically acceptable for industrial use.

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Connection:** A Six Sigma process operates at ±6σ from the target, meaning the defect rate is **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. This is the central insight Motorola engineers derived by extending Shewhart's ±3σ philosophy to ±6σ, dramatically reducing the probability of a defect reaching a customer.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Shewhart PDCA Cycle — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["P - PLAN: Define problem, set objectives, hypothesize solutions, design data collection"]
    nodeB["D - DO: Execute pilot run, document observations, gather data"]
    nodeC["C - CHECK: Compare results with targets using statistical tools and control charts"]
    nodeD["A - ACT: Standardize if successful, abandon if not, then restart the cycle"]
    nodeE["Process Improvement: Continuous incremental gains compound over iterations"]
    nodeF["Common Cause Variation: Address by system redesign and management action"]
    nodeG["Special Cause Variation: Address by root cause elimination"]

    nodeA ==> nodeB
    nodeB ==> nodeC
    nodeC ==> nodeD
    nodeD -.->|"If successful"| nodeE
    nodeD -.->|"If unsuccessful"| nodeA
    nodeC -.->|"Detect via control chart"| nodeF
    nodeC -.->|"Detect via control chart"| nodeG
```

## 4.2 Shewhart Control Chart — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Raw Process Data: Continuous measurements or attribute counts from production"]
    node2["Subgroup Formation: Group data into rational subgroups of size n"]
    node3["Statistic Computation: Calculate mean X-bar, range R, or proportion p per subgroup"]
    node4["Grand Statistics: Compute grand mean, average range, or average proportion"]
    node5["Control Limit Calculation: Apply Shewhart 3-sigma formulas"]
    node6["Reference Lines: Center Line, Upper Control Limit, Lower Control Limit"]
    node7["Chart Plotting: Time-series plot with UCL, CL, LCL as horizontal lines"]
    node8["Pattern Analysis: Check for points beyond limits, runs, or trends"]
    node9["Decision: In control - continue process; Out of control - investigate assignable cause"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
```

## 4.3 Variation Classification — Functional Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Observed Process Variation"]
    nodeY["Is the point beyond the 3-sigma control limits?"]
    nodeZ1["Special Cause Variation: Remove the specific identifiable cause"]
    nodeZ2["Is there a non-random pattern inside the limits (runs, trends, cycles)?"]
    nodeZ3["Common Cause Variation: Improve the underlying system via PDCA"]
    nodeZ4["Continue monitoring: Process is stable and predictable"]

    nodeX --> nodeY
    nodeY -- Yes --> nodeZ1
    nodeY -- No --> nodeZ2
    nodeZ2 -- Yes --> nodeZ3
    nodeZ2 -- No --> nodeZ4
```

## 4.4 Shewhart's Place in the Quality Evolution Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1924["1924: Shewhart invents the control chart at Bell Labs"]
    node1939["1939: Shewhart publishes the PDCA cycle"]
    node1950["1950s: Deming and Juran introduce Shewhart's methods to Japan"]
    node1970["1970s-80s: Japanese quality revolution; TQM frameworks emerge"]
    node1986["1986: Motorola's Bill Smith develops Six Sigma, extending Shewhart 3-sigma to 6-sigma"]
    node1990["1990s: Six Sigma deployed at GE and AlliedSignal; global adoption"]
    node2000["2000s-Present: Lean Six Sigma integrates Shewhart's statistical foundations"]

    node1924 --> node1939
    node1939 --> node1950
    node1950 --> node1970
    node1970 --> node1986
    node1986 --> node1990
    node1990 --> node2000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Briefly explain Shewhart's contributions to Total Quality Management.**

**Model Answer:**

Walter A. Shewhart is recognized as the father of Statistical Quality Control (SQC). His three landmark contributions are:

1. **The Control Chart (1924):** A graphical tool to monitor process variation over time, distinguishing between common cause and special cause variation using ±3σ control limits.
2. **The PDCA Cycle (1939):** A four-stage iterative learning loop — **P**lan, **D**o, **C**heck, **A**ct — that provides a systematic methodology for continuous improvement.
3. **The Concept of Statistical Control:** The principle that a process is "in control" only when its variation is purely random (common cause) and predictable within statistical bounds.

These contributions form the statistical and philosophical foundation of modern TQM, Six Sigma, and ISO 9000 quality systems. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between common cause and special cause variation with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Inherent, random noise in the system | External, identifiable interference |
| **Frequency** | Affects every output | Affects specific outputs |
| **Detectability** | Requires statistical methods | Visible on control chart |
| **Example** | Minor tool wear over many cycles | Sudden machine breakdown, defective raw material batch |
| **Remedy** | System-level improvement (management) | Root cause removal (operator/engineer) |

Confusing the two leads to *tampering* (over-adjustment) or *neglect* (inaction). *(3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Shewhart Cycle (PDCA) in detail. Why is it considered the foundation of continuous improvement in TQM? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data relate to the diameter (in cm) of pistons manufactured in a process. Subgroup size $n = 4$. Construct an $\bar{X}$-chart and R-chart and comment on the process stability. **(7 marks)**

| Subgroup | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | $X_4$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.3 |
| 2 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.0 |
| 3 | 10.3 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.0 |
| 4 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 10.2 | 10.3 |
| 5 | 10.2 | 10.3 | 10.0 | 10.1 |

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) The Shewhart PDCA Cycle — 7 marks:**

The Shewhart Cycle (also called the Deming Cycle) is a four-step iterative methodology for continuous process improvement:

- **PLAN (2 marks):** Identify a problem or improvement opportunity, analyze current data, formulate a hypothesis about the root cause, and design a pilot intervention. Tools used: Pareto chart, fishbone diagram, VOC analysis.
- **DO (2 marks):** Execute the plan on a small scale. Collect detailed data and document every observation, deviation, and side effect.
- **CHECK (2 marks):** Statistically evaluate whether the intervention achieved the planned objectives. Use **Shewhart control charts**, hypothesis testing, and before/after comparison.
- **ACT (1 mark):** Standardize the change if successful (update SOPs, train staff); if not, revise the plan and restart. The cycle never terminates — it is a continuous spiral of improvement.

**Why it is foundational:** It embeds the *scientific method* into operational management, ensures decisions are data-driven rather than opinion-driven, and creates an organizational culture of learning and incremental improvement — the very essence of TQM.

---

**(b) Control Chart Construction — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Subgroup means and ranges**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{X}_1 &= (10.2 + 10.1 + 10.0 + 10.3)/4 = 10.150, \quad R_1 = 10.3 - 10.0 = 0.3 \\
\bar{X}_2 &= (10.0 + 10.2 + 10.1 + 10.0)/4 = 10.075, \quad R_2 = 10.2 - 10.0 = 0.2 \\
\bar{X}_3 &= (10.3 + 10.2 + 10.1 + 10.0)/4 = 10.150, \quad R_3 = 10.3 - 10.0 = 0.3 \\
\bar{X}_4 &= (10.1 + 10.0 + 10.2 + 10.3)/4 = 10.150, \quad R_4 = 10.3 - 10.0 = 0.3 \\
\bar{X}_5 &= (10.2 + 10.3 + 10.0 + 10.1)/4 = 10.150, \quad R_5 = 10.3 - 10.0 = 0.3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Grand statistics**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = (10.150 + 10.075 + 10.150 + 10.150 + 10.150)/5 = 10.135$$

$$\bar{R} = (0.3 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 + 0.3)/5 = 0.28$$

**Step 3: Control limits (using $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$ for $n=4$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 10.135 + (0.729)(0.28) = 10.135 + 0.204 = 10.339 \\
\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} &= 10.135 - 0.204 = 9.931 \\
\text{UCL}_R &= (2.282)(0.28) = 0.639 \\
\text{LCL}_R &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** All $\bar{X}_i$ values (10.150, 10.075, 10.150, 10.150, 10.150) lie within [9.931, 10.339] and all $R_i$ values (0.3, 0.2, 0.3, 0.3, 0.3) lie within [0, 0.639]. **The process is in statistical control**, though a larger sample (20–25 subgroups) is recommended for reliable inference.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What is a Shewhart control chart? Explain its components and the procedure to construct one. Discuss why 3-sigma limits are used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a manufacturing process, 10 samples each of size 100 are inspected. The number of defectives in each sample are: 5, 7, 6, 8, 4, 9, 5, 7, 6, 8. Construct a p-chart and interpret. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Shewhart Control Chart — 7 marks:**

A **Shewhart control chart** is a time-series plot of a process quality characteristic, with a center line (process mean) and upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL) at ±3 standard deviations, used to monitor whether a process remains in a state of statistical control.

**Components (3 marks):**
1. **Center Line (CL):** Average value of the statistic (e.g., $\bar{\bar{X}}$ or $\bar{p}$).
2. **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** $\text{CL} + 3\sigma_{\text{statistic}}$.
3. **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** $\text{CL} - 3\sigma_{\text{statistic}}$.
4. **Plotted points:** Subgroup statistics in time order.

**Construction Procedure (2 marks):** Collect 20–25 subgroups → compute statistic per subgroup → compute grand statistic → look up chart constants → compute UCL/LCL → plot → interpret.

**Why 3-sigma? (2 marks):** Provides a practical balance between Type I error (false alarm rate ≈ 0.27%, ARL₀ ≈ 370) and Type II error (missed detection). Wider limits reduce false alarms but delay detection; narrower limits cause excessive false alarms. Shewhart's economic analysis concluded 3-sigma is optimal for industrial SPC.

---

**(b) p-chart Construction — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute $p_i$ for each sample (n = 100):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_1 = 0.05, \quad p_2 = 0.07, \quad p_3 = 0.06, \quad p_4 = 0.08, \quad p_5 = 0.04 \\
p_6 = 0.09, \quad p_7 = 0.05, \quad p_8 = 0.07, \quad p_9 = 0.06, \quad p_{10} = 0.08
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Average proportion defective:

$$\bar{p} = \frac{5 + 7 + 6 + 8 + 4 + 9 + 5 + 7 + 6 + 8}{10 \times 100} = \frac{65}{1000} = 0.065$$

**Step 3:** Standard error:

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{(0.065)(0.935)}{100}} = \sqrt{0.0006078} = 0.02465$$

**Step 4:** Control limits:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{UCL}_p &= 0.065 + 3(0.02465) = 0.065 + 0.07395 = 0.13895 \\
\text{LCL}_p &= 0.065 - 0.07395 = -0.00895 \rightarrow \text{take as } 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — All $p_i$ values (0.04 to 0.09) lie within [0, 0.139]. **The process is in statistical control.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting that LCL cannot be negative for p-chart or c-chart; always set negative values to zero.
> - **Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Using wrong chart constants — $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ depend on subgroup size $n$. KTU expects students to recall or look up these values; misreading the table costs heavy marks.
> - **Mistake 3 (–2 marks):** Confusing **control limits** (±3σ, derived from data) with **specification limits** (given by customer/design). These are completely different concepts.
> - **Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Computing $\bar{p}$ incorrectly as the *average of daily $p_i$* when sample sizes vary — use the weighted average formula $\bar{p} = \Sigma np_i / \Sigma n$.
> - **Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Drawing the chart without units, labels, or a clear title — KTU examiners deduct for missing axis labels.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Shewhart**

- **Shewhart (1891–1967)** is the father of SPC; he invented the control chart in 1924 at Bell Labs.
- **Shewhart Cycle = PDCA:** Plan → Do → Check → Act; also called the Deming Cycle. It is *iterative and never-ending*.
- **Two types of variation:** **Common cause** (inherent, system-level) vs. **Special cause** (assignable, external). Confusing them = tampering or neglect.
- **3-sigma limits:** UCL = CL + 3σ, LCL = CL − 3σ. ARL₀ ≈ 370. This is the universal Shewhart standard.
- **Variable charts:** $\bar{X}$ (mean), R (range), S (standard deviation). Use $\bar{X}$-R for $n \leq 10$, $\bar{X}$-S for $n > 10$.
- **Attribute charts:** p (proportion), np (count defective), c (defects per unit), u (defects per unit, variable n).
- **Chart constants to memorize:** $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ (or at least for $n = 5$: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$).
- **Procedure:** Collect 20–25 subgroups → compute statistics → grand statistics → limits → plot → interpret.
- **Out-of-control signals:** Any point beyond UCL/LCL, 7+ consecutive points on one side of CL, obvious trends or cycles.
- **Control limits ≠ Specification limits:** Control limits describe *what the process is doing*; specification limits describe *what the customer wants*.
- **Six Sigma connection:** Six Sigma extends Shewhart's ±3σ to ±6σ, achieving 3.4 DPMO — a direct descendant of Shewhart's statistical philosophy.
- **Deming's 14 Points** and Japan's post-war quality revolution were all built directly on Shewhart's statistical foundations.
- **KTU-favorite exam trap:** Always report UCL, CL, AND LCL — partial control chart construction loses marks.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Shewhart | PEIET631 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Crosby's Quality Philosophy

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001)** was an American quality management thinker and author of the seminal book *"Quality is Free"* (1979). He is globally recognized for defining quality as **"conformance to requirements"** (not goodness or excellence), and for championing the doctrine of **"Zero Defects" (ZD)** and **"Quality Prevention"** rather than costly quality inspection.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Total Quality Management and Six Sigma* (PEIET631), Crosby's contribution is studied as one of the **Guru Theories of Quality Management**, alongside Deming, Juran, and Feigenbaum. His philosophy reframes quality from being an abstract ideal into a **measurable, binary, and cost-driven engineering discipline**.

### 1.1.1 Crosby's One-Sentence Definition of Quality

$$\text{Quality} \;\equiv\; \text{Conformance to Requirements} \;\; (\text{Crosby, 1979})$$

If a product or service conforms to the stated specifications the first time, it is a *quality product*. If not, it *lacks quality*—regardless of how elegant or expensive it appears.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Order Analogy**
> Imagine you order a *Veg Burger with no onions* at a restaurant.
> - **Deming's view**: The chef should *continuously improve* the burger taste over time (statistical, incremental optimization).
> - **Juran's view**: The chef should design a *fitness-for-use* burger (customer-focused fitness).
> - **Crosby's view**: If the specification says *"no onions,"* then **ANY onion in the burger is a defect**—period. The goal is **Zero Defects on the first try**, and prevention (the cook being trained, recipe being standardized) is cheaper than checking every plate at the table.
>
> For Crosby, quality is not about *making it better*; it is about **making it right the first time, every time, according to a written standard**.

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks of Crosby's Philosophy

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to memorize **three foundational pillars** of Crosby's thought:

| Pillar | Concept | KTU Key Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| **1. The Four Absolutes of Quality** | Non-negotiable universal truths about quality | *Conformance, Prevention, Zero Defects, Cost of Quality* |
| **2. The Five Steps to Quality Improvement** | A sequential action roadmap | *Involvement → Determine → Evaluate → Cost → Awareness → Action* |
| **3. The Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG)** | A 5-stage ladder measuring an organization's TQ readiness | *Uncertainty → Awakening → Enlightenment → Wisdom → Certainty* |
| **4. The Cost of Quality (COQ)** | The price of *not* doing things right the first time | *Prevention + Appraisal + Failure (Internal + External)* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Highlight for KTU Exams:** Crosby's COQ (Cost of Quality) is fundamentally a **management accounting tool**, and is the most frequently asked 3-mark question on him.

## 1.4 Standard Quality Metrics Used by Crosby

Crosby introduced and popularized two **measurable yardsticks** in management engineering:

1. **Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** – The total monetary cost incurred when work is *not* done right the first time (wasted labor, rework, warranty claims, lost goodwill). Often expressed in **% of sales revenue**.
2. **Cost of Quality (COQ)** – The sum of *Prevention* + *Appraisal* + *Failure* costs. Crosby's signature claim: **"Quality is free. It is not a gift, but it is free. What costs money are the unquality things."**

Both metrics are expressed in **standard units of currency (USD/INR)** or as a **percentage of sales turnover** in board-level reporting.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality (The Foundational Doctrine)

Crosby declared that **quality management is governed by four absolute truths** that apply to *every* organization, in *every* industry, at *every* stage of maturity. These are non-negotiable and form the philosophical bedrock of his school of thought.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Absolutes (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (not elegance, not luxury).
> 2. **Prevention, not Inspection** (do it right the first time; checking afterwards is wasteful).
> 3. **Zero Defects Performance Standard** (the only acceptable target; there is no acceptable defect rate).
> 4. **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** (the cost of doing things wrong).

### 2.1.1 Detailed Breakdown

**Absolute 1 — Quality is Conformance to Requirements.**
- Crosby rejected the romantic notion of "quality as goodness." A Rolls-Royce with a malfunctioning brake is *not* a quality product; a Maruti with perfectly working brakes *is*.
- Implication for engineering: **Specifications must be clearly written, measurable, and agreed upon** by both producer and customer.

**Absolute 2 — Prevention, not Inspection.**
- Inspection is a *late-stage filter* that finds defects *after* they have occurred. It is reactive and expensive.
- Prevention means **building quality into the process** through training, process standardization, and root-cause elimination.
- KTU board tip: Write the answer as *"Inspection is expensive and ineffective; prevention is cheap and effective."*

**Absolute 3 — Zero Defects Performance Standard.**
- Traditional quality thinking accepted an "acceptable quality level" (AQL), e.g., 1% defects are tolerable.
- Crosby insisted that **the only acceptable defect level is zero**. "Acceptable" defects are merely *scheduled non-conformances.*
- This is a **behavioral/attitudinal standard**, not merely a statistical one.

**Absolute 4 — Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC).**
- Quality cannot be measured by "how good" something is—it must be measured **financially** by what it costs when it is *wrong*.
- PONC includes rework, scrap, warranty returns, lost customers, and reputational damage.

## 2.2 Crosby's Five Steps to Quality Improvement

Crosby prescribed a **practical, sequential roadmap** for transforming an organization's quality posture. Unlike Deming's PDCA cycle, Crosby's steps are **linear and explicit**.

| Step # | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Management Commitment** | Top management publicly declares its dedication to quality improvement. | Without C-suite buy-in, no quality program survives. |
| **2. Quality Improvement Team** | A cross-functional team is appointed (often headed by a *Quality Improvement Facilitator*). | Drives and coordinates the program. |
| **3. Measurement of Quality** | Establish current quality levels through Quality Costs, employee awareness surveys, and capability indices. | *"You can't manage what you don't measure."* |
| **4. Cost of Quality Evaluation** | Quantify the PONC in monetary terms (₹/%). | Converts quality from an abstract goal to a *P&L line item*. |
| **5. Quality Awareness and Action** | Train all employees; set Zero Defects standards; establish a recognition system. | Embeds quality into the organization's cultural DNA. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Cue:** A common 14-mark question reads: *"Explain Crosby's Five Steps to Quality Improvement with examples."* Always structure the answer using the **tabular format above**, then narrate each step with one industry example (e.g., a manufacturing line, an IT helpdesk).

## 2.3 Crosby's Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG)

The **QMMG** is Crosby's *diagnostic tool*—a five-stage self-assessment ladder that helps an organization identify where it currently stands and what actions are required to climb higher.

| Stage | Name | Organizational Attitude | Typical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1** | **Uncertainty** | "We don't know why we have problems." | Management hides errors; no quality system exists. |
| **Stage 2** | **Awakening** | "We realize we have quality problems, but no one is assigned to fix them." | Quality department exists but is reactive; quality is treated as a fire-fighting activity. |
| **Stage 3** | **Enlightenment** | "We are beginning to take action—quality is a defined goal." | Quality policy is published; prevention programs begin; a quality manager is appointed. |
| **Stage 4** | **Wisdom** | "Quality is a continuous process and is measured." | Cost of quality is tracked; benchmarking begins; defects are decreasing. |
| **Stage 5** | **Certainty** | "Quality is ingrained in the corporate culture." | Zero Defects is the norm; prevention costs dominate the COQ; continuous improvement is instinctive. |

### 2.3.1 Engineering Utility of QMMG

- **Diagnostic use**: A consulting auditor uses the QMMG to pinpoint a client's *current maturity level* and prescribe targeted interventions.
- **Benchmarking tool**: Two factories of the same company can be compared on the grid.
- **Roadmap tool**: It functions as a *project plan* for a multi-year TQM rollout.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost of Quality (COQ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Crosby COQ Formula — High-Yield for Numerical Questions:**

$$\boxed{\text{COQ} \;=\; C_{\text{Prevention}} \;+\; C_{\text{Appraisal}} \;+\; C_{\text{Internal Failure}} \;+\; C_{\text{External Failure}}}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Term | Definition | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_{\text{Prevention}}$ | **Prevention Cost** | Money spent to *prevent* defects from occurring | Training, process design, quality planning, supplier capability audits |
| $C_{\text{Appraisal}}$ | **Appraisal Cost** | Money spent to *detect* defects | Incoming inspection, in-process testing, final QC testing, calibration of measuring equipment |
| $C_{\text{Internal Failure}}$ | **Internal Failure Cost** | Costs of defects found *before* shipment | Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis |
| $C_{\text{External Failure}}$ | **External Failure Cost** | Costs of defects found *after* shipment | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, loss of goodwill |

### 2.4.1 The PONC (Price of Non-Conformance) Formula

$$\boxed{\text{PONC} \;=\; C_{\text{Internal Failure}} \;+\; C_{\text{External Failure}}}$$

**Crosby's central managerial claim:** A firm should *invest in prevention and appraisal* to *dramatically reduce* PONC. The net effect is a **net cost saving**, even when prevention and appraisal budgets rise.

### 2.4.2 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Manufacturing**: A ₹500 crore auto-components plant uses COQ to justify a ₹2 crore investment in a robotic vision inspection system that drops external failures by 60%.
- **Software Engineering**: COQ is applied in CMMI Level 5 organizations where defect injection is prevented via formal code reviews (prevention) instead of late-stage QA testing.
- **Healthcare**: Hospital-acquired infection (HAI) rates are reduced by prevention training, lowering the catastrophic external-failure cost of patient lawsuits.

## 2.5 Crosby vs. Deming vs. Juran — KTU Comparison Table

> [!NOTE]
> This comparison is a *high-weightage question* in KTU exams (frequently asked for 7 or 14 marks).

| Dimension | **Crosby** | **Deming** | **Juran** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition of Quality** | Conformance to requirements | A predictable degree of uniformity and dependability | Fitness for use |
| **Focus** | Prevention, Zero Defects | Statistical Process Control, system thinking | Planning, control, improvement trilogy |
| **Standards** | Zero Defects is the standard | AQLs are acceptable; use SPC | Goal is *no* defects, but accepts statistical realities |
| **Measurement** | Price of Non-Conformance (PONC) | Process capability indices ($C_p$, $C_{pk}$) | Cost of poor quality, voice of customer |
| **Implementation** | 5 Steps, QMMG | 14 Points, PDCA, System of Profound Knowledge | Quality trilogy (plan–control–improve) |
| **Origin / Industry** | Corporate training, ITT | Post-WWII Japan (Statistical) | Post-WWII Japan (Engineering) |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem: Cost of Quality (COQ) Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Model KTU 14-Mark Style Question):**
> *"A precision-machined components manufacturer, Bharat Precision Pvt. Ltd., reports the following annual quality-related expenditure:*
>
> | Cost Element | Annual Spend (₹ Lakhs) |
> |---|---|
> | Operator and supervisor training | 12.0 |
> | Design of quality manuals and SOPs | 6.0 |
> | Incoming raw-material inspection | 8.0 |
> | In-process quality audits | 15.0 |
> | Final product testing and certification | 18.0 |
> | Scrap and rework (found in-house before dispatch) | 22.0 |
> | Warranty claims and field failures | 35.0 |
> | Customer returns and recall campaigns | 14.0 |
> | Loss of customer goodwill (estimated) | 9.0 |
>
> *Calculate:*
> *(a) The total Cost of Quality (COQ) and express it as a percentage of sales, given that annual turnover is ₹1,200 Lakhs.*
> *(b) The Price of Non-Conformance (PONC).*
> *(c) Comment on whether the company should increase its prevention spending."*

---

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Classification and Solution

**Step 1: Classify each cost element into one of the four COQ categories.**

We apply Crosby's strict definitions:

- *Operator and supervisor training* → **Prevention**
- *Design of quality manuals and SOPs* → **Prevention**
- *Incoming raw-material inspection* → **Appraisal**
- *In-process quality audits* → **Appraisal**
- *Final product testing and certification* → **Appraisal**
- *Scrap and rework (in-house)* → **Internal Failure**
- *Warranty claims and field failures* → **External Failure**
- *Customer returns and recall campaigns* → **External Failure**
- *Loss of customer goodwill* → **External Failure**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classification matters:** In a 14-mark question, examiners allocate marks for *correct classification logic*, not just the final number. Always justify *why* a cost belongs in a particular bucket.

**Step 2: Aggregate each category.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Prevention}} \; &= \; 12.0 + 6.0 \;=\; 18.0 \;\text{Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{Appraisal}} \; &= \; 8.0 + 15.0 + 18.0 \;=\; 41.0 \;\text{Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{Internal Failure}} \; &= \; 22.0 \;\text{Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_{\text{External Failure}} \; &= \; 35.0 + 14.0 + 9.0 \;=\; 58.0 \;\text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the total Cost of Quality.**

$$
\text{COQ} \;=\; C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} \; &= \; 18.0 \;+\; 41.0 \;+\; 22.0 \;+\; 58.0 \\[4pt]
&= \; 139.0 \;\text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute COQ as a percentage of sales.**

$$\text{COQ as \% of Sales} \;=\; \frac{\text{COQ}}{\text{Annual Turnover}} \times 100$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ as \% of Sales} \; &=\; \frac{139.0}{1200.0} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&=\; 11.58\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PONC} \; &=\; C_{\text{Internal Failure}} \;+\; C_{\text{External Failure}} \\[4pt]
&=\; 22.0 \;+\; 58.0 \\[4pt]
&=\; 80.0 \;\text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

PONC as a % of sales:

$$\frac{80.0}{1200.0} \times 100 \;=\; 6.67\%$$

**Step 6: Ratio analysis — Failure costs vs. Prevention + Appraisal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Failure Ratio} \; &=\; \frac{C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}}{C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}}} \\[4pt]
&=\; \frac{80.0}{18.0 + 41.0} \;=\; \frac{80.0}{59.0} \\[4pt]
&\approx\; 1.36
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Managerial interpretation (this is where KTU marks are awarded).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Key Insight from the Numbers:**
> - The company is spending **₹1.36 in failure costs for every ₹1 spent on prevention and appraisal.** This is a *classic Stage-2 (Awakening) organization* in the QMMG.
> - Failure costs (₹80 L) are dominated by **External Failure (₹58 L)**—a *red flag* indicating that defects are *escaping into customer hands*. External failures are 5–10× more expensive than internal ones (returns, recalls, lawsuits).
> - **Recommendation**: The firm should *increase prevention spending* (training, process design, supplier audits). Each additional ₹1 in prevention typically *saves* ₹3–₹10 in failure costs, according to Crosby's "Quality is Free" thesis.
> - The target Quality Management Maturity stage should be **Stage 4 (Wisdom)**, where the failure/prevention ratio is < 0.5.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Crosby's Five Steps Mapped to a Real Manufacturing Case

| Step | Action | Real-World Case: Defective PCB Soldering Line | Outcome Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Management Commitment** | CEO signs a *Zero Defects Quality Policy* displayed at every shop-floor entry. | Policy compliance audit score ≥ 95% |
| 2 | **Quality Improvement Team (QIT)** | A cross-functional QIT is formed (production, QA, design, supplier, finance). | Weekly QIT meetings with logged minutes |
| 3 | **Measurement of Quality** | Track **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities) on solder joints. Current DPMO = 18,500. | DPMO trending downward |
| 4 | **Cost of Quality Evaluation** | Calculate COQ: Prevention ₹5 L + Appraisal ₹12 L + Internal Failure ₹22 L + External Failure ₹48 L = ₹87 L on sales of ₹1,000 L → **8.7% COQ**. | COQ % target: < 5% |
| 5 | **Quality Awareness + Action** | Train 200 operators in *IPC-A-610* soldering standards; introduce *poka-yoke* (mistake-proofing) fixtures; award monthly *Zero Defect Champion* certificates. | DPMO drops to 4,200; external failures fall by 60%. |

---

## 3.3 Derived Insight: The Crosby Prevention-Effect Multiplier

For every additional ₹1 invested in prevention, Crosby observed (empirically) that firms saved ₹3 to ₹10 in failure costs. We can express this as a **Prevention Multiplier (PM):**

$$\boxed{\text{PM} \;=\; \frac{\Delta C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + \Delta C_{\text{External Failure}}}{\Delta C_{\text{Prevention}}}}$$

- If PM > 1 → *Prevention investment is financially justified.*
- If PM < 1 → The organization is in a *Stage-1* or *Stage-2* maturity state, where basic quality infrastructure is still being built.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Crosby's Quality Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Commitment] --> B[Quality Improvement Team Appointed]
    B --> C[Define Quality Requirements and Specifications]
    C --> D{Measure Current Performance}
    D --> E[Compute Cost of Quality COQ]
    E --> F[Identify Price of Non-Conformance PONC]
    F --> G[Set Zero Defects Target]
    G --> H[Design Prevention Programs]
    H --> I[Implement Awareness and Training]
    I --> J[Execute Zero Defects Action Plan]
    J --> K{Monitor and Audit}
    K --> L[Recognize and Reward ZD Champions]
    L --> M[Re-Measure COQ and PONC]
    M --> D
    H --> N[Appraisal Activities]
    N --> K
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**
- The diagram is **closed-loop**, reflecting Crosby's emphasis on continuous reinforcement of standards.
- Block `D → E → F` represents the **measurement–monetization pipeline** that converts abstract quality into hard currency.
- Block `H (Prevention)` is the **primary lever** in Crosby's doctrine; `N (Appraisal)` is secondary.
- The entire loop is fed by `A (Management Commitment)`—without which the loop collapses (Crosby's first absolute).

---

## 4.2 Crosby's Quality Management Maturity Grid — Stage Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Uncertainty] -->|Recognize Problems Exist| S2[Stage 2 Awakening]
    S2 -->|Appoint Quality Manager| S3[Stage 3 Enlightenment]
    S3 -->|Measure COQ and SPC| S4[Stage 4 Wisdom]
    S4 -->|Embed Zero Defects Culture| S5[Stage 5 Certainty]
    S5 -.->|Continuous Improvement Loop| S5
```

**Reading the Ladder:**
- **S1 → S2**: From *denial* to *acknowledgement* of quality problems.
- **S2 → S3**: From *reactive fire-fighting* to *proactive policy*.
- **S3 → S4**: From *informal effort* to *measurable systems*.
- **S4 → S5**: From *measured improvement* to *cultural entrenchment*.

---

## 4.3 Cost of Quality — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    COQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ] --> P[Prevention Costs]
    COQ --> Ap[Appraisal Costs]
    COQ --> IF[Internal Failure Costs]
    COQ --> EF[External Failure Costs]
    P --> P1[Training]
    P --> P2[Process Design]
    P --> P3[Supplier Audits]
    Ap --> Ap1[Inspection]
    Ap --> Ap2[Testing]
    Ap --> Ap3[Calibration]
    IF --> IF1[Scrap]
    IF --> IF2[Rework]
    IF --> IF3[Re-inspection]
    EF --> EF1[Warranty Claims]
    EF --> EF2[Returns and Recalls]
    EF --> EF3[Lawsuits]
    EF --> EF4[Loss of Goodwill]
    IF --> PONC[Price of Non-Conformance PONC]
    EF --> PONC
```

**Engineering Insight:**
- The **PONC** is the *sum* of the two *failure* branches. It is the metric managers must *minimize*.
- Prevention and Appraisal are *enablers*—they are investments that *reduce* the PONC.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
*State and explain Crosby's four absolutes of quality management.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

Crosby proposed four universal, non-negotiable truths about quality:

1. **Quality means conformance to requirements**, not elegance or luxury. A product that meets its written specifications is a quality product.
2. **Prevention, not inspection**, is the path to quality. Inspecting for defects after they occur is costly and reactive; building quality into the process is cheap and proactive.
3. **Zero Defects** is the only acceptable performance standard. There is no economic reason to accept a tolerable defect rate.
4. **Measure quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)**. Quality must be quantified in monetary terms (₹/% of sales) to be managed at the board level.

*(1 mark for listing the four absolutes; 2 marks for explaining each in one sentence.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define Cost of Quality (COQ). List its four components with one example each.*

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition**: Cost of Quality is the total expenditure an organization incurs to ensure product/service conformance and the costs incurred due to non-conformance.

**Formula**:

$$\text{COQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}$$

**Components**:

| Component | Example |
|---|---|
| Prevention | Operator training programs |
| Appraisal | Incoming material inspection |
| Internal Failure | Scrap and rework in-house |
| External Failure | Warranty claims and product recalls |

*(1 mark for definition and formula; 2 marks for correct examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question carries **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both options are provided below. Students answer **one** complete option.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *Crosby's Cost-of-Quality Driven Case Study*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
*(a)* Discuss in detail **Crosby's Four Absolutes of Quality**. How do they differ from the romantic notion of quality as "goodness"? *(7 marks)*

*(b)* A medium-scale electronics assembly unit has annual sales of ₹600 Lakhs. The following annual quality-related costs are recorded:
- Operator training: ₹4 L
- Quality manual design: ₹2 L
- Incoming inspection: ₹5 L
- In-process testing: ₹7 L
- Final QC testing: ₹6 L
- In-house rework: ₹10 L
- Warranty claims: ₹18 L
- Customer returns: ₹9 L
- Goodwill loss (estimated): ₹5 L

Calculate the **total Cost of Quality**, the **PONC**, and the **COQ as a percentage of sales**. Comment on the company's maturity level using Crosby's QMMG and recommend two actionable steps. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**(a) Crosby's Four Absolutes (7 marks)**

1. **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** — Crosby rejected the "goodness" view because it is unmeasurable and subjective. Quality is binary: the product either meets the written specification or it does not. *Example*: A wooden chair that meets the load-bearing specification of 150 kg is a quality chair even if its design is plain.

2. **Prevention, Not Inspection** — Inspection discovers defects *after* they are produced and is thus expensive. Prevention (training, process standardization, mistake-proofing) stops defects from arising in the first place.

3. **Zero Defects Performance Standard** — Crosby declared that "acceptable quality levels" (AQLs) are merely *planned non-conformances*. The only performance standard should be Zero Defects. This is a behavioural target that mobilizes employees toward perfection.

4. **Measure Quality by the Price of Non-Conformance (PONC)** — Quality is best quantified through the monetary cost of failures. PONC converts an abstract ideal into a hard ₹/% number that the CFO and CEO can act upon.

**Difference from "goodness" view**: The goodness view (rooted in philosophy/aesthetics) treats quality as a *spectrum*. Crosby treats quality as a *binary, contractual, measurable state*—an engineering standard rather than an artistic judgment.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Classification**

| Cost Element | Category | Amount (₹ L) |
|---|---|---|
| Operator training | Prevention | 4 |
| Quality manual design | Prevention | 2 |
| Incoming inspection | Appraisal | 5 |
| In-process testing | Appraisal | 7 |
| Final QC testing | Appraisal | 6 |
| In-house rework | Internal Failure | 10 |
| Warranty claims | External Failure | 18 |
| Customer returns | External Failure | 9 |
| Goodwill loss | External Failure | 5 |

**Step 2: Aggregation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{Prevention}} &= 4 + 2 = 6\;\text{Lakhs} \\
C_{\text{Appraisal}} &= 5 + 7 + 6 = 18\;\text{Lakhs} \\
C_{\text{Internal Failure}} &= 10\;\text{Lakhs} \\
C_{\text{External Failure}} &= 18 + 9 + 5 = 32\;\text{Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total COQ**

$$
\text{COQ} = 6 + 18 + 10 + 32 = 66\;\text{Lakhs}
$$

**Step 4: PONC**

$$
\text{PONC} = 10 + 32 = 42\;\text{Lakhs}
$$

**Step 5: COQ as % of Sales**

$$\frac{66}{600} \times 100 = 11.0\%$$

**Step 6: Failure-to-Prevention Ratio**

$$\frac{42}{6 + 18} = \frac{42}{24} = 1.75$$

**Interpretation**: A failure-to-prevention ratio of 1.75 indicates that for every ₹1 spent on prevention and appraisal, the firm suffers ₹1.75 in failure costs. The COQ at 11% of sales is significantly above the world-class benchmark of 2.5%.

**QMMG Maturity Level**: The firm is at **Stage 2 (Awakening)** — quality problems are recognized but not systematically managed; no major prevention programs exist.

**Recommended Actions**:

1. **Establish a Quality Improvement Team (QIT)** chaired by a senior manager, with cross-functional representation (production, design, supplier QA, finance).
2. **Quadruple the prevention budget** (e.g., invest ₹8 L in operator re-training, supplier capability development, and mistake-proofing fixtures) — projected to reduce PONC by 40–50% within 12 months, per Crosby's Prevention Multiplier principle.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Crosby's Five Steps & QMMG Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**
*(a)* Explain in detail **Crosby's Five Steps to Quality Improvement**. Describe the role of management commitment in making the program succeed. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Describe the **Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG)** developed by Philip Crosby. Explain the characteristics of each of the five stages and outline how an organization can move from Stage 2 to Stage 4. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**(a) Crosby's Five Steps to Quality Improvement (7 marks)**

| Step | Title | Key Activities |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Management Commitment** | Top management (CEO/MD) must publicly declare and document its commitment to quality improvement. Without this, employees perceive quality as a passing fad. |
| 2 | **Quality Improvement Team** | A cross-functional QIT is appointed. A *Quality Improvement Facilitator* coordinates the effort; team members represent production, design, purchasing, finance. |
| 3 | **Measurement of Quality** | Quality must be *measured* before it can be *managed*. Crosby recommended three key measurements: **Cost of Quality, Quality Awareness (employee surveys), and Quality Capability Indices**. |
| 4 | **Cost of Quality Evaluation** | Translate the quality data into monetary terms. Identify the Price of Non-Conformance and set targets for reduction. |
| 5 | **Quality Awareness and Action** | Train all employees; communicate Zero Defects expectations; establish *Recognition and Reward* programs to celebrate defect-free work; install *Corrective Action* systems for when defects slip through. |

**Role of Management Commitment**:
- Crosby insisted that *no quality program can succeed without visible, sustained C-suite involvement*. Management must:
  - Communicate the quality vision in *writing*.
  - Allocate **budget and personnel** (not just slogans).
  - *Participate* in QIT reviews (not merely receive reports).
  - Hold themselves *accountable* for PONC reduction targets.
- Without top-management commitment, the program degenerates into a paperwork exercise and dies within 6–12 months.

---

**(b) Quality Management Maturity Grid (QMMG) (7 marks)**

Crosby's QMMG is a **diagnostic and benchmarking tool** that classifies an organization into one of five maturity stages:

| Stage | Name | Defining Characteristics | Symptoms |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Uncertainty** | Management is unaware of quality as a strategic concern. | Quality is treated as the *production department's problem*; defects are hidden; no quality system exists. |
| **2** | **Awakening** | Management realizes quality problems exist but lacks a structured response. | A quality department exists but is reactive; *fighting fires*; customer complaints are rising. |
| **3** | **Enlightenment** | Quality improvement is recognized as a strategic goal; formal programs are launched. | A quality policy is published; a quality manager is appointed; prevention programs begin. |
| **4** | **Wisdom** | Quality is measured and continuously improved using data-driven methods. | Cost of quality is tracked; defects decline steadily; benchmarking is practiced; supplier quality is audited. |
| **5** | **Certainty** | Quality is embedded in the organization's DNA. | Zero Defects is the norm; prevention dominates the COQ; employees self-initiate improvements. |

**How to Move from Stage 2 to Stage 4**:

| From → To | Action Plan |
|---|---|
| **Stage 2 → Stage 3** | Appoint a senior **Quality Manager** reporting to the CEO. Publish a written quality policy. Establish a formal **Quality Improvement Team (QIT)**. Allocate a prevention budget (≥ 5% of COQ). |
| **Stage 3 → Stage 4** | Implement a **Cost of Quality measurement system** (monthly tracking). Conduct **employee quality-awareness training** (≥ 20 hours/year per employee). Adopt **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **Corrective Action (CAPA)** systems. Recognize and reward *Zero Defect* performers publicly. |

**Outcome**: An organization progressing from Stage 2 to Stage 4 typically reduces its COQ from 10–15% of sales to 3–5% of sales within 24–36 months — a transformative economic impact.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Crosby Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Conformance with Goodness** — Many students answer *"Quality means the product is good"* → ZERO marks. The KTU answer key strictly demands *"Conformance to requirements."*
> 2. **Treating Zero Defects as a Statistical Target** — Zero Defects is a *behavioural/attitudinal* standard, not a Six-Sigma-style ppm target. Writing it as *"3.4 defects per million opportunities"* (a Six Sigma metric) is conceptually wrong in a Crosby context.
> 3. **Missing the External Failure Bucket** — Students often include warranty claims in *appraisal* or *internal failure*. Warranty and recall costs are *external failure*; this misclassification loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Management Commitment** — When asked to describe Crosby's philosophy, students list the 4 absolutes but forget to highlight that *management commitment is the foundational enabler*. Always begin Crosby answers with management leadership.
> 5. **Numerical Miscalculation** — In COQ problems, students often forget to add goodwill loss to *external failure*. Always re-read the cost list before calculating.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crosby's Quality Philosophy — KTU One-Page Rapid Revision**

- **Quality = Conformance to Requirements** (binary, contractual — *not* goodness, *not* luxury).
- **Prevention > Inspection** (training, process design, mistake-proofing *before* defects occur).
- **Zero Defects** is the *only* acceptable performance standard (behavioural target).
- **PONC (Price of Non-Conformance)** is the management metric — measured in ₹ or % of sales.
- **Five Steps to Quality Improvement**: Commitment → QIT → Measurement → COQ Evaluation → Awareness & Action.
- **QMMG Maturity Stages**: Uncertainty → Awakening → Enlightenment → Wisdom → Certainty.
- **COQ Formula**:
  $$\text{COQ} = C_{\text{Prevention}} + C_{\text{Appraisal}} + C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}$$
- **PONC Formula**:
  $$\text{PONC} = C_{\text{Internal Failure}} + C_{\text{External Failure}}$$
- **Prevention Multiplier (PM)**: Every ₹1 in prevention saves ₹3–₹10 in failure costs → *"Quality is Free."*
- **Quality Maturity Indicator**: Failure-to-Prevention ratio > 1 indicates Stage 2; ratio < 0.5 indicates Stage 4–5 maturity.
- **Source Book**: *"Quality is Free" (1979), Philip B. Crosby* — the foundational text for KTU exam references.
- **Key Distinction**: Crosby focuses on *behavioural standards and cost economics*; Deming focuses on *statistical control*; Juran focuses on *customer fitness*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feigenbaum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Feigenbaum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Armand V. Feigenbaum — The Architect of Total Quality Control (TQC)

**Armand Vallin Feigenbaum** (1922–2014) was an American quality management pioneer and a former President of the American Society for Quality (ASQ). He introduced the revolutionary concept of **Total Quality Control (TQC)** in his landmark 1956 Harvard Business Review article *"Total Quality Control"* and later expanded it in his classic book *"Total Quality Control"* (1961, revised 1983 and 1991).

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feigenbaum's Total Quality Control (TQC)** is an organisation-wide quality management system in which quality is built into every product, process, and service through the integrated effort of every department, function, and employee — from the CEO to the shop-floor worker — in order to achieve the highest levels of customer satisfaction at the lowest total cost.

In Feigenbaum's own words:

> *"Quality is everyone's job, not just the quality department's."*

### Key Contributions of Feigenbaum to Quality Management

1. **Coined the term "Total Quality Control (TQC)"** — the first holistic, integrated quality philosophy.
2. Introduced the concept of **"Hidden Quality Costs"** and structured the **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** framework.
3. Proposed the **PAF Model** (Prevention – Appraisal – Failure costs).
4. Emphasised **customer-driven quality** long before TQM became mainstream.
5. Identified **9 critical practical areas** for TQC implementation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The House Building Team":**
> Imagine building a house. The mason builds walls, the electrician wires, the plumber fixes pipes, and the painter colours. Earlier, only a separate "Inspector" (the quality department) checked the house. Feigenbaum's insight was: *the mason must lay the bricks straight, the electrician must wire safely, and the plumber must prevent leaks — quality is built at the source, by every worker, not inspected in at the end.* Quality is a **team sport**, not a "Quality Police" activity. Every player must be skilled, motivated, and accountable.

### The 9 Practical Areas of TQC (Feigenbaum)

> [!NOTE]
> Feigenbaum stated that TQC must be comprehensively addressed across these 9 areas:

1. Marketing
2. Materials
3. Product / Process Engineering
4. Manufacturing (Production)
5. Inspection & Test
6. Assembly & Installation
7. Shipping & Distribution
8. After-Sales Service
9. Quality Cost Measurement

### Core Philosophy in Bullet Form

- **Customer is the ultimate judge of quality** — not the engineer or the factory.
- **Quality must be built in, not inspected in.**
- **Every department is a quality department.**
- **The cost of poor quality is far higher than the cost of prevention.**
- **Quality is a *system*, not a *programme*** — it must be permanently embedded in organisational culture.
- **TQC is a people-driven process** — leadership and human development are central.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feigenbaum's Principles of Total Quality Control

Feigenbaum articulated **three fundamental steps to quality**, which form the backbone of TQC:

### Step 1: Quality Leadership
- Quality must start with **top management commitment**.
- The CEO is the *Chief Quality Officer* in spirit.
- Quality goals must be integrated into the **business strategy**.

### Step 2: Modern Quality Technology
- Use of **statistical quality control (SQC)**, but integrated across the entire organisation.
- Quality engineering is not a tool — it is a *mindset*.
- New product and process design must consider reliability, maintainability, and customer use conditions.

### Step 3: Organisational Commitment
- Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, must be involved.
- Continuous **training and education** in quality concepts.
- Recognition and reward systems for quality contributions.

## 2.2 Feigenbaum's Quality Cost (Cost of Quality) Framework

Feigenbaum was the **first to systematically classify quality-related costs** into the now-famous **PAF Model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAF Model — Prevention, Appraisal, Failure Costs**

### A. Prevention Costs (Cost of Avoiding Defects)
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place.

| Sub-Category | Example |
|---|---|
| Quality planning | Developing quality manuals, procedures |
| Product/process design review | Design FMEA, robust design |
| Training | Quality awareness programmes |
| Supplier capability assessment | Vendor audits |
| Quality data analysis | SPC system installation |

### B. Appraisal Costs (Cost of Detecting Defects)
Costs of *measuring, inspecting, and testing* products to detect non-conformances.

| Sub-Category | Example |
|---|---|
| Incoming inspection | Testing raw materials |
| In-process inspection | Line checks |
| Final inspection | Pre-dispatch testing |
| Test equipment maintenance | Calibration of gauges |
| Quality audits | System audits |

### C. Internal Failure Costs (Cost of Defects Caught Inside)
Costs arising from defects *discovered before* the product reaches the customer.

| Sub-Category | Example |
|---|---|
| Scrap | Material rejected as unusable |
| Rework | Labour to fix defective items |
| Re-inspection | Re-testing after rework |
| Failure analysis | Engineering investigation |
| Downtime | Production line stoppage |

### D. External Failure Costs (Cost of Defects Reaching Customer)
Costs arising from defects *discovered by the customer* after delivery.

| Sub-Category | Example |
|---|---|
| Warranty claims | Free repairs under guarantee |
| Field service | On-site repair visits |
| Returned goods | Product recalls |
| Complaint handling | Customer service overhead |
| Loss of goodwill | Damaged brand reputation, lost future sales |
| Product liability | Legal costs, settlements |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cost of Quality (CoQ)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly testable in KTU University Exams**.

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Total Quality Cost (TQC)} = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ | Sum of all four cost categories |
| 2 | $\text{PC} = \text{Prevention Costs}$ | Cost of preventing defects |
| 3 | $\text{AC} = \text{Appraisal Costs}$ | Cost of detecting defects |
| 4 | $\text{IFC} = \text{Internal Failure Costs}$ | Cost of in-house defects |
| 5 | $\text{EFC} = \text{External Failure Costs}$ | Cost of customer-found defects |
| 6 | $\text{Conformance Cost} = PC + AC$ | Cost of doing things right |
| 7 | $\text{Non-Conformance Cost} = IFC + EFC$ | Cost of doing things wrong |
| 8 | $\text{Quality Cost Ratio} = \dfrac{PC + AC}{IFC + EFC}$ | Efficiency indicator (ideal $\gg 1$) |
| 9 | $\text{CoQ as \% of Sales} = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Total Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Benchmarking metric |
| 10 | $\text{Hidden Quality Cost} \approx 4 \times \text{Visible Quality Cost}$ | Feigenbaum's empirical estimate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Feigenbaum's 4× Rule:** Feigenbaum famously stated that the *visible* quality costs (accounted in books) are only about **one-fourth** of the *hidden* costs (lost morale, lost customers, lost opportunities, rework time not logged, etc.). Thus, **actual quality cost ≈ 4 × reported quality cost**.

## 2.4 Feigenbaum's Quality Cost Curve (Conceptual)

Feigenbaum demonstrated that as **prevention and appraisal investment increases**, the **failure costs decrease at a much faster rate** — leading to a *net reduction* in total quality cost.

- At **low prevention investment** → failure costs are *extremely high* (scrap, rework, warranty, reputation loss).
- At **optimal prevention investment** → total quality cost reaches its **minimum**.
- Beyond the optimum, over-investment in prevention again raises total cost (diminishing returns).

This insight is the **economic justification** for TQC — investing in prevention *saves money* in the long run.

## 2.5 Feigenbaum's Distinction Between TQC and TQM

| Aspect | TQC (Feigenbaum) | TQM (Broader Movement) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | 1956 — Feigenbaum | 1980s — Deming, Juran, Ishikawa, Crosby |
| Scope | Quality is a managerial control function | Quality is a strategic, cultural philosophy |
| Focus | Cost of quality, systems approach | Customer focus, continuous improvement, people |
| Approach | Engineering + managerial | Holistic — including leadership, HR, strategy |
| View | Quality as a *system* managed by all | Quality as a *way of life* in the organisation |

> [!NOTE]
> TQC is the **conceptual precursor** of TQM. Feigenbaum's TQC laid the intellectual foundation upon which later Japanese-inspired TQM was built.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Breakdown & Cost-of-Quality Computation

## 3.1 Worked Example — Calculating Cost of Quality (14-Mark KTU Style)

### Problem Statement
A precision-components manufacturing firm reported the following quality-related expenses for FY 2023-24. Compute: (a) Total Quality Cost, (b) Conformance & Non-Conformance costs, (c) CoQ as a percentage of sales, and (d) Comment on Feigenbaum's hidden cost estimate.

| Cost Item | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
