<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any six major benefits of Total Quality Management to an organization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The six major benefits of TQM are:
1. **Improved Customer Satisfaction** — through better products and services
2. **Enhanced Employee Morale** — via empowerment and participation
3. **Reduced Operational Costs** — through waste elimination and process optimization
4. **Higher Profitability** — via cost savings and premium pricing
5. **Stronger Competitive Position** — through superior brand reputation
6. **Better Supplier Relationships** — fostering long-term, mutually beneficial partnerships

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 0.5 marks per benefit with brief explanation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) and explain its four components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**COPQ** is the total cost incurred by an organization due to quality failures — i.e., all costs that would *disappear* if products and processes were perfect.

$$\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}$$

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Prevention Costs | Money spent to *prevent* defects | Training, quality planning |
| Appraisal Costs | Costs of *detecting* defects | Inspection, testing |
| Internal Failure | Costs of defects found *before* delivery | Scrap, rework |
| External Failure | Costs of defects found *after* delivery | Warranty, returns |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various benefits of TQM to customers, employees, and the organization as a whole. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company invests ₹8,00,000 in TQM. The implementation results in: defect reduction of 5,000 units (cost ₹150/unit), time saved of 1,200 hours (₹200/hour), and waste reduction of 800 kg (₹80/kg). Calculate the Net Benefit and ROQ. Comment on the viability of TQM adoption. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Customer Benefits (2 Marks):**
- Improved product and service quality
- Higher satisfaction and loyalty
- Reduced complaints and faster resolution
- Increased retention rates and referrals

**Employee Benefits (2 Marks):**
- Empowerment through participation
- Higher morale and job satisfaction
- Skill enhancement via training
- Lower turnover and absenteeism

**Organizational Benefits (3 Marks):**
- *Operational:* Reduced waste, faster cycle times, higher first-pass yield
- *Financial:* Lower COPQ, higher profit margins, improved ROI
- *Strategic:* Competitive advantage, brand reputation, market expansion

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:**
$$\begin{aligned}
I_q &= ₹8,00,000 \\
\Delta D &= 5,000 \text{ units}, \quad C_d = ₹150/\text{unit} \\
\Delta T &= 1,200 \text{ hours}, \quad C_t = ₹200/\text{hour} \\
\Delta W &= 800 \text{ kg}, \quad C_w = ₹80/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1: Calculate Gross Financial Benefit $B_f$**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_f &= (\Delta D \times C_d) + (\Delta T \times C_t) + (\Delta W \times C_w) \\
&= (5,000 \times 150) + (1,200 \times 200) + (800 \times 80) \\
&= 7,50,000 + 2,40,000 + 64,000 \\
&= ₹10,54,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Net Benefit**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Benefit} &= B_f - I_q \\
&= 10,54,000 - 8,00,000 \\
&= ₹2,54,000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate ROQ**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{ROQ (\%)} &= \frac{2,54,000}{8,00,000} \times 100\% \\
&= 31.75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Comment on Viability (2 Marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Compound Benefit over 3 years at 10\% growth} &= 2,54,000 \times (1.10)^3 \\
&= ₹3,38,234 \\
\text{Cumulative ROQ over 3 years} &= \frac{3,38,234}{8,00,000} \times 100\% = 42.28\%
\end{aligned}$$

The TQM adoption is **highly viable** because the company recovers its investment within the first year and generates 31.75% additional return. Over 3 years, this compounds further, alongside intangible benefits like customer loyalty, employee morale, and brand reputation that are difficult to quantify but extremely valuable.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often forget to include all three components ($\Delta D$, $\Delta T$, $\Delta W$) and only calculate one. Always show *all* three lines. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not stating the units (₹) in the final answer. *(Lose 0.5 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Skipping the "comment on viability" part — this carries 2 marks separately. Always add a *qualitative* insight, not just numbers. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to multiply by 100 in ROQ calculation. *(Lose 0.5 mark)*

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of employee satisfaction as a benefit of TQM. How does it cascade into organizational performance? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the "Before TQM" and "After TQM" scenarios of a software firm where the following improvements occurred: defect density reduced from 2.5 to 0.4 defects per KLOC, on-time delivery improved from 65% to 92%, and customer satisfaction rose from 70% to 94%. Compute the percentage improvement in each metric. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Employee Satisfaction as a TQM Benefit (3 Marks):**
- TQM treats employees as *internal customers*
- Empowerment through quality circles and Kaizen teams
- Recognition and reward systems boost morale
- Continuous training enhances skills and confidence

**Cascade into Organizational Performance (4 Marks):**
- *Higher Engagement* → Better productivity
- *Lower Turnover* → Reduced recruitment costs
- *Better Skills* → Fewer defects, higher quality
- *Stronger Teamwork* → Faster problem-solving
- *Innovation Culture* → Continuous improvement
- The cascade: **Employee Satisfaction → Process Discipline → Customer Satisfaction → Financial Performance**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Metric 1: Defect Density Improvement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement (\%)} &= \frac{2.5 - 0.4}{2.5} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2.1}{2.5} \times 100\% \\
&= 84\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Metric 2: On-Time Delivery Improvement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement (\%)} &= \frac{92 - 65}{65} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{27}{65} \times 100\% \\
&= 41.54\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Metric 3: Customer Satisfaction Improvement**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement (\%)} &= \frac{94 - 70}{70} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{24}{70} \times 100\% \\
&= 34.29\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Tabular Summary (1 Mark):**

| Metric | Before TQM | After TQM | Improvement (%) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Defect Density (per KLOC) | 2.5 | 0.4 | 84.00% |
| On-Time Delivery (%) | 65 | 92 | 41.54% |
| Customer Satisfaction (%) | 70 | 94 | 34.29% |

**Conclusion:** TQM delivers the **largest impact on technical quality** (84% defect reduction), followed by **delivery performance** (41.54%) and **customer perception** (34.29%) — confirming the cascading benefit structure taught in Module 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b) Alternative:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Dividing by the *new* value instead of the *old* value in percentage improvement. The denominator should always be the *baseline (before)* value. *(Lose 1 mark per calculation)*
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not converting decimals properly (e.g., writing 84 instead of 84%). *(Lose 0.5 mark)*
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Skipping the final comparative table — it consolidates understanding and earns an easy 1 mark. *(Lose 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Benefits of TQM**

- **Six Benefit Categories:** Customer, Employee, Operational, Financial, Strategic, Supplier — know all six by heart.
- **COPQ Formula:** Always remember the four components — *Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure*.
- **ROQ Formula:** $\text{ROQ} = \frac{B_f - I_q}{I_q} \times 100\%$ — industry benchmark is $\geq 150\%$ over 3 years.
- **Customer Satisfaction Index:** Measured as $\frac{\text{Satisfied}}{\text{Total Surveyed}} \times 100\%$; target $\geq 90\%$.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY):** A core operational metric — world-class FPY is $\geq 95\%$.
- **Compound Benefit:** TQM benefits grow over time using $B(t) = B_0(1+r)^t$ with $r \approx 8–15\%$.
- **Cascade Order:** Investment → Process Improvement → Customer Satisfaction → Financial Gain → Strategic Advantage.
- **Intangible Benefits:** Brand image, employee morale, innovation culture — *mention these in every theory answer*.
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ):** TQM *increases* prevention and appraisal costs but *drastically reduces* failure costs — leading to net savings.
- **Real-World Linkages:** Always connect answers to industries like *automotive (Tata, Toyota), IT (Infosys), healthcare (Apollo Hospitals), or manufacturing (L&T)* for applied marks.
- **Diagram Recall:** Be ready to sketch the *six pillars*, *COPQ breakdown*, and *Before-After TQM comparison*.
- **Numericals:** Always show three lines in benefit calculation — defect cost + time cost + waste cost — and conclude with a *qualitative* comment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Pro-Tip:** Examiners reward students who connect theory to *numbers*. In every 7-mark answer, include at least one quantitative illustration (ROQ, COPQ, or CSI) to stand out and earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Circles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality Circles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quality Circle (QC)** is a small group of workers, typically **6 to 12 members**, who perform similar or related work in an organization and meet regularly to identify, analyze, and solve work-related quality, productivity, safety, and operational problems within their own work area. The concept was formally introduced by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa** in **Japan in 1962**, and it forms the human-centric foundation of Japanese Total Quality Management (TQM) philosophy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A Quality Circle is a voluntary, self-managed group of employees (doing similar work) who meet regularly to solve problems affecting their workplace, using simple statistical and brainstorming tools, with active management support but no managerial authority.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Neighborhood Watch" Analogy:**
> Imagine a residential apartment complex. The residents know their building best — they know which lights flicker, which lift makes noise, where children are unsafe. So, instead of waiting for the distant city corporation to fix things, a small group of residents forms a "Neighborhood Watch" circle. They meet every week, discuss local issues, propose fixes, and submit small suggestions to the building committee. The committee (management) supports them with resources but does not command them.

**A Quality Circle is exactly this — but inside a factory or office.** Workers who *actually do the job* are the closest to the problem and therefore the best at solving it. Quality Circles institutionalize this grassroots problem-solving approach.

## 1.3 Core Attributes of a Quality Circle (KTU High-Yield)

| Attribute | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Size** | Typically **6 to 12 members** (often cited as **8 ± 2**) |
| **Membership** | Workers from the **same work area / department** |
| **Volunteerism** | Participation is **voluntary**, not forced |
| **Frequency** | Usually meets **once a week for about 1 hour** during paid time |
| **Leadership** | Elected **circle leader** from among members (rotational) |
| **Facilitation** | A **facilitator** (usually a supervisor) acts as a guide |
| **Methodology** | Uses **7 QC tools** (Ishikawa's Seven Tools) |
| **Authority** | Has **no managerial authority** — only recommends |
| **Continuity** | **Continuous activity**, not a one-time project |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** Quality Circles are *participative*, not *delegative*. Members retain their normal duties; QC work is *additional*. Management is supportive (provides time, training, resources) but does *not* control the circle's decisions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Historical Genesis and Evolution

1. **Origin (1962, Japan):** Dr. **Kaoru Ishikawa** at the University of Tokyo introduced the concept while advising the **Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)**.
2. **First Implementation (1962):** **Nippon Wireless and Telegraph Company** registered the first formal Quality Circle.
3. **Legal Backing (1963):** Japan's **"Worker Participation in Quality Control"** conference institutionalized QC under the **Japanese Productivity Centre**.
4. **Spread to India (1982):** **Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited (BHEL)** introduced QCs in India, followed by **Tata Steel** and **Indian Telephone Industries**.
5. **Global Adoption:** Today practiced in over **50+ countries**, including USA (under the name "Quality of Work Life" programs), UK, Korea, China, and India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always attribute Quality Circles to **Kaoru Ishikawa (1962, Japan)**. If the year of origin is asked, it is **1962** — not 1950 (Deming's lectures) or 1951 (Feigenbaum's COQ).

## 2.2 Objectives of Quality Circles (KTU Frequently Asked)

The objectives are classified into **two layers**:

### A. Primary (Hard) Objectives
- Improve **product and service quality**
- Reduce **defects, rework, and waste**
- Enhance **productivity and efficiency**
- Improve **workplace safety**
- Reduce **operational costs**

### B. Secondary (Soft / Human) Objectives
- Build **employee morale and job satisfaction**
- Develop a sense of **ownership and belongingness**
- Tap **hidden talent** of frontline workers
- Improve **supervisor-subordinate relationships**
- Create a **culture of continuous improvement (Kaizen)**

## 2.3 Structure / Hierarchy of Quality Circles

```
┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│   Steering Committee (Top Mgmt) │  ← Sets policy, approves budget
└────────────────┬────────────────┘
                 │
        ┌────────▼────────┐
        │   Facilitator   │  ← Trains & guides circles
        └────────┬────────┘
                 │
        ┌────────▼────────┐
        │  Circle Leader  │  ← Coordinates meetings
        └────────┬────────┘
                 │
    ┌────────────┼────────────┐
    ▼            ▼            ▼
[Member 1] [Member 2] ... [Member N]   ← 6 to 12 workers
```

## 2.4 The 7 QC Tools (Ishikawa's Seven Tools)

These are the analytical tools Quality Circles are trained to use:

| # | Tool | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone) | Identify root causes of a problem |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Collect data systematically at the source |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor process stability over time |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Show frequency distribution of data |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize problems using 80/20 rule |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Study correlation between two variables |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Map process steps to find bottlenecks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"Cute Chips Please Send Fresh fish"** → Control, Check, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart, Fishbone.

## 2.5 Benefits (Advantages) of Quality Circles

### For the Organization
- Higher **quality** and lower **cost of poor quality (COPQ)**
- Improved **productivity** and **on-time delivery**
- Reduced **absenteeism** and **labor turnover**
- Stronger **industrial relations**

### For the Employees
- Greater **job satisfaction** and **self-respect**
- Platform to **voice ideas** without fear
- Opportunity for **skill development**
- Sense of **participation** in management

### For the Supervisor
- **Easier communication** with workers
- Problems solved at **grassroots** level
- Less time spent on **firefighting**

## 2.6 Limitations / Pitfalls of Quality Circles

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** Examiners often ask "Why do QCs fail?" — the answer lies in these limitations.

- **Slow pace** of problem-solving (cannot match urgent business needs).
- **Limited scope** — circles only address problems *within their work area*.
- **Dependency on management** — if management ignores recommendations, morale collapses.
- **Quality Circles ≠ Quality Improvement** — small problems solved, but strategic issues remain with management.
- **Time-consuming** — eats into production hours.
- **May create labor unions' suspicion** that QCs replace collective bargaining.
- **Requires continuous training** — without it, circles lose direction.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quantitative Tools Used by QCs)

> [!NOTE]
> Although QCs are qualitative in spirit, the *7 tools* involve a few quantitative formulas. **No pipes used in tables below** to comply with markdown syntax.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Where Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Control charts, Six Sigma linkage |
| Pareto 80/20 Rule | Cumulative $\dfrac{\text{Frequency}_{i}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$ | Prioritization of defects |
| Mean | $\bar{x} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum x_{i}}{n}$ | Histogram construction |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}{n}}$ | Control chart limits |
| Control Chart UCL | $UCL \;=\; \bar{x} + 3\sigma$ | Control chart construction |
| Control Chart LCL | $LCL \;=\; \bar{x} - 3\sigma$ | Control chart construction |
| Cp (after centering) | $C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\bar{x}}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\bar{x}-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | One-sided capability |
| Defect Rate (DPMO base) | $\text{Defect \%} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units inspected}} \times 100$ | Quality reporting |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Problem-Solving Methodology of a Quality Circle

A Quality Circle follows a structured **PDCA-style problem-solving cycle**, often called the **"Quality Circle Storyboard"** (typically **8 steps** as standardized by JUSE).

## 3.1 The Standard Problem-Solving Procedure (Step-by-Step Derivation)

Let us walk through each step rigorously.

---

### **Step 1 — Topic Identification**

The circle identifies a **work-related problem** from observation, customer complaints, or supervisor suggestions.

**Example:** "Rework rate in the welding section has increased to 12% in the last quarter."

---

### **Step 2 — Problem Definition and Data Collection**

The problem is **clearly defined** in measurable terms. A **check sheet** is used to collect data.

$$\text{Defect Rate} \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of Defective Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}} \times 100$$

For example, in 1,000 welded joints inspected, 120 were defective:

$$\text{Defect Rate} \;=\; \frac{120}{1000} \times 100 \;=\; 12\%$$

---

### **Step 3 — Cause Analysis (Fishbone Diagram)**

The circle brainstorms **possible causes** and categorizes them using the **Ishikawa Fishbone** under standard 4M + E categories:

- **Man** (worker skill, training)
- **Machine** (equipment condition, calibration)
- **Material** (raw material quality)
- **Method** (procedure, work instructions)
- **Environment** (temperature, lighting, noise)

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to draw an Ishikawa diagram, label all five branches as **4M + E** and ensure at least 3–4 sub-causes per branch for full marks.

---

### **Step 4 — Root Cause Identification (5-Why Analysis)**

For each likely cause, the circle asks **"Why?"** repeatedly (typically 5 times) until the root cause is reached.

**Worked Example (5-Why):**

| Why # | Question | Answer |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Why is the rework rate 12%? | Because welds are cracking. |
| 2 | Why are welds cracking? | Because penetration is incomplete. |
| 3 | Why is penetration incomplete? | Because current setting is too low. |
| 4 | Why is current setting too low? | Because operators do not know the correct setting. |
| 5 | Why don't operators know the correct setting? | Because no standardized work instruction exists. |

**→ Root Cause:** Absence of a standardized welding procedure document.

---

### **Step 5 — Countermeasure Development**

The circle proposes **actionable countermeasures** for the root cause. For the example above:

| Countermeasure | Responsible | Timeline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Develop SOP for current setting | QC Leader | Week 1 |
| Train all 8 welders | Facilitator | Week 2 |
| Display setting chart at every station | Members | Week 2 |

---

### **Step 6 — Implementation**

The countermeasures are **piloted** and then **rolled out**. During implementation, **control charts** are used to verify stability.

The classical Shewhart control chart limits are derived as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Center Line (CL)} \;=\; \bar{x} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{i}}{n} \\[6pt]
\text{Upper Control Limit (UCL)} \;=\; \bar{x} + 3\sigma &= \bar{x} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}{n}} \\[6pt]
\text{Lower Control Limit (LCL)} \;=\; \bar{x} - 3\sigma &= \bar{x} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\sum(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}{n}}
\end{aligned}$$

Any data point outside $[LCL, \, UCL]$ indicates the process is **out of statistical control**.

---

### **Step 7 — Evaluation of Results**

Compare pre- and post-implementation defect rates.

Let pre-implementation defect rate be $R_{1}$ and post-implementation be $R_{2}$:

$$\text{Improvement \%} \;=\; \frac{R_{1} - R_{2}}{R_{1}} \times 100$$

Using our example: Pre = 12%, Post = 3%

$$\text{Improvement \%} \;=\; \frac{12 - 3}{12} \times 100 \;=\; 75\%$$

---

### **Step 8 — Standardization and Reporting**

Successful countermeasures are **standardized** through SOPs, training, and updated work instructions. The circle then **presents results** to management and submits a **Quality Circle Case Study Report**.

$$\text{ROI on QC Effort} \;=\; \frac{\text{Cost Savings from Improvement} - \text{Cost of QC Activities}}{\text{Cost of QC Activities}} \times 100$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix — When to Use Which QC Tool

| If the problem is about... | Then use... | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Identifying root causes | **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | Categorized cause list |
| Prioritizing many problems | **Pareto Chart** | Top 20% of causes = 80% of trouble |
| Checking data variation | **Histogram** | Distribution shape |
| Checking correlation | **Scatter Diagram** | Strength of relationship |
| Monitoring process over time | **Control Chart** | In-control / Out-of-control |
| Collecting raw data | **Check Sheet** | Frequency table |
| Mapping process flow | **Flowchart** | Process map |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quality Circle Workflow (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Problem Identification by Members] --> B[Data Collection via Check Sheet]
    B --> C[Cause Analysis using Fishbone Diagram]
    C --> D[Root Cause via 5-Why Analysis]
    D --> E[Countermeasure Development]
    E --> F[Implementation in Work Area]
    F --> G[Verification via Control Chart]
    G --> H{Result Improved?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Standardization and SOP Update]
    H -- No --> C
    I --> J[Presentation to Steering Committee]
    J --> K[Recognition and Next Problem]
```

## 4.2 Quality Circle Organizational Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Steering Committee - Top Management] --> B[QC Coordinator]
    B --> C[Facilitator - Usually Supervisor]
    C --> D1[Circle Leader]
    C --> D2[Circle Leader]
    C --> D3[Circle Leader]
    D1 --> E1[Members 6 to 12]
    D2 --> E2[Members 6 to 12]
    D3 --> E3[Members 6 to 12]
```

## 4.3 The PDCA Cycle as Applied to Quality Circles

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan - Identify Problem and Countermeasure] --> D[Do - Implement on Small Scale]
    D --> C[Check - Compare Result vs Target using Control Chart]
    C --> A[Act - Standardize if Successful or Revise Plan]
    A --> P
```

## 4.4 Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram — Functional Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Problem Statement: High Rework in Welding] --> M1[Man: Operator Skill Gap]
    P --> M2[Machine: Current Settings Inconsistent]
    P --> M3[Material: Electrode Quality Variation]
    P --> M4[Method: No Standard SOP]
    P --> M5[Environment: Poor Lighting at Station 3]
    M4 --> R[Root Cause: Absence of Documented Procedure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above render the **functional architecture and flow logic** of Quality Circle operations. In the KTU exam, students may replace the Mermaid block with a hand-drawn Fishbone / Pareto chart sketch for full credit, ensuring all branches and axes are properly labeled.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Quality Circle. Mention any two characteristics.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Quality Circle** is a small group of **6 to 12 workers** doing similar work, who meet voluntarily on a regular basis to identify, analyze, and solve quality and productivity problems in their work area, using simple statistical and brainstorming tools.
>
> **Two characteristics:**
> 1. **Voluntary participation** — members join out of their own will, not by force.
> 2. **Self-development** — members develop problem-solving, leadership, and presentation skills through QC activities.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 1 each for two characteristics.)*

---

### **Q2. Name the 7 QC tools developed by Ishikawa.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The seven quality control tools (Ishikawa's Seven Tools) are:
1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone)
2. **Check Sheet**
3. **Control Chart**
4. **Histogram**
5. **Pareto Chart**
6. **Scatter Diagram**
7. **Flowchart**

*(Marks: 0.5 per tool, total 3.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either (A) or (B)** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **origin and evolution of Quality Circles**. Discuss their **structure and hierarchy**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Quality Circles and Quality Control Teams**, listing **any six points of distinction**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(a) Origin, Evolution, Structure (7 Marks)

**Origin:**
Quality Circles were introduced in **1962** by **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo, in collaboration with the **Japanese Union of Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)**. The first circle was registered at **Nippon Wireless and Telegraph Company** in 1962. The model drew inspiration from earlier American **"Statistical Quality Control"** (Deming, Juran) lectures held in Japan in 1950.

**Evolution:**
- **1962:** Concept introduced in Japan.
- **1963:** JUSE institutionalized it through the **"Worker Participation in QC"** conference.
- **1970s:** Rapid spread across Japanese industries (Toyota, Nissan, Toshiba).
- **1980s:** Expansion to Korea, Taiwan, India.
- **1982:** **BHEL (Bhopal)** introduced QCs in India, followed by **Tata Steel** and **Indian Telephone Industries**.
- **Today:** Practiced in 50+ countries across manufacturing and services.

**Structure / Hierarchy:**
The standard structure consists of **four levels**:

1. **Steering Committee** — Top management; approves budgets and policies.
2. **Coordinator / Facilitator** — Usually a middle manager or HR executive; trains circles.
3. **Circle Leader** — Elected from members; conducts meetings.
4. **Members** — 6 to 12 volunteer workers.

*(Marks: 2 for origin, 2 for evolution, 3 for structure.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A(b) QC vs. QCT (7 Marks)

| # | Quality Circle (QC) | Quality Control Team (QCT) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Voluntary participation. | Appointed / nominated by management. |
| 2 | Members belong to the **same work area**. | Members drawn from **different departments / cross-functional**. |
| 3 | Focus on **human development** and morale alongside quality. | Focus is **purely on quality / problem-solving**. |
| 4 | Continuous, long-term activity. | **Temporary** — dissolved once problem is solved. |
| 5 | Uses **simple 7 QC tools**. | May use **advanced statistical tools** (DOE, SPC, Six Sigma). |
| 6 | Recommendations are **advisory** to management. | QCT decisions are often **binding** within its scope. |
| 7 | Small size (6–12). | Variable size, sometimes larger. |

*(Marks: 0.5 per correct distinction × 6 = 3 marks for table; 2 marks for correct conclusion; 2 marks for presentation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **objectives of Quality Circles** in detail, covering both **hard and soft objectives**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step problem-solving methodology** followed by a Quality Circle, with an example. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(a) Objectives (7 Marks)

The objectives of Quality Circles are classified into **two categories**:

**1. Hard (Tangible) Objectives — 3 Marks**

These directly impact organizational performance:
- **Quality improvement** — reduce defects, scrap, and rework.
- **Productivity enhancement** — optimize workflows and reduce downtime.
- **Cost reduction** — lower cost of poor quality (COPQ).
- **Safety improvement** — reduce workplace accidents and hazards.
- **Waste elimination** — through structured problem-solving.

**2. Soft (Intangible / Human) Objectives — 4 Marks**

These focus on people development:
- **Employee participation** — workers feel valued and involved.
- **Job enrichment** — work becomes more meaningful.
- **Skill development** — analytical, statistical, and communication skills.
- **Improved morale and motivation** — through recognition.
- **Stronger industrial relations** — better supervisor-worker rapport.
- **Culture of Kaizen (continuous improvement)** — embedded at grassroots.

> The interplay of hard and soft objectives is what makes QCs sustainable — economic benefits fund the program, while human benefits ensure its longevity.

*(Marks: 3 for hard, 3 for soft, 1 for interplay / conclusion.)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B(b) Problem-Solving Methodology (7 Marks)

Quality Circles follow an **8-step standardized problem-solving procedure** (JUSE Storyboard):

**Step 1 — Topic Selection (1 mark):**
Members identify a recurring problem in their area, e.g., *"Rejection rate in CNC machining has increased from 2% to 8%."*

**Step 2 — Problem Definition & Data Collection (1 mark):**
The problem is quantified using check sheets. Defect rate is calculated as:

$$\text{Defect \%} \;=\; \frac{\text{Defective Units}}{\text{Total Produced}} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Cause Analysis (1 mark):**
A Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram is drawn under 4M + E categories — *Man, Machine, Material, Method, Environment*.

**Step 4 — Root Cause via 5-Why Analysis (1 mark):**
"Why is rejection high? → Surface finish rough → Tool worn out → No replacement schedule → No maintenance SOP."
**Root cause:** Absence of preventive maintenance schedule.

**Step 5 — Countermeasure Development (1 mark):**
- Introduce monthly tool-change schedule.
- Train operators on tool-condition monitoring.
- Display maintenance calendar at each machine.

**Step 6 — Implementation & Monitoring (1 mark):**
Countermeasures are rolled out; control charts monitor rejection rate.

**Step 7 — Evaluation (1 mark):**
Post-implementation rejection = 1.5%.
Improvement = $\frac{8 - 1.5}{8} \times 100 = 81.25\%$

**Step 8 — Standardization (1 mark):**
The new SOP is documented and circulated to all shifts.

*(Marks: distributed across steps and the numerical example.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Quality Circles with Six Sigma / TQM teams** — QCs are *voluntary, small, homogeneous, and continuous*. Mentioning TQM in a QC answer often costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Always attribute Quality Circles to Ishikawa (1962, Japan)** — wrong attribution = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Do not write "Kaizen" and "Quality Circle" as synonyms** — Kaizen is the *philosophy*; QCs are *one mechanism* to implement Kaizen.
> 4. In numerical problems, students often forget to **show units and the ×100** in percentage calculations — losing 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 5. Avoid generic answers like *"QC improves quality"* without explaining *how* (which tool, which step, which formula) — examiners award marks for **specificity**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Quality Circle is a voluntary group of **6–12 workers** from the same work area, meeting regularly to solve work-related problems using **7 QC tools**.
- **Founder:** **Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa, 1962, Japan.**
- **First circle:** **Nippon Wireless and Telegraph Company, 1962.**
- **First in India:** **BHEL, Bhopal, 1982.**
- **Hierarchy:** Steering Committee → Coordinator → Facilitator → Circle Leader → Members.
- **7 QC Tools:** Fishbone, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.
- **Nature:** *Voluntary*, *participative*, *advisory*, *continuous*.
- **Hard objectives:** Quality, productivity, cost, safety, waste reduction.
- **Soft objectives:** Morale, motivation, skill development, participation, Kaizen culture.
- **Key limitation:** Depends entirely on management support; without it, QCs fail.
- **PDCA cycle** is the underlying methodology.
- **Quality Circles ≠ Quality Control Teams** — QCs are voluntary and continuous; QCTs are nominated and temporary.
- **Kaizen (continuous improvement)** is the philosophy; **QC** is one of its tools.
- **Standard problem-solving steps:** Identify → Define → Cause Analysis → Root Cause → Countermeasure → Implement → Verify → Standardize.
- **5-Why Analysis** is the most common root-cause technique used inside a QC.
- **For numericals:** remember defect %, control limits $(\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma)$, Pareto %, and improvement % formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cost of Quality (COQ)**, often termed *Quality Cost* or *Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)*, is the total sum of expenditures incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to specified quality standards, **plus** the costs arising from failures to meet those standards. It is a **financial quantification** of quality performance and serves as a strategic management tool for translating quality-related activities into monetary terms visible to the boardroom.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, COQ is defined under the **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) Model** and is considered a *critical financial metric* for evaluating the effectiveness of Total Quality Management (TQM) initiatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEIET631 - Module 1):**
> Cost of Quality is the *first major topic* of Module 1, forming the financial foundation upon which Six Sigma, DMAIC, and continuous improvement are evaluated. Examiners frequently test the **classification, calculation, and strategic interpretation** of COQ.

### 1.2 The Intuitive "Ice Cream Shop" Analogy

Imagine you own a small ice cream shop. To deliver *good quality*, you spend money on:
- **Prevention**: Training staff on hygiene, buying better ingredients, designing a clean kitchen layout.
- **Appraisal**: Random taste-tests by the manager, weighing each scoop to ensure 100 g, using a thermometer to check freezer temperature.

Now, when quality *fails*:
- **Internal Failure**: A customer complains *inside* the shop about a melted scoop → you remake it (rework) or throw it away (scrap).
- **External Failure**: A customer leaves a bad Google review *after* going home → you lose future sales, offer refunds, and your reputation dips (opportunity cost).

**Total Cost of Quality = Money spent to get it right + Money lost when you got it wrong.** As you invest more in prevention, your failure costs collapse — and at some magic point, your *total* bill is minimized. That sweet spot is what every Quality Manager chases.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in COQ

> [!NOTE]
> **Key COQ Metrics to Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Total Quality Cost (TQC)** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure
> - **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** = Prevention + Appraisal
> - **Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo)** = Internal Failure + External Failure
> - **COPQ** = CNCo (Industry shorthand)
> - **Quality Cost Ratio** = CoC / CNCo (a strategic balance indicator)

### 1.4 Why COQ Matters in Engineering Practice

COQ bridges the language gap between **Quality Engineers** (who speak in ppm, sigma levels, DPMO) and **Finance/CFO** (who speak in currency units). It transforms an abstract "defect" into a tangible rupee/dollar figure, enabling evidence-based decisions on whether to invest in a Six Sigma Black Belt project, an automated inspection line, or a vendor qualification program.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The PAF Model — The Four Pillars of COQ

The classical **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure Model** (popularized by Juran and Feigenbaum) categorizes every quality-related rupee into exactly four buckets:

#### Pillar 1 — Prevention Costs
Costs incurred to **prevent defects from occurring** in the first place. They are the *most strategic* investment in the COQ hierarchy.

- Quality planning and process design
- Employee training and certification
- Supplier capability audits
- Quality improvement projects (Kaizen, Six Sigma)
- Process validation and qualification
- Design of Experiments (DoE)
- Preventive equipment maintenance

#### Pillar 2 — Appraisal Costs
Costs incurred to **detect defects** that have already occurred, *before* the product reaches the customer.

- Incoming material inspection
- In-process inspection and testing
- Final product acceptance testing
- Calibration of measuring instruments
- Quality audits (system, process, product)
- Inspection labour and testing equipment depreciation

#### Pillar 3 — Internal Failure Costs
Costs arising from defects **detected before delivery** to the customer. They are *visible* to the organization.

- Scrap and waste disposal
- Rework and repair labour
- Re-inspection of reworked items
- Material wastage
- Downtime caused by quality issues
- Design changes to fix internal failures

#### Pillar 4 — External Failure Costs
The **most damaging** category — costs from defects discovered *after* delivery, when the product is already in the customer's hands.

- Warranty claims and repair costs
- Product recalls
- Customer returns and replacements
- Field service and support
- Legal liability and litigation
- Loss of brand reputation (hard to quantify but real)
- Lost future sales (opportunity cost)

### 2.2 Why the Pillar Order Matters — The "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Strategic Insight (high-yield for KTU):**
> Prevention and Appraisal are **costs of getting it right**. Internal and External Failure are **costs of getting it wrong**. A financially healthy organization shifts its COQ composition from being *failure-dominated* to being *prevention-dominated*. Mature TQM firms target a Prevention+Appraisal share of **60–80%** of total COQ, with Failure costs collapsing to <20%.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Typical Unit | Engineering Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|--------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Total Quality Cost (TQC)** | $TQC = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | ₹ / $ | Total quality budget |
| 2 | **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** | $CoC = C_P + C_A$ | ₹ / $ | Spending on doing it right |
| 3 | **Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo)** | $CNCo = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | ₹ / $ | Spending on failure |
| 4 | **COPQ** | $COPQ = CNCo$ | ₹ / $ | Industry shorthand for failure cost |
| 5 | **Prevention Ratio** | $R_P = \dfrac{C_P}{TQC} \times 100$ | % | Maturity indicator |
| 6 | **Failure Ratio** | $R_F = \dfrac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{TQC} \times 100$ | % | Risk exposure indicator |
| 7 | **Conformance/Non-Conformance Ratio** | $K = \dfrac{CoC}{CNCo}$ | Dimensionless | Strategic balance |
| 8 | **Quality Cost per Unit** | $QCPU = \dfrac{TQC}{N_{units}}$ | ₹/unit | Bench-marking across products |
| 9 | **Quality Cost as % of Sales** | $QCS = \dfrac{TQC}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100$ | % | Industry comparability |
| 10 | **Hypothesis Ratio (Crosby)** | $H_R = \dfrac{\text{Price of Conformance}}{\text{Price of Non-Conformance}}$ | Dimensionless | Used in Crosby's model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used in the table:**
> - $C_P$ = Prevention Cost
> - $C_A$ = Appraisal Cost
> - $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost
> - $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost
> - $N_{units}$ = Number of units produced in the period

### 2.4 Comparison of Quality Gurus' COQ Models

| Feature | Juran's Model | Crosby's Model | Feigenbaum's PAF |
|---------|---------------|----------------|------------------|
| Primary focus | Fitness for use | Conformance to requirements | Total quality cost |
| Categories | Prevention + Appraisal vs. Internal + External Failure | Price of Conformance vs. Price of Non-Conformance | 4 PAF categories |
| Strategic emphasis | Internal + External Failure must be reduced | Zero defects by prevention | P-model is the highest leverage |
| Optimal COQ point | Minimum total cost | Zero defects is the goal | Continuous improvement |
| Used for KTU question style | Yes (classic) | Yes (definition-based) | Yes (most common) |

### 2.5 The Optimal COQ Curve (Conceptual)

If we plot *Total Quality Cost vs. Level of Quality*:

$$\text{TQC}(Q) = C_P(Q) + C_A(Q) + C_{IF}(Q) + C_{EF}(Q)$$

- As $Q$ (quality level) **increases**, $C_P$ and $C_A$ **rise sharply**.
- As $Q$ increases, $C_{IF}$ and $C_{EF}$ **drop steeply**.
- The **minimum of TQC** defines the *economic optimum* quality level.
- Beyond this optimum, spending more on prevention *wastes money*.
- Below this optimum, *quality failures* bleed the firm dry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This principle drives the decision to invest in a Six Sigma project. The project should be sanctioned only if the expected savings (reduction in $CNCo$) exceed the cost of the project (additional $C_P + C_A$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — Part (a): COQ Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A precision components manufacturing firm operating in Kerala has compiled the following quality-related expenditures for the financial year 2023–24:

| Cost Element | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
|--------------|------------------|
| Quality planning and process design | 4.0 |
| Vendor capability audits | 2.0 |
| Incoming inspection labour | 3.5 |
| Final product acceptance testing | 2.5 |
| Scrap from in-process defects | 6.0 |
| Rework labour and re-inspection | 7.5 |
| Warranty claims from customers | 12.0 |
| Field service for product failures | 5.5 |
| Employee quality training | 1.5 |
| Calibration of measuring instruments | 2.0 |

The company produced **$N = 2{,}00{,}000$ units** and earned **Revenue = ₹ 5,000 Lakhs**.

**Required:** Compute (i) Total Quality Cost (TQC), (ii) Cost of Conformance (CoC), (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo), (iv) COPQ, (v) Quality Cost per Unit, (vi) Quality Cost as % of Sales, (vii) Conformance-to-Non-Conformance Ratio, (viii) Prevention Ratio, (ix) Failure Ratio.

#### Step 1: Classify each item into the PAF category

**Prevention Costs ($C_P$):**
- Quality planning and process design = 4.0
- Vendor capability audits = 2.0
- Employee quality training = 1.5

**Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):**
- Incoming inspection labour = 3.5
- Final product acceptance testing = 2.5
- Calibration of measuring instruments = 2.0

**Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):**
- Scrap from in-process defects = 6.0
- Rework labour and re-inspection = 7.5

**External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):**
- Warranty claims from customers = 12.0
- Field service for product failures = 5.5

#### Step 2: Aggregate each PAF category

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 4.0 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_A &= 3.5 + 2.5 + 2.0 = 8.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{IF} &= 6.0 + 7.5 = 13.5 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{EF} &= 12.0 + 5.5 = 17.5 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual justification:** Each PAF bucket is the *sum* of all items belonging to that pillar of the COQ model. This is a simple aggregation — no algebraic transformation is needed, but the classification logic is critical for examiner marks.

#### Step 3: Compute Total Quality Cost (TQC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TQC &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 7.5 + 8.0 + 13.5 + 17.5 \\
&= 46.5 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute Cost of Conformance (CoC)

$$
\begin{aligned}
CoC &= C_P + C_A \\
&= 7.5 + 8.0 \\
&= 15.5 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual justification:** CoC is the *combined expenditure* to ensure the product conforms — it includes both prevention (stopping defects) and appraisal (catching defects before they leave).

#### Step 5: Compute Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo) / COPQ

$$
\begin{aligned}
CNCo = COPQ &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
&= 13.5 + 17.5 \\
&= 31.0 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Quality Cost per Unit (QCPU)

$$
\begin{aligned}
QCPU &= \frac{TQC}{N_{units}} = \frac{46.5 \text{ Lakhs}}{2{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{46.5 \times 1{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} \text{ ₹/unit} \\
&= \frac{23.25 \text{ ₹/unit}}{}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Quality Cost as % of Sales (QCS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
QCS &= \frac{TQC}{\text{Revenue}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{46.5}{5000} \times 100 \\
&= 0.93\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹100 of sales, ₹0.93 is spent on quality — well within the world-class benchmark of 2.5% suggested by Juran, indicating the firm is in a *reasonable range* but has room for improvement.

#### Step 8: Conformance-to-Non-Conformance Ratio (K)

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &= \frac{CoC}{CNCo} = \frac{15.5}{31.0} = 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $K < 1$ means the firm is spending **less on prevention/appraisal** than it is **losing to failures**. A target of $K \geq 2$ is recommended by TQM literature for mature quality systems.

#### Step 9: Prevention Ratio and Failure Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_P &= \frac{C_P}{TQC} \times 100 = \frac{7.5}{46.5} \times 100 \approx 16.13\% \\
R_F &= \frac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{TQC} \times 100 = \frac{31.0}{46.5} \times 100 \approx 66.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** 66.67% of quality spend is on *failure*, while only 16.13% is on *prevention*. This is a **classic "firefighting" pattern** — the firm is treating symptoms, not root causes. A Six Sigma DMAIC project would shift this distribution toward prevention.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Part (b): Sensitivity Analysis (Optional High-Yield)

**Scenario:** The firm invests an additional **₹ 5.0 Lakhs in prevention training and process design**, which is expected to:
- Reduce internal failure cost by 40%
- Reduce external failure cost by 25%
- Increase appraisal cost by ₹ 1.0 Lakh (more robust validation)

**New $C_P$, $C_A$, $C_{IF}$, $C_{EF}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P^{new} &= 7.5 + 5.0 = 12.5 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_A^{new} &= 8.0 + 1.0 = 9.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{IF}^{new} &= 13.5 \times (1 - 0.40) = 13.5 \times 0.60 = 8.10 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{EF}^{new} &= 17.5 \times (1 - 0.25) = 17.5 \times 0.75 = 13.125 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**New Total Quality Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TQC^{new} &= 12.5 + 9.0 + 8.10 + 13.125 = 42.725 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta TQC &= TQC - TQC^{new} = 46.5 - 42.725 = 3.775 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**New $K$ ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K^{new} = \frac{C_P^{new} + C_A^{new}}{C_{IF}^{new} + C_{EF}^{new}} = \frac{12.5 + 9.0}{8.10 + 13.125} = \frac{21.5}{21.225} \approx 1.013
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strategic Verdict:** The investment of ₹ 5.0 Lakhs in prevention *saves* ₹ 3.775 Lakhs net — but more importantly, the **K ratio jumps from 0.50 to 1.013**, indicating a healthier balance. The firm has moved from a *firefighting* state to a *proactive* state. This is the kind of analysis a Six Sigma Black Belt presents to the management board.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Strategic Posture | $K$ Ratio | Failure Share | Action Recommended |
|--------------------|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Reactive (firefighting)** | $K < 1$ | >60% | Urgent Six Sigma DMAIC deployment |
| **Transitional** | $1 \leq K < 2$ | 30–60% | Increase prevention training, vendor audits |
| **Proactive (TQM mature)** | $K \geq 2$ | <20% | Maintain, focus on innovation and Six Sigma optimization |
| **Over-engineering** | $K > 4$ | <5% | Re-evaluate prevention spend for waste |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU ESE answers:** Always end a COQ problem with a *one-line strategic recommendation* based on the K-ratio you computed. This is a guaranteed **2–3 mark differentiator** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: COQ Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Total Quality Cost TQC"]
    nodeB["Cost of Conformance CoC"]
    nodeC["Cost of Non Conformance CNCo"]
    nodeD["Prevention Costs CP"]
    nodeE["Appraisal Costs CA"]
    nodeF["Internal Failure Costs CIF"]
    nodeG["External Failure Costs CEF"]
    nodeH["Quality Planning and Design"]
    nodeI["Training and Education"]
    nodeJ["Supplier Audits"]
    nodeK["Incoming Inspection"]
    nodeL["In Process Testing"]
    nodeM["Final QA Testing"]
    nodeN["Scrap and Rework"]
    nodeO["Re inspection"]
    nodeP["Warranty Claims"]
    nodeQ["Product Recalls"]
    nodeR["Lost Reputation"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeD --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
    nodeE --> nodeK
    nodeE --> nodeL
    nodeE --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeF --> nodeO
    nodeG --> nodeP
    nodeG --> nodeQ
    nodeG --> nodeR
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: How COQ Shifts as Quality Matures

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateA["Stage 1: REACTIVE Firefighting"]
    stateB["Stage 2: AWARENESS Inspection heavy"]
    stateC["Stage 3: TRANSITIONAL Prevention growing"]
    stateD["Stage 4: PROACTIVE TQM mature"]
    stateE["Stage 5: WORLD CLASS Six Sigma"]

    stateA -- "More appraisal and inspection" --> stateB
    stateB -- "Strategic prevention investment" --> stateC
    stateC -- "Continuous improvement and DMAIC" --> stateD
    stateD -- "Optimization and innovation" --> stateE

    stateA:::reactive
    stateB:::aware
    stateC:::transition
    stateD:::proactive
    stateE:::worldclass
```

### 4.3 The Optimal COQ Curve — Schematic Block Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    axisX["Quality Level Q increasing from low to high"]
    curveP["Prevention + Appraisal Cost RISES"]
    curveF["Failure Cost FALLS sharply"]
    curveT["Total Quality Cost TQC = U shaped curve with minimum"]
    opt["OPTIMAL Q point at minimum TQC"]

    axisX --> curveP
    axisX --> curveF
    curveP --> curveT
    curveF --> curveT
    curveT --> opt
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: How a Company Implements a COQ System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Identify all quality related expenditures"]
    step2["Step 2: Classify each into P A I E categories"]
    step3["Step 3: Aggregate into CP CA CIF CEF"]
    step4["Step 4: Compute TQC CoC CNCo and K ratio"]
    step5["Step 5: Benchmark against industry standards"]
    step6["Step 6: Identify high cost zones and root causes"]
    step7["Step 7: Launch Six Sigma DMAIC project for high CNCo items"]
    step8["Step 8: Monitor quarterly and feed back to step 1"]

    step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> step8
    step8 -.-> step1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram note for students:** The feedback loop from Step 8 back to Step 1 is the *continuous improvement cycle* — the same Deming PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) philosophy applied specifically to quality cost management.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Cost of Quality. List its four major categories.**

**Model Answer:**
Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure that its products and services conform to specified quality requirements, plus the costs arising from failure to meet those requirements.

The four major categories under the PAF model are:
1. **Prevention Costs** – costs to prevent defects (e.g., training, quality planning).
2. **Appraisal Costs** – costs to detect defects (e.g., inspection, testing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs** – costs of defects found before delivery (e.g., scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs** – costs of defects found after delivery (e.g., warranty, returns).

*(Expected length: 5–6 lines; full marks for correct list and one-line definition.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023] Differentiate between Cost of Conformance (CoC) and Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Cost of Conformance (CoC) | Cost of Non-Conformance (CNCo) |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Meaning | Money spent to *meet* quality standards | Money lost due to *failing* quality standards |
| Components | Prevention + Appraisal | Internal Failure + External Failure |
| Nature | Proactive investment | Reactive loss |
| Strategic signal | Higher CoC = mature TQM | Lower CNCo = better quality |
| Industry term | Often called "Price of Conformance" (Crosby) | Also known as COPQ |

*(3-mark answer: 2-line definition + comparison table.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module 1 Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – [KTU University Exam Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) Model** of Cost of Quality. With a suitable diagram, describe each category and give **two examples** for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A software services company has the following quality costs for one quarter (in ₹ Lakhs):

- Process definition and documentation = 2.5
- Customer requirements analysis = 1.5
- Code review and testing = 4.0
- QA team salaries = 6.0
- Defect fixing during development = 5.0
- Defect fixing after release = 8.0
- Customer support for reported bugs = 7.0
- Project management overhead for quality = 2.0
- Process audits = 1.0

Compute the **Total Quality Cost, Cost of Conformance, Cost of Non-Conformance, and the K-ratio**. Comment on the company's quality posture. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Solution to (a) — PAF Model Explanation

The **Prevention–Appraisal–Failure (PAF) Model**, also called the **Feigenbaum–Juran Model**, classifies all quality-related costs into four mutually exclusive categories:

**1. Prevention Costs ($C_P$):** Money spent to *stop defects from happening*.
- *Examples:* (i) Quality training programs, (ii) Vendor capability audits, (iii) Process design reviews.

**2. Appraisal Costs ($C_A$):** Money spent to *find defects before they reach customers*.
- *Examples:* (i) Incoming material inspection, (ii) In-process testing, (iii) Final acceptance sampling.

**3. Internal Failure Costs ($C_{IF}$):** Money lost on defects *caught inside the company*.
- *Examples:* (i) Scrap and waste, (ii) Rework labour, (iii) Re-inspection after rework.

**4. External Failure Costs ($C_{EF}$):** Money lost on defects *caught by the customer* — the most expensive category.
- *Examples:* (i) Warranty claims, (ii) Product recalls, (iii) Reputational loss and lost future sales.

The strategic principle: **Prevention + Appraisal increase with quality maturity, while Failure costs collapse**, yielding a U-shaped TQC curve with a unique economic optimum.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical COQ Computation

**Step 1: Classify each item into PAF categories**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= \text{Process definition} + \text{Customer req. analysis} + \text{Process audits} + \text{PM overhead for QA} \\
&= 2.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 2.0 = 7.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_A &= \text{Code review/testing} + \text{QA team salaries} \\
&= 4.0 + 6.0 = 10.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_{IF} &= \text{Defect fixing during development} = 5.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
C_{EF} &= \text{Defect fixing after release} + \text{Customer support} \\
&= 8.0 + 7.0 = 15.0 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the key COQ metrics**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TQC &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 7.0 + 10.0 + 5.0 + 15.0 = 37.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
CoC &= C_P + C_A = 7.0 + 10.0 = 17.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
CNCo &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 5.0 + 15.0 = 20.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\[4pt]
K &= \frac{CoC}{CNCo} = \frac{17.0}{20.0} = 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Strategic Commentary**

- $K = 0.85 < 1$ → The company is **reactive/firefighting**.
- Failure share = $\frac{20}{37} \times 100 \approx 54\%$ of TQC, dominated by external failures.
- Recommendation: Launch a **Six Sigma DMAIC project** to attack the high post-release defect fixing cost; increase prevention spend on better requirements analysis and automated testing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Internal Choice**

**(a)** Compare the **COQ models of Juran, Crosby, and Feigenbaum**. Which model is most widely used in industry, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm has an annual TQC of ₹ 80 Lakhs, with the following split: $C_P = 20\%$, $C_A = 30\%$, $C_{IF} = 25\%$, $C_{EF} = 25\%$. The CEO plans a new initiative costing ₹ 6 Lakhs annually in additional prevention and appraisal, expected to reduce internal failures by 60% and external failures by 30%.

Calculate (i) the new TQC, (ii) the new K-ratio, and (iii) recommend whether the initiative should be approved. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Solution to (a) — Comparison of Quality Gurus' COQ Models

| Feature | Juran | Crosby | Feigenbaum |
|---------|-------|--------|------------|
| **Title of Book/Concept** | Quality Control Handbook (Juran) | Quality is Free (Crosby) | Total Quality Control (Feigenbaum) |
| **Categories** | Internal + External Failure vs. Prevention + Appraisal | Price of Conformance vs. Price of Non-Conformance | Four-category PAF Model |
| **Strategic Emphasis** | Optimize COQ at the lowest economic point | Aim for Zero Defects via prevention | P-costs offer the highest leverage |
| **Tools** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) | 14 Steps, Maturity Grid | Hidden Plant concept |
| **Industry Adoption** | High (manufacturing, Six Sigma) | High (services, software) | Highest (universal PAF) |

**Most widely used:** The **Feigenbaum PAF model** dominates in industry because it provides *operationally distinct, mutually exclusive, and collectively exhaustive* cost buckets that can be directly mapped onto accounting line-items. Juran's lens adds strategic insight; Crosby's lens adds cultural motivation.

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Initiative Evaluation

**Step 1: Compute current TQC split in ₹**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 0.20 \times 80 = 16.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_A &= 0.30 \times 80 = 24.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{IF} &= 0.25 \times 80 = 20.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{EF} &= 0.25 \times 80 = 20.0 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute current K-ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{old} = \frac{C_P + C_A}{C_{IF} + C_{EF}} = \frac{16 + 24}{20 + 20} = \frac{40}{40} = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply initiative changes**

The ₹ 6 Lakhs extra is split as additional prevention/appraisal (assume 50:50 split):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P^{new} &= 16.0 + 3.0 = 19.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_A^{new} &= 24.0 + 3.0 = 27.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{IF}^{new} &= 20.0 \times (1 - 0.60) = 20.0 \times 0.40 = 8.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
C_{EF}^{new} &= 20.0 \times (1 - 0.30) = 20.0 \times 0.70 = 14.0 \text{ Lakhs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute new TQC and K-ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TQC^{new} &= 19.0 + 27.0 + 8.0 + 14.0 = 68.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
\Delta TQC &= 80.0 - 68.0 = 12.0 \text{ Lakhs saved net} \\[4pt]
CoC^{new} &= 19.0 + 27.0 = 46.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
CNCo^{new} &= 8.0 + 14.0 = 22.0 \text{ Lakhs} \\
K_{new} &= \frac{46.0}{22.0} \approx 2.09
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Recommendation**

The initiative is **strongly recommended**:
- **Net savings** = ₹ 12 Lakhs annually (huge ROI on ₹ 6 Lakh spend).
- **K-ratio improves** from 1.00 to 2.09 — moving the firm from "transitional" to "proactive" TQM maturity.
- **External failure drops** from 25% to 17.5% of TQC, drastically reducing reputational risk.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on COQ Questions:**
> 1. **Misclassifying items:** Students often confuse "scrap" (Internal Failure) with "inspection" (Appraisal). *Tip: ask yourself — does this prevent defects, detect defects, or fix defects caught before vs. after delivery?*
> 2. **Forgetting units in KTU exam sheets:** Always write **₹ Lakhs** or **₹ Crores** explicitly. Unspecified units = 0.5–1 mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring the strategic commentary:** The K-ratio must be *interpreted*, not just *computed*. Examiners look for the sentence "K = 1.2 implies transitional posture; recommendation: increase prevention spend" — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 4. **Adding Prevention+Appraisal incorrectly:** Many students sum them all together. Always show $TQC = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ in a single equation for clarity.
> 5. **Skipping the example list:** A 7-mark PAF explanation *requires at least two examples per category*. Skipping examples forfeits 2–3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cost of Quality:**
> - **COQ** = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ (PAF Model by Feigenbaum/Juran).
> - **Prevention + Appraisal** = CoC (cost of doing it right).
> - **Internal + External Failure** = CNCo = COPQ (cost of failure).
> - **K-ratio** = CoC / CNCo. Target: $K \geq 2$ for TQM maturity.
> - Failure share >60% → Reactive firm → needs urgent DMAIC/Six Sigma.
> - Prevention share >40% → Mature TQM → world-class performance.
> - The **optimal COQ point** is the U-minimum of TQC vs. quality level.
> - **Juran** = Internal/External Failure framework; **Crosby** = Price of Conformance/Non-Conformance; **Feigenbaum** = PAF (most used).
> - **Opportunity cost** (lost sales, lost reputation) is sometimes added as a 5th category.
> - Always include **strategic commentary** after numerical COQ questions in KTU answers.
> - **External Failure is the most expensive** category — a single post-delivery defect can cost **10–100×** more than catching it pre-delivery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Taguchi Quality Loss Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Taguchi Quality Loss Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

The **Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF)** is a quadratic mathematical model developed by the Japanese engineer and statistician **Dr. Genichi Taguchi** that quantifies the economic and societal loss incurred when a product's quality characteristic deviates from its **target value**. Unlike the traditional Western "conformance-based" definition of quality (which accepts any value lying inside the specification limits as "good"), Taguchi's philosophy asserts that **every deviation from the target generates a loss**, and this loss increases quadratically as the deviation grows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"The Quality Loss Function is a quadratic equation, L(x) = k(x − m)², which expresses the financial loss to society resulting from the deviation of a quality characteristic (x) from its target value (m). It transforms the abstract notion of 'quality' into a measurable monetary loss visible to both manufacturer and customer."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Carrot on the Plate" Story

Imagine a chef in a 5-star restaurant. The *target* on the plate is a small painted circle. The chef must place a perfectly cooked piece of salmon **exactly on the circle**.

* **Traditional View:** As long as the salmon lies *anywhere* on the plate (within tolerance), the customer is happy.
* **Taguchi's View:** Even if the salmon is 1 cm off-center, the customer feels a *tiny* drop in delight, eats slightly less, may not return, may leave a 4-star review instead of 5-star. **The restaurant loses revenue, reputation, and future business.** The further from the circle, the greater the loss — and it grows quadratically, not linearly!

### Real Engineering Analogy: Voltage Output of a Battery
A battery is rated **1.50 V (target, m)** with tolerance **±0.05 V (Δ₀)**.

| Scenario | Voltage (x) | Traditional View | Taguchi View |
|----------|-------------|------------------|--------------|
| Perfect | 1.500 V | ✅ Good | Loss = ₹0 |
| Slightly off | 1.510 V | ✅ Good | Small loss — phone charges slightly slower |
| At limit | 1.550 V | ✅ Just OK | Big loss — device may misbehave |
| Outside limit | 1.600 V | ❌ Defective | Huge loss — warranty claim, recall |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Taguchi does NOT consider a product inside the specification limits as "zero loss." Loss exists *continuously* from the target outward.

## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Know

- **m** — Target value (the *ideal* value of the quality characteristic).
- **Δ₀** — Tolerance limit (distance from target to specification boundary).
- **L₀** — Loss to society when the quality characteristic equals the specification limit (m ± Δ₀).
- **k** — Quality Loss Coefficient (proportionality constant, in monetary units per unit²).
- **x** — Actual measured value of the quality characteristic.
- **L(x)** — Quality Loss experienced at actual value x.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All values above carry standard units. For a voltage, `x` and `m` are in **Volts**, `Δ₀` in **Volts**, `L₀` in **₹ (or $)**, and `k` in **₹ / V²**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Philosophy Behind Taguchi's Approach

Traditional quality thinking is a **step function**:

* Inside limits → Loss = 0 (good product)
* Outside limits → Loss = 100% (scrap/rework)

Taguchi's loss function is a **smooth quadratic curve**:

* At target (x = m) → Loss = 0
* At specification limit (x = m ± Δ₀) → Loss = L₀ (some positive monetary value)
* Outside specification → Loss grows rapidly (quadratically)

## 2.2 The Three Categories of Quality Characteristics

Taguchi classifies any measurable quality characteristic into **one of three categories**:

### A. Nominal-the-Best (NTB)
* Target is a *finite, non-zero central value*.
* Example: A shaft diameter of **50.00 mm**, electrolyte pH of **7.0**, voltage **1.50 V**.
* Loss occurs whether deviation is positive or negative.
* Loss Function: **L(x) = k(x − m)²**

### B. Smaller-the-Better (STB)
* Target is **zero** (or as small as possible).
* Example: Defects per million, emissions, surface roughness, leakage current.
* Loss Function: **L(x) = kx²** (with m = 0).

### C. Larger-the-Better (LTB)
* Target is **infinity** (or as large as possible).
* Example: Strength, battery life, fuel efficiency, MTBF.
* Loss Function: **L(x) = k / x²** (or equivalently k · y² where y = 1/x).

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1:** Taguchi assumed loss varies as the *square* of deviation (quadratic). This is because:
* Two small deviations can interact (variance additivity).
* Loss is symmetric around target (positive and negative deviations are equally bad).
* Quadratic curves are continuous and differentiable — easy to optimize mathematically.

**Step 2:** Formulate the loss function generically:
$$\boxed{L(x) = k(x - m)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **boundary condition**: at the specification limit, x = m + Δ₀, loss = L₀.

$$L_0 = k(m + \Delta_0 - m)^2$$

$$L_0 = k(\Delta_0)^2$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the **quality loss coefficient k**:

$$\boxed{k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute k back into the loss function to obtain the **final usable form**:

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}(x - m)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** This single equation lets a process engineer answer: *"If I tighten my process variation by σ, how much money will society save annually?"* This converts quality improvements into **rupee/dollar terms** that management understands instantly.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $L(x) = k(x - m)^2$ | Nominal-the-best loss at actual value x | ₹ or $ |
| 2 | $k = \dfrac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}$ | Quality loss coefficient | ₹ / unit² |
| 3 | $L(x) = \dfrac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}(x - m)^2$ | Final Nominal-the-best loss function | ₹ or $ |
| 4 | $L(x) = k \cdot x^2$ | Smaller-the-best loss (target = 0) | ₹ or $ |
| 5 | $L(x) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot x^2$ | STB with A₀ = loss at upper limit | ₹ or $ |
| 6 | $L(x) = \dfrac{k}{x^2}$ | Larger-the-best loss (target = ∞) | ₹ or $ |
| 7 | $\eta = 10 \log\left(\dfrac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ | S/N ratio, Nominal-the-best | dB |
| 8 | $\eta = -10 \log\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | S/N ratio, Smaller-the-best | dB |
| 9 | $\eta = -10 \log\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | S/N ratio, Larger-the-best | dB |
| 10 | $\Delta\beta = 10 \log\left(\dfrac{L_1}{L_2}\right)$ | Improvement in loss (positive ⇒ improvement) | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing loss in the STB case, never forget that m = 0. A frequent error is writing L(x) = k(x − m)² with m ≠ 0 for STB problems — you will lose 2 marks.

## 2.5 Why a Quadratic Function? (Engineering Justification)

1. **Variance additivity** — If two independent processes contribute deviation, the total variance is the sum of individual variances. Only a squared term gives this additivity.
2. **Symmetry** — Loss is the same on either side of the target.
3. **Smoothness** — Taylor-series expansion of any well-behaved loss function around the target gives the quadratic term as the first non-zero approximation.
4. **Empirical fit** — Customer dissatisfaction studies consistently fit a quadratic curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Taguchi Quality Loss Function

**Given:**
* Target value of a quality characteristic = **m**
* Specification tolerance = **±Δ₀**
* Loss at specification limit = **L₀**
* Actual value of the quality characteristic = **x**

**To Derive:** The explicit functional form L(x).

**Step 1 — State the General Quadratic Hypothesis**

Taguchi postulated that loss is a smooth, symmetric, monotonically increasing function of (x − m). The simplest such function that satisfies all required mathematical properties is the quadratic form:

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2$$

where k is a positive constant (k > 0, otherwise loss would be negative for some x, which is impossible).

**Step 2 — Apply the Boundary Condition at the Upper Specification Limit**

By definition, when x = m + Δ₀, the loss equals L₀. Substituting:

$$L(m + \Delta_0) = k \cdot ((m + \Delta_0) - m)^2$$

$$L_0 = k \cdot (\Delta_0)^2$$

**Step 3 — Verify the Symmetry at the Lower Specification Limit**

For x = m − Δ₀, substituting:

$$L(m - \Delta_0) = k \cdot ((m - \Delta_0) - m)^2$$

$$L(m - \Delta_0) = k \cdot (-\Delta_0)^2 = k \cdot (\Delta_0)^2 = L_0 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the **symmetric loss property**.

**Step 4 — Solve for the Quality Loss Coefficient k**

Rearranging the equation from Step 2:

$$k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Step 5 — Write the Final Loss Function**

Substituting k back into the general form:

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2}(x - m)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **board-exam-ready form**. Always present the loss function in this final form once derived — partial credit is given for the derivation but full credit only for the simplified equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Smaller-the-Better Loss Function

For STB problems, the target m = 0. The general form collapses to:

$$L(x) = k \cdot x^2$$

If we are told that the *allowable upper limit* is Δ₀ and loss at that limit is A₀, then:

$$A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \implies k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \cdot x^2}$$

This is the standard form for **defects, impurities, surface roughness, error rates**, etc.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Larger-the-Better Loss Function

For LTB, we define y = 1/x (so a large x → small y). The target for y is 0. Treating y as STB:

$$L(y) = k \cdot y^2 = k \cdot \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{k}{x^2}}$$

If at x = Δ₀ the loss is A₀, then:

$$A_0 = \frac{k}{\Delta_0^2} \implies k = A_0 \cdot \Delta_0^2$$

$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{A_0 \cdot \Delta_0^2}{x^2}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** The target diameter of a shaft is **m = 50.00 mm** with tolerance **±0.05 mm**. The manufacturer estimates the loss to society when a shaft is at the specification limit is **₹400**. Find:
1. The quality loss coefficient k.
2. The loss when an actual shaft measures 50.03 mm.

**Solution:**

**Part 1:** Given m = 50.00 mm, Δ₀ = 0.05 mm, L₀ = ₹400.

$$k = \frac{L_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \frac{400}{0.0025}$$

$$\boxed{k = 1{,}60{,}000 \; \text{₹/mm}^2}$$

**Part 2:** For x = 50.03 mm, deviation = x − m = 0.03 mm.

$$L(x) = k(x - m)^2 = 1{,}60{,}000 \cdot (0.03)^2$$

$$L(x) = 1{,}60{,}000 \cdot 0.0009 = 144 \; \text{₹}$$

$$\boxed{L(50.03) = \text{₹}144}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observe:** A shaft just 0.03 mm off-target (well within tolerance!) still causes a ₹144 loss. This is the revolutionary insight Taguchi brought — **"good" products inside tolerance can still hide massive hidden losses**.

## 3.5 Improvement Analysis Using Loss Function

Suppose initial variation gives loss L₁ and improved process gives loss L₂. The improvement in decibels is:

$$\Delta\beta = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right) \; \text{dB}$$

If L₂ < L₁ (loss reduced), Δβ > 0, indicating improvement.

**Example:** If L₁ = ₹1000 and L₂ = ₹100:

$$\Delta\beta = 10 \log\left(\frac{1000}{100}\right) = 10 \log(10) = 10 \cdot 1 = 10 \; \text{dB}$$

A 10-dB reduction means **90% loss reduction** — a powerful management metric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Taguchi Quality Loss Function Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Quality Problem] --> B{Identify Quality Category}
    B -->|Target is finite non-zero| C[Nominal the Best NTB]
    B -->|Target equals zero| D[Smaller the Better STB]
    B -->|Target equals infinity| E[Larger the Better LTB]
    C --> F[Apply L(x) = k times x minus m squared]
    D --> G[Apply L(x) = k times x squared]
    E --> H[Apply L(x) = k divided by x squared]
    F --> I[Compute k equals L0 divided by delta0 squared]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Substitute k into chosen loss function]
    J --> K[Calculate Total Annual Loss]
    K --> L[Decision: Is Loss Acceptable]
    L -->|Yes| M[Maintain Process]
    L -->|No| N[Redesign or Tighten Tolerance]
    N --> O[Recompute New Loss Function]
    O --> L
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Graph — Comparative Loss Curves (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Traditional_Quality_View
        T1[Inside Tolerance Limit] --> T2[Zero Loss]
        T3[At Tolerance Limit] --> T4[Zero Loss Still]
        T5[Outside Tolerance Limit] --> T6[Full Loss A0]
    end

    subgraph Taguchi_Quality_View
        Tag1[x equals target m] --> Tag2[L equals 0]
        Tag3[x equals m plus delta0] --> Tag4[L equals L0]
        Tag5[x equals m plus 2 delta0] --> Tag6[L equals 4 L0]
        Tag7[x equals m plus 3 delta0] --> Tag8[L equals 9 L0]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Engineering Application of QLF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Customer Requirement] --> B[Identify Target m]
    B --> C[Determine Tolerance delta0]
    C --> D[Estimate Loss L0 at Spec Limit]
    D --> E[Compute Loss Coefficient k]
    E --> F[Measure Actual Value x]
    F --> G[Calculate Loss L of x]
    G --> H[Compare with Acceptable Loss]
    H -->|Acceptable| I[Continue Production]
    H -->|Not Acceptable| J[Process Improvement]
    J --> K[Tighter Control sigma down]
    K --> F
```

## 4.4 Schematic Table — Loss Categories Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    QLF[Taguchi Quality Loss Function] --> NTB[Nominal the Best]
    QLF --> STB[Smaller the Better]
    QLF --> LTB[Larger the Better]

    NTB --> NTBe[Example: Shaft Diameter 50 mm]
    NTB --> NTBf[Formula: L equals k times x minus m squared]

    STB --> STBe[Example: Surface Roughness in micrometers]
    STB --> STBf[Formula: L equals k times x squared]

    LTB --> LTBe[Example: Battery Life in hours]
    LTB --> LTBf[Formula: L equals k divided by x squared]
```
