\sigma_{level} = 3.5 \, \sigma
$$

The current process operates at **3.5σ**. The Quality Objective would be to drive this to **≥ 4.5σ** within 18 months.

**Step 6 — Calculate Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) for the full evaluation process.**

Suppose the exam evaluation has **3 sub-processes**: (i) Distribution to evaluators, (ii) Evaluation, (iii) Marks entry verification.

$$
Y_1 = 0.95, \quad Y_2 = 0.92, \quad Y_3 = 0.97
$$

$$
RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3
$$

$$
RTY = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.97
$$

$$
RTY = 0.8760 \times 0.97
$$

$$
RTY = 0.8497 \approx 84.97\ \%
$$

This means about **15 %** of answer papers suffer a defect somewhere along the chain — directly informing a Quality Objective for process improvement.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix Summary Table

| Metric | Symbol | Computed Value | Interpretation | Quality Statement Link |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SMART Validation | — | TRUE | Objective meets SMART criteria | Quality Objective Step 1 |
| Objective Achievement Ratio | $OAR$ | 62.5 % | 37.5 % gap exists | Quality Objective (deployment) |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO$ | 120{,}000 | Far below Six Sigma | Quality Objective (baseline) |
| Current Process Yield | $Y_{current}$ | 88 % | Maps to ~2.3σ | Quality Objective (Six Sigma link) |
| Process Capability | $\sigma_{level}$ | 3.5σ | Below industrial benchmark | Quality Objective (capability) |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY$ | 84.97 % | Significant improvement scope | Quality Objective (process) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Logic Recap:** Each Quality Objective must be (a) **stated in SMART terms**, (b) **tracked with OAR**, (c) **anchored to a metric** (DPMO, RTY, σ-level), and (d) **linked to a financial or stakeholder outcome** (COPQ). Skipping any of these four layers in your exam answer will lead to a **3–5 mark deduction** by the KTU examiner.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Quality Statements Hierarchy — Top-Down Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeV["VISION STATEMENT\nLong-term Aspirational Future State\n10 to 25 Year Horizon"]
    nodeM["MISSION STATEMENT\nCore Purpose and Scope\nPresent Day Identity"]
    nodeQP["QUALITY POLICY STATEMENT\nTop Management Commitment\nFramework for Objectives"]
    nodeQO["QUALITY OBJECTIVES\nSMART Measurable Targets\nProcess and Function Level"]
    nodeKPI["KEY PERFORMANCE INDICATORS\nDPMO RTY OAR COPQ\nContinual Monitoring"]
    nodeDMAIC["DMAIC SIX SIGMA PROJECTS\nKaizen 5S Poka Yoke\nDay to Day Execution"]

    nodeV ==> nodeM
    nodeM ==> nodeQP
    nodeQP ==> nodeQO
    nodeQO ==> nodeKPI
    nodeKPI ==> nodeDMAIC
    nodeDMAIC -.Feedback Loop.-> nodeQO
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Quality Statement Deployment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph strat["STRATEGIC LAYER"]
        boardA["Board of Directors\nowns Vision"]
        mgmtB["Top Management\nowns Mission and Policy"]
    end

    subgraph tact["TACTICAL LAYER"]
        deptC["Department Heads\ndeploy Quality Objectives"]
        procD["Process Owners\ndesign CTQ Trees"]
    end

    subgraph ops["OPERATIONAL LAYER"]
        empE["Frontline Employees\nexecute DMAIC and Kaizen"]
        custF["Customers and Stakeholders\nreceive Value"]
    end

    boardA -- communicates --> mgmtB
    mgmtB -- cascades --> deptC
    deptC -- allocates --> procD
    procD -- assigns --> empE
    empE -- delivers --> custF
    custF -- feedback --> boardA
```

### 4.3 SMART Quality Objective Construction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Start with Policy Intent"]
    specN["Make it Specific\nWho What Where"]
    measN["Make it Measurable\nKPI and Baseline"]
    achN["Make it Achievable\nResource Feasibility"]
    relN["Make it Relevant\nStrategic Alignment"]
    timeN["Make it Time Bound\nDeadline and Milestones"]
    validN{"All Five TRUE"}
    deployN["Deploy to Process Owners"]
    monitorN["Track via OAR DPMO RTY"]
    reviewN["Management Review and PDCA"]

    startNode --> specN --> measN --> achN --> relN --> timeN --> validN
    validN -- YES --> deployN --> monitorN --> reviewN
    validN -- NO --> specN
    reviewN -.Update.-> startNode
```

### 4.4 Alignment of Quality Statements with ISO 9001:2015

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    isoClause5["ISO 9001 Clause 5\nLeadership and Commitment"]
    isoClause52["Clause 5.2\nQuality Policy"]
    isoClause62["Clause 6.2\nQuality Objectives and Planning"]
    isoClause93["Clause 9.3\nManagement Review"]
    isoClause101["Clause 10.1\nContinual Improvement"]

    qPolicy["Quality Policy Statement"]
    qObj["Quality Objectives"]
    qVision["Vision and Mission"]

    isoClause5 -- mandates --> qVision
    isoClause52 -- requires --> qPolicy
    isoClause62 -- operationalizes --> qObj
    isoClause93 -- reviews --> qObj
    isoClause101 -- improves --> qPolicy
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (nodeV, isoClause5, etc.) and avoid reserved keywords. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no markdown bold or italics inside node labels — to ensure clean rendering across KTU study portal browsers and VS Code preview.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

Define **Quality Policy Statement** and list any **four essential elements** it must contain as per ISO 9001:2015.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Quality Policy Statement** is a formal, written declaration by top management that articulates the organization's overall intentions and direction with respect to quality, aligned with its strategic direction.

The **four essential elements** are:
1. **Appropriateness** to the organization's purpose and context.
2. **Framework** for setting Quality Objectives.
3. **Commitment** to satisfy applicable customer, statutory, and regulatory requirements.
4. **Commitment** to continual improvement of the QMS.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Writing Tip:** Always pair "commitment to satisfy requirements" and "commitment to continual improvement" — these two phrases are the **exact ISO 9001:2015 wording** and examiners reward verbatim recall.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

Differentiate between a **Mission Statement** and a **Vision Statement** with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Vision Statement | Mission Statement |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Future-oriented (10–25 years) | Present-oriented |
| Purpose | Describes desired future state | Defines core purpose and scope |
| Nature | Abstract, inspirational | Concrete, operational |
| Example | *"To be the world's most trusted digital partner"* | *"To deliver reliable, secure cloud services to enterprises across 40 countries"* |

The **Vision** answers *"What do we want to become?"* while the **Mission** answers *"What do we do today and for whom?"*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks** and typically contain two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Below, two completely independent alternatives are provided.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted Module 2 PEIET631]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four levels of Quality Statements** (Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, and Quality Objectives) and describe how they form an integrated hierarchy in a TQM-driven organization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **SMART criteria** for formulating Quality Objectives. Why is each criterion critical for Six Sigma project success? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Four Levels of Quality Statements (7 Marks):**

Quality Statements operate at four hierarchical levels in any TQM-driven organization:

1. **Vision Statement (1 Mark):** A long-term aspirational declaration (typically 10–25 years) describing the desired future state. *Example:* Tesla's vision of accelerating the world's transition to sustainable energy.

2. **Mission Statement (1 Mark):** A present-tense description of the organization's core business, customer base, products/services, and value proposition. *Example:* Infosys' mission to deliver next-generation digital services.

3. **Quality Policy Statement (2 Marks):** A formal, top-management-authored document that:
   - Aligns with strategic direction.
   - Provides a framework for setting Quality Objectives.
   - Commits to satisfying applicable requirements.
   - Commits to continual improvement.
   - *Example:* Tata Steel's quality policy on customer satisfaction and zero-defect manufacturing.

4. **Quality Objectives (2 Marks):** SMART, measurable targets at function/process level. They must be **monitored, communicated, updated, and linked to the Quality Policy**. *Example:* "Reduce supplier rejection rate from 3 % to 1 % by Q4 2026."

**Integration (1 Mark):** Vision → Mission → Quality Policy → Quality Objectives form a **cascading hierarchy** where each level derives from and feeds into the next. The Quality Objectives are the operational translation of strategic intent.

---

**(b) SMART Criteria for Quality Objectives (7 Marks):**

SMART is a validation framework ensuring Quality Objectives are actionable and trackable:

| Criterion | Meaning | Why it matters for Six Sigma (Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **S**pecific | Clearly defined scope, owner, target | Prevents scope creep in DMAIC projects (1.5) |
| **M**easurable | Quantifiable metric (DPMO, RTY, cost) | Enables statistical analysis and control charts (1.5) |
| **A**chievable | Realistic given resources | Ensures team motivation and project credibility (1.0) |
| **R**elevant | Linked to Quality Policy / customer CTQ | Aligns project to strategic goals (1.5) |
| **T**ime-bound | Clear deadline and milestones | Forces closure and prevents endless analysis (1.5) |

**Example:** *"Reduce average billing complaint resolution time from 48 hours to 12 hours by 31 March 2026 for the Kerala regional office."*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write SMART in vague terms like "achievable" without specifying the resource or capability that makes it achievable. **Always tie A (Achievable) to a data-backed feasibility** — this is where 1–2 marks are routinely lost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted Module 2 PEIET631]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of top management** in formulating and communicating Quality Statements. How does their involvement influence organizational culture and TQM adoption? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IT services firm has set the following Quality Objective: *"Improve first-pass yield of code deployment from 88 % to 96 % within 12 months."* Using appropriate Six Sigma metrics, **calculate the DPMO and σ-level** for both the current and target states. Assume **5 defect opportunities per deployment**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Role of Top Management in Quality Statements (7 Marks):**

1. **Strategic Ownership (2 Marks):** Top management formulates the Vision, Mission, and Quality Policy, ensuring alignment with market and stakeholder expectations.

2. **Communication and Deployment (2 Marks):** They personally communicate the statements to all levels via town halls, intranet portals, and induction programs — making quality a **visible leadership priority**.

3. **Resource Provision (1.5 Marks):** They allocate budget, training, and infrastructure for Quality Objectives and Six Sigma projects.

4. **Cultural Influence (1.5 Marks):** Their visible commitment shapes an **organization-wide quality culture** — the foundation for TQM adoption. Without leadership commitment, TQM collapses (Deming's famous Point 1: *Create constancy of purpose*).

---

**(b) DPMO and σ-level Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Current First Pass Yield ($FPY_{current}$) = 88 % = 0.88
- Target First Pass Yield ($FPY_{target}$) = 96 % = 0.96
- Opportunities per deployment ($O$) = 5
- Units in current state: Let $U = 1{,}000{,}000$ (normalized to 1 million)

**Step 1 — Compute Defect Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
D_{rate,current} = 1 - FPY_{current} = 1 - 0.88 = 0.12
$$

$$
D_{rate,target} = 1 - FPY_{target} = 1 - 0.96 = 0.04
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO (2 Marks):**

$$
DPMO_{current} = \dfrac{D_{rate,current} \times 1{,}000{,}000}{O}
$$

$$
DPMO_{current} = \dfrac{0.12 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5}
$$

$$
DPMO_{current} = \dfrac{120{,}000}{5}
$$

$$
DPMO_{current} = 24{,}000
$$

$$
DPMO_{target} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{5} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{5} = 8{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Determine σ-level (2 Marks):**

Using the standard yield-to-sigma conversion table:

| Yield | σ-level |
|---|---|
| 88.0 % | ≈ 2.30 σ |
| 96.0 % | ≈ 2.90 σ (interpolated) |

Thus:
- Current σ-level ≈ **2.30σ**
- Target σ-level ≈ **2.90σ**

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 Marks):**

The Quality Objective moves the process from a **2.30σ capability** (DPMO = 24,000) to a **2.90σ capability** (DPMO = 8,000). While this is a substantial improvement, the firm still needs further Six Sigma initiatives to reach **3.4 DPMO (6σ)** for world-class quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Calculation Trap:** Many students forget to **divide by Opportunities (O)** in the DPMO formula, computing $DPMO = D_{rate} \times 10^6$ instead of $DPMO = D_{rate} \times 10^6 / O$. This single error costs **2 marks**. Also, do not confuse **DPMO** with **DPU (Defects Per Unit)** — they are different metrics.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 PEIET631 — Module 2: Quality Statements**

- **Four Quality Statements** must be mastered: **Vision, Mission, Quality Policy, Quality Objectives**.
- They form a strict **top-down cascading hierarchy**: Vision → Mission → Quality Policy → Quality Objectives.
- **Quality Policy must be ISO 9001:2015 compliant** — must contain the **two mandatory commitments**: (i) satisfy applicable requirements and (ii) continual improvement.
- **Quality Objectives must be SMART** — Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound.
- **DPMO** is the most common Six Sigma metric linked to Quality Objectives: $DPMO = (D \times 1{,}000{,}000) / (U \times O)$.
- **RTY** (Rolled Throughput Yield) = product of all sub-process yields.
- **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure costs.
- **Top management ownership** of Quality Statements is mandatory — without it, TQM fails (Deming Point 1).
- **Management Review (ISO Clause 9.3)** is the formal re-evaluation cycle for Quality Objectives.
- **Continual Improvement (ISO Clause 10.1)** ensures Quality Statements evolve with organizational learning.
- For KTU 14-mark questions, always structure answers with **sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**, and include **one real-world example**.
- For 3-mark questions, **verbatim ISO 9001 wording** on Quality Policy earns full marks.
- **Avoid using pipe `|` inside markdown tables** in answer scripts — replace with `\vert` or `\mid` in any LaTeX answer.
- **Six Sigma σ-level table** for yield-to-sigma conversion is a high-yield asset — memorize at least: 99.99966 % → 6σ, 99.73 % → 3σ, 93.32 % → 2σ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Satisfaction (CS)** is a strategic marketing and quality management concept that measures the degree to which a product or service delivered by an organization meets or surpasses the **expectations of the customer**. In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, customer satisfaction is treated as a leading indicator of long-term business sustainability, customer retention, and profitability.

According to the **American Society for Quality (ASQ)** and the **ISO 9000:2015** family of standards, customer satisfaction is formally defined as:

> *"The customer's perception that the provider has met or exceeded the level of quality they expected from the product or service."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, customer satisfaction is positioned as **Outcome (CO) aligned to Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze)** — students are expected to compare models, evaluate determinants, and synthesize improvement strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Customer satisfaction is a **multi-dimensional construct**. It is not merely "the customer is happy"; it encompasses cognitive evaluation (perceived quality), affective response (emotional satisfaction), and behavioral intention (loyalty/repurchase).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

### 🍽️ Real-World Analogy: The Restaurant Experience

Imagine you walk into a **5-star restaurant** for dinner. Before you arrive, you form a set of **expectations** based on:
- Online reviews
- Star rating
- Word-of-mouth from friends
- Past dining experiences

Now, after your meal, you compare the **perceived performance** with your **expectation**:

| Scenario | Expectation | Perceived Performance | Result |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| Food is amazing, service fast | High | Higher | 😊 **Delighted** |
| Food is decent, service okay | Medium | Matches | 🙂 **Satisfied** |
| Food is cold, service rude | Medium | Lower | 😞 **Dissatisfied** |

**This mental comparison is exactly what Customer Satisfaction is.** The formal equation is:

$$\text{Customer Satisfaction} = f(\text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectation})$$

If the difference is **positive**, the customer is **satisfied or delighted**. If **negative**, the customer is **dissatisfied**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for B.Tech Students:** In TQM, the **customer is the ultimate judge of quality**. A product can pass every internal quality test, but if the end-customer is not satisfied, the product has *failed* in the marketplace.

---

## 1.3 Types of Customers (KTU High-Yield Concept)

In TQM, customers are classified based on their position in the value chain:

1. **External Customers** — End users, consumers, retailers who purchase the finished product. *Example:* A person buying a Samsung phone.
2. **Internal Customers** — Employees, departments, or processes within the organization that receive outputs from upstream processes. *Example:* The assembly line receiving parts from the welding shop.
3. **Intermediate Customers** — Distributors, dealers, and resellers who are neither the final consumer nor the producer.
4. **Hidden / Latent Customers** — Potential customers whose needs are not yet articulated but may emerge in the future. These are crucial for **innovation and R&D**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "EIIH":** External, Internal, Intermediate, Hidden. Remember this for KTU short-answer questions.

---

## 1.4 Importance of Customer Satisfaction in Engineering & Business

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Engineering Students Must Study This:**
> - **Module Outcome (CO) Linkage:** Directly tied to **CO2** of PEIET631 — *"Analyze customer-focused quality management practices."*
> - **Industry Relevance:** Concepts like **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** and **CSI (Customer Satisfaction Index)** are used by companies like Apple, Toyota, and Infosys.
> - **Six Sigma Connection:** Customer satisfaction is the **Voice of Customer (VOC)** driver that defines the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** tree in **DMAIC** projects.

**The Cost Reality:** Acquiring a new customer is **5× to 7× more expensive** than retaining an existing one. A **5% increase in customer retention** can boost profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Company research).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Customer Satisfaction (Conceptual Framework)

Customer satisfaction rests on **three interdependent pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Customer Expectations
Expectations are the **baseline reference standard** against which performance is judged. They are formed from:
- **Past experience** with the product/service
- **Word-of-mouth** communication
- **Explicit promises** made by the company (advertising, branding)
- **Implicit promises** (industry standards, price-quality heuristic)
- **Competitor benchmarks**

### Pillar 2: Perceived Performance
This is the **actual experience** of the customer after consuming the product/service. It is highly **subjective** and varies by:
- Customer mood
- Situational factors
- Personal preferences
- Cultural background

### Pillar 3: The Gap (Disconfirmation)
The mathematical difference between the two:

$$\text{Disconfirmation} = \text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectation}$$

- **Positive Disconfirmation** → Satisfaction or Delight
- **Zero Disconfirmation** → Mere Confirmation
- **Negative Disconfirmation** → Dissatisfaction

---

## 2.2 Determinants of Customer Satisfaction (KTU Board Favourite)

The following are the **key drivers** that determine satisfaction levels:

| # | Determinant | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|-------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Product Quality** | Conformance to specifications, reliability, durability |
| 2 | **Service Quality** | Tangibles, reliability, responsiveness, assurance, empathy (SERVQUAL) |
| 3 | **Price** | Perceived value for money |
| 4 | **Brand Image** | Trust and reputation |
| 5 | **Delivery / Timeliness** | On-time delivery performance (OTD %) |
| 6 | **After-Sales Support** | Warranty, maintenance, grievance handling |
| 7 | **Customer Effort** | Ease of doing business (CES — Customer Effort Score) |

---

## 2.3 The SERVQUAL Model (Service Quality Framework)

Developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988)**, the SERVQUAL model identifies **5 dimensions** of service quality that drive customer satisfaction:

1. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance
2. **Reliability** — Ability to perform the promised service dependably
3. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers promptly
4. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of staff inspiring trust
5. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention to customers

The **SERVQUAL Gap Equation:**

$$\text{SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)$$

Where:
- $SQ$ = Service Quality score
- $P_i$ = Perception of service on dimension $i$
- $E_i$ = Expectation on dimension $i$
- $w_i$ = Weight assigned to dimension $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Students are often asked to *"Apply SERVQUAL to a hospitality/IT industry scenario and identify gaps."* Always map the scenario to all 5 dimensions explicitly.

---

## 2.4 The Kano Model of Customer Satisfaction

Developed by **Professor Noriaki Kano (1984)**, this model classifies product features into **3 categories** based on their impact on satisfaction:

| Feature Type | Effect on Satisfaction | Example |
|--------------|------------------------|---------|
| **Must-Be (Basic)** | Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction; presence does NOT increase satisfaction | Car brakes working, phone battery not exploding |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | Satisfaction is linear with feature quality | Fuel efficiency, RAM size |
| **Attractive (Excitement)** | Presence causes delight; absence does NOT cause dissatisfaction | Self-driving mode, gesture control |
| **Indifferent** | No effect either way | Color of internal screws |
| **Reverse** | Causes dissatisfaction for some users | Auto-correct feature in messaging apps |

**Graphical Concept:**

```
Satisfaction
    |           /  Attractive (Excitement)
    |          /
    |---------X-----------------  One-Dimensional (Performance)
    |        /
    |       / 
    |------X----  Must-Be (Basic)
    |_____/____________________
                  Feature Implementation
```

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are directly testable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Range | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Disconfirmation** | $D = P - E$ | Dimensionless scalar | Core CS concept |
| 2 | **SERVQUAL Score** | $SQ = \sum w_i (P_i - E_i)$ | Weighted points (typically -7 to +7 Likert) | Service industries |
| 3 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | $-100$ to $+100$ | Loyalty measurement |
| 4 | **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** | $CSAT = \frac{\text{Positive Responses}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100$ | $0\%$ to $100\%$ | Direct satisfaction rating |
| 5 | **Customer Effort Score (CES)** | $CES = \text{Avg. rating on 1-7 ease scale}$ | 1 to 7 | Effort-based satisfaction |
| 6 | **Customer Retention Rate** | $CRR = \frac{(CE - CN)}{CS} \times 100$ | $0\%$ to $100\%$ | Loyalty metric |
| 7 | **ACSI Triplet** | $ACSI = f(\text{Expectation}, \text{Perceived Quality}, \text{Perceived Value})$ | 0 to 100 | National-level index |
| 8 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t)}{(1+d)^t}$ | Currency (₹, $) | Long-term value |

> **Notation Key:**
> - $P$ = Perceived Performance, $E$ = Expectation
> - $CE$ = Customers at End, $CN$ = New Customers, $CS$ = Customers at Start
> - $d$ = Discount rate, $t$ = time period

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Pipe-Symbol Warning:** When writing $\vert P - E \vert$ in your answer script or table, always use the LaTeX `\vert` command to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. In handwritten exams, simply write $|(P - E)|$ clearly.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **manufacturing engineering**:
- CSAT is collected via post-purchase surveys
- NPS is tracked quarterly for product lines
- SERVQUAL is applied in BPO, hospitality, and after-sales service centers

In **Six Sigma projects**:
- The **VOC (Voice of Customer)** becomes the **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** tree
- Customer satisfaction data drives the **Define** and **Measure** phases of **DMAIC**
- The **Kano model** helps prioritize which features to include in product redesign

In **software engineering**:
- CSAT scores guide **Sprint Retrospectives**
- NPS is used in product roadmap prioritization
- CES informs **UX/UI design** decisions

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Customer Satisfaction to Six Sigma DMAIC

The **DMAIC** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control) methodology integrates customer satisfaction at every phase. The following matrix is **exhaustive** and frequently tested in KTU 14-mark questions.

| DMAIC Phase | CS Activity | Tool Used | Output |
|-------------|-------------|-----------|--------|
| **Define** | Capture Voice of Customer (VOC) | Interviews, Focus Groups, Surveys | Customer Requirements (CRs) |
| **Measure** | Quantify current CS levels | CSAT, NPS, CES surveys | Baseline CS Score |
| **Analyze** | Identify root causes of dissatisfaction | Pareto, Fishbone, Regression | Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) factors |
| **Improve** | Design solutions to lift CS | Kano prioritization, QFD, FMEA | Improved CS prototype |
| **Control** | Sustain CS gains | Control charts, SPC, Dashboards | Sustained CS level |

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: NPS Calculation (Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** A mobile phone manufacturer receives 1,000 customer feedback responses in a quarter. The responses are categorized as:
> - **Promoters (rated 9-10):** 620
> - **Passives (rated 7-8):** 280
> - **Detractors (rated 0-6):** 100
>
> **Calculate the Net Promoter Score (NPS).**

### Step 1: Recall the NPS Formula

$$
NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Percentage of Promoters

$$
\%Promoters = \frac{620}{1000} \times 100 = 62\%
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Percentage of Detractors

$$
\%Detractors = \frac{100}{1000} \times 100 = 10\%
$$

### Step 4: Apply the NPS Formula

$$
NPS = 62\% - 10\% = 52
$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Score

$$
\text{An NPS of } +52 \text{ is considered } \textbf{Excellent} \text{ (Industry benchmark: } >50 \text{ is world-class).}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Error:** Many students incorrectly include **Passives** in the NPS calculation. Passives are **excluded** — they neither promote nor detract.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: SERVQUAL Gap Analysis (Application Type)

> **Problem:** A hotel chain surveys 200 customers on the **Responsiveness** dimension of SERVQUAL. The data is:
> - Average **Expectation** rating (out of 7): $E = 6.2$
> - Average **Perception** rating (out of 7): $P = 4.8$
> - Weight assigned to Responsiveness: $w = 0.25$
>
> **Compute the weighted service quality gap and interpret the result.**

### Step 1: Recall the SERVQUAL Equation

$$
SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
SQ_{\text{Resp}} = 0.25 \times (4.8 - 6.2)
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Disconfirmation

$$
P - E = 4.8 - 6.2 = -1.4
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Weight

$$
SQ_{\text{Resp}} = 0.25 \times (-1.4) = -0.35
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

$$
A \text{ negative score of } -0.35 \text{ indicates a } \textbf{Service Quality Gap} \text{ on the Responsiveness dimension.}
$$

The hotel is **underperforming** customer expectations in responsiveness. **Recommended Actions:**
- Train front-desk staff in rapid check-in protocols
- Implement a 24×7 concierge chatbot
- Set up an SLA of 5-minute response time for guest requests

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Strategy:** Always present the **interpretation** and **recommendation** in addition to the numerical answer. KTU examiners award 2-3 marks specifically for actionable insight.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Customer Retention Rate (Numerical Type)

> **Problem:** An e-commerce company had the following customer data for the year 2024:
> - Customers at Start (CS) = 5,000
> - New Customers Acquired (CN) = 1,200
> - Customers at End (CE) = 5,400
>
> **Calculate the Customer Retention Rate (CRR).**

### Step 1: Recall the CRR Formula

$$
CRR = \frac{(CE - CN)}{CS} \times 100
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$
CRR = \frac{(5400 - 1200)}{5000} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator

$$
5400 - 1200 = 4200
$$

### Step 4: Final Calculation

$$
CRR = \frac{4200}{5000} \times 100 = 84\%
$$

### Step 5: Interpretation

$$
\text{A CRR of } 84\% \text{ is } \textbf{Strong} \text{ for e-commerce. Industry leaders target } >85\%.
$$

---

## 3.5 Conceptual Derivation: The Expectation–Confirmation Theory (ECT)

The **Expectation-Confirmation Theory** (Oliver, 1980) provides the theoretical foundation for customer satisfaction. The logical derivation proceeds as follows:

### Step 1: Initial Expectation Formation

The customer forms a pre-purchase expectation:

$$
E = f(\text{Past Experience, WOM, Marketing, Competition})
$$

### Step 2: Post-Purchase Performance Perception

After consumption, the customer perceives actual performance:

$$
P = g(\text{Product/Service Attributes})
$$

### Step 3: Disconfirmation Computation

$$
D = P - E
$$

### Step 4: Satisfaction Formation

$$
S = f(D) = \begin{cases}
> 0 & \Rightarrow \text{Satisfaction} \\
= 0 & \Rightarrow \text{Simple Confirmation} \\
< 0 & \Rightarrow \text{Dissatisfaction}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 5: Behavioral Intention (Repurchase / Loyalty)

$$
\text{Loyalty} = h(S, \text{Trust}, \text{Commitment}, \text{Alternatives})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU Exams:** The ECT framework is the **theoretical backbone** of customer satisfaction. If a 14-mark question asks *"Explain the customer satisfaction model with examples,"* this 5-step derivation is the **model answer structure**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Customer Satisfaction Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Forms Expectations E] --> B[Customer Experiences Product or Service P]
    B --> C[Customer Computes Disconfirmation D equals P minus E]
    C --> D{Is D greater than equal to zero?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Customer is Satisfied]
    D -->|No| F[Customer is Dissatisfied]
    E --> G{Is P much greater than E?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Customer is Delighted]
    G -->|No| I[Customer is Content]
    H --> J[Loyalty and Repurchase]
    I --> J
    F --> K[Complaint or Churn]
    K --> L[Service Recovery Actions]
    L --> B
    J --> M[Positive Word of Mouth WOM]
    M --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** This flowchart follows the **ECT cycle**. Notice the closed-loop on the right (delighted customers generate WOM that influences new customers' expectations). This feedback loop is critical for sustainable business growth.

---

## 4.2 The SERVQUAL 5-Dimension Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    SQ[Service Quality SQ] --> T1[Tangibles]
    SQ --> T2[Reliability]
    SQ --> T3[Responsiveness]
    SQ --> T4[Assurance]
    SQ --> T5[Empathy]
    T1 --> P1[Physical Facilities]
    T1 --> P2[Equipment Appearance]
    T1 --> P3[Personnel Grooming]
    T2 --> P4[Dependable Performance]
    T2 --> P5[Accurate Service]
    T3 --> P6[Prompt Service]
    T3 --> P7[Willingness to Help]
    T4 --> P8[Staff Competence]
    T4 --> P9[Trust and Confidence]
    T5 --> P10[Personalized Attention]
    T5 --> P11[Customer Understanding]
```

---

## 4.3 The Kano Model Classification Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    K[Kano Model Feature Classification] --> MB[Must Be Basic]
    K --> OD[One Dimensional Performance]
    K --> AT[Attractive Excitement]
    K --> ID[Indifferent]
    K --> RV[Reverse]
    MB --> MB1[Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction]
    MB --> MB2[Presence does not increase satisfaction]
    OD --> OD1[Satisfaction increases linearly with quality]
    OD --> OD2[Example Fuel efficiency]
    AT --> AT1[Presence causes delight]
    AT --> AT2[Absence does not cause dissatisfaction]
    AT --> AT3[Example Self driving mode]
    ID --> ID1[No effect on satisfaction]
    RV --> RV1[Causes dissatisfaction for some segment]
```
