# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question 1: Define Customer Satisfaction and state any two of its determinants.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** Customer satisfaction is the customer's perception that a product or service has met or exceeded their expectations. It is a key outcome measure in Total Quality Management and serves as a leading indicator of customer loyalty and business performance.
>
> **Two Determinants (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Product Quality** — Conformance to specifications, reliability, and durability of the product.
> 2. **Service Quality** — Tangibles, reliability, responsiveness, assurance, and empathy (SERVQUAL dimensions).

---

### Question 2: What is the Net Promoter Score (NPS)? How is it calculated?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** The Net Promoter Score (NPS) is a customer loyalty metric developed by Frederick F. Reichheld (2003). It measures the willingness of customers to recommend a company's products or services to others, serving as a proxy for overall customer satisfaction and loyalty.
>
> **Formula (1 Mark):** $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$, where Promoters rate 9-10, Passives rate 7-8, and Detractors rate 0-6 on a 0-10 scale. The score ranges from $-100$ to $+100$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, End Semester Examination Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Analytical Question**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the SERVQUAL model in detail. Discuss its five dimensions and the gap analysis framework used to diagnose service quality problems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The SERVQUAL model, developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988)**, is a widely used instrument to measure **service quality** as the gap between customer expectations and perceptions. It is foundational to understanding service-based customer satisfaction.

**The Five Dimensions (4 Marks):**

1. **Tangibles** — The physical evidence of the service: buildings, equipment, personnel appearance, and communication materials. *Example:* The cleanliness and decor of a hotel lobby.

2. **Reliability** — The ability to perform the promised service **dependably and accurately**. *Example:* An airline delivering luggage on time and in good condition.

3. **Responsiveness** — The willingness to **help customers** and provide prompt service. *Example:* A call center answering within 30 seconds.

4. **Assurance** — The knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to **inspire trust and confidence**. *Example:* A bank manager explaining complex loan terms clearly.

5. **Empathy** — The provision of **caring, individualized attention** to customers. *Example:* A doctor remembering a patient's medical history.

**Gap Analysis Framework (2 Marks):**
SERVQUAL identifies **5 gaps** that cause service quality failures:

- **Gap 1:** Difference between management perceptions and customer expectations (*Marketing research gap*)
- **Gap 2:** Difference between management perceptions and service quality specifications (*Standards gap*)
- **Gap 3:** Difference between service specifications and actual service delivery (*Performance gap*)
- **Gap 4:** Difference between service delivery and external communications (*Communication gap*)
- **Gap 5:** Difference between expected service and perceived service (*Customer gap — the core SERVQUAL equation*)

**Equation (Bonus Point):**

$$
SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i \cdot (P_i - E_i)
$$

**Conclusion:** SERVQUAL is a powerful diagnostic tool that helps organizations identify and close service quality gaps, thereby enhancing customer satisfaction.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A restaurant chain collected feedback from 500 customers on a 1-5 satisfaction scale. Results: 80 customers rated 5, 150 rated 4, 120 rated 3, 100 rated 2, and 50 rated 1. Calculate the CSAT score and comment on the result. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Recall the CSAT Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
CSAT = \frac{\text{Number of Positive Responses (4 and 5)}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Positive Responses (1 Mark):**
Positive responses (rating 4 or 5):

$$
80 \text{ (rated 5)} + 150 \text{ (rated 4)} = 230
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Formula (2 Marks):**

$$
CSAT = \frac{230}{500} \times 100 = 46\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and Recommendation (3 Marks):**

A CSAT of **46%** is **below industry average** (typical benchmark for restaurants is 75-85%). This indicates a significant service quality problem.

**Root Cause Hypotheses:**
- Food quality inconsistency
- Slow service during peak hours
- Untrained staff leading to order errors
- Ambiance or hygiene issues

**Recommended Actions:**
- Conduct **Pareto analysis** on the 150 customers who rated 3 (the "passive" segment) to identify the most common complaints.
- Implement **staff retraining** in customer interaction.
- Introduce a **digital feedback kiosk** for real-time monitoring.
- Set a **target CSAT of 75%** within 6 months using the **DMAIC** framework.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Comprehensive Question**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Describe the Kano model of customer satisfaction. With a suitable diagram, explain the three primary feature categories and their implications for product design. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Kano model, developed by **Professor Noriaki Kano (1984)** of the Tokyo Rika University, is a product development and customer satisfaction framework that categorizes product features based on their **ability to satisfy customers**.

**Three Primary Categories (4 Marks):**

1. **Must-Be (Basic / Threshold) Features:**
   - These are the **fundamental requirements** that customers expect.
   - Their **absence** causes **extreme dissatisfaction**, but their **presence** does not increase satisfaction.
   - *Example:* A car must have functioning brakes and seatbelts.

2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Features:**
   - Satisfaction is **linearly proportional** to the level of feature implementation.
   - Better implementation → Higher satisfaction. Worse implementation → Dissatisfaction.
   - *Example:* Fuel efficiency in a car. The more km/litre, the more satisfied the customer.

3. **Attractive (Excitement) Features:**
   - These are **unexpected delights** that customers do not anticipate.
   - Their **presence** causes **delight**, but their **absence** does **not** cause dissatisfaction.
   - *Example:* A car with a built-in refrigerated glove compartment.

**Implications for Product Design (2 Marks):**

- **Prioritize Must-Be features first** — they are non-negotiable entry requirements.
- **Compete on One-Dimensional features** — these are the basis of market comparison.
- **Innovate with Attractive features** — these create **competitive advantage** and customer delight.
- Over time, **Attractive features become Must-Be** (e.g., touchscreen in phones was once delightful, now expected).

**Conclusion:** The Kano model helps engineers and product managers **strategically allocate R&D resources** to maximize customer satisfaction.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: "Customer satisfaction is the result of the comparison between expectations and perceived performance." Discuss this statement with reference to the Expectation–Confirmation Theory (ECT). Also explain the concepts of positive and negative disconfirmation. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The given statement captures the essence of the **Expectation–Confirmation Theory (ECT)**, proposed by **Richard L. Oliver (1980)**. ECT is one of the most widely accepted frameworks for understanding post-purchase customer satisfaction.

**The ECT Framework (3 Marks):**

The theory proposes that customer satisfaction is a function of **three sequential cognitive processes:**

1. **Expectation Formation (Pre-Purchase):** Customers form expectations based on past experience, marketing communications, word-of-mouth, and competitor offerings.

$$
E = f(\text{Past, WOM, Marketing, Competition})
$$

2. **Performance Perception (Post-Purchase):** After consuming the product/service, customers form a perception of actual performance.

$$
P = g(\text{Actual Product Attributes})
$$

3. **Confirmation / Disconfirmation:** The customer compares $P$ with $E$ to arrive at a satisfaction judgment.

$$
D = P - E
$$

**Positive and Negative Disconfirmation (2 Marks):**

- **Positive Disconfirmation** occurs when $P > E$. The product **exceeds** expectations, leading to **satisfaction** or even **delight**.
  - *Example:* A budget airline provides free snacks when none were expected.

- **Negative Disconfirmation** occurs when $P < E$. The product **falls short** of expectations, leading to **dissatisfaction**.
  - *Example:* A 5-star hotel has dirty rooms despite charging premium prices.

- **Zero Disconfirmation (Simple Confirmation)** occurs when $P = E$. The product meets but does not exceed expectations. The customer is **neutral**.

**Practical Implications (1 Mark):**

- Companies must **manage expectations carefully** — over-promising leads to negative disconfirmation.
- They must also **enhance perceived performance** through quality improvements.
- Both strategies can be combined to create **customer delight** — the holy grail of TQM.

**Conclusion:** The ECT framework provides a robust theoretical foundation for measuring and managing customer satisfaction in any industry.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Customer Satisfaction Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Formula Units and Range:** When calculating NPS or CSAT, students often forget to convert to percentage. Always write the final answer in **percentage or score range** explicitly. *(Penalty: -1 mark)*
>
> 2. **Confusing Passives with Promoters/Detractors in NPS:** Passives (rated 7-8) are **EXCLUDED** from NPS calculation. Many students incorrectly add them as promoters. *(Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)*
>
> 3. **Writing Definitions Without Examples:** KTU board examiners expect a **real-world example** with every theoretical concept. A definition without an example gets only 50% marks. *(Penalty: -1 mark per missing example)*
>
> 4. **Ignoring the "Recommendation" Step in Numerical Questions:** In 7-mark computational questions, the **interpretation and recommendation** carry **2-3 marks**. Students who stop at the numerical answer lose significant marks. *(Penalty: -2 to -3 marks)*
>
> 5. **Using Pipe Symbols in Tables Incorrectly:** When presenting the SERVQUAL equation in a table, writing $|(P - E)|$ with literal pipes breaks the table rendering. Use `\vert` in LaTeX or write "(P - E) absolute value" in plain text. *(Penalty: Formatting marks deducted)*
>
> 6. **Mixing Up Kano Categories:** "Must-be" and "One-dimensional" features are frequently confused. Remember: **Must-be = binary (present/absent)**; **One-dimensional = linear (more/less)**. *(Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)*

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Customer Satisfaction (Module 2)**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] **Definition:** Customer satisfaction = Perceived Performance **minus** Expectation
- [ ] **Three Types of Customers:** External, Internal, Intermediate (and Hidden)
- [ ] **ECT Framework (Oliver, 1980):** Expectation → Performance → Disconfirmation → Satisfaction → Loyalty
- [ ] **SERVQUAL 5 Dimensions:** Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy
- [ ] **5 Gaps of SERVQUAL:** Marketing research, Standards, Performance, Communication, Customer gap

### ✅ Critical Formulas (Must be at your fingertips)
- [ ] $D = P - E$ (Disconfirmation)
- [ ] $SQ = \sum w_i (P_i - E_i)$ (SERVQUAL)
- [ ] $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ (Range: $-100$ to $+100$)
- [ ] $CSAT = \frac{\text{Positive Responses}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100$
- [ ] $CRR = \frac{(CE - CN)}{CS} \times 100$

### ✅ Models to Know
- [ ] **Kano Model:** Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive, Indifferent, Reverse
- [ ] **SERVQUAL:** Service quality gap analysis
- [ ] **ECT:** Cognitive comparison process
- [ ] **ACSI Triplet:** Expectation, Perceived Quality, Perceived Value

### ✅ Key People & Dates (For 1-mark sub-questions)
- [ ] **Noriaki Kano (1984)** — Kano Model
- [ ] **Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry (1988)** — SERVQUAL
- [ ] **Richard L. Oliver (1980)** — Expectation-Confirmation Theory
- [ ] **Frederick F. Reichheld (2003)** — Net Promoter Score

### ✅ Real-World Examples to Use in Answers
- [ ] **Restaurant analogy** — Food, service, ambiance comparison
- [ ] **Hotel SERVQUAL** — Tangibles, empathy, responsiveness
- [ ] **Smartphone Kano** — Basic = battery life, Performance = RAM, Attractive = gesture control
- [ ] **E-commerce CSAT** — Delivery speed, return policy, website usability

### ✅ Industry Benchmarks to Quote
- [ ] **NPS > 50** = World-class
- [ ] **CSAT > 80%** = Industry-leading
- [ ] **CRR > 85%** = Best-in-class (e-commerce)
- [ ] **5% increase in retention → 25-95% profit increase** (Bain & Co.)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always structure your 14-mark answer in **3 layers** — (1) Definition/Theory, (2) Detailed Explanation with Examples, (3) Diagram/Formula/Application. This guarantees full marks allocation across all CO and Bloom's levels.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Perception of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Customer Perception of Quality ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Customer Perception of Quality** is the subjective judgement or mental image a customer forms about the actual or expected performance, features, reliability, conformance, durability, serviceability, aesthetics, and perceived quality of a product or service. It is a *cognitive construct* — meaning it cannot be measured directly but must be inferred from customer attitudes, expectations, satisfaction levels, and behavioural responses. As defined by quality gurus (David Garvin, Philip Kotler, and A. Parasuraman), customer perception is the result of a continuous comparison between **customer expectations (E)** and **perceived performance (P)**. Mathematically, this can be expressed as: $$\text{Customer Satisfaction} = f(E, P)$$ Where a positive gap $(P > E)$ leads to delight, a neutral gap $(P \approx E)$ leads to satisfaction, and a negative gap $(P < E)$ leads to dissatisfaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Per KTU Module 2 of PEIET631, students must be able to distinguish between **Quality** (engineering/technical conformance) and **Customer Perception of Quality** (subjective, psychological, market-driven). Examiners frequently test this distinction directly. > [!NOTE] > **Definition Box (Board-Ready):** > *"Customer perception of quality is the customer's overall impression or subjective assessment of a product or service based on his/her individual needs, expectations, prior experiences, cultural background, and the quality dimensions that the customer considers most important at the moment of evaluation."* — Authored synthesis from Garvin's Eight Dimensions of Quality and Parasuraman's SERVQUAL framework. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you walk into a restaurant after seeing a 5-star rating online. You order a dish priced at ₹800. - **Your expectation:** Based on the price, reviews, ambiance, and menu description, you mentally predict how the dish should taste, how it should be presented, and how warm/courteous the server should be. - **Your perception:** This is formed when the food actually arrives, when you take the first bite, when the server responds to your call, and when you receive the bill. The **gap** between these two is your perception of quality. Two people sitting at the same table may form wildly different perceptions — one might love the dish, the other may find it oversalted. This is because perception is *personal*, not universal. **Engineering Parallel:** In a manufacturing firm, a CNC-machined component may have a dimensional tolerance of $\pm 0.01\text{ mm}$ (engineering quality), but if the customer wanted a mirror-polished surface finish and instead receives a matte finish, the perception of quality is low — even though the part is technically perfect. ### 1.3 Three Foundational Constructs of Customer Perception | Construct | Symbolic Meaning | Description | |---|---|---| | **Expected Quality** ($Q_E$) | What the customer *believes* will be delivered | Formed by word-of-mouth, advertising, prior experience, brand reputation | | **Experienced Quality** ($Q_X$) | What the customer *actually* receives | Determined at the moment-of-truth during usage or service encounter | | **Perceived Quality** ($Q_P$) | The cognitive judgement of the customer | The subjective outcome: $Q_P = Q_X - Q_E$ | > [!NOTE] > The **Moment of Truth (MOT)** — a term popularized by Jan Carlzon (former CEO of SAS Airlines) — is the precise instant when the customer interacts with the product/service and forms a perception. In TQM, every MOT is a quality checkpoint. ### 1.4 KTU 2024 — Key Distinctions Students Must Know - **Quality (Engineering Sense):** Conformance to specifications, standards, and design tolerances — measurable objectively. - **Quality (Customer Sense):** Fitness for use *as perceived by the customer* — measured subjectively through surveys, complaints, and loyalty. - **Quality (Value Sense):** Trade-off between price paid and benefits received (Price-Quality ratio). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Determinants of Customer Perception of Quality (The Garvin-Kotler Synthesis) Customer perception is influenced by **eight major determinants**, classified into two categories: #### A. Pre-Purchase Determinants (Before the customer uses the product) 1. **Word-of-Mouth (WOM):** Recommendations from friends, family, online reviews. This is the most trusted source according to Nielsen's Global Trust Survey. 2. **Advertising and Marketing Communications:** The brand's promise as portrayed in media. 3. **Brand Reputation and Image:** Cumulative past experiences and public perception of the brand. 4. **Price as a Quality Signal:** Higher price often (but not always) signals higher quality. 5. **Cultural and Personal Factors:** Demographics, values, lifestyle, and socio-economic status. #### B. Post-Purchase Determinants (During/After usage) 1. **Functional Performance:** Does the product perform its primary function reliably? 2. **Reliability and Durability:** Does it work without failure over time? 3. **Serviceability:** How easy is it to repair, maintain, or get support? 4. **Aesthetics and Feel:** Visual appeal, tactile sensation, packaging. 5. **Conformance to Standards:** Adherence to safety, environmental, and quality norms (e.g., ISO 9001, BIS). ### 2.2 The Kano Model of Customer Satisfaction (Dr. Noriaki Kano, 1984) The Kano Model classifies product/service features into **five distinct categories** that affect customer perception differently: | Category | Customer Reaction When Present | Customer Reaction When Absent | Strategic Action | |---|---|---|---| | **Basic (Must-Be) Quality** $Q_B$ | Neutral (expected) | Strongly dissatisfied | Must be present; cannot be a differentiator | | **One-Dimensional (Performance) Quality** $Q_O$ | More satisfied | Less satisfied | Linear relationship with satisfaction | | **Exciter (Attractive) Quality** $Q_E$ | Highly delighted | Not dissatisfied | Powerful differentiator if included | | **Indifferent Quality** $Q_I$ | No change | No change | Avoid investing in these | | **Reverse Quality** $Q_R$ | Dissatisfied | Satisfied | Remove these features | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Examiners love to ask: *"Explain the Kano Model with a diagram and examples."* Always draw the satisfaction curve — *Basic* features don't increase satisfaction above neutrality, while *Exciter* features cause satisfaction to rise sharply. ### 2.3 Parasuraman's SERVQUAL Gap Model (1985) For services (which dominate the modern economy), perceived quality is governed by **five gaps** that exist between various stages of service design and delivery: $$ Q_P = \text{Service Quality} = \text{Perceived Service} - \text{Expected Service} $$ **The Five Gaps:** | Gap # | Gap Description | Cause | |---|---|---| | **Gap 1** | Customer Expectation ↔ Management Perception | Management does not know what customers expect | | **Gap 2** | Management Perception ↔ Service Quality Specification | Wrong standards or insufficient processes | | **Gap 3** | Service Quality Specification ↔ Service Delivery | Employee performance falls short of standards | | **Gap 4** | Service Delivery ↔ External Communications | Promises overstate actual delivery | | **Gap 5** | Expected Service ↔ Perceived Service | The final outcome gap experienced by the customer | $$ \text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4}) $$ ### 2.4 Five Dimensions of Service Quality (SERVQUAL) Customers judge service quality on **five dimensions** (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry): 1. **Tangibles:** Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance. 2. **Reliability:** Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. 3. **Responsiveness:** Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. 4. **Assurance:** Knowledge and courtesy of employees; ability to inspire trust and confidence. 5. **Empathy:** Caring, individualized attention provided to customers. > [!NOTE] > The five dimensions generate a 22-item SERVQUAL questionnaire, scored on a **7-point Likert scale** from "Strongly Disagree" (1) to "Strongly Agree" (7). ### 2.5 Customer Satisfaction Indices (Standard Metrics) Several standardized indices quantify customer perception at the macro level: | Index | Full Name | Region | Range | Formula Concept | |---|---|---|---|---| | **ACSI** | American Customer Satisfaction Index | USA | 0–100 | Weighted average of expectation, perceived quality, perceived value, loyalty, complaints | | **EPSI** | European Performance Satisfaction Index | Europe | 0–100 | ACSI adapted with image and brand assets | | **CSAT** | Customer Satisfaction Score | Global | 0–100% | $\frac{\text{Satisfied responses}}{\text{Total responses}} \times 100$ | | **NPS** | Net Promoter Score | Global | -100 to +100 | %Promoters − %Detractors | | **CES** | Customer Effort Score | Global | 1–7 | How easy was it to resolve your issue? | ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) $$ \boxed{ \begin{aligned} \text{Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)} &= \frac{\text{Number of Satisfied Customers}}{\text{Total Number of Survey Responses}} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{Net Promoter Score (NPS)} &= \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors} \\[6pt] \text{Perceived Quality Gap} &= Q_P = P - E \\[6pt] \text{Overall Gap 5} &= \sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{Gap}_i \\[6pt] \text{Customer Retention Rate} &= \frac{(C_E - C_N)}{C_S} \times 100 \\[6pt] \text{ACSI Score} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \quad \text{(weighted index model)} \end{aligned} } $$ Where: - $P$ = Perceived Performance, $E$ = Expectation - $C_E$ = Customers at End of period, $C_N$ = New Customers acquired, $C_S$ = Customers at Start - $w_i$ = weight of factor $i$, $x_i$ = score of factor $i$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Customer perception drives Six Sigma projects through **VOC (Voice of Customer)** capture. DMAIC's *Define* and *Measure* phases use CSAT, NPS, and Kano analysis to convert subjective perception into measurable CTQs (Critical-to-Quality) characteristics. This is a frequently-tested KTU board topic. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Kano Model Classification Procedure The Kano questionnaire uses **paired questions** — one *positive* (functional) and one *negative* (dysfunctional). Each response is categorized using a standard evaluation table: $$ \text{Kano Category} = f(\text{Functional Response}, \text{Dysfunctional Response}) $$ **Step 1:** Customer responds to: *"If the product HAS feature X, how would you feel?"* (Functional form) **Step 2:** Customer responds to: *"If the product LACKS feature X, how would you feel?"* (Dysfunctional form) **Step 3:** Combine answers using the Kano Evaluation Table (sample rules): | Functional → / Dysfunctional ↓ | Like | Must-be | Neutral | Live-with | Dislike | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Like** | Q | A | A | A | O | | **Must-be** | R | I | I | I | M | | **Neutral** | R | I | I | I | M | | **Live-with** | R | I | I | I | M | | **Dislike** | R | R | R | R | Q | Where: $A$ = Attractive, $O$ = One-Dimensional, $M$ = Must-be, $I$ = Indifferent, $R$ = Reverse, $Q$ = Questionable. **Step 4:** Plot the **Kano Satisfaction Curve** with two axes: - X-axis: Degree of feature implementation (from "absent" to "fully present") - Y-axis: Customer satisfaction (from "dissatisfied" to "delighted") The curve shows: - **Must-be** (basic): Horizontal line at satisfaction = 0 until feature is absent, then drops sharply. - **One-dimensional:** Linear upward slope. - **Attractive (Exciter):** Steep upward curve, especially as feature goes from absent to present. - **Indifferent:** Flat horizontal line. - **Reverse:** Downward slope. ### 3.2 Worked Example: NPS Calculation for a Manufacturing Firm **Problem:** A heavy-engineering firm surveyed 500 customers at year-end: - **Promoters** (rated 9 or 10): 280 customers - **Passives** (rated 7 or 8): 150 customers - **Detractors** (rated 0 to 6): 70 customers **Step-by-Step Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,\text{Promoters} &= \frac{280}{500} \times 100 = 56\% \\[6pt] \%\,\text{Detractors} &= \frac{70}{500} \times 100 = 14\% \\[6pt] \text{NPS} &= \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors} \\[6pt] \text{NPS} &= 56\% - 14\% = 42 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** An NPS of **42** is considered *excellent* in most B2B industries (above 50 is world-class). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Tested Variant:** Sometimes the question gives the NPS value and asks for the number of promoters given detractors. Always show all intermediate steps to earn full marks. ### 3.3 Worked Example: Gap Analysis for a Restaurant Chain **Problem:** A restaurant chain identifies the following average SERVQUAL scores (out of 7) from 1000 customer responses: | Dimension | Expected (E) | Perceived (P) | Gap (P − E) | |---|---|---|---| | Tangibles | 6.5 | 5.8 | −0.7 | | Reliability | 6.8 | 5.5 | −1.3 | | Responsiveness | 6.4 | 5.9 | −0.5 | | Assurance | 6.6 | 6.0 | −0.6 | | Empathy | 6.3 | 5.4 | −0.9 | **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Gap (Gap 5)} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} (\text{P}_i - \text{E}_i) \\[6pt] &= (-0.7) + (-1.3) + (-0.5) + (-0.6) + (-0.9) \\[6pt] &= -4.0 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average Service Quality Gap} &= \frac{-4.0}{5} = -0.8 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** A negative average gap of **−0.8** indicates that perceived service falls short of expectations by 0.8 points on a 7-point scale. The largest gap is in **Reliability (−1.3)**, signalling that management should prioritize improving dependability. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Note:** Always close the gap analysis by identifying the **largest negative gap** and recommending a corrective action aligned with Six Sigma's DMAIC framework. Examiners award full marks only when both computation and interpretation are complete. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Quality vs Perception vs Satisfaction | Aspect | Quality (Engineering) | Perception of Quality | Customer Satisfaction | |---|---|---|---| | **Nature** | Objective | Subjective | Subjective (transactional) | | **Measured By** | SPC, control charts, tolerances | Surveys, focus groups, NPS | CSAT, ratings, complaints | | **Time Frame** | Pre-shipment / at production | At and post-use | Immediately post-interaction | | **Variable** | Continuous (inches, mm, defects) | Categorical / ordinal | Ordinal / interval | | **Influenced By** | Process capability | Psychology, culture, prior experience | Single transaction experience | | **Standard Reference** | ISO 9001, Six Sigma | Kano, SERVQUAL | ACSI, J.D. Power | ### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Customer Retention Rate Calculation $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Customer Retention Rate (CRR)} &= \frac{(C_E - C_N)}{C_S} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ **Example:** An automotive OEM started the year with $C_S = 12{,}000$ customers, ended with $C_E = 14{,}500$ customers, and acquired $C_N = 2{,}800$ new customers during the year. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CRR} &= \frac{(14{,}500 - 2{,}800)}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= \frac{11{,}700}{12{,}000} \times 100 \\[6pt] &= 97.5\% \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** The OEM retained **97.5%** of its existing customer base — an excellent benchmark in the automotive industry (typical: 85–90%). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Customer Perception Formation Process ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Pre-Purchase Inputs
WOM, Ads, Brand, Price, Culture"] --> nodeB["Customer Expectation E"] nodeC["Product Service Encounter
Moment of Truth"] --> nodeD["Perceived Performance P"] nodeB --> nodeE{"Comparison
E vs P"} nodeD --> nodeE nodeE -->|P greater than E| nodeF["Customer Delight
Positive Gap"] nodeE -->|P equals E| nodeG["Customer Satisfaction
Zero Gap"] nodeE -->|P less than E| nodeH["Customer Dissatisfaction
Negative Gap"] nodeF --> nodeI["Customer Loyalty
Advocacy NPS up"] nodeG --> nodeJ["Repeat Purchase
Moderate Loyalty"] nodeH --> nodeK["Complaints Churn
NPS down"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Kano Model Classification Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Customer Need Identified"] --> nodeB{"Functional
and
Dysfunctional
Responses"} nodeB -->|Both Like| nodeC["Q Questionable"] nodeB -->|Like plus Dislike| nodeD["O One-Dimensional"] nodeB -->|Must-be plus Dislike| nodeE["M Must-Be Basic"] nodeB -->|Like plus Live-with| nodeF["A Attractive Exciter"] nodeB -->|Neutral plus Neutral| nodeG["I Indifferent"] nodeB -->|Must-be plus Like| nodeH["R Reverse"] nodeD --> nodeI["Linear Satisfaction Gain"] nodeE --> nodeJ["Threshold Quality Mandatory"] nodeF --> nodeK["Differentiator Delighter"] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: SERVQUAL Five Gap Model ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nodeA["Service Quality Ecosystem"] nodeA1["Customer Expectation"] -->|Gap 1 Knowledge| nodeA2["Management Perception"] nodeA2 -->|Gap 2 Standards| nodeA3["Service Quality Specifications"] nodeA3 -->|Gap 3 Delivery| nodeA4["Service Delivery Actual"] nodeA1 -->|Gap 5 Final Outcome| nodeA5["Perceived Service"] nodeA4 -->|Gap 4 Communication| nodeA6["External Communications"] nodeA6 -->|Gap 5 Final Outcome| nodeA5 end ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Determinants of Customer Perception ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nodeA["Pre-Purchase Determinants"] nodeA1["Word of Mouth"] nodeA2["Advertising"] nodeA3["Brand Reputation"] nodeA4["Price Signal"] nodeA5["Cultural Factors"] end subgraph nodeB["Post-Purchase Determinants"] nodeB1["Functional Performance"] nodeB2["Reliability"] nodeB3["Serviceability"] nodeB4["Aesthetics"] nodeB5["Conformance"] end nodeA --> nodeC["Customer Perception QP"] nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD["Customer Satisfaction Index CSAT"] nodeC --> nodeE["Net Promoter Score NPS"] nodeC --> nodeF["Loyalty and Retention"] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Voice of Customer (VOC) to CTQ Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Voice of Customer
Raw Unstructured Input"] --> nodeB["VOC Capture
Surveys, Interviews, Focus Groups"] nodeB --> nodeC["Affinity Diagram
Group Similar Statements"] nodeC --> nodeD["Kano Classification
A, O, M, I Categories"] nodeD --> nodeE["CTQ Tree
Critical to Quality Drivers"] nodeE --> nodeF["CTQ Flowdown
Measurable KPIs"] nodeF --> nodeG["Six Sigma DMAIC Project
Y=fX Inputs"] nodeG --> nodeH["Improved Customer Perception
Higher CSAT, Higher NPS"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Usage in KTU Exams:** Including 1–2 clean Mermaid-style block diagrams in your answer sheet (drawn manually) typically adds **2–3 marks** of impression points with KTU examiners. Always label arrows clearly and include the expected/perceived contrast. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define customer perception of quality. List any four factors that influence it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Standard):** **Definition (1 Mark):** Customer perception of quality is the customer's overall impression or subjective assessment of a product or service based on their individual needs, expectations, prior experiences, and the quality attributes they consider important. **Four Influencing Factors (½ Mark Each = 2 Marks):** 1. **Word-of-mouth** — recommendations from trusted sources. 2. **Brand image and reputation** — built over time through consistent performance. 3. **Price as a quality signal** — high price often implies high quality. 4. **Past experience** — repeat purchases reinforce positive/negative perception. 5. *(Optional extras)* Advertising, cultural background, personal preferences. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between customer satisfaction and customer perception of quality.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Customer Perception of Quality | Customer Satisfaction | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Overall impression/judgement of quality dimensions | Feeling of pleasure/displeasure from a specific transaction | | **Scope** | Broader, cumulative, long-term | Narrow, transactional, short-term | | **Time Frame** | Formed over time and across touchpoints | Measured immediately after purchase/use | | **Measurement** | Kano model, SERVQUAL, focus groups | CSAT score, J.D. Power ratings | | **Relationship** | An *input* to satisfaction | An *output* of perception meeting expectations | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write *one-line* answers for 3-mark questions. KTU valuation expects **at least 3–4 valid differentiation points** for full marks. Use a comparison table or bullet points — never write a single paragraph. --- ### 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Students must answer **ONE** question out of two. Both questions carry 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the Kano Model of customer satisfaction with a suitable diagram. Differentiate between the categories of features it identifies. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 Mark):** The Kano Model, developed by Professor Noriaki Kano of Tokyo Rika University in 1984, is a framework for categorizing customer preferences based on how features influence satisfaction. **Five Categories (1 Mark Each = 5 Marks):** 1. **Must-Be (Basic) Quality:** Features that customers expect as a given. Absence causes extreme dissatisfaction, but presence does not increase satisfaction. *Example:* Functional brakes in a car. 2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Quality:** Features where satisfaction is linearly proportional to performance. *Example:* Fuel efficiency in km/litre. 3. **Attractive (Exciter) Quality:** Features that delight customers when present but don't cause dissatisfaction when absent. *Example:* Heated seats in an entry-level sedan. 4. **Indifferent Quality:** Features that customers don't care about regardless of presence. *Example:* A specific font in the user manual. 5. **Reverse Quality:** Features whose presence actually dissatisfies certain customer segments. *Example:* Auto-play video ads on a website. **Satisfaction Curve Diagram (1 Mark — Must Include):** Draw two axes: X = Degree of feature implementation, Y = Customer satisfaction. Plot: - Must-be: Horizontal line until absent → drops sharply. - One-dimensional: Linear diagonal upward. - Attractive: Steep upward curve. - Indifferent: Flat horizontal line. **(b) What is SERVQUAL? Explain its five dimensions and the gap model for service quality. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 Mark):** SERVQUAL (Service Quality) is a 22-item instrument developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985) that measures service quality across five dimensions. **Five Dimensions (1 Mark Each = 5 Marks):** 1. **Tangibles:** Physical evidence — facilities, equipment, personnel, communication materials. 2. **Reliability:** Ability to perform the promised service consistently and accurately. 3. **Responsiveness:** Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. 4. **Assurance:** Employees' knowledge, courtesy, and ability to inspire trust. 5. **Empathy:** Caring, individualized attention given to customers. **Five-Gap Model (1 Mark):** - **Gap 1:** Customer expectations vs management perceptions. - **Gap 2:** Management perceptions vs service quality specifications. - **Gap 3:** Service specifications vs service delivery. - **Gap 4:** Service delivery vs external communications. - **Gap 5:** Expected service vs perceived service (the final outcome gap). $$ \text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1, Gap 2, Gap 3, Gap 4}) $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write SERVQUAL dimensions *without* the Gap Model, losing 1–2 marks. Conversely, students describe the gaps but forget to define each dimension. KTU requires **both** for the full 7 marks in part (b). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the concept of customer perception of quality. Discuss the pre-purchase and post-purchase determinants that influence it. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 Marks):** Customer perception of quality is the subjective cognitive evaluation by which a customer judges the overall excellence of a product or service. It is the *outcome* of comparing expected quality with experienced quality and is influenced by psychological, cultural, and contextual factors. **Pre-Purchase Determinants (2.5 Marks):** 1. **Word-of-Mouth:** Recommendations from friends, family, online reviews. 2. **Advertising and Promotion:** Brand communications that set initial expectations. 3. **Brand Reputation:** Historical trust and image. 4. **Price as a Quality Signal:** Premium pricing often infers superior quality. 5. **Cultural and Demographic Factors:** Values, age, income, education, lifestyle. **Post-Purchase Determinants (2.5 Marks):** 1. **Functional Performance:** Ability to deliver core benefits. 2. **Reliability and Durability:** Consistent performance over time. 3. **Serviceability:** Ease of repair, maintenance, and after-sales support. 4. **Aesthetics and Feel:** Visual appeal, packaging, ergonomic design. 5. **Conformance to Standards:** Adherence to certifications and quality norms. **(b) A manufacturing firm surveyed 800 customers. The results were: Promoters = 480, Passives = 200, Detractors = 120. Calculate the NPS and interpret the result. Discuss how customer perception of quality can be measured in a Six Sigma project using the DMAIC framework. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: NPS Calculation (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} \%\,\text{Promoters} &= \frac{480}{800} \times 100 = 60\% \\[6pt] \%\,\text{Detractors} &= \frac{120}{800} \times 100 = 15\% \\[6pt] \text{NPS} &= 60\% - 15\% = 45 \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation (1 Mark):** An NPS of **45** is considered *very good* for a manufacturing firm. The firm has strong customer advocacy but should convert the 200 Passives (25%) into Promoters through targeted improvement projects. **DMAIC Application to Customer Perception (3 Marks):** | DMAIC Phase | Customer Perception Activity | |---|---| | **Define** | Capture VOC (Voice of Customer) via surveys, complaints, focus groups; identify CTQs | | **Measure** | Calculate CSAT, NPS, CES; establish baseline using SERVQUAL scores | | **Analyze** | Use Pareto, fishbone, and Kano analysis to identify root causes of perception gaps | | **Improve** | Pilot solutions targeting the largest gaps; redesign service touchpoints | | **Control** | Implement SPC on CTQs; monitor CSAT/NPS dashboards; sustain gains | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently skip the **interpretation step** after calculating NPS. KTU valuation specifically deducts **1 mark** if the result is not interpreted in the context of the industry. Always state *what the value means* and *what action should follow*. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Notes for Customer Perception of Quality:** - **Definition:** Subjective customer judgement based on expectations vs. performance. - **Garvin's 8 Dimensions:** Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality. - **Kano Model:** Must-be (Basic), One-Dimensional (Performance), Attractive (Exciter), Indifferent, Reverse. - **SERVQUAL Dimensions:** Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy. - **Gap Model:** 5 Gaps — Gap 5 = f(Gap 1, Gap 2, Gap 3, Gap 4). - **Customer Satisfaction Formula:** $CSAT = \frac{\text{Satisfied}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100$. - **NPS Formula:** $NPS = \%\,\text{Promoters} - \%\,\text{Detractors}$ (Range: −100 to +100). - **Customer Retention Rate:** $CRR = \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \times 100$. - **Moment of Truth (MOT):** The instant the customer forms a perception. - **VOC → CTQ Flowdown:** Raw feedback → Affinity diagram → Kano → CTQ tree → KPIs. - **DMAIC Integration:** Customer perception metrics (CSAT, NPS, SERVQUAL) are critical inputs in *Define* and *Measure* phases. - **ACSI Index:** Weighted average of expectations, perceived quality, perceived value, loyalty, and complaints (0–100 scale). - **Quality vs Perception vs Satisfaction:** Quality is *objective*; Perception is *cumulative subjective*; Satisfaction is *transactional subjective*. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always structure long answers using **headings + tables + diagrams + equations + interpretation**. Examiners scan for these five elements and award impression marks generously when present. Answer length target: 4–6 pages per 14-mark question, 1 page per 3-mark question. ---

topics.title: Customer Retention
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Customer Retention

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Customer Retention (CR)** is a strategic process in Total Quality Management (TQM) that focuses on keeping existing customers engaged, satisfied, and loyal to a brand or organization over an extended period, thereby minimizing customer defection (churn) and maximizing long-term profitability. As defined by **Philip Kotler** and integrated into the **Six Sigma DMAIC framework**, customer retention is the operational outcome of consistent delivery of value that meets or exceeds customer expectations across the entire customer journey.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education)**, Customer Retention is studied as a critical **Voice of Customer (VOC)** output measure, an outcome of the **SERVQUAL** model, and a key **Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** parameter in the Six Sigma **Define** and **Measure** phases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Ready):** Customer Retention is the set of activities an organization undertakes to reduce customer defections and increase the longevity of the customer-brand relationship, measured through the **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** and **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of Customer Retention like a **healthy long-term marriage** versus a series of **one-night flings**:

- A **one-night fling** (no retention) is like a coffee shop where you stop by once, find the coffee mediocre, and never return. The shop has to constantly find new customers just to survive.
- A **healthy marriage** (high retention) is like a trusted family doctor who knows your history, anticipates your needs, and earns your loyalty for decades. You don't even consider switching — and you refer friends.

In engineering-business terms, **acquiring a new customer is 5x to 7x more expensive** than retaining an existing one (the **1:5 Rule of Retention**, attributed to **Bain & Company**). This is why Total Quality Management places customer retention at the heart of profitability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy:** A telecom company like **Airtel** or **Jio** offering a "Loyalty Bonus" for 5+ year subscribers is practicing customer retention. They are reducing churn (subscriber defection to competitors) by rewarding longevity.

## 1.3 Key Industry Metrics Introduced

The following standard metrics (must be memorized in **bold** for KTU exams):

- **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** — % of customers retained over a period.
- **Customer Churn Rate** — % of customers lost (defected) over a period.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV / CLTV)** — Total revenue a customer generates over their lifetime.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** — Likelihood of customers to recommend the brand.
- **Customer Loyalty Index (CLI)** — Composite score combining retention, advocacy, and purchase frequency.

## 1.4 Why Customer Retention Matters in TQM

TQM philosophy, championed by **Deming, Juran, and Ishikawa**, rests on the principle that quality is defined by the customer. Retention is the **ultimate proof of perceived quality** — because no customer stays with a poor-quality product or service voluntarily. The **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** in CRM states that **80% of a company's revenue comes from 20% of its repeat (retained) customers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Customer Retention is directly linked to the **SERVQUAL** model's five dimensions (Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy) and to the **Loyalty Ladder** framework (Suspect → Prospect → Customer → Client → Supporter → Advocate → Partner).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Retention Equation

Customer Retention is governed by a multi-factor equation. From a TQM lens, it can be decomposed into:

$$
\text{Customer Retention} = f(\text{Satisfaction}, \text{Service Quality}, \text{Trust}, \text{Value}, \text{Engagement})
$$

### 2.1.1 The Five Pillars of Customer Retention

| Pillar | Explanation | TQM Linkage |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Customer Satisfaction (CSAT)** | The degree to which a product/service meets customer expectations. | Output of **SERVQUAL** & Kano Model. |
| **2. Perceived Value** | Customer's assessment of benefits vs. costs paid. | Link to **CTQ (Critical-to-Quality)** trees in Six Sigma. |
| **3. Trust** | Confidence in the brand's reliability and integrity. | Built through **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) consistency. |
| **4. Switching Costs** | Economic, emotional, or technical barriers to leaving. | Increased by **loyalty programs** and customization. |
| **5. Emotional Engagement** | Emotional bond beyond transactional relationship. | Goal of **Relationship Marketing**. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Works (Logic Step-by-Step):**
> - **Step 1:** A customer experiences a service encounter.
> - **Step 2:** Quality gaps are measured via SERVQUAL.
> - **Step 3:** Gaps are closed via DMAIC improvements.
> - **Step 4:** Satisfaction increases → Trust builds → Loyalty forms → Retention happens.

## 2.2 The Loyalty Ladder (Loyalty Pyramid) — KTU High-Weightage Concept

This is a six-stage progression of customer relationship depth. Each stage reduces churn and increases CLV.

| Stage | Customer Type | Behavior | Marketing Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Suspect** | Potential customer, not yet contacted. | Unaware of brand. | Awareness campaigns. |
| **2. Prospect** | Has shown interest. | Considering purchase. | Lead nurturing. |
| **3. Customer** | Has made first purchase. | Transactional. | Onboarding, satisfaction. |
| **4. Client** | Regular repeat buyer. | Habitual purchasing. | Loyalty programs. |
| **5. Supporter** | Actively recommends brand. | Word-of-mouth advocacy. | Referral incentives. |
| **6. Advocate / Partner** | Brand ambassador, co-creator. | Defends brand publicly. | Co-creation, communities. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $CRR = \left( \dfrac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \right) \times 100$ | $C_E$ = Customers at end, $C_N$ = New customers acquired, $C_S$ = Customers at start | **%** |
| 2 | **Customer Churn Rate (CCR)** | $CCR = 1 - CRR$ (or $100\% - CRR$) | Same as above | **%** |
| 3 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{(R_t - C_t) \cdot r_t}{(1 + d)^t}$ | $R_t$ = Revenue, $C_t$ = Cost, $r_t$ = Retention rate, $d$ = Discount rate | **Currency (₹, $)** |
| 4 | **CLV (Simplified)** | $CLV = AOV \times PF \times CG \times AL$ | $AOV$ = Avg Order Value, $PF$ = Purchase Freq, $CG$ = Customer Gross Margin, $AL$ = Avg Lifespan (years) | **Currency** |
| 5 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%P - \%D$ | $P$ = Promoters (9-10), $D$ = Detractors (0-6) on 0-10 scale | **Score (-100 to +100)** |
| 6 | **Customer Loyalty Index (CLI)** | $CLI = \dfrac{NPS + CRR}{2}$ | Combined metric | **% (approx)** |
| 7 | **Customer Profitability Score** | $CPS = CLV - CAC$ | $CAC$ = Customer Acquisition Cost | **Currency** |
| 8 | **Repeat Purchase Ratio (RPR)** | $RPR = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Customers}}{\text{Total Customers}} \times 100$ | Direct retention proxy | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - CRR always lies in $[0\%, 100\%]$.
> - NPS lies in $[-100, +100]$.
> - CLV is always positive for a viable business; negative CLV implies unsustainability.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Telecom Industry:** Vodafone Idea uses CLV to decide which prepaid customers deserve expensive retention offers.
- **Automotive (Six Sigma Case Study):** Toyota's **"Kaizen"** philosophy keeps existing customers loyal for decades through continuous quality improvement.
- **IT Services (Indian Context):** TCS and Infosys measure NPS for Fortune 500 client retention.
- **Manufacturing (KTU Industrial Context):** Kerala-based spice exporters use CRR to manage B2B relationships with global buyers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Mapping:** This topic addresses **CO2** (Apply TQM tools for customer-focused process improvement) and **CO3** (Analyze Six Sigma DMAIC phases).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Customer Retention Rate (CRR) — Worked Numerical

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A mobile recharge company had **5,000 active subscribers** at the start of Q1 2024. During the quarter, they acquired **1,200 new subscribers**. At the end of Q1, the total active subscriber count was **5,800**. Calculate: (a) CRR, (b) Churn Rate, (c) Interpret the result.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the given variables from the problem statement.**

$$
C_S = 5000 \text{ customers (start)}, \quad C_N = 1200 \text{ customers (new)}, \quad C_E = 5800 \text{ customers (end)}
$$

**Step 2: Recall the canonical CRR formula (avoid the vertical pipe symbol inside tables; using inline form here).**

$$
CRR = \left( \frac{C_E - C_N}{C_S} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the values into the numerator.**

$$
C_E - C_N = 5800 - 1200 = 4600 \text{ customers}
$$

> **Logic of this step:** We subtract new customers from the end total because new acquisitions did not exist at the start — they cannot be "retained" from the baseline. We only count those who *survived* from the start.

**Step 4: Divide by starting customers and multiply by 100 to express as a percentage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CRR &= \left( \frac{4600}{5000} \right) \times 100 \\
    &= 0.92 \times 100 \\
    &= 92\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the Churn Rate as the complement.**

$$
CCR = 100\% - 92\% = 8\%
$$

**Step 6: Interpretation (critical for full marks in KTU).**

> **Answer:** The company retained **92%** of its existing customers and lost **8%** to competitors. According to industry benchmarks (Bain & Co.), a CRR above **85%** is considered excellent for subscription businesses. This indicates strong TQM implementation. A churn rate of 8%, however, translates to 400 lost customers per quarter, prompting Six Sigma **DMAIC** root-cause analysis to plug the leakage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — Simplified

> **KTU-Style Problem:** An online D2C brand reports the following annual data: **Average Order Value (AOV) = ₹1,500**, **Purchase Frequency (PF) = 4 times/year**, **Customer Gross Margin (CG) = 35%**, **Average Customer Lifespan (AL) = 5 years**. Calculate the simplified CLV.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: State the simplified CLV formula.**

$$
CLV = AOV \times PF \times CG \times AL
$$

**Step 2: Compute annual customer revenue.**

$$
AOV \times PF = 1500 \times 4 = \text{₹}6{,}000 \text{ per customer per year}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the gross margin (35%) to convert revenue to profit contribution.**

$$
6000 \times 0.35 = \text{₹}2{,}100 \text{ per customer per year}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by the average customer lifespan (5 years) to obtain lifetime contribution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= 2100 \times 5 \\
    &= \text{₹}10{,}500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

> **Answer:** Each customer is worth **₹10,500** in lifetime profit contribution. If **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) > ₹10,500**, the business is unprofitable. The **CLV : CAC ratio** should ideally be **≥ 3:1** for sustainable growth.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cause-Effect of Customer Defection

This is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question format. Below is the structured analytical matrix linking TQM tools to retention levers.

| Root Cause of Defection | TQM Tool Used | Six Sigma Phase | Expected Impact on CRR |
|---|---|---|---|
| Poor product quality | **Pareto Chart, Ishikawa Diagram** | Measure, Analyze | +5% to +15% |
| Slow service delivery | **SERVQUAL, Process Mapping** | Analyze, Improve | +10% to +20% |
| High price perception | **Value Stream Mapping** | Improve | +8% to +12% |
| Lack of personalization | **Voice of Customer (VOC) analysis** | Define, Measure | +15% to +25% |
| Inconsistent communication | **Control Charts, CRM dashboards** | Control | +5% to +10% |
| Competitor innovation | **Benchmarking, QFD (House of Quality)** | Improve | +10% to +18% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked "How does TQM improve customer retention?", always anchor your answer in **Deming's PDCA cycle** and **Juran's Quality Trilogy** (Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement). Linking back to gurus earns full marks.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Acquisition vs. Retention

| Parameter | Customer Acquisition | Customer Retention |
|---|---|---|
| **Cost** | High (5x–7x retention cost) | Low (1x baseline) |
| **Time to ROI** | 12–18 months typical | 3–6 months typical |
| **Risk** | Uncertain conversion | Higher predictability |
| **Marketing Focus** | Awareness, lead generation | Loyalty, advocacy |
| **TQM Tool** | VOC, Kano Model | SERVQUAL, QFD, CRM |
| **Six Sigma Phase** | Define, Measure | Improve, Control |
| **Key Metric** | CAC, Conversion Rate | CRR, CLV, NPS |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Customer Retention Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

This diagram illustrates the closed-loop process by which TQM/Six Sigma interventions feed back into customer retention outcomes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Customer Needs via VOC] --> B[Define CTQs in Six Sigma]
    B --> C[Measure Current CRR and Churn]
    C --> D[Analyze Root Causes of Defection]
    D --> E[Improve Processes via DMAIC]
    E --> F[Control via CRM Dashboards]
    F --> G[Customer Satisfaction Increases]
    G --> H[Trust and Loyalty Build]
    H --> I[Customer Retention Improves]
    I --> J[CLV and Profitability Rise]
    J --> K[Re-measure CRR for Continuous Improvement]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 The Loyalty Ladder (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram visualizes the six-stage progression of customer relationships that culminate in brand advocacy and retention.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1: Suspect] --> S2[Stage 2: Prospect]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Customer]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Client]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Supporter]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Advocate or Partner]
    S6 --> S7[Brand Champion: Maximum Retention]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: TQM-to-Retention Mapping

This functional flow maps TQM tools to the retention outcomes they produce, suitable for KTU diagram questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TQM_Toolset["TQM TOOLSET"]
        T1[Deming PDCA Cycle]
        T2[Juran Trilogy]
        T3[Ishikawa Fishbone]
        T4[Pareto Analysis]
        T5[SERVQUAL Model]
        T6[Kano Model]
    end
    subgraph Process_Layer["PROCESS IMPROVEMENT LAYER"]
        P1[Quality Planning]
        P2[Quality Control]
        P3[Quality Improvement]
    end
    subgraph Customer_Layer["CUSTOMER OUTCOME LAYER"]
        O1[Increased Satisfaction]
        O2[Enhanced Trust]
        O3[Improved Perceived Value]
    end
    subgraph Business_Layer["BUSINESS OUTCOME LAYER"]
        B1[Higher CRR]
        B2[Lower Churn]
        B3[Higher CLV]
        B4[Higher NPS]
    end
    T1 --> P1
    T2 --> P2
    T3 --> P3
    T4 --> P3
    T5 --> O1
    T6 --> O2
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O2
    P3 --> O3
    O1 --> B1
    O2 --> B2
    O3 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
```

## 4.4 Cause-and-Effect Schematic: Why Customers Defect (Ishikawa-Inspired)

```mermaid
fishbone-beta
    Defect[Customer Defection or Churn]
    Product[Product Issues: Poor quality, defects, no innovation]
    Price[Price Issues: Too high, no perceived value]
    Service[Service Issues: Slow response, rude staff]
    Process[Process Issues: Long waits, broken systems]
    People[People Issues: Untrained staff, high turnover]
    Policy[Policy Issues: Rigid return rules, no loyalty rewards]
    Defect --> Product
    Defect --> Price
    Defect --> Service
    Defect --> Process
    Defect --> People
    Defect --> Policy
```
