## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain the seven quality management principles of ISO 9001:2015.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The seven quality management principles (ISO 9000:2015) that form the foundation of ISO 9001:2015 are:

1. **Customer Focus** – The primary focus is to meet customer requirements and exceed expectations.
2. **Leadership** – Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and engage people in achieving quality objectives.
3. **Engagement of People** – Competent, empowered, and engaged people at all levels enhance organizational capability.
4. **Process Approach** – Consistent and predictable results are achieved more effectively when activities are managed as interrelated processes.
5. **Improvement** – Successful organizations have an ongoing focus on improvement as a permanent objective.
6. **Evidence-Based Decision Making** – Decisions based on the analysis and evaluation of data and information are more likely to produce desired results.
7. **Relationship Management** – For sustained success, organizations manage their relationships with interested parties such as suppliers.

> *(Each principle = 0.4 mark × 7 ≈ 3 marks; award marks for naming and explaining any 3-4 principles in depth if writing time is limited.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the "High-Level Structure (HLS)" of ISO standards? Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **High-Level Structure (HLS)**, also called **Annex SL**, is a common framework adopted by ISO for all management system standards (MSS), including ISO 9001, ISO 14001, ISO 45001, and ISO 27001.

It defines **10 core clauses** with standardized titles, sequence, and core text, allowing organizations to:
- **Integrate multiple management systems** (e.g., QMS + EMS) seamlessly.
- **Reduce duplication** of policies, procedures, and documentation.
- **Simplify audits** through a uniform audit approach.
- **Enhance organizational efficiency** in implementation.

> *(Definition 1 mark + Significance 2 marks = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Essay / Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain in detail the step-by-step implementation of ISO 9001:2015 in a manufacturing organization. Discuss the role of top management in this process.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The implementation of ISO 9001:2015 in a manufacturing organization follows a structured 12-phase approach:

**Step 1: Top Management Commitment (Clause 5.1)** *(1 mark)*
Top management demonstrates leadership and commitment by:
- Establishing a **Quality Policy** aligned with the organization's strategic direction.
- Appointing a **Management Representative (MR)**.
- Allocating adequate **resources** (people, infrastructure, budget).
- Communicating the importance of customer focus and QMS conformance.

**Step 2: QMS Scope Definition and Context Analysis (Clauses 4.1–4.3)** *(1 mark)*
- Internal and external issues are identified using **SWOT** and **PESTLE** analysis.
- Needs of **interested parties** (customers, suppliers, regulators) are determined.
- The **QMS scope** is documented and made available.

**Step 3: Gap Analysis** *(1 mark)*
- Current state is benchmarked against ISO 9001:2015 requirements.
- A **Gap Analysis Report** highlights non-conformities and prioritization based on **risk**.

**Step 4: Process Mapping (Clause 4.4)** *(1 mark)*
- Core processes (e.g., production, quality control, dispatch) and support processes (HR, procurement) are identified.
- **SIPOC** diagrams and **Turtle Diagrams** are prepared.
- Process owners, KPIs, and interaction maps are documented.

**Step 5: Risk-Based Thinking and Planning (Clause 6.1)** *(1 mark)*
- Risks and opportunities are assessed.
- **FMEA** is used; **RPN = S × O × D** is calculated for failure modes.
- Risk treatment and contingency actions are documented.

**Step 6: Documentation and Implementation (Clauses 7.5, 8)** *(1 mark)*
- The **Quality Manual, Procedures, SOPs, Work Instructions, Records** are developed.
- Operations are executed as per documented plans; nonconforming outputs are controlled.

**Step 7: Performance Evaluation and Certification (Clauses 9, 10)** *(1 mark)*
- Internal audits, management reviews, and customer satisfaction surveys are conducted.
- **Stage 1** (documentation) and **Stage 2** (on-site) certification audits are performed by an accredited body.
- Continual improvement is achieved through the **PDCA cycle**.

**Role of Top Management** *(cross-cutting 0 marks distributed, but emphasize):*
Without **leadership and commitment (Clause 5.1)**, the QMS fails. Top management must ensure integration of QMS into business processes, promote risk-based thinking, and champion a quality culture.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Discuss the documentation requirements under ISO 9001:2015. What is "Documented Information" and how does the new version differ from the 2008 version in terms of documentation?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Documented Information** is information that an organization is required to **control and maintain** (Clause 7.5). It can be in any format (paper, digital, video) and from any source. *(1 mark)*

**Mandatory Documented Information (ISO 9001:2015):**

| Sl. | Document | Clause |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | QMS Scope | 4.3 |
| 2 | Quality Policy | 5.2 |
| 3 | Quality Objectives | 6.2 |
| 4 | Criteria for Evaluation & Selection of External Providers | 8.4.1 |
| 5 | Nature & Extent of Documented Information for Production/Service Provision | 8.5.1 |
| 6 | Records of Customer Communication | 8.2.1 |
| 7 | Design & Development Records | 8.3 |
| 8 | Calibration Records | 7.1.5 |
| 9 | Internal Audit Programme & Results | 9.2 |
| 10 | Management Review Results | 9.3 |
| 11 | Nature of Nonconformities, Actions Taken, Results of Corrective Action | 10.2 |

*(2 marks for listing)*

**Differences from ISO 9001:2008:** *(2 marks)*

| Aspect | ISO 9001:2008 | ISO 9001:2015 |
|---|---|---|
| Terminology | "Documents" and "Records" | **"Documented Information"** (unified) |
| Quality Manual | **Mandatory** | **Not mandatory** (optional) |
| Documented Procedures | **6 mandatory procedures** (Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, Nonconformity, Corrective Action, Preventive Action) | **No mandatory procedures** — organization determines |
| Preventive Action | **Separate clause** | Integrated into **Risk-Based Thinking** |
| Focus | Compliance with documented system | **Effectiveness and outcomes** |

**Documentation Hierarchy (4-Tier Pyramid):** *(1 mark)*
- Tier 1: Quality Manual
- Tier 2: Procedures & SOPs
- Tier 3: Work Instructions
- Tier 4: Records & Forms

**Conclusion:** *(1 mark)*
ISO 9001:2015 has shifted from a **"compliance"** mindset to a **"performance and effectiveness"** mindset, giving organizations greater flexibility while demanding stronger leadership and risk-based decision-making.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the concept of "Risk-Based Thinking" in ISO 9001:2015. How does it differ from Preventive Action in ISO 9001:2008? Discuss the role of FMEA in risk management.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Risk-Based Thinking (RBT)** *(2 marks)*
Risk-Based Thinking is a **proactive and integrated approach** to identifying, assessing, and treating risks and opportunities that could affect the QMS. It is embedded throughout ISO 9001:2015 (especially in Clauses 4.1, 6.1, 8.1, 9.1, 10.1).

It is **not a separate clause** but a **mindset** that drives planning, decision-making, and continual improvement. The standard requires the organization to:
- Determine risks and opportunities (Clause 6.1.1)
- Plan actions to address them (Clause 6.1.2)
- Integrate and implement these actions into QMS processes (Clause 6.1.3)
- Evaluate the effectiveness of these actions (Clause 9.3)

**Difference from Preventive Action (ISO 9001:2008):** *(2 marks)*

| Aspect | ISO 9001:2008 (Preventive Action) | ISO 9001:2015 (Risk-Based Thinking) |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | **Reactive** — action taken after a potential issue is identified | **Proactive** — risks anticipated and treated in advance |
| Documentation | Required **separate procedure** | **No separate procedure**; integrated into processes |
| Scope | Focused on individual nonconformities | Covers **strategic, operational, and project-level risks** |
| Tools | Limited to root cause analysis | Uses **FMEA, SWOT, PESTLE, Brainstorming, Bow-Tie Analysis** |

**Role of FMEA in Risk Management:** *(3 marks)*

**FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** is a systematic tool to:
1. **Identify potential failure modes** of a process or product.
2. **Assess the Severity (S), Occurrence (O), and Detection (D)** of each failure mode.
3. **Calculate Risk Priority Number:**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

4. **Prioritize actions** — typically failure modes with **RPN > 100** are considered critical.
5. **Implement corrective/preventive actions** to reduce RPN.
6. **Review and re-evaluate** periodically.

> **Example**: In a CNC machining process, a potential failure mode "incorrect tool selection" may be rated as $S = 8$, $O = 5$, $D = 4$, giving $\text{RPN} = 160$ — a critical risk requiring a tool-management SOP and operator training.

**Conclusion:** Risk-Based Thinking replaces Preventive Action with a **strategic, integrated, and proactive risk management culture** that drives organizational resilience.

---

**Q. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Process Approach in ISO 9001:2015? Explain with the help of a PDCA cycle. Describe the contents of an internal audit as per ISO 19011.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Process Approach (Clause 4.4)** *(2 marks)*
The **Process Approach** is the application of a system of interrelated processes within an organization, with the goal of enhancing customer satisfaction and effectiveness.

Each process must have:
- **Inputs and Outputs** defined
- **Sequence and Interaction** mapped
- **Responsibility and Authority** assigned (Process Owner)
- **Risks and Opportunities** identified
- **Performance Indicators (KPIs)** established
- **Review and Improvement** mechanisms

**PDCA Cycle Mapped to ISO 9001:** *(2 marks)*

| PDCA Stage | ISO 9001 Clauses | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | 4, 5, 6, 7 | Context, leadership, planning, support |
| **Do** | 8 | Operation and process execution |
| **Check** | 9 | Monitoring, measurement, audits, MR |
| **Act** | 10 | Improvement and corrective action |

**Internal Audit Process (ISO 19011:2018):** *(3 marks)*

| Step | Activity |
|---|---|
| **1. Initiate Audit** | Define audit objectives, scope, criteria |
| **2. Prepare Audit** | Select audit team, prepare audit plan, assign tasks |
| **3. Conduct Audit** | Opening meeting → collect evidence (documents, interviews, observations) → record findings |
| **4. Prepare Audit Report** | Document findings (NCs, Observations, OFI) and conclusions |
| **5. Complete Audit** | Closing meeting, distribute report, follow-up on actions |
| **6. Audit Follow-up** | Verify effectiveness of corrective actions taken for NCs |

> **Terminology**:
> - **NC (Major)** – Absence of a required clause or systemic failure
> - **NC (Minor)** – Isolated lapse
> - **OFI (Opportunity for Improvement)** – Suggestion
> - **Observation** – Cautionary note

**Frequency:** At least **once per year**, covering all clauses over a 3-year cycle.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating ISO 9001:2008 with 2015** — Examiners explicitly test version differences. Writing 6 mandatory procedures (2008) in a 2015 answer = 1 mark deduction.
> 2. **Omitting clause numbers** — Always cite the **clause reference** (e.g., Clause 7.2) when answering. A correct answer without clause mapping = 0.5–1 mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting Risk-Based Thinking** — In any "Implementation" question, examiners expect explicit mention of **RBT and FMEA**. Omitting these = 2-mark loss in 7-mark sub-questions.
> 4. **Mermaid-rendered diagrams on answer sheets** — Since KTU answer sheets are paper-based, you **must draw the diagram manually**. Print/copy Mermaid diagrams and **hand-redraw** with a neat layout (boxes, arrows, labels). Credit is given for clarity.
> 5. **Misunderstanding "Documented Information"** — Many students write "documentation" and "documented information" interchangeably. They are **not the same**. The 2015 standard uses **Documented Information** as a unified term.
> 6. **Skipping PDCA mapping** — Always map the answer to **PDCA stages** for full marks on implementation questions.
> 7. **Wrong DPMO formula syntax** — Use $\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$ notation, not `DPMO = (D*10^6)/(N*O)` style.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (ISO 9001 Implementation)**

#### ✅ Core Knowledge Points

- [ ] **ISO 9001:2015** is the current version; 10 clauses (Annex SL HLS).
- [ ] Clauses **1–3** are informational; **4–10** are auditable.
- [ ] **Seven Quality Management Principles** (foundation): Customer Focus, Leadership, Engagement, Process Approach, Improvement, Evidence-Based Decision Making, Relationship Management.
- [ ] **PDCA cycle** is the structural backbone of ISO 9001.
- [ ] **Risk-Based Thinking** replaces Preventive Action.
- [ ] **"Documented Information"** replaces "Documents" and "Records" — no mandatory Quality Manual, no mandatory procedures.
- [ ] **Context of the Organization** (Clause 4) = Internal/External issues + Interested parties + Scope.
- [ ] **Top Management** (Clause 5) must demonstrate leadership, commitment, customer focus, and policy.
- [ ] **Risk Assessment** uses FMEA: $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$.
- [ ] **SIPOC** is the key process-mapping tool.
- [ ] **Internal audits** at least annually; **Management Review** every 6–12 months.
- [ ] Certification audit = **Stage 1 (document review) + Stage 2 (on-site)**, valid for **3 years** with annual surveillance audits.

#### ✅ High-Yield Keywords (Use These in Answers)

- Annex SL, High-Level Structure, Process Approach, PDCA
- Risk-Based Thinking, RPN, FMEA, DPMO, COPQ
- Management Representative (MR), Quality Policy, Quality Objectives
- SIPOC, Turtle Diagram, Documented Information, RACI Matrix
- ISO 19011 (Audit Guidelines), ISO 9000 (QMS Fundamentals)

#### ✅ Frequently Asked 14-Mark Question Types

1. "Explain the step-by-step implementation of ISO 9001:2015."
2. "Discuss Risk-Based Thinking with reference to FMEA."
3. "Compare ISO 9001:2008 vs 2015 with respect to documentation."
4. "Explain Process Approach and PDCA cycle in ISO 9001:2015."
5. "What are the documentation requirements under ISO 9001:2015?"

#### ✅ Formula Quick-Recall

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}, \quad C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}, \quad \text{Six Sigma} = 3.4 \text{ DPMO}, \quad C_{pk} = 2.0$$

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D, \quad \text{COPQ} = P + A + I + E$$

> 🎯 **Final Tip**: For KTU 14-mark answers, structure your response as **Introduction → PDCA-Stage-wise Discussion → Tools (FMEA, SIPOC) → Documentation → Audit & Certification → Conclusion**. This is the **scoring template** examiners look for.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Documentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Documentation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quality Documentation** is the systematic, structured, and controlled creation, storage, distribution, retrieval, and archival of all written or electronic information that defines, guides, evidences, and sustains an organization's **Quality Management System (QMS)**. It serves as the **organizational memory** of the enterprise, ensuring consistency, traceability, accountability, and continuous improvement in all processes. In the TQM and Six Sigma paradigm, documentation is not paperwork for its own sake — it is the **institutionalized voice of quality**. The ISO 9000 family of standards and the DMAIC (Define–Measure–Analyze–Improve–Control) methodology of Six Sigma both rely fundamentally on robust documentation to function effectively. ### 1.2 Intuitive Overview (Real-World Analogy) > [!NOTE] > **Think of Documentation as the "Instruction Manual of a Spacecraft."** > Imagine a multinational space agency sending a probe to Mars. Every single wire, bolt, calibration value, software command, and emergency protocol must be written down in a structured, retrievable format. The probe cannot "ask a colleague" what to do when an anomaly occurs in deep space. It must rely on the **documents** uploaded before launch. > > Similarly, in an organization, employees join, leave, shift, forget, and rotate. Documentation ensures that the **collective intelligence of the organization** survives individual turnover, prevents "tribal knowledge" losses, and guarantees that a process executed in Kochi produces the same outcome as the same process executed in Tokyo. ### 1.3 The Four Pillars of TQM Documentation Every QMS documentation structure rests on **four distinct hierarchical levels**: | Pillar | Document Type | Question It Answers | Audience | |---|---|---|---| | **Level 1** | **Quality Manual** | *What do we do and why?* | Top Management, External Auditors, Customers | | **Level 2** | **Quality Procedures (SOPs)** | *Who does what, when, and how?* | Process Owners, Middle Management | | **Level 3** | **Work Instructions** | *How exactly is each step performed?* | Shop-floor Operators, Frontline Staff | | **Level 4** | **Quality Records / Forms** | *What was actually done and what was the result?* | Auditors, Quality Inspectors, Legal | ### 1.4 The KTU Highlight > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus Areas:** > (a) Purpose, scope, and benefits of documentation > (b) Types of quality documents > (c) Document control and life-cycle management > (d) Documentation requirements under ISO 9001:2015 > (e) Six Sigma project documentation (Charter, SIPOC, etc.) > (f) Common pitfalls in documentation --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Documentation Pyramid (Detailed Breakdown) > [!NOTE] > The classical documentation pyramid in TQM is often visualized as a **four-tier structure**, with policy at the top (least detail, broadest scope) and records at the bottom (maximum detail, narrowest scope). Each tier must be **traceable upward** to the tier above it and **verifiable downward** through the tier below. **Level 1 – Quality Manual (The Constitution)** - The topmost policy document of the QMS. - States the **Quality Policy**, **Quality Objectives**, and the **scope of the QMS**. - Describes the **interaction between processes** (often shown as a process map). - Usually a single bound document of 20–50 pages. - Mandatory under **ISO 9001:2015 Clause 4**. **Level 2 – Quality Procedures / Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** - Document the **"what, who, when, where, and how"** of each major process. - One SOP per process (e.g., "SOP for Incoming Material Inspection," "SOP for Customer Complaint Handling"). - Typically 3–10 pages each. - Format: **Purpose → Scope → Responsibilities → Procedure → Related Documents → Records**. **Level 3 – Work Instructions (The Recipe)** - Granular, step-by-step instructions for a **specific task** within a process. - Example: "How to calibrate a Mitutoyo digital caliper to ±0.01 mm accuracy." - Often includes **pictographs, photographs, decision trees**. - Used directly by shop-floor personnel. - Must be **language-friendly** for the lowest literacy level on the shop floor. **Level 4 – Quality Records / Forms / Logbooks** - The **objective evidence** that a process was performed as documented. - Examples: Inspection reports, calibration certificates, training records, audit reports, customer satisfaction surveys. - Required for **legal traceability** and **ISO audit compliance**. - Retention periods are legally defined (often 5–7 years or "lifetime of the product + 3 years"). ### 2.2 Document Control – The 5D Framework Effective document control follows the **5D Cycle**: 1. **Draft** – Creation by the process owner. 2. **Distribute** – Controlled release with version numbers (e.g., Rev. 03, dated 12-Mar-2024). 3. **Deploy** – Training and acknowledgment by all users. 4. **Discard** – Removal of obsolete versions from all points of use. 5. **Document** – Audit trail of changes, approvals, and revisions. > [!IMPORTANT] > The cardinal rule: **A document that is not controlled is worse than no document at all**, because it conveys false confidence. An obsolete SOP still pinned on a notice board can lead to scrap, rework, or customer fatality. ### 2.3 ISO 9001:2015 Documentation Requirements The 2015 revision of ISO 9001 moved from a **"documented procedures"** mindset to a **"documented information"** mindset. The mandatory documentation includes: - **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3) - **Quality Policy** (Clause 5.2) - **Quality Objectives** (Clause 6.2) - **Process map and criteria for evaluation** (Clause 4.4) - **Documented information** deemed necessary by the organization (Clause 7.5) > [!NOTE] > The 2015 standard does **not** prescribe specific procedures. It mandates that the organization **determine what documented information is necessary**. This shifts the burden of justification from "Why is this documented?" to "Why is this NOT documented?" ### 2.4 Six Sigma Project Documentation Six Sigma projects require their own layered documentation system: | Document | DMAIC Phase | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **Project Charter** | Define | Problem statement, business case, goal, scope, team, timeline | | **SIPOC Diagram** | Define | Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers – high-level process map | | **VOC / CTQ Tree** | Define | Voice of Customer translated to Critical-to-Quality characteristics | | **Data Collection Plan** | Measure | What data, from where, how often, who collects, measurement system | | **Pareto Chart / FMEA** | Analyze | Prioritization of vital few causes | | **Pilot Run Records** | Improve | Evidence that the proposed solution works at scale | | **Control Plan** | Control | How the improved process will be sustained (SPC, FMEA, reaction plan) | ### 2.5 Benefits and Challenges of Documentation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Benefits (the "5 Cs"):** > - **Consistency** – Same process, same outcome, every time. > - **Communication** – Single source of truth across departments and geographies. > - **Compliance** – Auditable evidence for ISO, regulatory, and customer audits. > - **Continuity** – Survives employee turnover. > - **Continuous Improvement** – Baseline against which improvements are measured. > [!WARNING] > **Challenges (the "3 Ds"):** > - **Document Inflation** – Over-documentation slows processes and frustrates employees. > - **Document Stagnation** – Documents become outdated but remain in circulation. > - **Document Theater** – Documents exist for audit compliance but are never actually followed on the shop floor (the "formality trap"). ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (For Quantitative Documentation Metrics) While documentation is largely qualitative, a few **quantitative metrics** are frequently examined: $$ \text{Document Compliance Rate (DCR)} = \frac{\text{Number of Compliant Documents}}{\text{Total Number of Documents Audited}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Record Retrieval Time (RRT)} = T_{\text{request}} - T_{\text{access}} $$ $$ \text{Documentation Defect Rate (DDR)} = \frac{\text{Number of Non-Conforming Documents}}{\text{Total Documents Reviewed}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Training Effectiveness Ratio (TER)} = \frac{\text{Employees Trained on Latest Revision}}{\text{Total Document Users}} \times 100\% $$ $$ \text{Obsolete Document Incidence (ODI)} = \frac{\text{Obsolete Documents Found in Active Use}}{\text{Total Active Locations Inspected}} \times 100\% $$ | Metric | Symbol | Ideal Benchmark | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Document Compliance Rate | DCR | $\geq 95\%$ | Percentage | | Record Retrieval Time | RRT | $\leq 4$ hours | Hours | | Documentation Defect Rate | DDR | $\leq 5\%$ | Percentage | | Training Effectiveness Ratio | TER | $100\%$ | Percentage | | Obsolete Document Incidence | ODI | $0\%$ | Percentage | > [!NOTE] > Use $\vert$ for absolute value if needed (e.g., $\vert T_{\text{request}} - T_{\text{access}} \vert$). Avoid the literal pipe character $\vert$ in markdown tables as it breaks table syntax. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Document Creation Framework ### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Creating a Quality Manual **Step 1 – Define the Scope** - Identify the boundaries of the QMS (e.g., "Design, manufacture, and servicing of hydraulic cylinders at the Cochin plant"). - Document excluded clauses, if any, with justification. **Step 2 – State the Quality Policy** - Must be **appropriate to the purpose and context** of the organization. - Must include a **commitment to satisfy applicable requirements**. - Must include a **commitment to continual improvement**. - Must be **available as documented information**. - Must be **communicated, understood, and applied** within the organization. - Must be **available to relevant interested parties**. **Step 3 – Define Quality Objectives** - Must be **measurable**. - Must be **consistent with the Quality Policy**. - Must consider **applicable requirements**. - Must be **monitored**, **communicated**, and **updated** as appropriate. **Step 4 – Map the Processes** $$ \text{Process Map} = \{P_1, P_2, P_3, \ldots, P_n\} $$ where each $P_i$ is defined by: $$ P_i = \{\text{Inputs}_i, \text{Outputs}_i, \text{Responsibility}_i, \text{Criteria}_i, \text{Risks}_i\} $$ **Step 5 – Define Interaction and Sequence** - Use a **process interaction matrix** showing which process outputs feed which process inputs. **Step 6 – Approval and Release** - Approval matrix: typically the **Managing Director** (or equivalent) approves, with **MR (Management Representative)** as custodian. **Step 7 – Distribution and Training** - Controlled copy numbering (e.g., Copy No. 07 of 25). - Acknowledgment signature from each recipient. **Step 8 – Periodic Review** - Annual review or upon any major change (regulatory, organizational, process). ### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Writing a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) **Step 1 – Identify the Process Owner** - The single individual accountable for the SOP. **Step 2 – Gather Inputs** - Existing tribal knowledge from operators. - Customer/regulatory requirements. - Equipment manuals. - Previous non-conformance reports (NCRs). **Step 3 – Define the SOP Structure** $$ \text{SOP} = \{S_{\text{id}}, S_{\text{rev}}, S_{\text{eff\_date}}, S_{\text{owner}}, \text{Purpose}, \text{Scope}, \text{Responsibility}, \text{Procedure}, \text{Records}, \text{References}\} $$ where: - $S_{\text{id}}$ = SOP identifier (e.g., SOP-PUR-014) - $S_{\text{rev}}$ = Revision number (e.g., Rev. 04) - $S_{\text{eff\_date}}$ = Effective date **Step 4 – Draft the Procedure Body** - Use **active voice, present tense, imperative mood**. - Example: "Open valve V-12 fully. Wait 30 seconds. Record pressure on Form QC-104." **Step 5 – Attach Flowchart** $$ \text{Process Flow} = \text{Start} \rightarrow \text{Step 1} \rightarrow \text{Decision} \rightarrow \text{Step 2A or 2B} \rightarrow \text{End} $$ **Step 6 – Internal Review** - Cross-functional review (production, quality, safety, maintenance). **Step 7 – Approval Signature Block** $$ \text{Approvals} = \{\text{Prepared By}, \text{Reviewed By}, \text{Approved By}\} $$ **Step 8 – Pilot Run and Validation** - Use the SOP for a defined trial period (typically 30 days) and gather operator feedback. **Step 9 – Final Release with Version Control** - Replace all previous versions in active locations. - Archive old version with "OBSOLETE – DO NOT USE" watermark. ### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Six Sigma Project Charter **Step 1 – Problem Statement (5W1H Format)** $$ \text{Problem} = \{W_{\text{who}}, W_{\text{what}}, W_{\text{where}}, W_{\text{when}}, W_{\text{why}}, H_{\text{how}}\} $$ **Step 2 – Business Case (Quantified Pain)** $$ \text{Annual Loss} = (\text{Defect Rate} \times \text{Unit Cost of Defect} \times \text{Annual Volume}) + \text{Rejection Cost} + \text{Warranty Cost} $$ **Step 3 – Goal Statement (SMART)** $$ \text{Goal} = \{\text{Specific}, \text{Measurable}, \text{Achievable}, \text{Relevant}, \text{Time-bound}\} $$ Example: "Reduce Customer Complaint Rate from 4.2% to 1.5% within 6 months." **Step 4 – Project Scope (In/Out Matrix)** $$ \text{Scope} = \{\text{IN: Product X, Line Y, Plant Z}\}, \quad \overline{\text{Scope}} = \{\text{OUT: Product W, Line V}\} $$ **Step 5 – Team Definition (RACI Matrix)** | Activity | Champion | Black Belt | Green Belt | Process Owner | Operator | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Project Initiation | A (Accountable) | R (Responsible) | C (Consulted) | I (Informed) | I | | Data Collection | I | A | R | C | R | | Solution Implementation | A | C | R | R | I | **Step 6 – Timeline (Gantt Chart Equivalent)** $$ \text{Timeline} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i \quad \text{where } T_i \text{ is the duration of milestone } i $$ **Step 7 – Risk Assessment** $$ \text{Risk Score}_j = P_j \times S_j \times D_j $$ where $P_j$ = Probability, $S_j$ = Severity, $D_j$ = Detectability of risk $j$. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Four-Tier Documentation Pyramid ```mermaid graph TD A[LEVEL 1 - QUALITY MANUAL
What and Why]:::level1 B[LEVEL 2 - SOPs and PROCEDURES
Who, When, How]:::level2 C[LEVEL 3 - WORK INSTRUCTIONS
Step by Step Tasks]:::level3 D[LEVEL 4 - RECORDS and FORMS
Objective Evidence]:::level4 A --> B B --> C C --> D A1[Quality Policy]:::l1a A2[Quality Objectives]:::l1a A3[Process Map]:::l1a A4[Scope Statement]:::l1a A --> A1 A --> A2 A --> A3 A --> A4 B1[Material Inspection SOP]:::l2a B2[Customer Complaint SOP]:::l2a B3[Calibration SOP]:::l2a B4[Training SOP]:::l2a B --> B1 B --> B2 B --> B3 B --> B4 C1[Calibrate Caliper Steps]:::l3a C2[Operating CNC Lathe]:::l3a C3[Issuing NCR Procedure]:::l3a C --> C1 C --> C2 C --> C3 D1[Inspection Reports]:::l4a D2[Calibration Certificates]:::l4a D3[Training Records]:::l4a D4[Audit Reports]:::l4a D --> D1 D --> D2 D --> D3 D --> D4 classDef level1 fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef level2 fill:#2E75B6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef level3 fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef level4 fill:#BDD7EE,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef l1a fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef l2a fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef l3a fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef l4a fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Document Control Life Cycle (The 5D Cycle) ```mermaid graph LR A[DRAFT
Process Owner Creates]:::step1 B[DISTRIBUTE
Controlled Release]:::step2 C[DEPLOY
Train and Acknowledge]:::step3 D[DISCARD
Remove Obsolete]:::step4 E[DOCUMENT
Audit Trail Maintained]:::step5 F[FEEDBACK
User Inputs Collected]:::step6 A --> B B --> C C --> F F --> D D --> E E --> A classDef step1 fill:#C00000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef step2 fill:#ED7D31,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef step3 fill:#FFC000,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef step4 fill:#70AD47,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef step5 fill:#4472C4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef step6 fill:#7030A0,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ### 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC Documentation Flow ```mermaid graph TD D[DEFINE PHASE]:::define M[MEASURE PHASE]:::measure A[ANALYZE PHASE]:::analyze I[IMPROVE PHASE]:::improve C[CONTROL PHASE]:::control D --> M M --> A A --> I I --> C D1[Project Charter]:::doc D2[SIPOC Diagram]:::doc D3[VOC and CTQ Tree]:::doc D --> D1 D --> D2 D --> D3 M1[Data Collection Plan]:::doc M2[MSA Report]:::doc M3[Baseline Capability Cpk]:::doc M --> M1 M --> M2 M --> M3 A1[Pareto Chart]:::doc A2[FMEA Sheet]:::doc A3[Hypothesis Test Results]:::doc A --> A1 A --> A2 A --> A3 I1[Pilot Run Records]:::doc I2[Cost Benefit Analysis]:::doc I3[Solution Selection Matrix]:::doc I --> I1 I --> I2 I --> I3 C1[Control Plan]:::doc C2[SPC Charts]:::doc C3[Reaction Plan]:::doc C --> C1 C --> C2 C --> C3 classDef define fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef measure fill:#2E75B6,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef analyze fill:#5B9BD5,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef improve fill:#9DC3E6,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef control fill:#BDD7EE,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef doc fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a QMS Documentation System ```mermaid graph TB subgraph EXTERNAL[EXTERNAL DRIVERS] E1[ISO 9001 2015]:::ext E2[Customer Requirements]:::ext E3[Regulatory Standards]:::ext E4[Six Sigma DMAIC]:::ext end subgraph POLICY[POLICY LAYER] P1[Quality Policy]:::pol P2[Quality Objectives]:::pol P3[QMS Scope]:::pol end subgraph PROCESS[PROCESS LAYER] PR1[Process Map]:::proc PR2[SOPs]:::proc PR3[Work Instructions]:::proc end subgraph EVIDENCE[EVIDENCE LAYER] EV1[Quality Records]:::evd EV2[Audit Reports]:::evd EV3[NCR and CAPA Logs]:::evd end subgraph CONTROL[CONTROL MECHANISM] CT1[Document Control Board]:::ctrl CT2[Version Control System]:::ctrl CT3[Distribution Matrix]:::ctrl end EXTERNAL --> POLICY POLICY --> PROCESS PROCESS --> EVIDENCE CONTROL --> POLICY CONTROL --> PROCESS CONTROL --> EVIDENCE classDef ext fill:#C00000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef pol fill:#1F4E79,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef proc fill:#2E75B6,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef evd fill:#70AD47,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef ctrl fill:#FFC000,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]** > **"Define a Quality Manual. List any four contents of a Quality Manual as per ISO 9001:2015."** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **Quality Manual** is the top-level document of a Quality Management System that specifies the **quality policy, quality objectives, and scope** of the QMS, along with a description of the interaction between processes. Four contents of a Quality Manual: 1. **Title page and approval page** (with signatures of top management). 2. **Table of contents and revision history**. 3. **Scope of the QMS** (including any exclusions with justification under Clause 4.3). 4. **Quality Policy and Quality Objectives**. 5. **Process map and interaction of processes**. 6. **Reference to supporting procedures**. *(Any four well-explained points = 3 marks; one mark for the definition and two marks for the four contents.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]** > **"Explain the term 'Document Control' in TQM. Why is control of obsolete documents critical?"** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** **Document Control** refers to the systematic procedure for the creation, review, approval, distribution, retrieval, archival, and disposal of all QMS documents to ensure that only the **current, approved, and relevant versions** are in active use at the point of operation. **Why obsolete document control is critical:** 1. **Safety** – An outdated safety SOP may reference a valve that no longer exists or a chemical that has been substituted, leading to accidents. 2. **Quality** – A superseded inspection standard can cause shipment of non-conforming product. 3. **Legal compliance** – Auditors finding obsolete documents in use is a major non-conformance that can lead to **loss of ISO certification**. 4. **Cost** – Rework, scrap, and warranty claims caused by outdated procedures drain profit. *(2 marks for the definition and 1 mark for the criticality explanation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **Q.A. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Pattern, Module 3]** > **(a)** Explain the **four-tier documentation hierarchy** in a Quality Management System. Describe the purpose, audience, and typical content of each tier with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** Discuss the **benefits and challenges** of documentation in TQM. How can an organization avoid the "document theater" trap? *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** The four-tier documentation hierarchy in a QMS is: **Tier 1 – Quality Manual** - **Purpose:** Top-level policy document; defines the QMS scope, policy, and objectives. - **Audience:** Top management, external auditors, customers, certification bodies. - **Content:** Quality policy, quality objectives, process map, scope statement, exclusions (if any), and reference to supporting procedures. - **Example:** The "ISO 9001:2015 Quality Manual" of Bosch Ltd., Cochin Plant, Rev. 06, dated 15-Apr-2024. **Tier 2 – Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)** - **Purpose:** Document the "what, who, when, where, and how" of each major process. - **Audience:** Process owners, middle management, internal auditors. - **Content:** Purpose, scope, responsibilities, step-by-step procedure, related documents, and records. - **Example:** SOP-PUR-014 titled "Procedure for Incoming Material Inspection of Cold-Rolled Steel Sheets." **Tier 3 – Work Instructions** - **Purpose:** Granular, task-level guidance for a specific activity. - **Audience:** Shop-floor operators, frontline technicians. - **Content:** Step-by-step instructions, photographs, decision trees, safety warnings, tool lists. - **Example:** WI-MAINT-007 titled "How to Replace the Spindle Bearing of CNC Lathe Model L20." **Tier 4 – Quality Records / Forms** - **Purpose:** Provide objective evidence that processes were executed as documented. - **Audience:** Auditors, quality inspectors, legal authorities. - **Content:** Filled-in data, signatures, dates, photographs, test results. - **Example:** Form QC-104 titled "Daily In-Process Dimensional Inspection Log for Product X." **Key Linkage Rule:** Every record must be traceable to a work instruction, every work instruction to an SOP, and every SOP to a clause of the Quality Manual. *(7 marks: 4 marks for tier descriptions + 3 marks for examples and linkage.)* --- **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **Benefits of Documentation (the "5 Cs"):** 1. **Consistency** – The same process produces the same result every time and everywhere. 2. **Communication** – A single source of truth across departments, shifts, and geographies. 3. **Compliance** – Auditable evidence for ISO, regulatory, and customer audits. 4. **Continuity** – Organizational knowledge is preserved despite employee turnover. 5. **Continuous Improvement** – Provides the baseline against which Kaizen and Six Sigma improvements are measured. **Challenges of Documentation (the "3 Ds"):** 1. **Document Inflation** – Over-documentation slows down processes and frustrates employees who perceive it as bureaucratic. 2. **Document Stagnation** – Documents become outdated but remain in circulation because no one owns the review process. 3. **Document Theater** – Documents exist for audit compliance but are never actually followed on the shop floor (the "formality trap"). **How to Avoid the "Document Theater" Trap:** - **Co-creation** – Involve shop-floor operators in drafting the documents they will actually use. - **Visual SOPs** – Use pictures, pictographs, and one-page flowcharts instead of dense text. - **Tiered Approval** – Route documents through the people who execute the process, not just through quality department. - **Field Audits** – Periodically observe whether the documented procedure is actually being followed (the "say-do gap" audit). - **Feedback Loop** – Establish a simple mechanism (a "yellow card" suggestion system) for operators to flag impractical steps. - **Training Effectiveness Verification** – Do not just train employees; verify through observation and competency tests that they can actually perform the task per the SOP. *(7 marks: 2 for benefits, 2 for challenges, 3 for avoidance strategies.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** > **Q.B. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Pattern, Module 3]** > **(a)** Describe the **documentation requirements of ISO 9001:2015**. How does the 2015 revision differ from the 2008 revision in its approach to documentation? *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** Explain the **Six Sigma project documentation system** across the five DMAIC phases. List the key documents generated in each phase and their purpose. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** **ISO 9001:2015 Documentation Requirements:** The 2015 revision requires the organization to maintain and retain the following **documented information**: 1. **Scope of the QMS** (Clause 4.3) – Boundaries, applicability, and exclusions. 2. **Quality Policy** (Clause 5.2) – Must include commitment to satisfy requirements and to continual improvement. 3. **Quality Objectives** (Clause 6.2) – Measurable objectives consistent with the quality policy. 4. **Process map and criteria for process evaluation** (Clause 4.4) – Inputs, outputs, sequence, responsibility, risks, and KPIs. 5. **Documented information necessary for the operation of the QMS** (Clause 7.5) – Determined by the organization itself. 6. **Documented information as evidence of the results achieved** (Clause 7.5) – Records. 7. **Internal audit results and management review outputs** (Clauses 9.2 and 9.3). **Key Difference Between ISO 9001:2008 and ISO 9001:2015:** | Aspect | 2008 Revision | 2015 Revision | |---|---|---| | **Terminology** | Used "documents" and "records" | Uses unified term **"documented information"** | | **Mandatory Procedures** | Required 6 specific procedures (Document Control, Record Control, Internal Audit, NCR, CAPA, Preventive Action) | Does **not mandate specific procedures**; organization determines what is needed | | **Approach** | Prescriptive (procedure-based) | Performance-based (process-based) | | **Preventive Action** | Separate clause | Merged into risk-based thinking | | **Documentation Volume** | High (often criticized as bureaucratic) | Reduced emphasis on volume; emphasis on relevance and effectiveness | | **Context of Organization** | Not explicitly required | Mandatory (Clause 4.1 and 4.2) | The 2015 revision shifts the burden of proof from "Why did you create this document?" to "Why is this document necessary for your QMS?" *(7 marks: 3 for listing requirements, 2 for differences, 2 for analysis.)* --- **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **Six Sigma DMAIC Documentation System:** **DEFINE Phase Documentation:** 1. **Project Charter** – Problem statement, business case, SMART goal, scope, team (RACI), timeline, risks, and expected savings. 2. **SIPOC Diagram** – Suppliers → Inputs → Process → Outputs → Customers at a high level. 3. **VOC and CTQ Tree** – Voice of Customer translated to Critical-to-Quality measurable characteristics. 4. **Stakeholder Analysis** – Mapping of all interested parties and their influence. **MEASURE Phase Documentation:** 1. **Data Collection Plan** – Operational definition, data source, sampling plan, collection frequency, responsible person. 2. **Measurement System Analysis (MSA) Report** – Gage R&R, bias, linearity, stability. 3. **Baseline Process Capability Study** – $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$ values. 4. **Current State Process Map** – Detailed flowchart of the as-is process. **ANALYZE Phase Documentation:** 1. **Pareto Chart** – Vital few vs. trivial many causes. 2. **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** – Severity, Occurrence, Detection scoring and RPN ranking. 3. **Hypothesis Testing Reports** – t-test, ANOVA, chi-square, regression outputs. 4. **Root Cause Analysis** – 5-Why, Fishbone (Ishikawa), Fault Tree Analysis. **IMPROVE Phase Documentation:** 1. **Pilot Run Records** – Log of the trial execution of the proposed solution. 2. **Cost-Benefit Analysis** – Quantified financial impact of the improvement. 3. **Solution Selection Matrix** – Weighted scoring of alternative solutions against criteria. 4. **Updated FMEA** – Risk re-rating after the improvement is implemented. **CONTROL Phase Documentation:** 1. **Control Plan** – Process parameter, specification, measurement method, sample size, frequency, and responsible person. 2. **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** – Updated or newly created procedure for the improved process. 3. **SPC Charts** – X-bar R, p-chart, c-chart, etc. 4. **Reaction Plan** – Step-by-step response to out-of-control conditions. 5. **Project Closure Report** – Lessons learned, savings achieved, and handoff to process owner. *(7 marks: 5 marks for listing the documents in each phase and 2 marks for the purpose linkage.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > - **Mistake 1:** Writing a "Quality Manual" definition without listing Clause 4.3 of ISO 9001:2015 → *Lose 1 mark.* > - **Mistake 2:** Confusing the **Quality Manual** with the **Quality Plan** (a Quality Plan is project-specific; a Quality Manual is organization-wide) → *Lose 1 mark.* > - **Mistake 3:** Forgetting to mention **"objective evidence"** when describing records → *Lose 0.5 mark.* > - **Mistake 4:** Writing the DMAIC phases out of order or skipping the **Control** phase (which is the most heavily documented phase) → *Lose 1–2 marks.* > - **Mistake 5:** Describing documentation benefits without addressing the **"document theater"** concept → *Lose 1 mark on Q.A (b).* --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Cheat Sheet for KTU Module 3 – Documentation:** - **Documentation** is the **institutionalized voice of quality** — it converts tacit knowledge into explicit, retrievable, auditable information. - **Four-Tier Pyramid:** Quality Manual (Level 1) → SOPs (Level 2) → Work Instructions (Level 3) → Records (Level 4). - **Quality Manual** = top-level policy document; contains Quality Policy, Quality Objectives, QMS Scope, and Process Map. - **SOPs** = process-level; format is **Purpose → Scope → Responsibility → Procedure → Records → References**. - **Work Instructions** = task-level, step-by-step, often visual; designed for the **lowest literacy level** on the shop floor. - **Records** = **objective evidence**; must be retained for legally defined periods. - **Document Control** follows the **5D Cycle**: Draft → Distribute → Deploy → Discard → Document (Audit Trail). - **ISO 9001:2015** uses the term **"Documented Information"** (not "documents" and "records" separately). - **ISO 9001:2015 does not mandate 6 specific procedures** (unlike 2008); the organization decides what is necessary. - **Six Sigma DMAIC** generates: Charter, SIPOC, VOC/CTQ (Define) → DCP, MSA, Capability (Measure) → Pareto, FMEA, Hypothesis Tests (Analyze) → Pilot, Cost-Benefit (Improve) → Control Plan, SPC, Reaction Plan (Control). - **Key Metrics:** DCR ($\geq 95\%$), DDR ($\leq 5\%$), TER ($100\%$), ODI ($0\%$). - **Golden Rule:** *A document that is not controlled is worse than no document at all.* - **Pitfalls to Avoid:** Document Inflation, Document Stagnation, Document Theater. - **Modern Trend:** Shift from paper-based to **digital QMS (eQMS)** with cloud-based version control, electronic signatures, and automated workflow approvals. - **Examiner Triggers:** Always use the words "objective evidence," "controlled distribution," "traceability," and "continuous improvement" — these are KTU-magnet keywords. > **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Documentation** ---

topics.title: Internal Audits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Internal Audits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of Internal Audits

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Internal Audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented process conducted within an organization to evaluate and verify the effectiveness of its **Quality Management System (QMS)**, determine conformity to planned arrangements, standard operating procedures, and the requirements of standards such as **ISO 9001:2015**, and assess the suitability and effectiveness of these arrangements in achieving quality objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Internal auditing is a self-examination mechanism by which an organization objectively evaluates its own processes against documented procedures, regulatory requirements, and quality standards to identify non-conformities and drive continual improvement.

According to **ISO 19011:2018** (the international standard governing auditing of management systems), an internal audit is a *first-party audit* performed by, or on behalf of, the organization itself for internal purposes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Health Check-up of a Company"**
>
> Imagine a human body. Even when a person feels perfectly fine, doctors recommend periodic health check-ups — blood tests, ECG, X-rays. These check-ups don't *cure* the patient directly, but they **reveal hidden issues** before they become serious.
>
> An **Internal Audit** is exactly this "organizational health check-up." Employees within the company (not external inspectors) systematically examine each department's processes — like checking whether invoices are processed correctly, whether raw material inspections are done properly, or whether customer complaints are resolved on time. They produce a "diagnostic report" (the audit report) listing "symptoms" (non-conformities) and "prescriptions" (corrective actions).

**Key characteristics that make internal audits unique:**
- Conducted **by the organization's own trained personnel** (or hired external auditors acting on the organization's behalf)
- **Independent** of the function being audited (auditor should not audit their own work)
- **Planned and scheduled** as part of the QMS
- Focuses on **conformity**, **effectiveness**, and **continual improvement**
- Forms a mandatory input to **Management Review** under ISO 9001 Clause 9.2

### 1.3 Core Terminology — KTU Must-Know Glossary

| Term | Definition | KTU Relevance |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Audit** | Systematic, independent, documented process for obtaining audit evidence | Foundation concept |
| **First-Party Audit** | Internal audit conducted by the organization on itself | Core to Module 3 |
| **Second-Party Audit** | Conducted by customers or by organizations on their behalf | Contrast term |
| **Third-Party Audit** | Conducted by independent certification bodies (e.g., ISO auditors) | Contrast term |
| **Audit Criteria** | Set of policies, procedures, or standards used as reference | Used in checklist |
| **Audit Evidence** | Records, statements, or other information verifiable | Outputs of audit |
| **Non-Conformity (NC)** | Non-fulfillment of a requirement | Major output |
| **Observation** | Statement of fact made during an audit that may lead to improvement | Soft finding |
| **Audit Programme** | Arrangements for a set of audits planned for a specific timeframe | Planning tool |
| **Auditor Competence** | Demonstrated personal attributes and demonstrated ability to apply knowledge and skills | Auditor selection |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this:** Internal Audit = First-Party Audit = Self-Inspection = Self-Regulation. All four terms are interchangeable in KTU exam answers.

### 1.4 Purpose and Objectives of Internal Audits

1. **Verify conformity** of the QMS to ISO 9001, customer requirements, and regulatory standards.
2. **Evaluate effectiveness** of implemented processes in achieving quality objectives.
3. **Identify non-conformities, risks, and opportunities for improvement.**
4. **Provide feedback** to top management for informed decision-making during Management Review.
5. **Promote a culture of continual improvement** across all levels.
6. **Prepare the organization** for external third-party certification audits.
7. **Ensure compliance** with legal and statutory requirements.

### 1.5 Types of Internal Audits

| Type | Description | Frequency |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| **Compliance Audit** | Checks adherence to standards, policies, regulations | Annually / Semi-annually |
| **Process Audit** | Examines specific process inputs, outputs, controls | Per process cycle |
| **Product Audit** | Verifies product conformance to specifications | Pre-shipment / Random |
| **System Audit** | Evaluates the entire QMS | Annually |
| **Departmental Audit** | Reviews a specific department's activities | Quarterly |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework

### 2.1 The Internal Audit Process — Structured Logic

The internal audit process follows a well-defined sequence. KTU examiners frequently ask students to "explain the steps of conducting an internal audit" for full marks.

**Step 1: Initiation of the Audit**
- Define the audit objectives, scope, and criteria.
- Appoint the **Lead Auditor** and the audit team.
- Establish initial contact with the auditee.
- Confirm the availability of resources and logistics.

**Step 2: Audit Planning (Pre-Audit Preparation)**
- Develop the **Audit Plan** — schedule, dates, locations, departments to be audited.
- Assign tasks to team members based on competence.
- Prepare **Working Documents** — checklists, forms, previous audit reports.
- Conduct a **document review** of the procedures to be audited.

**Step 3: Conducting the Audit (On-Site Activities)**
- **Opening Meeting:** Confirm scope, introduce team, agree on communication channels.
- **Collecting Evidence:** Use interviews, document review, observation, and data analysis.
- **Generating Findings:** Compare evidence against audit criteria.
- **Recording Observations:** Document conformities, non-conformities, and opportunities for improvement.

**Step 4: Audit Reporting**
- Prepare the **Audit Report** containing findings, conclusions, and recommendations.
- Distribute the report to relevant stakeholders.
- **Closing Meeting:** Present findings, agree on timelines for corrective action.

**Step 5: Follow-Up and Corrective Action**
- Verify that **corrective actions** are implemented within agreed timelines.
- Confirm effectiveness of corrective actions.
- Close out non-conformities in the audit records.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The "Why" behind each step:
> - **Planning** reduces disruption and ensures coverage.
> - **Evidence collection** ensures objectivity.
> - **Reporting** creates accountability.
> - **Follow-up** closes the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** — the heart of TQM.

### 2.2 Audit Planning — Detailed Framework

A robust **Audit Programme** typically covers a 12-month period. The planning hierarchy is:

**Level 1: Audit Programme Objectives** (set by top management)
- Align with strategic quality objectives
- Ensure coverage of all QMS elements
- Address risks and prior audit findings

**Level 2: Individual Audit Plan** (set by Lead Auditor)
- Audit criteria
- Scope and boundaries
- Activities and locations
- Dates, times, and duration
- Team assignments

**Level 3: Audit Checklist** (set by each auditor)
- Question-based tool used to gather evidence
- Should be flexible and not a "yes/no" rigid form
- Includes references to clauses, procedures, records

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Answers:** A good audit checklist is **process-oriented**, not person-oriented. It asks "How is the calibration of measuring equipment controlled?" — not "Did Mr. X calibrate the equipment?"

### 2.3 Roles and Responsibilities

| Role | Responsibility |
|------|----------------|
| **Audit Programme Manager** | Establishs, implements, monitors, and reviews the audit programme |
| **Lead Auditor** | Manages the audit team, prepares the report, presents findings |
| **Auditor** | Collects evidence, interviews personnel, records findings |
| **Technical Expert** | Provides specialized knowledge (does not act as auditor) |
| **Auditee** | Cooperates with auditors, provides access to records and personnel |
| **Top Management** | Ensures resources, reviews audit results, initiates improvements |

### 2.4 Non-Conformity Classification (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Major NC** | Absence of, or total breakdown in, a system/process; raises doubt on quality of product | No calibration of testing equipment affecting product safety |
| **Minor NC** | Single observed lapse; does not indicate systemic failure | One missing signature on a single quality record |
| **Observation** | Potential weakness; not an NC but a preventive note | Workstation layout could lead to mix-ups in future |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse "Observation" with "Minor NC." Remember — an NC means a **requirement is not being met**. An observation is **a warning about a potential future NC**.

### 2.5 The Auditor Competence Framework (ISO 19011)

A competent auditor must possess:

1. **Personal Attributes:** Ethical, open-minded, diplomatic, observant, perceptive, versatile, tenacious, decisive, self-reliant.
2. **Knowledge:** Audit principles, procedures, applicable standards, organizational context, applicable legal/regulatory requirements.
3. **Skills:** Applying audit methods, planning and organizing, communicating effectively, interviewing, listening, evaluating, reporting.

The competence equation commonly written in KTU answers is:

$$
\text{Auditor Competence} = f(\text{Knowledge} + \text{Skills} + \text{Attributes} + \text{Experience})
$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Expression | Use / Significance |
|------------------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| **Audit Coverage Rate** | $\text{ACR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Audits Conducted}}{\text{Number of Audits Planned}} \times 100$ | Measures audit programme effectiveness |
| **NC Closure Rate** | $\text{NCCR} = \dfrac{\text{NCs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100$ | Measures follow-up effectiveness |
| **Audit Effectiveness Index** | $\text{AEI} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Repeat NCs from Prior Cycle}}{\text{Total NCs in Current Cycle}} \times 100$ | Lower value = better |
| **Auditor Independence Ratio** | $\text{AIR} = \dfrac{\text{Audits Conducted by Independent Auditors}}{\text{Total Audits Conducted}} \times 100$ | Should be **100%** per ISO 19011 |
| **Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ)** | $\text{CoPQ} = \text{Prevention} + \text{Appraisal} + \text{Internal Failure} + \text{External Failure}$ | Where internal audit reduces failure costs |
| **Process Capability Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left( \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right)$ | Often verified during process audits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Problem Pattern:** You may be asked to compute **ACR, NCCR, AEI, or AIR** given a scenario. Use the formulas above. Always express in **percentage (%)**.

### 2.7 Real-World Utility of Internal Audits

- **Manufacturing:** Verifies that production lines follow Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
- **Service Industry:** Audits customer service response times, complaint resolution.
- **Software:** Confirms adherence to CMMI, ISO 27001, or Agile process definitions.
- **Healthcare:** Verifies compliance with NABH/JCI standards, patient safety protocols.
- **Education:** Confirms that academic processes follow accreditation criteria (NBA/NAAC).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations and Worked Examples

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Audit Findings to Quality Cost

This matrix demonstrates how internal audit findings directly influence the **Cost of Poor Quality (CoPQ)** — a key TQM concept.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CoPQ}_{\text{total}} &= \text{Prevention Cost (P)} + \text{Appraisal Cost (A)} \\
&\quad + \text{Internal Failure Cost (IF)} + \text{External Failure Cost (EF)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where each component represents:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Prevention Cost (P)} &= \text{Cost of quality planning, training, audits, design reviews} \\
\text{Appraisal Cost (A)} &= \text{Cost of inspection, testing, calibration, audits} \\
\text{Internal Failure Cost (IF)} &= \text{Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime} \\
\text{External Failure Cost (EF)} &= \text{Warranty claims, returns, recalls, legal costs}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight:** Every dollar spent on **internal audit (Appraisal + Prevention)** saves **multiple dollars** of Internal and External Failure costs. This is the financial justification for robust internal audit programmes.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Audit Coverage Rate (ACR)

**Problem Statement:**
A manufacturing company planned to conduct **48 internal audits** in the year 2024 as per its annual audit programme. However, due to production emergencies, only **42 audits** were actually completed. Calculate the **Audit Coverage Rate (ACR)**.

**Step 1: Identify given values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Audits} &= 48 \\
\text{Conducted Audits} &= 42
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**

$$
\text{ACR} = \dfrac{\text{Conducted}}{\text{Planned}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACR} &= \dfrac{42}{48} \times 100 \\
&= 0.875 \times 100 \\
&= 87.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation**

An ACR of **87.5%** indicates the audit programme is operating below target. The shortfall is **12.5%**, signalling a need for better scheduling, resource allocation, and management commitment.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — NC Closure Rate (NCCR)

**Problem Statement:**
During Q1 and Q2 of 2024, a software firm raised **30 non-conformities** in its internal audits. By the end of Q3, **24 NCs** were closed within the agreed timeline. Calculate the **NC Closure Rate (NCCR)**.

**Step 1: Identify values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total NCs Raised} &= 30 \\
\text{NCs Closed on Time} &= 24
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply formula**

$$
\text{NCCR} = \dfrac{\text{NCs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCCR} &= \dfrac{24}{30} \times 100 \\
&= 0.80 \times 100 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

The NCCR of **80%** means **20%** of the non-conformities remain unaddressed, indicating gaps in the corrective action process. ISO 9001 requires that all NCs are addressed with documented evidence of effectiveness.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Audit Effectiveness Index (AEI)

**Problem Statement:**
A company's 2023 internal audit cycle raised **50 non-conformities**. Out of these, **10 were repeat NCs** carried over from the 2022 audit. In 2024, **40 new NCs** were raised. Calculate the **AEI** for 2024 and interpret the result.

**Step 1: Definition clarification**

The **AEI** (lower is better) measures the proportion of repeat NCs. If 10 of the 50 NCs from 2023 were repeated in 2024:

$$
\text{AEI}_{2024} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat NCs from Prior Cycle}}{\text{Total NCs in Current Cycle}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2: Substitute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AEI}_{2024} &= \dfrac{10}{40} \times 100 \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

A 25% AEI means **1 in 4 NCs** recurred, suggesting that **root cause analysis (RCA)** was insufficiently rigorous in 2023. The organization must adopt stronger tools such as **5-Why Analysis** or **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagrams**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — The 5-Why Root Cause Method

When an internal audit identifies a non-conformity, KTU answers often require the use of the **5-Why** technique:

| Why Level | Sample Question | Typical Answer |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Why 1** | Why was the NC raised? | Calibration record was missing |
| **Why 2** | Why was the record missing? | The technician forgot to log it |
| **Why 3** | Why did the technician forget? | No checklist exists at the workstation |
| **Why 4** | Why is there no checklist? | The SOP was never updated after process change |
| **Why 5** | Why was the SOP not updated? | No document control system alerts process owners |

**Root Cause Identified:** Absence of a document control trigger linked to process change.

**Corrective Action:** Implement a **Document Change Request (DCR)** workflow that automatically flags all SOPs linked to a modified process.

---

### 3.6 Audit Time Estimation — A Useful Formula

For planning purposes, the time required for an audit can be estimated as:

$$
T_{\text{audit}} = N \times t \times (1 + R)
$$

**Where:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{audit}} &= \text{Total audit time (person-hours)} \\
N &= \text{Number of auditors in the team} \\
t &= \text{Base audit time per element (hours)} \\
R &= \text{Risk/Complexity factor (typically } 0.2 \leq R \leq 0.5 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example Calculation:**

For an audit of 8 process elements with a base time of 2 hours each, a 3-person team, and a complexity factor of 0.3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{audit}} &= 3 \times 8 \times 2 \times (1 + 0.3) \\
&= 48 \times 1.3 \\
&= 62.4 \text{ person-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

This result is used to plan auditor schedules, departmental coordination, and resource allocation.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Internal Audit Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Audit Programme Initiation] --> B[Define Scope, Criteria, Objectives]
    B --> C[Select Audit Team and Lead Auditor]
    C --> D[Prepare Audit Plan and Checklist]
    D --> E[Conduct Document Review]
    E --> F[Hold Opening Meeting with Auditee]
    F --> G[On-Site Evidence Collection: Interviews, Observation, Records]
    G --> H[Identify Findings: Conformance and Non-Conformance]
    H --> I[Classify NCs: Major, Minor, Observation]
    I --> J[Prepare Draft Audit Report]
    J --> K[Hold Closing Meeting: Present Findings]
    K --> L[Submit Final Audit Report to Top Management]
    L --> M[Initiate Corrective and Preventive Actions CAPA]
    M --> N[Conduct Follow-Up Audit to Verify Effectiveness]
    N --> O{NCs Effectively Closed?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Close NC Records and Update Risk Register]
    O -->|No| M
    P --> Q[Feed Results into Management Review]
    Q --> R[Continual Improvement: Next Audit Cycle]
    R --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Loop:** The diagram illustrates the **PDCA cycle** embedded in the audit process — the **Plan** is the audit programme, the **Do** is the on-site audit, the **Check** is the finding analysis, and the **Act** is the corrective action and follow-up.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Audit Finding Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FIND[Audit Finding Identified] --> CHECK{Does Evidence Meet Criteria?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| CONFORM[Conformance: Documented Strength]
    CHECK -->|No| NC[Non-Conformity Identified]
    NC --> SEVERITY{Assess Severity and Impact}
    SEVERITY -->|High: Systemic or Product Safety| MAJOR[Major Non-Conformity]
    SEVERITY -->|Low: Isolated Lapse| MINOR[Minor Non-Conformity]
    SEVERITY -->|Potential Future Risk| OBS[Observation for Improvement]
    MAJOR --> ROOT[Apply Root Cause Analysis: 5-Why / Fishbone]
    MINOR --> ROOT
    OBS --> ROOT
    ROOT --> CAPA[Define Corrective and Preventive Action]
    CAPA --> TIMELINE{Action Realistic and Timely?}
    TIMELINE -->|Yes| ASSIGN[Assign Owner and Due Date]
    TIMELINE -->|No| CAPA
    ASSIGN --> VERIFY[Verify Effectiveness in Follow-Up]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Audit Programme Management Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TM[Top Management] -->|Defines Quality Policy| PM[Audit Programme Manager]
    PM -->|Establishes Annual Programme| AP[Audit Programme]
    AP -->|Allocates Resources| LA[Lead Auditor]
    LA -->|Plans Individual Audits| IP[Individual Audit Plans]
    IP -->|Allocates Tasks| AU[Auditors]
    AU -->|Execute| EV[Evidence Collection]
    EV -->|Generate| FN[Findings and Report]
    FN -->|Submit to| TM
    FN -->|Input to| MR[Management Review ISO 9.3]
    MR -->|Drives| CI[Continual Improvement]
    CI -->|Updates| AP
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Auditor Competence Wheel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CENTER[Competent Internal Auditor] --> KA[Knowledge Area]
    CENTER --> SK[Skills Area]
    CENTER --> AT[Personal Attributes]
    CENTER --> EX[Experience and Education]
    KA --> K1[Audit Principles and Methods]
    KA --> K2[Standards: ISO 9001, ISO 19011]
    KA --> K3[Organizational Context]
    KA --> K4[Legal and Regulatory Requirements]
    SK --> S1[Interviewing and Listening]
    SK --> S2[Effective Communication]
    SK --> S3[Report Writing]
    SK --> S4[Analytical Thinking]
    AT --> A1[Ethical Conduct]
    AT --> A2[Open-Mindedness]
    AT --> A3[Diplomacy and Tact]
    AT --> A4[Tenacity and Decisiveness]

    EX --> E2[Audit Experience]
    EX --> E3[Continuous Professional Development]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice the cyclical arrows from *Continual Improvement* back to *Audit Programme* and from *Management Review* back to *Top Management* — these are not decorative. They represent the **feedback loops** that make the QMS a **living system**, not a static document.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
**Define internal audit. List any four objectives of conducting an internal audit in an organization.**

**Model Answer:**

An **internal audit** is a systematic, independent, and documented evaluation conducted within an organization to verify that its Quality Management System conforms to planned arrangements, applicable standards (such as ISO 9001:2015), and the requirements established by the organization itself, while also assessing the effectiveness of these arrangements in achieving quality objectives.

**Four key objectives of internal audit are:**

1. **Verification of conformity** of the QMS to ISO 9001 and internal procedures.
2. **Identification of non-conformities** and risks before they impact customers.
3. **Evaluation of process effectiveness** in achieving defined quality objectives.
4. **Provision of inputs** to the Management Review for evidence-based decision-making.

> *(Any other valid objective — preparing for external certification, promoting continual improvement, ensuring regulatory compliance, etc. — may be credited.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**Differentiate between a Major Non-Conformity and a Minor Non-Conformity with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Major Non-Conformity | Minor Non-Conformity |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Definition** | Total absence or systematic breakdown of a QMS element | Single observed lapse that does not indicate systemic failure |
| **Impact** | Raises serious doubt about the quality of the product or service | Limited impact; isolated in nature |
| **Example** | No calibration programme exists for measuring equipment used in safety-critical testing | One record from a batch of 200 inspection records is missing a signature |
| **Response Time** | Requires immediate corrective action and management notification | Requires correction within normal CAPA cycle |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Note:** In the End Semester Examination, students must answer **one full question per module** carrying 14 marks, typically with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are mutually exclusive choices.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the step-by-step process of conducting an internal audit in an organization as per ISO 19011 guidelines.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing company planned 60 internal audits in 2024. Due to operational disruptions, only 51 were completed. Out of 75 non-conformities raised across the year, 60 were closed within the agreed timeline. Calculate the **Audit Coverage Rate (ACR)** and **NC Closure Rate (NCCR)**. Comment on the results and suggest two corrective actions to improve them.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Internal Audit Process (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Initiating the Audit (1 mark):**
Define audit objectives, scope, and criteria. Appoint the **Lead Auditor** and team. Confirm authority and resources.

**Step 2 — Preparing Audit Activities (1 mark):**
Develop the **Audit Plan** covering dates, locations, departments. Review prior audit reports. Prepare **working documents** — checklists, sampling plans, forms.

**Step 3 — Conducting the Audit (3 marks):**
- **Opening Meeting:** Confirm scope, introduce team, agree on communication.
- **Collecting Evidence:** Use interviews, document review, observation, and data analysis.
- **Generating Findings:** Compare evidence against audit criteria. Identify **conformance, non-conformance, and observations**.
- **Recording:** Use standardized formats; cite specific clause numbers, records, dates.

**Step 4 — Preparing and Distributing the Audit Report (1 mark):**
Issue a **structured report** with findings, conclusions, and recommendations. Distribute to process owners, top management, and the MR team.

**Step 5 — Completing the Audit and Follow-Up (1 mark):**
Conduct a **closing meeting** to present findings. Verify implementation of **corrective actions** within agreed timelines. Confirm effectiveness. Feed results into the **Management Review**.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Planned Audits} &= 60 \\
\text{Conducted Audits} &= 51 \\
\text{Total NCs Raised} &= 75 \\
\text{NCs Closed on Time} &= 60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Calculate ACR (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ACR} &= \dfrac{\text{Conducted Audits}}{\text{Planned Audits}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{51}{60} \times 100 \\
&= 0.85 \times 100 \\
&= 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate NCCR (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCCR} &= \dfrac{\text{NCs Closed on Time}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{60}{75} \times 100 \\
&= 0.80 \times 100 \\
&= 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment and Recommendations (1 mark):**

- **ACR = 85%** indicates **15% of planned audits were missed**, suggesting a need for better resource planning and management commitment to the audit programme.
- **NCCR = 80%** means **20% of NCs remain unresolved**, indicating weak follow-up culture.
- **Two Corrective Actions:**
  1. Implement a **digital audit management system** with automated reminders and escalation alerts.
  2. Conduct **Management Review focus groups** to identify root causes for missed audits and unclosed NCs.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the roles and responsibilities of the various participants in an internal audit. List any **six personal attributes** that a competent internal auditor must possess as per ISO 19011.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is an **Audit Checklist**? Explain its importance, and design a sample audit checklist for the **"Incoming Material Inspection" process** in a manufacturing unit. The checklist should contain at least **six questions**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Roles and Responsibilities + Auditor Attributes (7 Marks):**

**Roles and Responsibilities (4 marks):**

1. **Audit Programme Manager (1 mark):** Establishes, implements, monitors, and reviews the annual audit programme. Ensures resources and competence.
2. **Lead Auditor (1 mark):** Manages the audit team, develops the audit plan, coordinates on-site activities, prepares and presents the audit report.
3. **Auditors (1 mark):** Collect objective evidence through interviews, document review, and observation. Document findings fairly and accurately.
4. **Auditee (1 mark):** Cooperates with auditors, provides access to records and personnel, implements corrective actions.

**Six Personal Attributes of a Competent Auditor (3 marks):**
1. **Ethical** — fair, truthful, sincere, honest, and discreet.
2. **Open-Minded** — willing to consider alternative ideas or viewpoints.
3. **Diplomatic** — tactful in dealing with people.
4. **Observant** — actively aware of physical surroundings and activities.
5. **Perceptive** — instinctively aware of and able to understand situations.
6. **Decisive** — able to reach timely conclusions based on logical reasoning and analysis.

*(Tenacious, self-reliant, versatile may also be credited.)*

---

**(b) Audit Checklist — Definition, Importance, and Sample (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
An **Audit Checklist** is a structured, question-based tool prepared by the auditor to guide the systematic collection of evidence during an internal audit. It ensures that all relevant QMS elements are covered and provides a documented record of the audit's progress.

**Importance (2 marks):**
- Ensures **completeness and consistency** across audits.
- Reduces auditor **bias and omission**.
- Serves as **working evidence** of audit conduct.
- Provides a **training aid** for new auditors.
- Helps in **time management** during on-site audits.

**Sample Checklist for "Incoming Material Inspection" (3 marks):**

| # | Audit Question | Reference | Evidence Required | Conformance (Y/N) | Remarks |
|---|----------------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Is there a documented SOP for incoming material inspection? | ISO 9001 Cl. 7.5 / 8.5 | SOP document number, version, approval date | | |
| 2 | Are raw material inspection records complete and signed? | ISO 9001 Cl. 7.5.3 | Random sample of last 10 GRNs | | |
| 3 | Are measuring instruments used in inspection calibrated? | ISO 9001 Cl. 7.1.5 | Calibration certificates with valid due dates | | |
| 4 | Is there evidence of supplier evaluation and selection? | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.4 | Approved supplier list, evaluation records | | |
| 5 | Are non-conforming materials segregated and identified? | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.7 | Visual observation, NCR register | | |
| 6 | Are Certificates of Analysis (COA) obtained from suppliers? | Customer / Regulatory | Sample COAs attached to GRNs | | |
| 7 | Is there a feedback mechanism for supplier non-conformance? | ISO 9001 Cl. 8.4 | Supplier complaint log | | |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** list audit steps in random order — the **sequence matters** for full marks. Always follow ISO 19011:2018 structure: *Initiating → Preparing → Conducting → Reporting → Follow-up.*
> - **Do NOT** confuse **observation** with **non-conformity**. An observation is a *potential issue*; an NC is a *failed requirement*.
