- ☐ **DO phase principle:** Always execute on a **pilot / small scale** to contain risk.
- ☐ **CHECK phase tools:** Control Charts (SPC), t-tests, histograms, run charts.
- ☐ **ACT phase outcome:** Either **standardize the validated change** OR **re-enter Plan** with refined hypothesis.
- ☐ **Cumulative Improvement Formula:** $\eta_{n} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_{i})$ — explains why small consistent gains outperform rare large leaps.
- ☐ **Linkage to Module 3 tools:** PDCA is the **engine** for Kaizen, Benchmarking, and Six Sigma DMAIC.
- ☐ **DMAIC vs PDCA:** DMAIC's *Improve* ↔ PDCA's *Do*; DMAIC's *Control* ↔ PDCA's *Act*. DMAIC is a **statistically enriched** extension of PDCA.
- ☐ **Common exam trap:** Forgetting to mention **tools per phase** — costs 2–3 marks.
- ☐ **Mandatory diagram:** Always draw a **labelled PDCA loop with feedback arrow** in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ **Iteration language:** Use the words *"continuous," "iterative," "closed-loop," "learning cycle"* explicitly in answers to satisfy KTU's OBE cognitive verb mapping (Apply / Analyze).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kaizen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kaizen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Kaizen** (改善) is a Japanese management philosophy and methodology that translates literally to **"change for the better"** or **"continuous improvement"**. In the context of Total Quality Management (TQM), Kaizen refers to a **systematic, ongoing process of engaging all employees — from the CEO to the shop-floor worker — in making small, incremental, low-cost improvements to products, services, and processes over time**.

The term was popularized by **Masaaki Imai** through his seminal 1986 book *"Kaizen: The Key to Japan's Competitive Success"*, where he positioned Kaizen as the foundational operating philosophy of Japanese industrial competitiveness, particularly within the **Toyota Production System (TPS)**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Terminology Decoded**
> - **Kai (改)** = Change
> - **Zen (善)** = Good / For the better
> - Combined meaning = Continuous, incremental improvement involving everyone

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden" of Kaizen

> [!TIP] **Intuition Pump for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine you own a small **home garden**. You don't wait for the garden to become a wild jungle before tending to it. Every single day — for just 10 minutes — you pull out a few weeds, water a dry patch, prune an overgrown branch, and add a spoonful of compost. After months, no single day's work looked dramatic, but the garden has transformed into a lush paradise.
>
> **Kaizen works exactly the same way in an organization.**
> - The **garden** = your business process
> - The **daily 10 minutes** = small, low-cost improvements
> - The **gardener** = every employee, from CEO to floor worker
> - The **lush paradise** = world-class operational excellence

The opposite approach — a **single massive overhaul** (rebuilding the entire garden from scratch) — is what Kaizen calls **"Innovation"**. Kaizen deliberately rejects this in favor of small, cumulative, low-risk steps.

## 1.3 Position of Kaizen in the TQM Framework

Kaizen is positioned as a **strategic management philosophy** that supports the operational tools of TQM. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Kaizen sits at the intersection of:

| Layer | Element | Role of Kaizen |
|---|---|---|
| **Philosophy** | TQM, Customer Focus | Provides the *mindset* |
| **Strategy** | Continuous Improvement (CI) | Provides the *direction* |
| **Methodology** | PDCA, 5S, Gemba | Provides the *tools* |
| **People** | Employee Involvement | Provides the *culture* |

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Kaizen (Imai's Framework)

Masaaki Imai famously stated that any business operation consists of:

1. **Maintenance (Status Quo Management)** — Maintaining current technological, managerial, and operational standards through discipline and training.
2. **Improvement (Kaizen)** — Increasing standards through small, incremental improvements.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Favourite Line**
> *"Without Maintenance, there can be no Kaizen. The two pillars work together — Maintenance provides the stable baseline, and Kaizen continuously raises that baseline."*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Fundamental Principles of Kaizen

The philosophy is built on **five non-negotiable principles**, which examiners love to test:

### Principle 1 — **Process-Oriented Thinking**
Kaizen believes that **good results come from good processes**, not from forcing good results directly. If you improve the process, results automatically follow.

### Principle 2 — **PDCA / Deming Cycle as the Operating System**
Every Kaizen improvement is executed through the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle. There are no "one-shot fixes" in Kaizen — every change is a hypothesis to be tested and refined.

### Principle 3 — **Speak with Data, Manage by Facts**
Improvements must be **measured**, not assumed. Before-and-after data is mandatory to prove a change actually improved things.

### Principle 4 — **Root-Cause Problem Solving**
Kaizen targets the **root cause** of a problem using tools like the **5 Whys**, not just the symptom. Fixing symptoms leads to recurrence; fixing root causes eliminates the problem forever.

### Principle 5 — **Standardization Locks in the Gain**
After improvement, the new (better) method must be written down as the **new standard (SOP)**. Without standardization, old habits will creep back in 30 days later.

## 2.2 The Gemba Philosophy — Where the Truth Lives

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition (Exam-Critical)**
> **Gemba (現場)** = The "actual place" where work happens — the shop floor, the hospital ward, the airport check-in counter, the call center floor.
>
> **Gemba Walk** = A management practice where leaders physically go to the Gemba to observe the process firsthand, ask questions, and identify improvement opportunities.

The famous Kaizen saying: **"Go to Gemba first, see the problem, solve the problem."**

## 2.3 The 5S Methodology — Kaizen's Housekeeping Foundation

5S is the **gateway to Kaizen**. You cannot improve a chaotic workplace; you must first clean and organize it.

> [!NOTE] **The 5S System (Originally Japanese, now universally used)**
>
> | # | Japanese | English | Meaning |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace |
> | 2 | **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange necessary items so they are easy to find and use |
> | 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace and equipment thoroughly |
> | 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish procedures and schedules for the first 3S |
> | 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Maintain discipline through training and audits |

## 2.4 Kaizen Events (Blitz Kaizen) vs. Daily Kaizen

| Feature | **Daily Kaizen** | **Kaizen Event / Blitz** |
|---|---|---|
| Duration | Continuous, ongoing | 3 to 5 days intensive workshop |
| Scope | Small, incremental | Targeted, focused on one process |
| Team | Anyone in the process | Cross-functional dedicated team |
| Goal | Culture of CI | Rapid breakthrough on a specific issue |
| Frequency | Always | Periodic (when a problem arises) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — The Quantitative Side of Kaizen

> [!TIP] **KTU Formula Box — Memorize These Five Equations**
>
> Although Kaizen is qualitative, KTU expects students to know its numerical metrics. Use **\vert** for absolute value bars inside tables.

| # | Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kaizen Savings (Cost Reduction)** | $S = C_{before} - C_{after}$ | Monetary value saved per unit/period |
| 2 | **Productivity Improvement %** | $\Delta P\% = \dfrac{P_{after} - P_{before}}{P_{before}} \times 100$ | % increase in output per input |
| 3 | **Cycle Time Reduction %** | $\Delta CT\% = \dfrac{CT_{before} - CT_{after}}{CT_{before}} \times 100$ | % decrease in process time |
| 4 | **Defect Rate Reduction %** | $\Delta D\% = \dfrac{D_{before} - D_{after}}{D_{before}} \times 100$ | % drop in defects |
| 5 | **First-Pass Yield (FPY)** | $FPY = \dfrac{N_{good\_first\_time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % of units made right the first time |

## 2.6 Difference Between Kaizen and Innovation

This is a **guaranteed exam question** (2-marker or 5-marker).

| Parameter | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
|---|---|---|
| Japanese meaning | Continuous improvement | Renewal / revolutionary change |
| Magnitude | Small, incremental | Large, breakthrough |
| Time horizon | Continuous, ongoing | Discrete, one-time |
| Risk | Low | High |
| Investment | Minimal | Heavy R&D / CAPEX |
| Origin of idea | Suggestions from workers | R&D labs, specialists |
| Approach | Bottom-up | Top-down |
| Focus | Process | Technology / Product |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kaizen is deployed in industries far beyond car manufacturing:

- **Manufacturing**: Reducing setup time (SMED), eliminating the **7 Wastes (Muda)**.
- **Healthcare**: Reducing patient waiting time in OPDs.
- **IT/Software**: Reducing bug-fix cycle time, Agile sprints.
- **Service sector**: Reducing passenger check-in time at airports.
- **Logistics**: Reducing warehouse picking time.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Standard Kaizen Implementation Roadmap (12-Step Model)

This is the **12-step framework** published by the Kaizen Institute and used as a textbook reference globally.

> [!IMPORTANT] **The 12 Steps of Kaizen Implementation**

| Step | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Form a Kaizen Promotion Office (KPO) | Central coordination body |
| 2 | Develop a master plan | Strategic roadmap |
| 3 | Identify target areas | Focus efforts |
| 4 | Establish baseline performance | "As-Is" measurement |
| 5 | Form Kaizen teams | Workforce engagement |
| 6 | Train in 5S & PDCA | Build capability |
| 7 | Conduct Gemba walks | Find real problems |
| 8 | Generate suggestions | Crowdsource ideas |
| 9 | Implement improvements | Execute changes |
| 10 | Measure results | Validate with data |
| 11 | Standardize the improvement | Lock in the gain |
| 12 | Recognize & reward | Sustain motivation |

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Cycle Time Reduction Problem

> [!NOTE] **Numerical Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU ESE)**
> A machining cell at an Indian auto-components plant currently has a **cycle time of 12 minutes per part**. The plant applies Kaizen and through 5S + setup time reduction (SMED), reduces it to **8 minutes per part**. Calculate:
> (a) The Cycle Time Reduction %.
> (b) The Productivity Improvement % (assuming daily output before = 8-hour shift × 60 min / 12 min).
> (c) The annual Kaizen Savings if one operator's wage is ₹400/hour and the saved time is fully utilized.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Given:**
- $CT_{before} = 12$ minutes
- $CT_{after} = 8$ minutes
- Shift length = 8 hours = 480 minutes
- Wage = ₹400/hour

**Step 1 — Cycle Time Reduction (%)**

$$
\Delta CT\% = \dfrac{CT_{before} - CT_{after}}{CT_{before}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta CT\% = \dfrac{12 - 8}{12} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta CT\% = \dfrac{4}{12} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta CT\% = 33.33\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The process became **33.33% faster**.

**Step 2 — Productivity Improvement (%)**

Output before ($P_{before}$):
$$
P_{before} = \dfrac{480}{12} = 40 \text{ parts/day}
$$

Output after ($P_{after}$):
$$
P_{after} = \dfrac{480}{8} = 60 \text{ parts/day}
$$

Productivity gain:
$$
\Delta P\% = \dfrac{60 - 40}{40} \times 100 = \dfrac{20}{40} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** The same operator now produces **50% more parts** in the same shift — without any new equipment.

**Step 3 — Annual Kaizen Savings**

Extra parts produced per day = $60 - 40 = 20$ parts/day

Assuming 300 working days/year:
$$
Extra\ parts/year = 20 \times 300 = 6000\ parts
$$

If each part has a contribution margin of ₹50:
$$
S = 6000 \times 50 = \text{₹ } 3,00,000 \text{ per year}
$$

Alternatively, if we compute **labour-time savings**:
$$
Time\ saved\ per\ part = 12 - 8 = 4\ minutes
$$
$$
Total\ time\ saved\ per\ day = 20 \times 4 = 80\ minutes
$$
$$
Hours\ saved\ per\ year = \dfrac{80 \times 300}{60} = 400\ hours
$$
$$
S = 400 \times 400 = \text{₹ } 1,60,000 \text{ per year}
$$

## 3.3 The 5 Whys Root-Cause Technique — Worked Example

> [!NOTE] **Toyoda Production System Tool — Often Asked in KTU**
> **Problem:** A CNC machine stopped in the middle of a shift.
>
> | Why # | Question | Answer |
> |---|---|---|
> | Why 1 | Why did the machine stop? | Because a fuse blew. |
> | Why 2 | Why did the fuse blow? | Because the motor was overloaded. |
> | Why 3 | Why was the motor overloaded? | Because the bearing was not lubricated. |
> | Why 4 | Why was the bearing not lubricated? | Because the lube pump was not pumping. |
> | Why 5 | Why was the lube pump not pumping? | Because the suction pipe was clogged with sludge. |
>
> **Root Cause:** Sludge accumulation in the suction pipe → **Solution:** Install a regular cleaning schedule (a new standard).

Notice how the **symptom** was a blown fuse, but the **root cause** was 5 levels deep. Fixing only the fuse would have led to recurrence.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Role of Management vs. Workers in Kaizen

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Exam Mapping Table**

| Activity | Management's Role | Worker's Role |
|---|---|---|
| Setting improvement targets | ✓ Decides | — |
| Suggesting improvements | Welcomes | ✓ Submits suggestions |
| Gemba Walks | ✓ Walks and observes | — |
| Implementing changes | Approves & funds | ✓ Executes |
| Standardization | ✓ Writes SOPs | ✓ Follows SOPs |
| Training | ✓ Provides | ✓ Attends |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The PDCA / Deming Cycle (Kaizen's Operating Engine)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["PLAN Identify problem and propose countermeasure"]
    nodeB["DO Implement the plan on a small scale"]
    nodeC["CHECK Measure results against the expected outcome"]
    nodeD["ACT Standardize the improvement or revise the plan"]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeA
```

> [!TIP] **Reading Guide**
> The cycle is **continuous** — it never ends. After standardizing (ACT), a new PLAN begins for the next improvement opportunity. This is the heart of Kaizen.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — The Two Pillars of Kaizen (Imai's Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRoot["BUSINESS OPERATION"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeA["MAINTENANCE Pillar 1 Discipline and Standardization"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB["IMPROVEMENT Pillar 2 Continuous Kaizen"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Stable Baseline"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Rising Standards"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Operational Excellence"]
    nodeD --> nodeE
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — The 5S Pyramid of Kaizen Foundation

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS5["SHITSUKE SUSTAIN Discipline and Training"]
    nodeS4["SEIKETSU STANDARDIZE Procedures and Schedules"]
    nodeS3["SEISO SHINE Clean Workplace and Equipment"]
    nodeS2["SEITON SET IN ORDER Arrange Items Logically"]
    nodeS1["SEIRI SORT Remove Unnecessary Items"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS1
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading Guide**
> 5S is built **bottom-up**. Without Sort at the base, the higher levels cannot exist. Skipping 5S is the #1 reason Kaizen programs fail in Indian SMEs.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — The 12-Step Kaizen Implementation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["1 Form Kaizen Promotion Office"]
    node2["2 Develop Master Plan"]
    node3["3 Identify Target Areas"]
    node4["4 Measure Baseline As-Is State"]
    node5["5 Form Cross-Functional Teams"]
    node6["6 Train Teams in 5S and PDCA"]
    node7["7 Conduct Gemba Walks"]
    node8["8 Generate Suggestions"]
    node9["9 Pilot Implement Improvements"]
    node10["10 Measure To-Be Results"]
    node11["11 Standardize the New Method"]
    node12["12 Recognize and Reward Teams"]
    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
    node12 -.->|Start New Cycle| node1
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Kaizen vs. Innovation Positioning Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph K["KAIZEN ZONE"]
        k1["Small Incremental Change"]
        k2["Low Cost and Low Risk"]
        k3["Bottom-Up Suggestions"]
        k4["Process Focused"]
    end
    subgraph I["INNOVATION ZONE"]
        i1["Large Breakthrough Change"]
        i2["High Investment and High Risk"]
        i3["Top-Down R and D"]
        i4["Technology Focused"]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight**
> Kaizen and Innovation are **complementary, not competing**. Toyota famously uses **"Kaizen + Innovation"** — small improvements daily, with occasional revolutionary breakthroughs. KTU expects students to articulate this balanced view.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define Kaizen. Explain the meaning of its two Japanese syllables.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Words Highlighted):**
>
> **Kaizen (改善)** is a Japanese management philosophy meaning **"continuous improvement"**. The term is composed of two kanji characters:
>
> 1. **Kai (改)** = "Change" — referring to the act of transformation.
> 2. **Zen (善)** = "Good / For the better" — referring to the direction of the change.
>
> Together, Kaizen means making small, incremental, continuous changes **for the better** in processes, products, and services. It was popularized by **Masaaki Imai** as the foundational philosophy of the **Toyota Production System** and Japanese industrial success.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain the 5S methodology of Kaizen.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks — Each S Worth ~0.6 Marks):**
>
> The 5S methodology is the foundational housekeeping framework of Kaizen. It consists of:
>
> 1. **Seiri (Sort)** — Remove all unnecessary items from the workplace.
> 2. **Seiton (Set in Order)** — Arrange required items so they are easy to locate and use.
> 3. **Seiso (Shine)** — Clean the workplace and equipment thoroughly.
> 4. **Seiketsu (Standardize)** — Establish schedules and procedures for the first three S.
> 5. **Shitsuke (Sustain)** — Maintain discipline through training, audits, and management commitment.
>
> 5S creates the stable, organized workplace that is a **prerequisite** for all further Kaizen improvements.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE] **Module Internal Choice**
> Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length Model Question

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Predicted Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **five fundamental principles of Kaizen**. How do these principles differentiate Kaizen from traditional Western management approaches?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the role of **Gemba Walks** and **5S** in implementing Kaizen in a manufacturing organization. Illustrate with one real-world example.

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Five Fundamental Principles of Kaizen

The five fundamental principles are:

**1. Process-Oriented Thinking (1.5 Marks)**
Kaizen believes that **good results emerge from good processes**, not from forcing good results directly. Improvement is achieved by systematically improving the process rather than blaming workers for poor outcomes.

**2. PDCA / Deming Cycle (1.5 Marks)**
Every improvement is executed using the **Plan-Do-Check-Act** cycle. The change is planned as a hypothesis, tested at a small scale, verified with data, and then standardized.

**3. Speak with Data (1 Mark)**
Improvements must be backed by **measurable data** — before/after metrics like defect %, cycle time, downtime hours. Assumptions and opinions are insufficient.

**4. Root-Cause Problem Solving using 5 Whys (1.5 Marks)**
Kaizen targets the **root cause** of a problem, not the symptom. Tools like the **5 Whys** analysis are used to drill down to the actual cause, ensuring the problem does not recur.

**5. Standardization as the Lock-In Step (1.5 Marks)**
After improvement, the new method must be written into **Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs)**. Without this, employees drift back to old habits within weeks, and the improvement is lost.

**Differentiation from Western Management (Bonus Point):**
Traditional Western management often focuses on **big-bang innovations, individual heroics, and quarterly results**. Kaizen emphasizes **collective effort, incremental gains, long-term culture, and respect for people**.

#### Part (b) — Gemba Walks and 5S in Practice

**Role of Gemba Walks (3.5 Marks)**

A **Gemba Walk** is a structured management practice where senior leaders physically go to the actual workplace (shop floor, service counter, hospital ward) to observe processes and identify improvement opportunities. It has three purposes:
- **See the reality** (data on paper can be misleading).
- **Show respect** to frontline workers (their work is valued).
- **Spot problems** that workers may have stopped noticing because they are "normalized."

**Role of 5S (3.5 Marks)**

5S creates the **stable, organized, visual workplace** without which no Kaizen improvement can be sustained. It eliminates the wastes of searching, motion, and unsafe conditions.

**Real-World Example (Bonus Point):**
At the **Toyota Georgetown Plant (USA)**, managers conduct daily Gemba walks of 30 minutes on the shop floor. Combined with 5S, this led to a reduction in **setup time from 11 hours to under 3 minutes** for die changes — a classic SMED success story.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Alternative Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Distinguish clearly between **Kaizen and Innovation** with a comparison on at least six parameters. When should a company choose one over the other?

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** in detail. How does it act as the operating engine for Kaizen improvement? Describe one practical example of its application.

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Kaizen vs. Innovation

| Parameter | **Kaizen** | **Innovation** |
|---|---|---|
| **Meaning** | Continuous incremental improvement | Revolutionary breakthrough change |
| **Magnitude** | Small steps | Large leaps |
| **Time horizon** | Continuous, ongoing | Discrete, one-time |
| **Risk** | Low risk | High risk |
| **Investment** | Minimal | Heavy CAPEX / R&D |
| **Source of ideas** | Workers (bottom-up) | R&D labs (top-down) |
| **Focus** | Process improvement | Technology / Product breakthrough |

**When to choose (1 Mark for choice logic):**
- Choose **Kaizen** when the existing process is fundamentally sound and only needs fine-tuning, or when the company wants to build a culture of daily improvement.
- Choose **Innovation** when the existing technology is obsolete, competitors have leapfrogged, or a new market demands entirely new capabilities.
- **Best practice**: Use both together — Toyota's philosophy is "Kaizen as the river, Innovation as the stones."

#### Part (b) — The PDCA Cycle in Detail

**Plan (1.5 Marks):** Identify the problem, analyze the root cause (using 5 Whys or fishbone), and propose a countermeasure. Set a measurable target (e.g., reduce defects from 8% to 2%).

**Do (1.5 Marks):** Implement the proposed change on a **small pilot scale** to minimize risk. Train the involved workers and document the procedure.

**Check (1.5 Marks):** Compare the measured results against the target using data collection. If target met → move to Act; if not → revise plan and loop back.

**Act (1 Mark):** Standardize the new method by updating SOPs, training all workers, and locking in the gain.

**Role as Kaizen Engine (1 Mark):** PDCA is the **repeatable operating system** that turns Kaizen from a slogan into a structured discipline. Every Kaizen event follows PDCA, and after one cycle, the cycle restarts for the next improvement — driving the never-ending journey of continuous improvement.

**Practical Example (0.5 Marks):** A hospital's Emergency Department applied PDCA to reduce patient waiting time. Plan: identify triage bottleneck. Do: implement parallel registration system. Check: waiting time dropped from 45 min to 18 min. Act: standardized new triage SOP across all shifts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE**
>
> 1. **Confusing 5S with 5 Why**: 5S is the housekeeping methodology (Sort, Set, Shine, Standardize, Sustain). 5 Whys is the root-cause technique. Students often write one in place of the other — this costs **2 full marks**.
>
> 2. **Spelling "Kaizen" as "Kaizan", "Kaisen" or "Kaizn"**: Examiners deduct marks for phonetic spelling errors. Memorize it as **K-A-I-Z-E-N**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting "Gemba"**: Many students define Kaizen without mentioning Gemba. Since the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly includes Gemba, omitting it costs at least **1 mark**.
>
> 4. **Writing Innovation and Kaizen as synonyms**: They are *complementary opposites*. Writing them as the same thing is a fatal conceptual error.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning Masaaki Imai**: In any Kaizen question, citing the original thinker (Imai, 1986) and origin (Japan / Toyota) is mandatory in the opening line for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist for Exam Day**

- ⭐ **Definition**: Kaizen = continuous improvement (kai = change, zen = good).
- ⭐ **Origin**: Japan, post-WWII; popularized by **Masaaki Imai (1986)**; core of **Toyota Production System**.
- ⭐ **Two Pillars**: Maintenance + Improvement (both required).
- ⭐ **Five Principles**: Process focus, PDCA, data-based, root-cause solving, standardization.
- ⭐ **Gemba**: The actual place where work happens — leaders must visit it.
- ⭐ **5S**: Seiri, Seiton, Seiso, Seiketsu, Shitsuke — the foundation of all Kaizen work.
- ⭐ **PDCA Cycle**: Plan → Do → Check → Act — the engine of every Kaizen event.
- ⭐ **5 Whys**: Root-cause analysis by asking "why" five times.
- ⭐ **Kaizen vs. Innovation**: Incremental vs. breakthrough; low risk vs. high risk; bottom-up vs. top-down.
- ⭐ **12-Step Roadmap**: KPO, master plan, baseline, teams, training, Gemba, suggestions, implement, measure, standardize, reward.
- ⭐ **Formulae**: $\Delta CT\%$, $\Delta P\%$, $\Delta D\%$, $FPY$, $S$ — must be memorized.
- ⭐ **Daily Kaizen vs. Kaizen Event**: Continuous vs. 3–5-day blitz.
- ⭐ **Key Metric to Quote**: "Without Maintenance, there is no Kaizen."
- ⭐ **KTU Citation Rule**: Always mention "Kaizen is a Japanese philosophy popularized by Masaaki Imai in 1986 as part of TQM."

---
*End of Premium KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 3, Topic: Kaizen*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven Basic Tools of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seven Basic Tools of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Basic Tools of Quality (7QC Tools)** — A standardized set of graphical and statistical techniques, popularized by **Professor Kaoru Ishikawa** of the University of Tokyo in 1943, used by quality professionals worldwide to **diagnose, analyze, monitor, and resolve** quality-related problems in manufacturing, service, and process industries. These tools form the foundational layer of Total Quality Management (TQM) and are explicitly recommended by KTU for quality practitioners as a prerequisite before adopting advanced tools like **SPC, FMEA, or Six Sigma**.

The seven tools are:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone Diagram)
2. **Check Sheet** (Tally Sheet)
3. **Control Chart** (Statistical Process Control Chart)
4. **Histogram** (Frequency Distribution Plot)
5. **Pareto Chart** (80/20 Analysis)
6. **Scatter Diagram** (Correlation Plot)
7. **Flowchart** (Process Mapping)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "A Mechanic's Diagnostic Toolkit"**
> Imagine you are a doctor visiting a patient who complains of chronic headaches. Before prescribing medicine, you don't immediately write a prescription — you first record symptoms (Check Sheet), draw a body map of pain zones (Cause-and-Effect), track blood pressure over time (Control Chart), analyze historical patient data (Histogram), identify the most common triggers (Pareto Chart), examine the link between stress and sleep (Scatter Diagram), and map out the diagnostic procedure (Flowchart).
> **The 7QC tools play the EXACT same role for a Quality Engineer.** They are the *first line of investigation* before invoking advanced statistical weaponry. Ishikawa believed that **>90% of all quality problems in an organization can be solved using only these seven tools** — without any sophisticated software.

### 1.3 Why These Tools Are Mandatory in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** As per the 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, students must be able to **identify, construct, and interpret** each of the seven tools. The Board Examination explicitly tests:
> - **Construction logic** (how to draw/tabulate)
> - **Interpretation** (what the chart *tells* you)
> - **Selection** (which tool is appropriate for a given problem)

### 1.4 The Ishikawa Philosophy

Kaoru Ishikawa firmly believed that quality control should **not be restricted to specialized statisticians**. His objective was to democratize quality by creating tools so simple that **any worker on the shop floor** could apply them. This is why the 7QC tools rely primarily on **visual reasoning, graphical data, and elementary arithmetic** — no advanced calculus or probability theory is required.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Tool 1 — Cause-and-Effect Diagram (Fishbone / Ishikawa Diagram)

**Operational Purpose:** Identifies the *potential root causes* of a problem (the "effect") by categorizing them into major cause branches.

**The 6M Categorization (Manufacturing Context):**

| Branch | Typical Causes |
|---|---|
| **Man (People)** | Skill gaps, training, fatigue, motivation |
| **Machine** | Wear, calibration, breakdown, age |
| **Material** | Supplier quality, storage, specifications |
| **Method** | Procedures, workflow, work instructions |
| **Measurement** | Gauge accuracy, calibration, sampling |
| **Mother Nature (Environment)** | Temperature, humidity, dust, lighting |

> [!NOTE]
> In **service industries**, the branches are often modified to **4S**: *Surroundings, Suppliers, Systems, Skills*.

**Construction Logic:**
- Draw a horizontal arrow pointing right (the "spine")
- Write the **problem statement** at the arrowhead
- Draw diagonal "bones" branching off the spine for each major cause category
- Under each branch, list sub-causes as smaller "fishbones"

---

### 2.2 Tool 2 — Check Sheet

**Operational Purpose:** A **structured data collection form** that records the *frequency* of defect occurrences in real-time at the workplace.

**Design Logic:**
- Define the event to be counted (e.g., defect type)
- Create columns for date, shift, location, defect category
- Workers place **tally marks (||||)** as defects occur
- At the end of the period, totals are computed

> [!NOTE]
> A check sheet is the **input data source** for Pareto charts, histograms, and control charts. Without clean raw data, the other tools cannot function.

---

### 2.3 Tool 3 — Control Chart (Shewhart Chart)

**Operational Purpose:** Monitors a process **over time** to detect whether the process is in a state of **statistical control**.

**Key Components:**
- **Center Line (CL):** The process mean
- **Upper Control Limit (UCL):** CL + 3σ
- **Lower Control Limit (LCL):** CL − 3σ
- **Plot:** Time-ordered measurements of a quality characteristic

**Two Types of Variation:**
- **Common Cause Variation:** Inherent, predictable, within control limits → improve the system
- **Special Cause Variation:** Unexpected, due to external shocks → investigate immediately

---

### 2.4 Tool 4 — Histogram

**Operational Purpose:** Visualizes the **frequency distribution** of a continuous variable to assess its shape, central tendency, and spread.

**Interpretation Guidelines:**

| Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Normal (Bell curve)** | Process is stable, well-behaved |
| **Skewed Right** | Upper specification limit may be at risk |
| **Skewed Left** | Lower specification limit may be at risk |
| **Bimodal** | Two distinct populations are mixed |
| **Plateau** | Process has wide uncontrolled variation |

---

### 2.5 Tool 5 — Pareto Chart

**Operational Purpose:** Applies the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)** — typically *80% of the problems* are caused by *20% of the causes*. Helps prioritize which problems to tackle first.

**Construction Logic:**
- Sort defect categories in **descending order of frequency**
- Plot a **bar chart** for frequency
- Overlay a **cumulative percentage curve** (secondary y-axis)

---

### 2.6 Tool 6 — Scatter Diagram

**Operational Purpose:** Reveals the **relationship (correlation)** between two continuous variables.

**Correlation Patterns:**

| Pattern | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| Tight upward line | **Strong Positive** correlation |
| Tight downward line | **Strong Negative** correlation |
| Loose upward trend | **Weak Positive** correlation |
| Random cloud | **No correlation** |
| Curved pattern | **Non-linear** relationship |

> [!NOTE]
> **Correlation ≠ Causation.** A scatter diagram shows association but does **not** prove one variable causes another.

---

### 2.7 Tool 7 — Flowchart

**Operational Purpose:** A **graphical representation of a process** showing the sequence of steps, decision points, and loops.

**Standard Symbols:**

| Symbol | Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Oval | `Oval` | Start / End (Terminator) |
| Rectangle | `Box` | Process step (Action) |
| Diamond | `Diamond` | Decision (Yes/No question) |
| Parallelogram | `Parallelogram` | Input / Output |
| Arrow | `Arrow` | Flow direction |

---

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU Board Examinations.** Memorize all entries below.

| # | Formula / Constant | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | X-bar chart Upper Control Limit | — |
| 2 | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | X-bar chart Lower Control Limit | — |
| 3 | $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$ | R chart Upper Control Limit | — |
| 4 | $LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R}$ | R chart Lower Control Limit | — |
| 5 | $UCL_X = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$ | Individuals chart UCL (Shewhart) | — |
| 6 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index | Dimensionless, target $\geq 1.33$ |
| 7 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Process Capability (shifted) | Target $\geq 1.33$ |
| 8 | $\sigma \approx \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Estimate of σ from average range | — |
| 9 | **Pareto Rule** | 80% problems from 20% causes | Qualitative |
| 10 | **Zone Rule** (Western Electric) | 1 point beyond 3σ = out of control | 0.27% probability |

**Standard Control Chart Constants Table (n = subgroup size):**

| n | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0 | 2.575 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |

---

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Domains:**
> - **Manufacturing:** Monitor dimensional tolerances of machined parts (Control Chart + Histogram)
> - **Software Engineering:** Track bug categories and prioritize fixes (Pareto + Fishbone)
> - **Healthcare:** Reduce patient wait times (Flowchart + Check Sheet)
> - **Service Industry:** Analyze customer complaints (Pareto + Scatter)
> - **Six Sigma DMAIC:** These 7 tools are the **Define & Measure phase workhorses**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Control Chart Limits from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define the Central Limit Theorem Foundation**

When samples of size *n* are drawn from a process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the sampling distribution of the sample mean $\bar{X}$ has:

- **Mean of $\bar{X}$:** $\mu_{\bar{X}} = \mu$
- **Standard deviation of $\bar{X}$:** $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

This follows directly from the Central Limit Theorem.

**Step 2 — Apply the 3-Sigma Shewhart Principle**

Walter Shewhart (1924) demonstrated that almost all (**99.73%**) of a normally distributed variable lies within $\pm 3\sigma$ of its mean. Therefore, the natural control limits are:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} + 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu + \dfrac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

$$
LCL_{\bar{X}} = \mu_{\bar{X}} - 3\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \mu - \dfrac{3\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

$$
CL_{\bar{X}} = \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Range-Based Estimator**

Since the process standard deviation $\sigma$ is rarely known in practice, we **estimate it using the average range $\bar{R}$**:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}
$$

Where $d_2$ is a tabulated constant dependent on the subgroup size *n* (see table above).

**Step 4 — Derive the A₂ Constant**

Substituting $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$ into the UCL equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \mu + \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{n}} \cdot \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} \\
&= \mu + \left(\dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}\right) \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, the constant $A_2$ equals $\dfrac{3}{d_2 \sqrt{n}}$, which yields:

$$
UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}
$$

Where $\bar{\bar{X}}$ is the grand mean of all sample averages. This elegant relationship is **THE most important formula** in SPC.

**Step 5 — Similar Derivation for R-chart**

For the range chart, the average range is the centerline, and the control limits are based on the standard deviation of the range, denoted $\sigma_R = d_3 \sigma$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= \bar{R} + 3 d_3 \sigma = \bar{R}\left(1 + \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}\right) = D_4 \bar{R} \\
LCL_R &= \bar{R} - 3 d_3 \sigma = \bar{R}\left(1 - \dfrac{3 d_3}{d_2}\right) = D_3 \bar{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $D_3 = 1 - 3d_3/d_2$ and $D_4 = 1 + 3d_3/d_2$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Constructing an X-bar Chart

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU Model):** A bearing manufacturer takes 5 samples of size n = 4 every hour. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.20$ mm and the average range is $\bar{R} = 0.80$ mm. Specification: $50.00 \pm 1.50$ mm. Construct the X-bar and R charts.

**Step 1 — Look up the constants for n = 4**

From the table: $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.282$

**Step 2 — Compute X-bar chart limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.20 + (0.729)(0.80) \\
&= 50.20 + 0.5832 = 50.783 \text{ mm} \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= 50.20 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 50.20 - 0.5832 = 49.617 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute R-chart limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.282)(0.80) = 1.826 \text{ mm} \\
CL_R &= 0.80 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.80) = 0.00 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Process Capability Index**

First estimate σ:

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.80}{2.059} = 0.3885 \text{ mm}
$$

Then compute $C_p$:

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{51.50 - 48.50}{6 \times 0.3885} = \dfrac{3.00}{2.331} = 1.287
$$

Since $C_p < 1.33$, the process is **marginally capable** but does not meet the KTU recommended threshold of 1.33.

---

### 3.3 Pareto Chart Cumulative Percentage Formula

For the *i*-th ranked category:

$$
C_i = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{k} f_j} \times 100\%
$$

Where:
- $f_j$ = frequency of the *j*-th category
- $k$ = total number of categories
- $C_i$ = cumulative percentage through rank *i*

**Numerical Example (KTU Practice):**

| Defect Type | Frequency | Cumulative | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Surface Scratch | 42 | 42 | 52.5% |
| Dimensional Out | 23 | 65 | 81.3% |
| Color Mismatch | 8 | 73 | 91.3% |
| Packaging Damage | 5 | 78 | 97.5% |
| Other | 2 | 80 | 100.0% |

**Interpretation:** The **first two categories** (Surface Scratch + Dimensional Out) account for **81.3%** of all defects. According to the Pareto Principle, the Quality Manager should prioritize eliminating these two defects to gain the maximum benefit.

---

### 3.4 Scatter Diagram Correlation Coefficient

The **Pearson correlation coefficient** quantifies linear association:

$$
r = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$

**Interpretation Bands:**

| r Range | Strength |
|---|---|
| $0.8 \leq \vert r \vert \leq 1.0$ | Strong |
| $0.5 \leq \vert r \vert < 0.8$ | Moderate |
| $0.3 \leq \vert r \vert < 0.5$ | Weak |
| $\vert r \vert < 0.3$ | Negligible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Map of the 7 Basic Quality Tools

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1["Seven Basic Tools of Quality - Ishikawa 1943"]
    root1 --> cat1["Problem Identification"]
    root1 --> cat2["Data Collection"]
    root1 --> cat3["Data Analysis"]
    root1 --> cat4["Process Monitoring"]

    cat1 --> tool1["Cause and Effect Diagram"]
    cat1 --> tool7["Flowchart"]

    cat2 --> tool2["Check Sheet"]

    cat3 --> tool4["Histogram"]
    cat3 --> tool5["Pareto Chart"]
    cat3 --> tool6["Scatter Diagram"]

    cat4 --> tool3["Control Chart"]

    style root1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style cat1 fill:#fde68a,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style cat2 fill:#bbf7d0,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style cat3 fill:#fecaca,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style cat4 fill:#c7d2fe,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool1 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool2 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool3 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool4 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool5 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool6 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style tool7 fill:#ffffff,color:#000,stroke:#000
```

### 4.2 Fishbone Diagram Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    eff1["EFFECT - High Defect Rate"]
    eff1 --> man1["Man - Operator Skill Gap"]
    eff1 --> mac1["Machine - Tool Wear"]
    eff1 --> mat1["Material - Supplier Variability"]
    eff1 --> met1["Method - Outdated SOP"]
    eff1 --> msr1["Measurement - Gauge Drift"]
    eff1 --> env1["Environment - Temperature Fluctuation"]

    man1 --> man2["Inadequate Training"]
    man1 --> man3["Shift Fatigue"]

    mac1 --> mac2["No Preventive Maintenance"]
    mac1 --> mac3["Wrong Tool Selection"]

    mat1 --> mat4["Multiple Suppliers"]
    mat1 --> mat5["Storage Damage"]

    met1 --> met6["No Version Control"]
    met1 --> met7["Skipped Steps"]

    msr1 --> msr8["No Calibration Schedule"]
    msr1 --> msr9["Rounding Errors"]

    env1 --> envA["AC Failure in Shop Floor"]
    env1 --> envB["Dust Contamination"]

    style eff1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style man1 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000,stroke:#000
    style mac1 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000,stroke:#000

```


### 4.4 Control Chart Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    inpA["Raw Measurement Data X1 X2 ... Xn"]
    inpB["Subgroup Size n"]
    inpA --> calc1["Calculate Sample Mean"]
    inpA --> calc2["Calculate Sample Range"]
    inpB --> calc1
    inpB --> calc2

    calc1 --> chartA["X-bar Chart Plot"]

    chartA --> dec1["Within Control Limits?"]

    dec1 -->|Yes| okA["Process Stable"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the Seven Basic Tools of Quality as proposed by Kaoru Ishikawa. State the primary purpose of any TWO tools in brief.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Seven Basic Tools of Quality proposed by **Kaoru Ishikawa (1943)** are:
1. Cause-and-Effect Diagram
2. Check Sheet
3. Control Chart
4. Histogram
5. Pareto Chart
6. Scatter Diagram
7. Flowchart

**Purpose of any two (with 1.5 marks each):**
- **Cause-and-Effect Diagram:** Identifies and organizes the possible root causes of a quality problem into major categories (6M: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature), helping teams perform structured brainstorming.
- **Pareto Chart:** Applies the 80/20 principle to prioritize problems by separating the "vital few" causes (responsible for ~80% of the problem) from the "trivial many" causes (remaining ~20%).

*(Equivalent explanation for any other tool also earns full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Common Cause Variation and Special Cause Variation in the context of a Control Chart. Give one example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Common Cause Variation | Special Cause Variation |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Inherent to the process | External to the process |
| **Predictability** | Stable, predictable | Unpredictable, sporadic |
| **Control Chart** | All points within 3σ limits | One or more points outside 3σ limits |
| **Action** | Fundamental system change required | Investigate and remove the specific cause |
| **Example** | Slight dimensional variation due to ambient temperature fluctuation in a CNC machine | Sudden spike in defect rate due to power outage or operator error |

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction methodology of a **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram**. Discuss the **6M** framework with two example sub-causes under each category. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A bearing manufacturer collects 5 samples of size n = 5 every hour. The grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = 32.05$ mm and average range $\bar{R} = 0.45$ mm. Compute the **X-bar and R-chart control limits**. Specification is $32.00 \pm 1.00$ mm. Comment on the process capability. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Fishbone Diagram:**

A Cause-and-Effect Diagram is constructed as follows:
1. Draw a horizontal arrow pointing from left to right (this is the "spine").
2. Write the **problem statement** (the EFFECT) in a box at the right end.
3. Draw 6 diagonal "bones" branching from the spine — one for each of the 6M categories.
4. Under each bone, list 2–4 specific sub-causes identified through team brainstorming.

**6M Framework with Sub-causes (in a manufacturing context):**

| 6M Category | Sub-Cause 1 | Sub-Cause 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Man (People)** | Inadequate operator training | Fatigue due to long shifts |
| **Machine** | Lack of preventive maintenance | Improper tool selection |
| **Material** | Incoming material variability | Improper storage conditions |
| **Method** | Outdated work instructions | Skipped quality checkpoints |
| **Measurement** | Uncalibrated gauges | Human rounding errors |
| **Mother Nature (Environment)** | Temperature variation | Dust and humidity |

**Part (b) — Control Chart Computation:**

For n = 5, from standard table: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$

**X-bar Chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_{\bar{X}} &= \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 32.05 + (0.577)(0.45) \\
&= 32.05 + 0.2597 = 32.310 \text{ mm} \\
CL_{\bar{X}} &= 32.050 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_{\bar{X}} &= 32.05 - 0.2597 = 31.790 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**R-chart:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
UCL_R &= D_4 \bar{R} = (2.114)(0.45) = 0.951 \text{ mm} \\
CL_R &= 0.450 \text{ mm} \\
LCL_R &= D_3 \bar{R} = (0)(0.45) = 0.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process Capability:**

$$
\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.45}{2.326} = 0.1935 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{33.00 - 31.00}{6 \times 0.1935} = \dfrac{2.00}{1.161} = 1.723
$$

Since $C_p > 1.33$, the process is **highly capable** and meets the KTU recommended standard.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternate Set]**

**(a)** What is the **Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)**? Explain with a step-by-step procedure to construct a **Pareto Chart**. Using the data below, construct the cumulative percentage table. *(7 marks)*

| Defect | Frequency |
|---|---|
| A | 56 |
| B | 32 |
| C | 18 |
| D | 10 |
| E | 4 |

**(b)** Explain the **Scatter Diagram** as a quality tool. With reference to the Pearson correlation coefficient, explain the categories of correlation strength and the **important caveat** that correlation does not imply causation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pareto Principle:**

The **Pareto Principle** (named after Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto, 1897**) states that roughly **80% of the effects come from 20% of the causes**. In quality management, this is applied to defect data: ~80% of the defects are caused by ~20% of the defect categories (the "vital few").

**Construction Procedure:**
1. Identify defect categories and collect frequency data.
2. Sort categories in **descending order** of frequency.
3. Calculate the cumulative frequency and cumulative percentage.
4. Plot bars for individual frequencies (left y-axis).
5. Overlay a line plot for cumulative percentages (right y-axis, 0–100%).
6. Identify the "vital few" by locating where the cumulative curve crosses ~80%.

**Cumulative Percentage Table:**

| Defect | Frequency | Cumulative Frequency | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 56 | 56 | 46.7% |
| B | 32 | 88 | 73.3% |
| C | 18 | 106 | 88.3% |
| D | 10 | 116 | 96.7% |
| E | 4 | 120 | 100.0% |

**Total defects = 120**

**Interpretation:** Defects **A and B together = 73.3%** of all defects. Adding defect C pushes the cumulative to **88.3%**, which crosses the 80% threshold. Therefore, **A, B, and C are the "vital few"** that the quality team should prioritize.

**Part (b) — Scatter Diagram:**

A **Scatter Diagram** is a graphical tool used to study the **relationship between two continuous variables**. Each point on the plot represents a paired observation (x, y). The pattern of points reveals the nature of correlation.

**Pearson Correlation Coefficient:**

$$
r = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$

**Correlation Strength Categories:**

| Coefficient Range | Strength |
|---|---|
| $0.8 \leq \vert r \vert \leq 1.0$ | Strong correlation |
| $0.5 \leq \vert r \vert < 0.8$ | Moderate correlation |
| $0.3 \leq \vert r \vert < 0.5$ | Weak correlation |
| $\vert r \vert < 0.3$ | Negligible / No correlation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Caveat — Correlation vs Causation:**
> A scatter diagram shows **statistical association**, not **cause-and-effect**. Two variables may be highly correlated because of:
> 1. **Coincidence** (spurious correlation)
> 2. **Common Cause** (a third hidden variable Z drives both X and Y)
> 3. **Reverse Causation** (Y actually causes X)
> Example: Ice cream sales and drowning deaths are positively correlated, but **summer (heat)** is the hidden common cause — neither causes the other.
> Therefore, the **KTU Examiner expects students to explicitly state** that engineering decisions must not be based on correlation alone.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong formula in Part B (control charts):** Students often confuse $A_2$ with $A_3$ or use $D_4$ instead of $A_2$. Always cross-check the constant against the subgroup size *n*.
> 2. **Missing $d_2$ in $\sigma$ estimation:** Many students compute $C_p$ using $\bar{R}$ instead of $\bar{R}/d_2$. This is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Forgetting to sort Pareto data:** Pareto chart *requires* descending order. Unsorted data = full marks lost for construction.
> 4. **Omitting the "Why" in fishbone:** Listing only the 6M categories without sub-causes is considered incomplete. **Always include at least 2 sub-causes per branch.**
> 5. **Confusing control limits with specification limits:** Specification limits (USL/LSL) come from the **customer/design**. Control limits (UCL/LCL) come from the **process itself**. Mixing these is a fatal error.
> 6. **Stating "correlation implies causation"** in scatter diagram answers — KTU deducts 1 mark explicitly for this misconception.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Seven Basic Tools of Quality**

- ✅ The 7 tools were developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** and published in his 1943/1968 work on quality control.
- ✅ They are designed for **shop-floor workers**, not statisticians — minimal math required.
- ✅ **Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram:** Used for **root cause brainstorming**. Six branches: 6M (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature).
- ✅ **Check Sheet:** Pre-formatted **data collection** form; the *raw input* for all other tools.
- ✅ **Control Chart (Shewhart):** Plots quality characteristic **over time** with $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ limits. Distinguishes common cause vs special cause variation.
- ✅ **Histogram:** Visualizes **frequency distribution** of continuous data; reveals shape (normal, skewed, bimodal).
- ✅ **Pareto Chart:** Applies **80/20 rule** to **prioritize** problems; data must be sorted in descending order with cumulative % overlay.
- ✅ **Scatter Diagram:** Studies **correlation** between two variables using Pearson's *r*. **Remember:** correlation ≠ causation.
- ✅ **Flowchart:** Maps **process steps** using standardized symbols (oval = start/end, rectangle = process, diamond = decision, parallelogram = I/O).
- ✅ Key formulas: $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$, $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$, $C_p = (USL-LSL)/(6\sigma)$.
- ✅ For **n = 5**, remember: $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_4 = 2.114$, $d_2 = 2.326$.
- ✅ KTU requires $C_p \geq 1.33$ for a "capable" process; $C_p < 1.00$ = incapable process.
- ✅ **The 7 tools are typically deployed in the Define & Measure phases of Six Sigma DMAIC.**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven New Management Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Seven New Management Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SEVEN NEW MANAGEMENT TOOLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Seven New Management Tools (7NMT)** — also referred to in KTU 2024 Scheme literature as the **Seven New Quality Control (7NQC) Tools** — constitute a structured set of qualitative planning, management, and communication techniques developed and formalized by the **Union of Japanese Scientists and Engineers (JUSE)** in **1976–1979** as a natural complement to the original seven statistical *Kaoru Ishikawa* tools (cause-and-effect diagrams, histograms, control charts, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The seven tools are intended specifically for **planning**, **quality assurance**, and **management decision-making** at the **group/organizational level**, particularly in the *planning* (P) and *doing* (D) phases of the PDCA cycle where the seven old tools proved insufficient for **non-numerical, language-based, and strategic reasoning tasks**.

The seven tools are:

| # | Tool Name | Core Function |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Affinity Diagram** (KJ Method) | Grouping chaotic ideas into logical clusters |
| 2 | **Interrelationship Digraph (ID)** | Mapping cause-effect logic networks |
| 3 | **Tree Diagram** | Breaking goals into hierarchical sub-tasks |
| 4 | **Matrix Diagram** | Tabulating strength of relationships between two sets |
| 5 | **Prioritization Matrices** | Ranking alternatives based on weighted criteria |
| 6 | **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)** | Anticipating contingencies in implementation plans |
| 7 | **Activity Network Diagram (AND)** / Arrow Diagram | Sequencing, scheduling and critical-path analysis |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Planning a College Festival"**
> Imagine you and your team have just been handed a vague task: *"Organize a successful college fest."* Within minutes you are drowning in chaotic sticky notes — ideas about *budget, sponsors, guests, food stalls, registration, security, music, marketing, stage design.* Now ask yourself: how do you actually *make sense* of this jungle of words, connect the dots, prioritize, break it down, schedule it, and prepare for the worst?
>
> - The **Affinity Diagram** is what you do when you scoop up those scattered sticky notes and group *"food stalls, music, sponsors"* under one heading called *"Resources."*
> - The **Interrelationship Digraph** is when you draw arrows showing *"low budget"* causes *"few sponsors"* which causes *"no celebrity guest."*
> - The **Tree Diagram** is when you split *"Run the fest"* into the WBS: stage, stalls, marketing, hospitality.
> - The **Matrix Diagram** is the L-shaped grid showing who is responsible for what task.
> - The **Prioritization Matrix** is when you finally ask *"which task is most urgent and most important?"*
> - The **PDPC** is your *"Plan B"* — what if the rain comes? What if the chief guest cancels?
> - The **Activity Network Diagram** is the Gantt/CPM chart scheduling *Day 1: Setup → Day 2: Fest → Day 3: Wind-up.*

**In essence: The 7NMT turn fuzzy managerial thinking into structured visual plans.**

### 1.3 Why They Are Called "NEW" — Historical Context

The original **Seven QC Tools** (Pareto chart, cause-and-effect, histogram, scatter, control chart, flowchart, check sheet) were excellent for *numerical analysis on the shop floor*. However, by the **mid-1970s**, with the rise of company-wide quality control (CWQC/TQM) in Japan, planners at the **management level** required tools for:

- Qualitative, language-based reasoning
- Cross-functional team consensus building
- Strategic planning, not just statistical control

The JUSE research society, chaired by **Professor Teruo Koura** and inspired by **Professor Jiro Kawakita** (creator of the KJ Method), compiled and published these tools in **1979**, christening them the *"Seven New QC Tools for the Management Era."*

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Memory Aid:** If the question says "language data" or "planning phase" → think **Seven New Tools**. If it says "numerical data" or "control phase" → think **Seven Old Tools**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Tool Reference Sheet

### 2.1 Tool 1 — Affinity Diagram (KJ Method)

- **Purpose:** Organize a large mass of ideas, opinions, or facts (language data) into natural groupings based on their **mutual affinity or intuitive relationship**.
- **Inventor:** **Jiro Kawakita** (hence KJ Method), 1960s.
- **When to Use:** Brainstorming phase, chaotic situation, when ideas are subjective and unstructured.
- **Construction Logic:**
  1. Form a cross-functional team (typically 5–8 members).
  2. Each member writes ideas on sticky notes/cards (one idea per card, ~50–100 cards).
  3. Cards are silently moved into clusters of "naturally related" items.
  4. Each cluster receives a concise **affinity header card** summarizing the group.
  5. Optionally, build a **Header Affinity Diagram** (second level) where the header cards themselves are clustered.

> [!NOTE]
> The KJ Method differs from ordinary sorting because it relies on **intuition and gestalt grouping** rather than pre-defined categories — appropriate for truly unknown problems.

### 2.2 Tool 2 — Interrelationship Digraph (ID)

- **Purpose:** Map **cause-and-effect relationships** among a complex set of ideas in a directed graph/network.
- **Key Idea:** Each idea is a node; arrows show influence. The arrow exiting a node points to whatever that node *affects or causes*.
- **Convention:** A **cause** is placed at the **tail** of the arrow; an **effect** is placed at the **head**.
- **Analysis Output:**
  - **Driver items** (have many outgoing arrows) — root causes.
  - **Outcome items** (have many incoming arrows) — key effects.
  - Items connected to many others are flagged for priority attention.
- **Application:** Used to confirm the *true root cause* once an Affinity Diagram has clustered ideas.

### 2.3 Tool 3 — Tree Diagram

- **Purpose:** Systematically decompose a **broad goal/target** into progressively finer levels of detail.
- **Forms:** Read top-down (goal → sub-goals → tasks → actions) — the most common **Goal Tree / Means Tree / Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**.
- **Construction Logic:**
  1. State the **top-level objective** (the "root").
  2. Identify 3–5 **main branches (sub-objectives)** required to achieve it.
  3. Decompose each branch into **detailed actions** until the tasks are actionable by a single individual/department.
  4. Use the *MECE* principle: **Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive.**
- **Application:** Translates *strategy into deliverables*. The single most important TQM deployment tool.

### 2.4 Tool 4 — Matrix Diagram

- **Purpose:** Display the **strength of relationships between two or more sets of items** in a tabular format.
- **Common Shapes:**
  - **L-Matrix:** Two sets (rows × columns) — simplest, most common.
  - **T-Matrix:** Three sets where two L-matrices share a common axis.
  - **Y-Matrix:** Three sets in three branches (cylindrical layout).
  - **X-Matrix:** Four sets in a star pattern.
  - **C-Matrix / Roof-Matrix:** Three sets with one on the roof — used for function-to-component mapping in **QFD (Quality Function Deployment)**.
- **Symbol Convention (Japanese Industrial Standard, JIS):**
  - ⊙ Strong relationship (**9** points)
  - ○ Moderate relationship (**3** points)
  - △ Weak relationship (**1** point)
  - *(Blank)* No relationship (**0** points)

### 2.5 Tool 5 — Prioritization Matrices

- **Purpose:** Reduce a large list of items to a ranked shortlist using **weighted criteria**.
- **Types:**
  - **Full Analytical Hierarchy (AHP-style)** — pairwise comparison of every criterion pair.
  - **Consensus Prioritization Matrix** — team scores each alternative against each criterion on a 1–5/1–10 scale.
- **Workflow:**
  1. List *alternatives* (rows).
  2. List *criteria* (columns).
  3. Assign **weight** $w_j$ to each criterion such that $\sum_j w_j = 1$ (or $100\%$).
  4. Rate each alternative $i$ against criterion $j$ to get score $s_{ij}$.
  5. Compute weighted score.
- **Engineering Utility:** Selecting suppliers, prioritizing Six Sigma projects, ranking improvement ideas.

### 2.6 Tool 6 — Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)

- **Purpose:** Map out an implementation plan together with **all conceivable contingencies** ("what if" branches) so the team can react rapidly when reality deviates from the plan.
- **Construction Logic:**
  1. Start from a desired **goal/state** at the top.
  2. Identify the **initial implementation path** (sequence of steps).
  3. For every step, brainstorm what could go wrong (defects, delays, risks).
  4. Draw **counter-measure branches** off the main path.
  5. Each counter-measure either returns execution to the main path or redirects to an alternate path.
- **Application:** New product launch, regulatory compliance, project risk management.

### 2.7 Tool 7 — Activity Network Diagram (AND) — Arrow Diagram / PERT-CPM

- **Purpose:** Determine the **optimal sequence, duration, and critical path** of a project consisting of many interdependent activities.
- **Key Concepts:**
  - **Activity (arrow or box):** Time-consuming task.
  - **Event / Node (circle):** A milestone, start or completion of an activity.
  - **Predecessor / Successor:** A → B means B cannot start until A finishes.
  - **Critical Path:** Longest path of dependent activities; defines minimum project duration.
  - **Float / Slack:** $SL_i = LS_i - ES_i = LF_i - EF_i$. Activities with $SL = 0$ are critical.
  - **PERT:** 3-estimate formula $t_e = (t_o + 4t_m + t_p)/6$ using *optimistic, most likely, and pessimistic* times.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Reference Cheat Sheet

| Tool | Primary Output | Data Type | PDCA Phase | Typical Activity |
|------|----------------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| Affinity Diagram | Grouped clusters | Qualitative / Language | Plan | Brainstorming |
| Interrelationship Digraph | Cause-effect graph | Qualitative | Plan | Root-cause mapping |
| Tree Diagram | Hierarchical decomposition | Both | Plan | Strategy deployment, WBS |
| Matrix Diagram | Relationship grid | Qualitative | Plan | QFD, responsibility matrix |
| Prioritization Matrix | Ranked alternatives | Both | Plan / Do | Selecting projects |
| PDPC | Contingency flowchart | Qualitative | Do | Risk planning |
| Activity Network Diagram | CPM/PERT schedule | Quantitative | Do / Check | Project scheduling |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** When asked "Which tool converts qualitative data into structured plans?" — the answer in **planning context** is generally the **Tree Diagram**. When asked about **language-based brainstorming**, the answer is the **Affinity Diagram**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Prioritization Matrix Weighted Score

Let there be $n$ **alternatives** indexed $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ and $m$ **criteria** indexed $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$.

**Step 1: Normalize criterion weights.**

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j = 1
$$

**Step 2: Define the raw scoring matrix.** Each $s_{ij} \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ is the team-assigned rating of alternative $i$ on criterion $j$.

**Step 3: Compute the weighted total score of alternative $i$.**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

**Step 4: Rank alternatives by descending $S_i$.**

> The alternative with the highest $S_i$ is selected. This is the exact logic used in **Six Sigma project selection** in the **Define** phase.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — PERT Three-Point Expected Duration

For each activity in an AND, the most realistic time $t_e$ is the Beta-distribution expected value:

$$
t_e = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6}
$$

**Derivation logic (beta-distribution PERT, $t_o \le t_m \le t_p$):**

$$
\mu_{\beta} = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} (t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

For the standard PERT assumption $\alpha + \beta = 6$ and mode $t_m$:

$$
t_m = \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha + \beta - 2} (t_p - t_o) + t_o
$$

Solving the simultaneous equations $\alpha + \beta = 6$ and $\alpha - 1 = 4$ gives $\alpha = 5,\ \beta = 1$, hence:

$$
\mu_{\beta} = \frac{5}{6}(t_p - t_o) + t_o = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6}
$$

**Variance of the activity:**

$$
\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Matrix Diagram Total Score

For an L-matrix with $n$ row items and $m$ column items, let the symbol-to-numeric mapping be ⊙ = 9, ○ = 3, △ = 1, blank = 0. Define $r_{ij}$ as the numeric symbol at cell $(i,j)$. The row total is:

$$
R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} r_{ij}
$$

The column total is:

$$
C_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ij}
$$

Items with the highest $R_i + C_j$ are flagged for attention. This is the basis of the **House of Quality (QFD)** *roof matrix*.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Prioritization Matrix

A firm has **3 Six Sigma projects (A, B, C)** to be prioritized using **3 criteria**: ROI (weight 0.5), Feasibility (0.3), Strategic Fit (0.2). Team scores on a 1–5 scale.

| Project | ROI ($s_{i1}$) | Feasibility ($s_{i2}$) | Strategic Fit ($s_{i3}$) |
|---------|----|----|----|
| A | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| B | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| C | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Score of Project A:**

$$
S_A = (0.5)(5) + (0.3)(3) + (0.2)(4) = 2.50 + 0.90 + 0.80 = 4.20
$$

**Score of Project B:**

$$
S_B = (0.5)(4) + (0.3)(5) + (0.2)(3) = 2.00 + 1.50 + 0.60 = 4.10
$$

**Score of Project C:**

$$
S_C = (0.5)(3) + (0.3)(4) + (0.2)(5) = 1.50 + 1.20 + 1.00 = 3.70
$$

**Ranking:** A (4.20) > B (4.10) > C (3.70) → **Project A is selected.**

---

### 3.5 Exhaustive Worked Example — PERT Expected Time & Critical Path

Three activities A, B, C form a chain A → B → C. Estimates:

- A: $t_o = 2$, $t_m = 4$, $t_p = 6$ days
- B: $t_o = 3$, $t_m = 5$, $t_p = 13$ days
- C: $t_o = 1$, $t_m = 3$, $t_p = 5$ days

**Expected time of A:**

$$
t_{e,A} = \frac{2 + 4(4) + 6}{6} = \frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = \frac{24}{6} = 4.0 \text{ days}
$$

**Expected time of B:**

$$
t_{e,B} = \frac{3 + 4(5) + 13}{6} = \frac{3 + 20 + 13}{6} = \frac{36}{6} = 6.0 \text{ days}
$$

**Expected time of C:**

$$
t_{e,C} = \frac{1 + 4(3) + 5}{6} = \frac{1 + 12 + 5}{6} = \frac{18}{6} = 3.0 \text{ days}
$$

**Total expected project duration (sequential chain → entire chain is critical):**

$$
T_{proj} = 4.0 + 6.0 + 3.0 = 13.0 \text{ days}
$$

**Standard deviation of each activity:**

$$
\sigma_A = \frac{6 - 2}{6} = 0.667 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\sigma_B = \frac{13 - 3}{6} = 1.667 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\sigma_C = \frac{5 - 1}{6} = 0.667 \text{ days}
$$

**Project variance (sum of variances along the chain):**

$$
\sigma_{proj}^2 = (0.667)^2 + (1.667)^2 + (0.667)^2
$$

$$
\sigma_{proj}^2 = 0.444 + 2.778 + 0.444 = 3.667 \text{ days}^2
$$

**Project standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_{proj} = \sqrt{3.667} \approx 1.915 \text{ days}
$$

**Probability of completing in ≤ 14 days:**

$$
Z = \frac{14 - 13}{1.915} = \frac{1}{1.915} \approx 0.522
$$

From standard normal tables: $P(Z \le 0.52) \approx 0.698$ or **69.8 %**.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 7NMT vs. 7QC

| Dimension | Seven OLD Tools (Ishikawa) | Seven NEW Tools (JUSE 1979) |
|-----------|---------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Era of Origin** | 1950s–1960s | Late 1970s |
| **Data Type** | Numerical, statistical | Language-based, qualitative |
| **Typical User** | Shop-floor engineer | Middle/top manager, QC facilitator |
| **Phase in PDCA** | Check & Act (control) | Plan & Do (planning) |
| **Format** | Numerical charts | Visual diagrams |
| **Examples** | Pareto, Histogram, Control Chart | Affinity, Tree, Matrix, PDPC |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — Sequence of Application of the 7NMT

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Affinity Diagram - Cluster chaotic ideas] --> B[Step 2: Interrelationship Digraph - Map cause-effect logic]
    B --> C[Step 3: Tree Diagram - Break into actionable sub-tasks]
    C --> D[Step 4: Matrix Diagram - Cross-check responsibilities and relationships]
    D --> E[Step 5: Prioritization Matrix - Rank alternatives]
    E --> F[Step 6: PDPC - Add contingency branches]
    F --> G[Step 7: Activity Network Diagram - Schedule critical path]
    G --> H[Execute Plan via PDCA Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Affinity Diagram — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["Raw Brainstorm Cards"]
        C1[Budget tight]
        C2[Sponsor shortage]
        C3[Stage design]
        C4[Music choice]
        C5[Registration portal]
        C6[Marketing plan]
        C7[Food stalls]
        C8[Security volunteers]
        C9[Chief guest cancel]
        C10[Rain contingency]
    end
    subgraph B["Cluster 1: Resources"]
        B1[Budget tight]
        B2[Sponsor shortage]
    end
    subgraph C["Cluster 2: Event Design"]
        C1A[Stage design]
        C2A[Music choice]
    end
    subgraph D["Cluster 3: Operations"]
        D1[Registration portal]
        D2[Marketing plan]
    end
    subgraph E["Cluster 4: Logistics"]
        E1[Food stalls]
        E2[Security volunteers]
    end
    subgraph F["Cluster 5: Risks"]
        F1[Chief guest cancel]
        F2[Rain contingency]
    end
    C1 --> B1
    C2 --> B2
    C3 --> C1A
    C4 --> C2A
    C5 --> D1
    C6 --> D2
    C7 --> E1
    C8 --> E2
    C9 --> F1
    C10 --> F2
```

### 4.3 Interrelationship Digraph — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Low Budget] -- Strong cause --> N2[Few Sponsors]
    N2 -- Strong cause --> N3[No Celebrity Guest]
    N2 -- Moderate cause --> N4[Low Marketing Spend]
    N4 -- Moderate cause --> N5[Low Registrations]
    N3 -- Strong cause --> N5
    N5 -- Strong cause --> N6[Poor Brand Image]
    N1 -- Moderate cause --> N7[Limited Food Variety]
    N6 -- Feedback --> N1
```

### 4.4 Tree Diagram — Strategic Goal Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GOAL[Run Successful College Fest] --> OBJ1[Stage Management]
    GOAL --> OBJ2[Stall Operations]
    GOAL --> OBJ3[Marketing Outreach]
    GOAL --> OBJ4[Hospitality and Security]
    OBJ1 --> ACT11[Book venue]
    OBJ1 --> ACT12[Hire sound crew]
    OBJ1 --> ACT13[Schedule rehearsals]
    OBJ2 --> ACT21[Vendor contracts]
    OBJ2 --> ACT22[Quality inspection]
    OBJ3 --> ACT31[Social media campaign]
    OBJ3 --> ACT32[Sponsor outreach]
    OBJ4 --> ACT41[Chief guest invite]
    OBJ4 --> ACT42[Volunteer training]
```

### 4.5 Matrix Diagram — L-Matrix for QFD-style Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROW["Customer Requirements (Whys)"]
        R1[Low Cost]
        R2[High Sound Quality]
        R3[Quick Registration]
        R4[Safe Environment]
    end
    subgraph COL["Engineering Specs (Hows)"]
        C1[Vendor Count]
        C2[Sound System Wattage]
        C3[Online Portal]
        C4[Security Staff Count]
    end
    R1 -. Strong 9 .-> C1
    R2 -. Strong 9 .-> C2
    R3 -. Strong 9 .-> C3
    R4 -. Strong 9 .-> C4
    R1 -. Weak 1 .-> C4
    R2 -. Moderate 3 .-> C3
    R3 -. Moderate 3 .-> C2
```

### 4.6 PDPC — Contingency Planning Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Goal: Fest Held on Day 2] --> S1[Step A: Book Open Auditorium]
    S1 -->|Risk: Rain| S2A[Contingency: Shift to Indoor Hall]
    S1 -->|Risk: Chief guest cancels| S2B[Contingency: Pre-recorded video address]
    S1 -->|Risk: Low registrations| S2C[Contingency: Day-2 free entry push]
    S2A --> S3[Proceed to Fest Execution]
    S2B --> S3
    S2C --> S3
    S3 --> S4[Outcome: Fest Successfully Held]
```

### 4.7 Activity Network Diagram — PERT/CPM Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Start: Event Zero] --> A[Activity A: Setup Stalls 4 days]
    N0 --> B[Activity B: Marketing Campaign 6 days]
    A --> C[Activity C: Sound Check 3 days]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Activity D: Dress Rehearsal 2 days]
    D --> E[Activity E: Fest Day 1 day]
    E --> N1[End: Fest Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Path 1 (A → C → D → E) = 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = **10 days**.
> Path 2 (B → C → D → E) = 6 + 3 + 2 + 1 = **12 days**.
> Therefore Path 2 is the **Critical Path**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the Seven New Management Tools and state in one sentence the primary purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Affinity Diagram (KJ Method):** Groups large volumes of unstructured, language-based ideas into naturally related clusters for shared understanding.
2. **Interrelationship Digraph (ID):** Displays the causal relationships among ideas in a network so that root causes and key effects can be identified.
3. **Tree Diagram:** Systematically decomposes a broad objective into progressively detailed, actionable sub-tasks (means–ends or WBS).
4. **Matrix Diagram:** Tabulates the strength of relationships between two or more sets of items (rows vs. columns).
5. **Prioritization Matrices:** Rank alternatives using weighted multi-criteria scoring to support selection decisions.
6. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC):** Maps planned steps along with contingency counter-measures for foreseen risks.
7. **Activity Network Diagram (Arrow Diagram):** Sequences and schedules project activities, identifies the critical path, and computes project duration (PERT/CPM).

*(1 mark for full list, 2 marks for clear one-line purposes. Total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the Seven Old Quality Control Tools and the Seven New Management Tools on the basis of (i) data type, (ii) PDCA phase, and (iii) typical user.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Seven OLD Tools | Seven NEW Tools |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| (i) Data type | Numerical / statistical | Language / qualitative |
| (ii) PDCA phase | Check & Act | Plan & Do |
| (iii) Typical user | Shop-floor operator / engineer | Middle & top management / QC facilitator |

*(1 mark per correct row.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Affinity, ID, Tree & Prioritization*

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
*(a)* Explain the construction logic and application of the **Affinity Diagram**. How does it differ from ordinary classification? *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A manufacturing firm has four improvement projects P1, P2, P3, P4. The selection criteria and weights are: Cost Saving (0.4), Customer Impact (0.4), Ease of Implementation (0.2). Raw team scores (1–5) are:

| Project | Cost Saving | Customer Impact | Ease |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| P1 | 5 | 3 | 4 |
| P2 | 4 | 5 | 2 |
| P3 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| P4 | 2 | 4 | 4 |

Compute weighted scores and rank the projects using a **Prioritization Matrix**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Affinity Diagram (7 marks):**

The Affinity Diagram (KJ Method) was developed by Jiro Kawakita to organize **chaotic, language-based** data into intuitive clusters.

**Construction steps:**
1. **Form a team** of 5–8 cross-functional members.
2. **Generate ideas** — each member writes ideas on cards/sticky notes (≈ 50–100 cards). One idea per card.
3. **Silent shuffling** — cards are physically moved into piles that "feel right" together. No discussion is allowed during this stage; the grouping is intuitive.
4. **Name the clusters** — once stable, each cluster receives a **header card** summarizing the theme.
5. **Optional second level** — header cards themselves are affinity-grouped into higher-order categories.
6. **Interpret and act** — clusters reveal natural problem structure and consensus.

**Key differences from ordinary classification:**
- Ordinary classification uses **predefined categories**; the KJ Method **discovers** the categories from the data itself.
- Ordinary classification is **analytical and deductive**; the KJ Method is **synthetic and inductive** — it uses gestalt/intuition rather than rules.
- Ordinary classification works on **numerical attributes**; KJ works on **semantic, language-based attributes.**
