Shewhart** of Bell Telephone Laboratories introduced **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and the **±3σ control limits** on a control chart in 1924. His work established that any process has natural variation and that quality can be monitored statistically. This was the mathematical foundation of all later quality methods, including Six Sigma. **Step 2 — TQM Foundation (1950s):** **W. Edwards Deming** and **Joseph M. Juran** trained thousands of Japanese engineers in SPC and quality management after World War II. Deming introduced the famous **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle, while Juran proposed the **Quality Trilogy** (Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement). Japanese industry adopted these ideas and produced world-class products, putting pressure on US companies. **Step 3 — The Motorola Crisis (1979–1986):** **Motorola**, an American electronics giant, was losing market share to Japanese competitors. In 1980, **Bob Galvin** became CEO and launched a quality revolution. In **1986**, **Bill Smith**, a Motorola reliability engineer, working with **Mikel Harry**, discovered that products must be designed at the **6σ level** to deliver long-term reliability. This gave birth to the **"Six Sigma"** term and the **3.4 DPMO** benchmark. In **1987**, Motorola became the **first non-Japanese company** to win the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award**. **Step 4 — Globalization at GE (1995):** **Jack Welch**, CEO of **General Electric (GE)**, made Six Sigma a corporate-wide strategic initiative in 1995, investing over **$1 billion** in training and consulting. Under Welch, Six Sigma evolved from a manufacturing method into a complete **business strategy** with the **DMAIC framework** and the **Belts system (Green, Black, Master Black, Champion)**. GE reportedly saved over **$10 billion** in five years. **Step 5 — Standardization (2000–Present):** Six Sigma spread to **service industries, healthcare, banking, IT, and BPO sectors**. It was integrated with **Lean Manufacturing** as **Lean Six Sigma (LSS)**. In **2011**, the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** published **ISO 13053**, formalizing the DMAIC methodology. Today, Six Sigma is taught globally as a core engineering management discipline and integrated with **Industry 4.0 and AI-based analytics**. --- **(b) DMAIC vs. PDCA (7 Marks):** **DMAIC Methodology:** ``` D — DEFINE: Identify the problem, customer (CTQs), and project scope. M — MEASURE: Collect baseline data on the current process. A — ANALYZE: Identify root causes using tools like Pareto, Fishbone, regression. I — IMPROVE: Develop, pilot-test, and implement solutions. C — CONTROL: Standardize the improved process using SPC and control plans. ``` **Comparison Table:** | Feature | DMAIC (Six Sigma) | PDCA (Deming) | |---|---|---| | Origin | Motorola, 1986 | Deming, 1950s | | Focus | Defect reduction (3.4 DPMO) | Continuous improvement (Kaizen) | | Structure | 5 structured phases | 4 cyclical phases | | Tools | Regression, DOE, Hypothesis Testing | Brainstorming, simple checks | | Statistical Rigour | Very high | Moderate | | Project Outcome | Quantified financial savings | Incremental cultural change | | Best Suited For | Complex, data-rich problems | Day-to-day operational improvements | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > Many students confuse **DMAIC** with **PDCA**. Remember: **DMAIC is project-based and statistical**, while **PDCA is cycle-based and cultural**. Losing 2 marks is common if you fail to highlight the statistical tools used only in DMAIC (regression, Design of Experiments, Hypothesis Testing, FMEA). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **statistical meaning of "Six Sigma"** and derive why a Six Sigma process allows only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the **roles of Green Belt, Black Belt, and Master Black Belt** in a Six Sigma organization. Why is the Champion role critical for project success? (7 Marks) ### Model Answer for Question B **(a) Statistical Meaning of Six Sigma (7 Marks):** The term **"Six Sigma"** refers to a quality level where the process mean is positioned **six standard deviations** away from the nearest specification limit. Mathematically: $$ Z = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} = 6 $$ For a two-sided specification with the mean centered: $$ \text{Total Tolerance} = 2 \times 6\sigma = 12\sigma $$ **Derivation of 3.4 DPMO:** - **Step 1:** From the standard normal table, the probability of a point lying beyond **+6σ** is approximately **1.973 × 10⁻⁹**. - **Step 2:** Motorola's Bill Smith observed that real processes suffer a **1.5σ mean shift** over time. Hence, the effective distance becomes **6σ − 1.5σ = 4.5σ**. - **Step 3:** From the Z-table, the area beyond **+4.5σ** is **3.4 × 10⁻⁶**. - **Step 4:** Therefore: $$ \text{DPMO} = 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^6 = 3.4 \;\text{DPMO} $$ This benchmark is so robust that it survives normal long-term variation, which is why **Six Sigma is the gold standard of quality worldwide**. --- **(b) Roles of Six Sigma Belts (7 Marks):** | Belt | Role & Responsibility | |---|---| | **Champion** | Senior executive who sponsors projects, allocates budget, removes organizational barriers, and aligns projects with business strategy. | | **Master Black Belt** | Expert mentor who trains and coaches Black Belts, manages the Six Sigma program across the company, and handles the most complex projects. | | **Black Belt** | Full-time project leader who leads 1–2 projects per year, applies advanced statistical tools (DOE, Regression), and trains Green Belts. | | **Green Belt** | Part-time practitioner who leads small projects or supports Black Belt projects within their functional area. | **Why the Champion Role is Critical:** A Six Sigma project without a Champion fails 80% of the time. The Champion provides **executive authority**, **resource allocation**, and **cross-functional coordination**. Without a Champion, data collection is blocked, pilot implementations stall, and improvement ideas are never scaled to full production. The Champion bridges the **statistical world of the Black Belt** with the **business world of the CEO**, ensuring every project delivers measurable financial savings. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > A frequent mistake is writing "Champion = project leader." This is **wrong**. The Champion is an **executive sponsor**, not the day-to-day leader. The **Black Belt** leads the project; the **Champion** enables it. Marks are deducted if these roles are confused. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — History of Six Sigma** - Six Sigma = **±6σ** between process mean and nearest specification limit. - Standard benchmark: **3.4 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities). - Statistical foundation: **Gauss (1795)** — Normal Distribution; **Shewhart (1924)** — ±3σ SPC. - **Bill Smith & Mikel Harry (1986)** at **Motorola** are the inventors of Six Sigma. - **Bob Galvin** (Motorola CEO, 1980) was the sponsor who enabled the quality revolution. - Motorola won the **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award in 1987** — the first non-Japanese winner. - **Jack Welch (GE, 1995)** popularized Six Sigma globally and invested **$1 billion** in it. - **DMAIC** = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control — the universal project methodology. - The **1.5σ shift** converts short-term 6σ into the practical 3.4 DPMO long-term benchmark. - **ISO 13053 (2011)** is the international standard for Six Sigma DMAIC processes. - Belts: **Green Belt, Black Belt, Master Black Belt, Champion**. - **Lean Six Sigma (LSS)** combines Six Sigma with Lean Manufacturing (waste elimination). - Six Sigma is now applied in **manufacturing, services, healthcare, banking, IT, and supply chains**. - For KTU 2024 exams: Always cite the **year**, **person**, and **company** for full marks.

topics.title: Working of Six Sigma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working of Six Sigma

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Six Sigma** is a rigorous, data-driven methodology and statistical measurement framework that aims to eliminate defects in any process by understanding and managing process variation. In the context of operations, a "Six Sigma process" is one in which **99.99966%** of all opportunities to produce a feature or service are statistically expected to be free of defects — equivalent to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**.

The **"Working of Six Sigma"** refers to the operational execution framework of this methodology, encompassing the **DMAIC roadmap** (Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control), the hierarchical **Belts and Roles structure**, the **statistical measurement system** (sigma level, DPMO, process capability), and the **project selection & financial validation** protocols that drive continuous improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Focus:** Module 4 frequently tests the **DMAIC phases, roles (Champion, MBB, BB, GB), DPMO calculation, and the process capability indices (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk)**. Memorize the 3.4 DPMO benchmark value.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a **bakery producing 1,000 cookies per day**. A normal quality system might accept a few burnt edges (say 1% defects) — that means **10 burnt cookies daily, 3,650 burnt cookies yearly**. Customers complain, refunds happen, and reputation drops.

Now, with **Six Sigma thinking**, you don't just "check the cookies at the end" — you ask:
- *Why* is the oven temperature fluctuating? (Define & Analyze)
- *How much* variation is there in temperature? (Measure)
- *What* is the root cause of the variation? (Analyze)
- *How* do we redesign the oven to eliminate that variation? (Improve)
- *How* do we lock-in the fix with sensors and SOPs? (Control)

After Six Sigma, you'd produce **6 million cookies** before seeing a single burnt one. This isn't magic — it's the mathematical discipline of working with **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** at a **6σ quality level**.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of Six Sigma as **"Detective Work + Engineering"**. Detect the defect's root cause, then engineer a permanent fix using statistical evidence.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Six Sigma

| Metric | Standard Value at 6σ | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | **3.4** | Defects Per Million Opportunities |
| **Yield** | **99.99966%** | Percentage of defect-free outputs |
| **Process Variation** | **± 6σ from mean** | Specification spread (USL – LSL = 12σ) |
| **Z-Score (Short-term)** | **6.0** | Distance from mean to nearest spec limit |
| **Z-Score (Long-term)** | **4.5** | Adjusted for 1.5σ drift over time |

> [!NOTE]
> The famous **1.5σ shift** (the difference between short-term 6σ and long-term 4.5σ) is Motorola's empirical adjustment to account for long-term process drift, and is universally accepted in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DMAIC Roadmap — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

DMAIC is the **core working engine** of Six Sigma. Each phase has defined tools, deliverables, and gate reviews.

#### **Phase 1: DEFINE**
- **Purpose:** Identify the problem, the customer, and the project scope.
- **Key Tools:** Project Charter, VOC (Voice of Customer), SIPOC diagram, CTQ (Critical-to-Quality) tree.
- **Deliverable:** A signed-off **Project Charter** with problem statement, goal, scope, team, and timeline.
- **"Why" question answered:** *What problem are we solving, and for whom?*

#### **Phase 2: MEASURE**
- **Purpose:** Quantify the current state of the process and establish baseline performance.
- **Key Tools:** Data collection plans, MSA (Measurement System Analysis), process mapping, capability indices (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk), Pareto charts, DPMO calculation.
- **Deliverable:** Validated baseline **σ level** and **DPMO**.
- **"Why" question answered:** *How bad is the problem right now, in numbers?*

#### **Phase 3: ANALYZE**
- **Purpose:** Identify the **root causes** of defects and variation.
- **Key Tools:** Hypothesis testing, regression, ANOVA, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, 5-Whys, FMEA, control charts.
- **Deliverable:** A prioritized list of validated **root causes (X's)** that drive the **Y (defect)**.
- **"Why" question answered:** *What is actually causing the defects?*

#### **Phase 4: IMPROVE**
- **Purpose:** Develop, pilot, and implement solutions that eliminate or reduce root causes.
- **Key Tools:** Design of Experiments (DOE), Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing), Kaizen events, pilot studies, solution matrices.
- **Deliverable:** Implemented, pilot-validated solutions with predicted **σ improvement**.
- **"Why" question answered:** *How do we permanently fix the root causes?*

#### **Phase 5: CONTROL**
- **Purpose:** Sustain the gains by locking in the improvements through standardisation.
- **Key Tools:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) charts, control plans, updated SOPs, training plans, dashboards.
- **Deliverable:** Updated **Control Plan**, **SOPs**, and a **handover** to the process owner.
- **"Why" question answered:** *How do we ensure the fix lasts?*

### 2.2 The Six Sigma Organizational Structure (Belts & Roles)

Six Sigma uses a **martial-arts inspired hierarchy** to define expertise levels and project responsibilities:

| Belt / Role | Core Responsibility | Typical Profile |
|---|---|---|
| **Executive / Sponsor** | Sets vision, allocates budget, removes roadblocks | CEO, VP, Plant Head |
| **Champion** | Project sponsor, selects projects, coaches Black Belts | Senior Manager |
| **Master Black Belt (MBB)** | Trains/coaches BBs & GBs, mentors complex projects, sets standards | Full-time Six Sigma expert (internal consultant) |
| **Black Belt (BB)** | Leads cross-functional projects, full-time, certified | Project leader (full-time for 1-2 years) |
| **Green Belt (GB)** | Leads smaller projects or supports BBs (part-time) | Department employee (part-time on Six Sigma) |
| **Yellow Belt** | Participates as team members, basic awareness | Floor employees, SMEs |
| **White Belt** | Basic awareness only | All employees (introductory level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the different roles and responsibilities in a Six Sigma organization."* Master the table above — it is a guaranteed 7-10 marks.

### 2.3 Process Selection & Financial Justification

Projects are chosen based on:
1. **Strategic alignment** with business goals
2. **Customer impact** (CTQ linkage)
3. **Financial impact:** Calculated using **ROI, NPV, Payback Period**
4. **Feasibility:** Achievable within 4-6 months

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$ | D = defects, N = units, O = opportunities/unit | Measure phase |
| 2 | $\text{Yield} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100\%$ | Rolled throughput yield (RTY) | Process performance |
| 3 | $Z = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{\sigma}$ (one-sided) | Z-score for capability | Measure phase |
| 4 | $Z_{\text{long-term}} = Z_{\text{short-term}} - 1.5$ | Motorola's 1.5σ drift | Standard 6σ equivalence |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability (potential) | Statistical capability |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability (actual/centered) | Real capability |
| 7 | $P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s}$ | Process performance (using sample s) | Long-term performance |
| 8 | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3s},\ \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3s}\right)$ | Performance index (actual) | Stability assessment |
| 9 | $\text{Centering Factor} = \dfrac{C_{pk}}{C_p}$ | Measures shift from target | Tells if process is centered |
| 10 | $\sigma = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | σ estimated from average range | For X-bar-R charts |
| 11 | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Upper control limit (X-bar chart) | Control phase |
| 12 | $LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | Lower control limit (X-bar chart) | Control phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Cp / Cpk** → uses **σ** (within-subgroup), reflects **short-term potential** with **stable** process.
> - **Pp / Ppk** → uses **s** (overall standard deviation), reflects **long-term actual** performance.
> - **Cpk ≤ Cp always**. If **Cpk = Cp**, process is **perfectly centered**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Semiconductor fabs (Intel, TSMC) — defect density per cm².
- **Healthcare:** Reducing surgical errors and hospital readmission rates.
- **Service Industry:** Banking (loan processing), IT (software defect reduction — CMM Level 5 mapping).
- **Logistics:** Reducing shipment damage claims in e-commerce.
- **HR:** Reducing employee attrition through Root Cause Analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solved Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: The Link Between DPMO and Sigma Level (Z)

The probability of a defect occurring beyond a specification limit, for a **normally distributed** process with mean $\bar{X}$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, is given by the standard normal CDF tail:

$$P(\text{Defect}) = 1 - \Phi(Z)$$

where $Z = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{\sigma}$ for an upper-spec process.

The expected number of defects per million opportunities is:

$$\text{DPMO} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times P(\text{Defect})$$

For a perfectly centered process, **6σ on each side** of the mean corresponds to $Z = 6$:

$$P(\text{Defect}) = 2 \times \left[1 - \Phi(6)\right]$$

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{short-term}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2 \times \left[1 - \Phi(6)\right] \approx 0.002 \text{ (negligible)}$$

Motorola's **1.5σ shift correction** for long-term drift gives the practical benchmark:

$$Z_{\text{LT}} = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5$$

$$\text{DPMO}_{\text{long-term}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 2 \times \left[1 - \Phi(4.5)\right] = 3.4$$

This is the **defining 6σ benchmark** the world uses.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: DPMO & Sigma Level Calculation

**Problem:** A call center processes **5,000 calls per day**. Each call has **4 opportunities** for error. Over **30 days**, the team recorded **600 defects**.
*Find: (a) DPMO, (b) Yield, (c) Sigma Level (using standard conversion tables).*

#### Step 1 — Calculate Total Opportunities
Total opportunities = Units × Opportunities per unit
$$\text{Total Opps} = 5{,}000 \times 30 \times 4 = 600{,}000$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate DPMO
Using the standard formula:
$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O} = \dfrac{600 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{600{,}000{,}000}{600{,}000} = 1{,}000$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Yield
$$\text{Yield} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100\% = 99.9\%$$

#### Step 4 — Determine Sigma Level
From the standard DPMO-to-Sigma conversion table:

| DPMO Range | Sigma Level |
|---|---|
| ≤ 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 233 | 5.0 |
| 3,210 | 4.5 |
| 6,210 | 4.0 |
| 66,807 | 3.0 |

For **DPMO = 1,000**, the process is operating at approximately **4.6σ** (long-term).

> **Conclusion:** The call center is at **4.6σ**, which is better than 4σ industry average but **1.4σ short** of a true Six Sigma process. Improvement projects (DMAIC) are warranted.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Process Capability Indices (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk)

**Problem:** A shaft diameter specification is **LSL = 24.90 mm, USL = 25.10 mm**.
Process data: $\bar{X} = 25.04$ mm, $\sigma = 0.015$ mm (within-subgroup), $s = 0.018$ mm (overall std dev from 100 samples).
*Find: Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk, and interpret the process.*

#### Step 1 — Calculate Cp (Potential Capability)
$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.090} = 2.222$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate Cpk (Actual Capability)
$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.10 - 25.04}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.045} = 1.333$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{25.04 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.045} = 3.111$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.333,\ 3.111) = 1.333$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Pp (Performance)
$$P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s} = \dfrac{0.20}{6 \times 0.018} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.108} = 1.852$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Ppk (Performance Index)
$$P_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3s} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.054} = 1.111$$

$$P_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3s} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.054} = 2.593$$

$$P_{pk} = \min(1.111,\ 2.593) = 1.111$$

#### Step 5 — Interpretation Summary

| Index | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cp** | 2.222 | Excellent potential — wide spec vs. spread |
| **Cpk** | 1.333 | Process is **shifted off-center** (towards USL) |
| **Pp** | 1.852 | Long-term performance is acceptable but not world-class |
| **Ppk** | 1.111 | Actual long-term performance shows the shift |

> **Centering Factor:** $\dfrac{C_{pk}}{C_p} = \dfrac{1.333}{2.222} = 0.60$ → **Process is only 60% centered** (50% shift from target). DMAIC Analyze phase must find the cause of the upward shift (likely tool wear or machine calibration drift).

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Control Chart Limits (X-bar-R)

**Problem:** 5 samples of size $n=5$ were collected. $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50$ mm, $\bar{R} = 0.8$ mm. From the standard table, $A_2 = 0.577$ for $n=5$.
*Find: UCL and LCL of the X-bar chart.*

#### Step 1 — Control Limits
$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50 + (0.577 \times 0.8) = 50 + 0.4616 = 50.462 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50 - 0.4616 = 49.538 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 2 — Estimation of σ
Using $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n = 5$:
$$\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.8}{2.326} = 0.344 \text{ mm}$$

> **Conclusion:** Future sample means are expected to fall between 49.538 and 50.462 mm. If a sample mean falls outside, the process is **out of statistical control** and an **assignable cause investigation** is triggered (Control phase).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each DMAIC Tool

| Phase | Tool | Statistical / Qualitative? | Question Answered |
|---|---|---|---|
| Define | SIPOC | Qualitative | What is the process? |
| Define | VOC, CTQ | Qualitative | What does the customer want? |
| Measure | Pareto | Quantitative | Which defects dominate? |
| Measure | MSA (Gage R&R) | Statistical | Is our measurement system reliable? |
| Measure | DPMO, Cp, Cpk | Statistical | What is the baseline σ level? |
| Analyze | Fishbone (Ishikawa) | Qualitative | What are potential causes? |
| Analyze | 5-Whys | Qualitative | Why does each cause occur? |
| Analyze | Hypothesis Testing | Statistical | Is the cause statistically significant? |
| Analyze | Regression | Statistical | What is the relationship strength? |
| Improve | DOE | Statistical | What is the optimal X setting? |
| Improve | Poka-Yoke | Engineering | How do we make it fail-safe? |
| Control | SPC Charts | Statistical | Is the process stable? |
| Control | Control Plan | Documentation | How do we sustain the fix? |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1: The DMAIC Roadmap — Functional Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Business Problem Identified] --> phase1
    phase1["PHASE 1: DEFINE"] --> p1a["Identify Customer CTQs"]
    p1a --> p1b["Develop Project Charter"]
    p1b --> p1c["Build SIPOC Map"]
    p1c --> gateA[Gate Review: Charter Approved]
    gateA --> phase2
    phase2["PHASE 2: MEASURE"] --> p2a["Validate Measurement System MSA"]
    p2a --> p2b["Collect Baseline Data"]
    p2b --> p2c["Calculate DPMO and Sigma Level"]
    p2c --> p2d["Compute Cp Cpk Pp Ppk"]
    p2d --> gateB[Gate Review: Baseline Confirmed]
    gateB --> phase3
    phase3["PHASE 3: ANALYZE"] --> p3a["Fishbone Diagram"]
    p3a --> p3b["Five Whys Analysis"]
    p3b --> p3c["Hypothesis Testing Regression ANOVA"]
    p3c --> p3d["Identify Validated Root Causes X"]
    p3d --> gateC[Gate Review: Root Causes Approved]
    gateC --> phase4
    phase4["PHASE 4: IMPROVE"] --> p4a["Design Experiments DOE"]
    p4a --> p4b["Pilot Solutions Poka Yoke"]
    p4b --> p4c["Validate Sigma Improvement"]
    p4c --> gateD[Gate Review: Solution Validated]
    gateD --> phase5
    phase5["PHASE 5: CONTROL"] --> p5a["Develop Control Plan"]
    p5a --> p5b["Implement SPC Charts"]
    p5b --> p5c["Update SOPs and Training"]
    p5c --> p5d["Handover to Process Owner"]
    p5d --> endZ[End: Sustained Sigma Improvement]
```

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2: Six Sigma Organizational Belt Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topA["EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP"] --> roleB
    roleB["CHAMPION"] --> roleC
    roleC["MASTER BLACK BELT MBB"] --> roleD
    roleD["BLACK BELT BB"] --> roleE
    roleE["GREEN BELT GB"] --> roleF
    roleF["YELLOW BELT"] --> roleG
    roleG["WHITE BELT All Employees"]
    roleC -.Mentors and Trains.-> roleD
    roleD -.Leads Projects.-> roleE
    roleE -.Supports.-> roleF
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3: Process Capability Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Process Data X-bar R"] --> calcA[Calculate sigma and s]
    calcA --> calcB["Compute USL minus LSL Spec Spread"]
    calcB --> idxA["Cp: Potential Capability"]
    calcB --> idxB["Cpk: Actual Capability Short term"]
    calcA --> idxC["Pp: Long term Performance"]
    calcA --> idxD["Ppk: Long term Performance Index"]
    idxA --> interA{Is Cpk greater than 1.33}
    interA -- Yes --> outA[Process Capable]
    interA -- No --> outB[Process Needs Improvement DMAIC]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram 4: DPMO to Sigma Level Conversion Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1["Raw Defect Count D"] --> a2["Total Units N"]
    a2 --> a3["Opportunities per Unit O"]
    a3 --> a4["DPMO equals D times 1M divided by N times O"]
    a4 --> a5["Convert to Yield percent"]
    a5 --> a6["Apply Normal CDF to find Z"]
    a6 --> a7["Sigma Level equals Z plus 1.5 long term"]
    a7 --> a8["Benchmark against 6 sigma equals 3.4 DPMO"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define DPMO. What is the DPMO value corresponding to a 6σ process?**

> **Model Answer:**
> **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is the number of defects observed per million opportunities in a process. It is calculated as:
> $$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 1{,}000{,}000}{N \times O}$$
> A 6σ process corresponds to **DPMO = 3.4**, which translates to a yield of **99.99966%**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for the 3.4 value)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List the five phases of DMAIC and state the primary objective of the "Control" phase.**

> **Model Answer:**
> The five phases of DMAIC are: **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol.
> The primary objective of the **Control** phase is to **sustain the improvements achieved** by implementing statistical process control (SPC) charts, updating standard operating procedures (SOPs), creating control plans, and handing over the improved process to the process owner — ensuring the gains do not regress. *(3 marks: 2 for phases, 1 for Control objective)*

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the five phases of the DMAIC methodology in detail. For each phase, mention the key tools used and the deliverable produced. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **1. DEFINE Phase** — *Objective: Identify the problem and project scope*
> - **Key Tools:** Project Charter, VOC (Voice of Customer), SIPOC diagram, CTQ tree, Kano model.
> - **Deliverable:** A signed **Project Charter** containing the problem statement, business case, goal, scope, team, timeline, and metrics.
> - *Why:* Forces the team to clearly define "what are we solving, and for whom?"
>
> **2. MEASURE Phase** — *Objective: Quantify the current process performance*
> - **Key Tools:** Data collection plan, MSA (Gage R&R), process mapping, baseline DPMO, Cp/Cpk, Pareto charts, run charts.
> - **Deliverable:** A validated **baseline σ level** with statistical confidence.
> - *Why:* You cannot improve what you cannot measure.
>
> **3. ANALYZE Phase** — *Objective: Identify root causes of defects*
> - **Key Tools:** Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, 5-Whys, hypothesis testing (t-test, chi-square), regression, ANOVA, FMEA.
> - **Deliverable:** A prioritized, statistically validated list of **vital few X's (root causes)**.
> - *Why:* Symptom treatment wastes resources; root-cause analysis is sustainable.
>
> **4. IMPROVE Phase** — *Objective: Develop and implement solutions*
> - **Key Tools:** Design of Experiments (DOE), Poka-Yoke, Kaizen, pilot testing, solution matrix, FMEA re-evaluation.
> - **Deliverable:** Implemented, pilot-validated solutions that demonstrably raise the σ level.
> - *Why:* Solutions must be tested, not assumed.
>
> **5. CONTROL Phase** — *Objective: Sustain the improvements*
> - **Key Tools:** SPC control charts (X-bar, R, p, c), control plans, updated SOPs, training plans, visual management.
> - **Deliverable:** Updated **Control Plan** and **SOPs** handed over to the process owner.
> - *Why:* Without control, the process drifts back to its old state.
>
> *(7 marks: 1 per phase × 5 = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for tools/deliverables integration)*

---

**(b) A manufacturing process produces 8,000 units per day. Each unit has 5 opportunities for defects. Over 25 working days, a total of 800 defects were recorded. Calculate the DPMO, Yield, and the long-term Sigma Level. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1: Total Units (N)**
> $$N = 8{,}000 \times 25 = 200{,}000 \text{ units}$$
>
> **Step 2: Total Opportunities**
> $$\text{Total Opps} = N \times O = 200{,}000 \times 5 = 1{,}000{,}000$$
>
> **Step 3: DPMO**
> $$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{800 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 800$$
>
> **Step 4: Yield**
> $$\text{Yield} = \left(1 - \dfrac{800}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) \times 100\% = 99.92\%$$
>
> **Step 5: Long-term Sigma Level**
> From the standard DPMO-to-Sigma conversion table:
> - DPMO = 233 → 5.0σ
> - DPMO = 3,210 → 4.5σ
> - DPMO = 800 → ≈ **4.65σ (long-term)**
>
> **Conclusion:** The process operates at ~4.65σ, falling short of the 6σ benchmark (3.4 DPMO). Improvement projects are recommended to move closer to a 5σ+ level.
>
> *(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1-3, 1 for step 4, 2 for step 5 conversion and conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the roles of Champion, Master Black Belt, Black Belt, and Green Belt in a Six Sigma organization. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **1. CHAMPION**
> - **Role:** Acts as the **project sponsor and sponsor**.
> - **Responsibilities:** Selects and prioritizes Six Sigma projects aligned with business strategy; secures funding and resources; removes organizational roadblocks; reviews project progress at gate reviews; coaches Black Belts on project alignment.
> - **Profile:** Senior manager (Director / VP level) — *not full-time on Six Sigma*.
>
> **2. MASTER BLACK BELT (MBB)**
> - **Role:** The **technical expert and mentor** of the Six Sigma program.
> - **Responsibilities:** Trains and certifies Black Belts and Green Belts; mentors Black Belts on complex statistical tools (DOE, regression); develops the organization's Six Sigma standards, tools, and roadmap; leads cross-functional strategic projects.
> - **Profile:** Full-time internal consultant with 5+ years of Six Sigma experience and multiple BB certifications.
>
> **3. BLACK BELT (BB)**
> - **Role:** The **project leader** who drives DMAIC projects to completion.
> - **Responsibilities:** Leads 1-2 cross-functional projects per year; applies DMAIC and advanced statistical tools; coaches Green Belts; reports progress to the Champion; delivers quantified financial savings (typically $100K–$1M+ per project).
> - **Profile:** Full-time on Six Sigma for 1-2 years; must complete rigorous certification including a completed project.
>
> **4. GREEN BELT (GB)**
> - **Role:** A **part-time project leader** who leads smaller-scale improvements.
> - **Responsibilities:** Leads departmental or sub-process DMAIC projects; supports Black Belts on larger projects as a team member; applies basic Six Sigma tools (Pareto, fishbone, control charts); implements solutions in their functional area.
> - **Profile:** Department employee, ~25% of time on Six Sigma; ideal for process owners and team leads.
>
> **Hierarchy of Authority & Expertise:** Champion > MBB > BB > GB. Each level acts as a multiplier of the next.
>
> *(7 marks: ~1.5 marks per role, 0.5 for hierarchy note)*

---

**(b) A CNC lathe produces shafts with specification LSL = 19.95 mm and USL = 20.05 mm. The process mean is $\bar{X} = 20.01$ mm, within-subgroup standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.012$ mm, and overall standard deviation is $s = 0.015$ mm.
Calculate Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk, and the Centering Factor. Interpret the results. (7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Step 1: Cp (Potential Capability)**
> $$C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{20.05 - 19.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389$$
>
> **Step 2: Cpk (Actual Capability)**
> $$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{20.05 - 20.01}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.036} = 1.111$$
> $$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{20.01 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.036} = 1.667$$
> $$C_{pk} = \min(1.111,\ 1.667) = 1.111$$
>
> **Step 3: Pp (Long-term Performance)**
> $$P_p = \dfrac{0.10}{6 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.090} = 1.111$$
>
> **Step 4: Ppk (Long-term Performance Index)**
> $$P_{pk,\text{upper}} = \dfrac{0.04}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.045} = 0.889$$
> $$P_{pk,\text{lower}} = \dfrac{0.06}{3 \times 0.015} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.045} = 1.333$$
> $$P_{pk} = \min(0.889,\ 1.333) = 0.889$$
>
> **Step 5: Centering Factor**
> $$\text{CF} = \dfrac{C_{pk}}{C_p} = \dfrac{1.111}{1.389} = 0.80$$
>
> **Interpretation:**
> - **Cp = 1.389** indicates good **potential** (process spread vs. spec is healthy).
> - **Cpk = 1.111** shows the process is **off-center** (shifted toward the USL — Upper Specification Limit).
> - **Pp < Cp** (1.111 < 1.389) indicates the **long-term variation is greater** than short-term — process is **not stable** over time (likely tool wear, temperature drift, or material variation).
> - **Ppk = 0.889 < 1.0** confirms the long-term process is **NOT capable** — it is producing out-of-spec parts over time.
> - **Centering Factor 0.80** means the process mean is shifted 20% away from target → **requires recentering** at the Analyze/Improve phase.
> - **Action:** Reduce σ (process variation), recenter $\bar{X}$ to 20.00 mm, and implement SPC to detect drift.
>
> *(7 marks: 1 each for Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk, CF, 2 for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing Cp with Cpk** — *Cp assumes centering, Cpk does not.* Writing Cpk = Cp is a **-2 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Using wrong formula for DPMO** — Students forget the **1,000,000 multiplier**. Always check the formula is dimensionless-correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift** — DPMO-to-Sigma conversion must be **long-term (Z − 1.5)**, not short-term. Mistake here loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Mixing σ and s** — Use σ (within-subgroup) for Cp/Cpk, and s (overall) for Pp/Ppk. A common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Skipping the interpretation** — KTU specifically awards marks for **interpretive comments**, not just numerical answers. Always close with "the process is/is not capable because..."

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Six Sigma = 3.4 DPMO = 99.99966% yield = 6σ quality level** (Motorola, 1986, Bill Smith).
- ✅ **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control. Each phase has **specific tools** and **deliverables**.
- ✅ **Belt Hierarchy:** Executive → Champion → MBB → BB → GB → Yellow → White. **MBB trains BB, BB leads projects, GB supports.**
- ✅ **DPMO Formula:** $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$ — memorize exactly.
- ✅ **Capability vs. Performance:** Cp/Cpk use **σ** (short-term); Pp/Ppk use **s** (long-term).
- ✅ **Centering Factor** $C_{pk}/C_p$ = 1.0 (perfectly centered) < 1.0 (off-center).
- ✅ **1.5σ shift:** Empirical long-term drift correction. **Long-term Z = Short-term Z − 1.5.**
- ✅ **Process capable if Cpk ≥ 1.33** (industry minimum); **Cpk ≥ 1.67** is the Six Sigma target.
- ✅ **Control Charts** use $A_2$ for X-bar limits, $d_2$ for σ estimation from R-bar.
- ✅ **DMAIC is a closed-loop cycle** — Control hands back to Define for the next project.
- ✅ **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing) is a critical **Improve** phase tool.
- ✅ **SIPOC** = Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers — a **Define** phase tool.
- ✅ **VOC & CTQ** translate customer voice into measurable quality targets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability - Meaning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Process Capability - Meaning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a manufacturing or service process to produce output that conforms to the engineering specifications or customer requirements. It quantifies the relationship between the **natural variability of the process** (Voice of the Process) and the **specified tolerance limits** (Voice of the Customer).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, process capability is formally expressed as the ratio of the **tolerance spread (specification width)** to the **process spread (natural variation width)**, typically measured in standard deviation units (sigma).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Process capability is the measured, inherent reproducibility of a product or process under controlled operating conditions, expressed as the capability index **Cp** or the centered index **Cpk**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain English Explanation with Real-World Analogy:**

Imagine you are a baker trying to bake cookies that must weigh exactly between **48 grams and 52 grams** (this is the customer's requirement — the specification limits). However, your oven, mixing habits, and ingredient humidity cause natural fluctuations. Some cookies come out at **47g**, others at **53g**, even though you tried to make them all **50g**.

**Process Capability** asks: *"How well does my baking process fit within the customer's acceptable weight range?"*

- If your cookies naturally vary only between **49g and 51g** → Your process easily fits within 48–52g → **High Capability (Cp is large)**
- If your cookies naturally vary between **45g and 55g** → Your process often misses the target → **Low Capability (Cp is small, defects occur)**

The key insight: **A capable process produces output that consistently falls within the specification limits** without defects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU):**
> - **Specification Limits (SL)** = Customer's Requirement (External, Contractual)
> - **Control Limits (CL)** = Process's Natural Behavior (Internal, Statistical)
> - Process Capability compares the **SL** (what customer wants) with the **CL** (what process delivers).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Highlights

- **Voice of the Customer (VOC):** The specification limits (USL, LSL) demanded by the customer.
- **Voice of the Process (VOP):** The natural process spread, typically $\pm 3\sigma$ from the mean.
- **Tolerance:** The total allowable variation, $T = USL - LSL$.
- **Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):** The fundamental unit of process variation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Voices

A Six Sigma process management framework relies on comparing two distinct boundaries:

1. **Voice of the Customer (VOC)** — Specification Limits:
   - Upper Specification Limit (**USL**)
   - Lower Specification Limit (**LSL**)

2. **Voice of the Process (VOP)** — Natural Process Spread:
   - Defined by $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ for a stable, normally distributed process
   - Represents the natural capability envelope of the process itself

### 2.2 Why Process Capability Matters — The 'Why' Behind the Concept

**The 'Why':** Traditional quality control (inspection) only catches defects after they occur. Process capability shifts the focus from **detection to prevention**. By quantifying how well a process meets specifications, engineers can:
- Predict the defect rate before production
- Benchmark suppliers objectively
- Justify capital investment in process improvements
- Drive continuous improvement (Kaizen) projects

**The 'How':** Process capability is computed using sample data collected under statistical process control (SPC). The mean ($\bar{x}$) and standard deviation ($s$ or $\sigma$) of the process are calculated, and then compared to the specification limits.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Expression | Interpretation | Target Value |
|---|------------------|--------------------------|----------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Process Capability Index (Cp)** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Compares specification width to natural process spread | $C_p \geq 1.00$ (Minimum); $C_p \geq 1.33$ (Industry Standard); $C_p \geq 2.00$ (Six Sigma) |
| 2 | **One-Sided Capability (Cpu)** | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Capability relative to upper specification only | $C_{pu} \geq 1.00$ |
| 3 | **One-Sided Capability (Cpl)** | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | Capability relative to lower specification only | $C_{pl} \geq 1.00$ |
| 4 | **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Capability considering process centering | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ |
| 5 | **Process Performance Index (Pp)** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Uses sample standard deviation (overall variation) | $P_p \geq 1.33$ |
| 6 | **Process Performance Index (Ppk)** | $P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Performance considering centering (overall) | $P_{pk} \geq 1.33$ |
| 7 | **Sigma Level** | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma} \times 3 + 1.5$ | Equivalent sigma quality level | $\geq 4.5\sigma$ (World Class); $6\sigma$ (Six Sigma) |
| 8 | **DPMO** | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects} \times 10^6}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $\leq 3.4$ (Six Sigma) |
| 9 | **Tolerance** | $T = USL - LSL$ | Total specification width | Engineering defined |
| 10 | **Standard Normal Z-Value** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardized distance from mean | $Z \geq 4.5\sigma$ for Six Sigma |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Industries:** Used to qualify CNC machining, injection molding, and assembly lines.
- **Service Sectors:** Applied to call center call duration, banking transaction times, hospital patient discharge cycles.
- **Software Industry:** Defect density tracking per lines of code.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Vendor capability assessment before awarding contracts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Six Sigma demands a **short-term capability** of $6\sigma$ between the mean and the nearest specification limit, which translates to **3.4 DPMO** after accounting for the long-term **1.5-sigma shift**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cp Formula (Process Capability Index)

**Starting Premise:**
The natural spread of a stable, normally distributed process occupies approximately $\pm 3\sigma$ from the process mean ($\mu$). This is a direct consequence of the **Empirical Rule** in statistics, where 99.73% of observations fall within this range.

**Step 1: Define the Natural Process Spread**
The total width of the natural variation of the process is given by:
$$\text{Process Spread} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma)$$
$$\text{Process Spread} = \mu + 3\sigma - \mu + 3\sigma$$

**Step 2: Simplify the Process Spread**
$$\text{Process Spread} = 6\sigma$$

*Explanation:* The mean terms cancel out, leaving exactly six standard deviations as the total natural process width.

**Step 3: Define the Specification Width**
The specification width is the difference between the upper and lower specification limits:
$$\text{Specification Width} = USL - LSL$$

**Step 4: Formulate the Ratio**
Process capability compares the specification width to the process spread. The ratio is:

$$C_p = \dfrac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Process Spread}}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and Finalize**
$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

*Explanation:* This ratio tells us how many "natural process widths" of $\pm 3\sigma$ fit inside the specification tolerance. A value of 1.0 means the process just barely fits.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Cpk Formula (Centered Capability)

**Limitation of Cp:** The Cp index assumes the process mean is exactly centered between USL and LSL. In reality, this is rarely true.

**Step 1: Calculate Upper Capability**
$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

*Explanation:* This measures how many 3-sigma units exist between the process mean and the upper specification limit.

**Step 2: Calculate Lower Capability**
$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

*Explanation:* This measures how many 3-sigma units exist between the process mean and the lower specification limit.

**Step 3: Take the Minimum**
The actual process capability is governed by the **worst-case side**, because if either side produces defects, the process is incapable:
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$$

**Step 4: Consolidated Form**
$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

*Explanation:* This formulation inherently penalizes off-center processes, making it a more realistic measure than Cp alone.

---

### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Problem Solving Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC lathe produces steel shafts with specification limits of **LSL = 24.90 mm** and **USL = 25.10 mm**. From a sample of 100 shafts, the process mean is calculated as $\bar{x} = 25.03$ mm and the standard deviation is $s = 0.015$ mm.

**Required:** Calculate Cp, Cpu, Cpl, Cpk, and interpret the result.

**Step 1: Calculate Cp (Process Capability Index)**
$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

Substituting the values:
$$C_p = \dfrac{25.10 - 24.90}{6 \times 0.015}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{0.20}{0.090}$$

$$C_p = 2.222$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cpu (Upper Capability)**
$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{25.10 - 25.03}{3 \times 0.015}$$

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.045}$$

$$C_{pu} = 1.556$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cpl (Lower Capability)**
$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{25.03 - 24.90}{3 \times 0.015}$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.045}$$

$$C_{pl} = 2.889$$

**Step 4: Calculate Cpk (Centered Capability)**
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.556, 2.889)$$

$$C_{pk} = 1.556$$

**Step 5: Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - $C_p = 2.222$ indicates the **process spread is more than twice narrower** than the tolerance — excellent precision.
> - $C_{pk} = 1.556$ (lower than Cp) indicates the **process is shifted toward the USL** (since Cpu is the minimum).
> - Both indices exceed the industry benchmark of **1.33**, so the process is **capable but slightly off-center**.
> - **Recommendation:** Re-center the machine by adjusting the offset downward by 0.03 mm to achieve $C_{pk} \approx 2.222$.

---

### 3.4 Capability Interpretation Matrix

| Capability Index Value | Interpretation | Action Required |
|------------------------|----------------|------------------|
| $C_{pk} < 1.00$ | **Incapable Process** — Producing defects | Urgent process overhaul |
| $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ | **Marginally Capable** — High defect risk | Continuous monitoring required |
| $1.33 \leq C_{pk} < 1.67$ | **Capable Process** — Industry standard | Maintain current controls |
| $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ | **Highly Capable** — World class | Best practice benchmarking |
| $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ | **Six Sigma Capability** | Lean Six Sigma benchmark |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Capability Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Materials] --> B[Process Under SPC Control]
    B --> C{Is Process Stable?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Calculate Mean and Sigma]
    C -->|No| E[Investigate Assignable Cause]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Determine Natural Process Spread]
    F --> F1[Process Spread equals 6 Sigma]
    D --> G[Retrieve Specification Limits]
    G --> G1[USL from Voice of Customer]
    G --> G2[LSL from Voice of Customer]
    F1 --> H[Compute Cp Index]
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    D --> I[Compute Cpu and Cpl]
    G1 --> I
    G2 --> I
    I --> J[Calculate Cpk equals min Cpu Cpl]
    H --> K[Compare Cp and Cpk]
    J --> K
    K --> L{Cpk greater than 1.33}
    L -->|Yes| M[Process is Capable]
    L -->|No| N[Process Improvement Required]
    M --> O[Monitor and Sustain]
    N --> P[Apply DMAIC Methodology]
    P --> B
```

### 4.2 Visual Logic: Comparison of Cp vs Cpk

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Cp Formula] --> A1[Uses USL minus LSL divided by 6 sigma]
    A1 --> A2[Assumes perfect centering]
    B[Cpk Formula] --> B1[Uses minimum of two one-sided indices]
    B1 --> B2[Penalizes off-center processes]
    A2 --> C{When to use}
    B2 --> C
    C -->|Initial assessment| D[Use Cp]
    C -->|Realistic evaluation| E[Use Cpk]
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: From Data Collection to Capability Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sample Size greater than 100 subgroups] --> S2[Check Normality using Histogram]
    S2 --> S3[Verify Stability via Control Charts]
    S3 --> S4[Compute X-bar and R or S]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Capability Formulas]
    S5 --> S6[Generate Capability Report]
    S6 --> S7[Decision: Capable or Not]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These flow diagrams map the logical architecture of process capability analysis rather than physical drawings, as the underlying statistical operations cannot be visually depicted through a physical schematic. This follows KTU Module 4 conceptual expectations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1:** Define the term **Process Capability**. State its significance in Total Quality Management.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Process capability is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a process to produce output within the engineering specification limits (USL and LSL). It is mathematically quantified by the capability indices **Cp** and **Cpk**.

**Significance in TQM:**
1. It enables the shift from **reactive inspection** to **proactive prevention** of defects.
2. It provides a common numerical benchmark for comparing processes, machines, and suppliers.
3. It forms the quantitative foundation for **Six Sigma** and **continuous improvement (Kaizen)** programs.

---

**Question 2:** Differentiate between **Cp** and **Cpk**. Why is Cpk considered a more realistic indicator?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4 Test]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Cp | Cpk |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Formula | $(USL - LSL) / (6\sigma)$ | $\min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ |
| Centering Assumption | Assumes process is perfectly centered | Accounts for actual process mean |
| Realism | Theoretically optimistic | Realistic — penalizes off-center processes |

**Reason for Cpk being more realistic:** In real production, the process mean rarely coincides exactly with the midpoint of the specification limits. Cpk captures this shift and reports the worst-case capability, making it a true reflection of process performance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, Question A]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of process capability with a neat sketch showing the relationship between specification limits and natural process spread. Discuss the importance of the **1.5-sigma shift** in Six Sigma. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The diameter of pistons manufactured in an automotive plant must lie between **74.90 mm (LSL)** and **75.10 mm (USL)**. From 50 subgroups of size 5, the average diameter is **75.04 mm** and the standard deviation is **0.018 mm**. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and the equivalent **sigma level**. Comment on the process capability. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

The natural process spread is $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, while specification limits are fixed by design requirements. When the process spread is narrower than the specification width, the process is capable.

**Sketch Concept (to be drawn on answer sheet):**
- Draw a horizontal axis representing the dimension.
- Mark LSL on the far left, USL on the far right.
- Plot a bell-shaped normal curve in the middle.
- Mark $\mu$ (process mean) and show $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ as the natural spread.
- If the bell curve fits comfortably between LSL and USL → Capable process.

**Importance of 1.5-Sigma Shift:**
In long-term production, processes experience a mean shift of approximately **1.5 sigma** due to minor disturbances, tool wear, and environmental variations. Motorola's Six Sigma methodology accounts for this shift, requiring a **short-term capability of 6 sigma** so that even after a 1.5-sigma drift, the defect rate does not exceed **3.4 DPMO**.

**Diagrammatic Explanation:**
Without the 1.5-sigma shift, $C_p = 2.00$ corresponds to 6-sigma quality only in the short term. After accounting for the shift, true long-term performance corresponds to **4.5 sigma** with **3.4 defects per million opportunities**.

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $LSL = 74.90$ mm
- $USL = 75.10$ mm
- $\mu = \bar{x} = 75.04$ mm
- $\sigma = s = 0.018$ mm

**Step 1: Calculate Cp**
$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{75.10 - 74.90}{6 \times 0.018}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{0.20}{0.108} = 1.852$$

**Step 2: Calculate Cpu**
$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{75.10 - 75.04}{3 \times 0.018}$$

$$C_{pu} = \dfrac{0.06}{0.054} = 1.111$$

**Step 3: Calculate Cpl**
$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{75.04 - 74.90}{3 \times 0.018}$$

$$C_{pl} = \dfrac{0.14}{0.054} = 2.593$$

**Step 4: Calculate Cpk**
$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl}) = \min(1.111, 2.593) = 1.111$$

**Step 5: Equivalent Sigma Level**

The short-term sigma level is:
$$\text{Sigma Level} = 3 \times C_{pk} + 1.5$$

$$\text{Sigma Level} = 3 \times 1.111 + 1.5 = 3.333 + 1.5 = 4.833\,\sigma$$

**Step 6: Interpretation**
- $C_p = 1.852$ → Process has the *potential* to be highly capable.
- $C_{pk} = 1.111$ → Process is marginally capable and **shifted toward the USL**.
- The shift between mean (75.04) and midpoint of specifications (75.00) is **0.04 mm**.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process by **0.04 mm downward** to improve $C_{pk}$ to the level of $C_p$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4, Question B]**

**(a)** Explain the terms **Cp, Cpk, Pp, and Ppk**. Under what conditions should Pp and Ppk be used instead of Cp and Cpk? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pharmaceutical company fills injection vials with a target volume of **10.00 ml**. The specification limits are **LSL = 9.80 ml** and **USL = 10.20 ml**. After collecting 200 vials, the sample mean is **10.05 ml** and the sample standard deviation is **0.045 ml**. Calculate Pp, Ppk, and estimate the **DPMO** of the process. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Cp (Process Capability Index):** Uses the **within-subgroup standard deviation** estimated from control chart data (typically R-bar / d2 or S-bar / c4). Represents short-term, inherent capability of a stable process.

**Cpk (Centered Process Capability Index):** Same as Cp but adjusted for the actual position of the process mean relative to specification limits.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma_{within}}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{within}}\right)$$

**Pp (Process Performance Index):** Uses the **overall standard deviation** (sample standard deviation, $s$) of the entire dataset. Reflects total process variation including both common-cause and special-cause variations.

$$P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$$

**Ppk (Performance Index Centered):** Same as Pp but adjusted for centering.

$$P_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$$

**When to Use Pp/Ppk Instead of Cp/Cpk:**

| Condition | Preferred Index |
|-----------|-----------------|
| Process is **stable** and in statistical control | Cp, Cpk |
| Process is **unstable** or not yet verified | Pp, Ppk |
| **Short-term** capability assessment | Cp, Cpk |
| **Long-term / overall** performance evaluation | Pp, Ppk |
| Initial pilot studies before control charts are established | Pp, Ppk |

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Given Data:**
- $LSL = 9.80$ ml
- $USL = 10.20$ ml
- $\bar{x} = 10.05$ ml
- $s = 0.045$ ml

**Step 1: Calculate Pp**
$$P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \dfrac{10.20 - 9.80}{6 \times 0.045}$$

$$P_p = \dfrac{0.40}{0.270} = 1.481$$

**Step 2: Calculate Upper Performance Index**
$$P_{pu} = \dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s} = \dfrac{10.20 - 10.05}{3 \times 0.045} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.135} = 1.111$$

**Step 3: Calculate Lower Performance Index**
$$P_{pl} = \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s} = \dfrac{10.05 - 9.80}{3 \times 0.045} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.135} = 1.852$$

**Step 4: Calculate Ppk**
$$P_{pk} = \min(P_{pu}, P_{pl}) = \min(1.111, 1.852) = 1.111$$

**Step 5: Estimate DPMO**

The short-term sigma level is:
$$\text{Sigma Level} = 3 \times P_{pk} + 1.5 = 3 \times 1.111 + 1.5 = 4.833\,\sigma$$

For a 4.83-sigma process, the defect rate corresponds approximately to:
$$\text{DPMO} \approx 850 \text{ defects per million opportunities}$$

*(Based on standard Six Sigma yield tables: 4.83σ corresponds to roughly 0.085% defect rate, i.e., 850 DPMO.)*

**Step 6: Interpretation**
- $P_p = 1.481$ indicates the **overall variation** is within industry acceptable limits.
- $P_{pk} = 1.111$ shows the process is **shifted upward by 0.05 ml** from the target of 10.00 ml.
- **Recommendation:** Recalibrate the filling machine downward by 0.05 ml to center the process and improve $P_{pk}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Cp with Cpk:** Students often report only one index. Examiners expect both, with an explicit interpretation of the difference (centering).
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.5-Sigma Shift:** When asked for "sigma level," many students simply multiply $C_{pk}$ by 3 and stop. The correct formula is $\text{Sigma Level} = 3 \times C_{pk} + 1.5$.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Failing to convert measurements to consistent units (e.g., mixing mm and cm) results in a full-mark deduction.
> 4. **Missing the Sketch in Part (a):** KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for a labeled sketch showing USL, LSL, $\mu$, and $\pm 3\sigma$ regions. Skipping this loses easy marks.
> 5. **Reporting Pp as Cp:** When sample standard deviation ($s$) is given without subgroup data, the correct index is **Pp/Ppk**, not Cp/Cpk.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Process Capability** is the quantitative comparison between the **Voice of the Customer (specification limits)** and the **Voice of the Process (natural variation)**.
- **Cp** measures potential capability (assumes centering); **Cpk** measures actual capability (considers centering).
- The natural process spread is always **$6\sigma$** (i.e., $\mu \pm 3\sigma$).
- $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ is the **realistic** capability index used in industry.
- **Pp and Ppk** use sample standard deviation ($s$); used when process stability is not confirmed.
- A **capable process** requires $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$; a **Six Sigma process** demands $C_{pk} \geq 2.00$ (i.e., 6σ short-term).
- **DPMO** for Six Sigma: **3.4 defects per million opportunities** after accounting for the **1.5-sigma shift**.
- Always **re-center** an off-center process before attempting to reduce variation.
- Capability indices are **only valid for stable, normally distributed processes** — verify using control charts and normality tests first.
- KTU most frequently tests: definitions, Cp/Cpk formulas, numerical computation, and interpretation.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance and Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Significance and Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Significance and Measurement" in TQM & Six Sigma?

In the context of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** and **Six Sigma**, "Significance and Measurement" is the **backbone of data-driven decision-making**. It refers to the systematic process of:

1. **Quantifying quality** through statistical and mathematical metrics.
2. **Determining statistical significance** — i.e., whether observed process variations are *real* or simply due to random chance.
3. **Measuring process capability** — i.e., how well a process performs relative to its specifications.
4. **Assessing measurement systems** — i.e., whether the data we collect is reliable, accurate, and repeatable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is foundational for Module 4 because every quality improvement decision (DMAIC phases) rests on accurate, statistically validated measurements. Without significance testing and capability measurement, Six Sigma becomes a buzzword, not a methodology.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus-Aligned)

### **A. Statistical Significance**
> **Statistical Significance** is a measure of whether a observed difference or effect in a process is *unlikely to have occurred by random chance alone*, given a pre-defined probability threshold (called the **significance level, α**).

If the calculated **p-value** is less than **α** (typically **α = 0.05** or **5%**), we reject the **null hypothesis (H₀)** and declare the result *statistically significant*.

### **B. Process Capability**
> **Process Capability** is the ability of a process to consistently produce output within specified tolerance limits. It is measured using indices such as **Cp, Cpk, Pp, and Ppk**.

### **C. Six Sigma Measurement Metrics**
Six Sigma quantifies quality using four interrelated metrics:
- **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities
- **DPM** — Defects Per Million
- **Yield (Y)** — Percentage of defect-free units
- **Sigma Level (Z)** — Short-term process standard deviation equivalent

### **D. Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA)**
> **MSA** is an experimental methodology used to determine the amount of variation introduced by the measurement system itself, ensuring that observed process variation reflects *real* process behavior, not measurement noise.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Gym Scale Story"**

Imagine you weigh yourself daily on a bathroom scale.

- **Significance**: You lost 2 kg in a week. Is this a *real* weight loss or just the scale fluctuating? Statistical significance tells you **whether the change is real**.
- **Measurement**: Your goal weight is 65 kg, with an acceptable range of 63–67 kg. Process capability tells you **how reliably your body stays in that range** day after day.
- **MSA**: If your scale adds ±0.5 kg of random noise, then you cannot trust readings within that margin. MSA tells you **how trustworthy your scale is**.

In a manufacturing plant:
- **The process** = the machine producing parts.
- **The specification** = the customer's tolerance.
- **The measurement system** = the gauge/caliper measuring the part.
- **The decision** = ship or reject.

**Significance and Measurement** answers the four critical questions:
1. *Is the data trustworthy?* (MSA)
2. *Is the process capable?* (Cp, Cpk)
3. *Is the difference real?* (Hypothesis testing)
4. *How good is "good"?* (Sigma level, DPMO)

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Weightage in KTU Pattern |
|---|---|
| Direct conceptual questions on Cp, Cpk, DPMO | Very High (frequent 3-mark & 14-mark questions) |
| Numerical problems on Sigma Level conversion | High (commonly asked 7-mark problems) |
| Hypothesis testing setup | Moderate (asked in Part B) |
| MSA & Gage R&R explanation | Emerging topic (frequently tested in newer schemes) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Sigma Measurement Framework

Six Sigma uses a hierarchical measurement structure. Let us break it down logically:

### **Step 1: Counting Defects**
Every unit produced has multiple *opportunities* for a defect. A defect is *any failure to meet a customer requirement*.

- **Opportunities** = the total number of chances for a defect per unit.
- **Defects** = the number of times a specification is missed.

### **Step 2: Computing Defect Rates**
From raw defect counts, we compute:

- **DPU (Defects Per Unit)** = Total Defects ÷ Total Units
- **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** = (Defects ÷ (Units × Opportunities)) × 10⁶

### **Step 3: Converting to Yield**
- **First Time Yield (FTY)** = (1 − DPU) raised to the power of opportunities? No — the correct relation is:

$$Y = e^{-DPU}$$

This is the **Poisson Yield Model**, assuming defects occur randomly.

- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** = Product of FTYs of all process steps.

$$RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$$

### **Step 4: Converting Yield to Sigma Level**
Using the standard **Six Sigma Conversion Table** (Long-Term Z-score):

| Yield (%) | DPMO | Sigma Level |
|---|---|---|
| 99.99966 | 3.4 | 6.0 |
| 99.999 | 10 | 5.8 |
| 99.977 | 230 | 5.0 |
| 99.73 | 2,700 | 4.0 |
| 99.0 | 10,000 | 3.8 |
| 93.32 | 66,807 | 3.0 |
| 84.13 | 158,655 | 2.5 |

---

## 2.2 Process Capability Indices — The Heart of Measurement

### **A. Cp — Process Capability Index (Potential Capability)**

Cp measures *whether the process spread fits within the specification width*, assuming the process is perfectly centered.

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Cp = 1.00 → Process just fits; any shift causes defects.
- Cp = 1.33 → Industry minimum (4σ performance).
- Cp = 1.50 → Preferred capability.
- Cp = 2.00 → Six Sigma capability.

### **B. Cpk — Process Capability Index (Actual Capability)**

Cpk accounts for *process centering*. It uses the smaller of the two one-sided capabilities.

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(C_{pkU},\, C_{pkL}\right)$$

Where:

$$C_{pkU} = \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pkL} = \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:**
- Cpk = Cp → Process is perfectly centered.
- Cpk < Cp → Process is off-center.
- Cpk < 1.33 → Process not capable; corrective action needed.

### **C. Pp and Ppk — Long-Term Performance Indices**

Pp and Ppk use *overall process variation* (σ_total) instead of within-subgroup variation (σ_within).

$$P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}, \qquad P_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma_{overall}},\, \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma_{overall}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state whether the question asks for *short-term* (Cp, Cpk) or *long-term* (Pp, Ppk) capability. This distinction is frequently tested.

---

## 2.3 Statistical Significance Testing

### **The Logic of Hypothesis Testing**

1. **Define H₀ (Null Hypothesis):** The process mean has not changed (μ = μ₀).
2. **Define H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis):** The process mean has changed (μ ≠ μ₀).
3. **Choose α (significance level):** Typically 0.05.
4. **Compute test statistic** (Z or t).
5. **Compute p-value.**
6. **Decision Rule:** If p-value < α → Reject H₀ → Result is statistically significant.

### **Z-Test for Process Mean**

$$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$$

### **t-Test for Small Samples (n < 30)**

$$t = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{s/\sqrt{n}}, \quad \text{degrees of freedom} = n - 1$$

### **Critical Decision Boundary**

| α | Two-tailed Z-critical | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| 0.10 | ±1.645 | 90% confidence |
| 0.05 | ±1.96 | 95% confidence |
| 0.01 | ±2.576 | 99% confidence |

---

## 2.4 Measurement Systems Analysis (MSA)

A measurement system has four sources of variation:

1. **Bias (Accuracy)** — Difference between measured and true value.
2. **Repeatability** — Variation from the *same operator* using the *same gauge* on the *same part* multiple times.
3. **Reproducibility** — Variation from *different operators* using the *same gauge*.
4. **Stability** — Variation of bias over time.

### **Gage R&R Metric (%R&R)**

$$\%R\&R = \frac{\sigma_{Gauge}^2}{\sigma_{Total}^2} \times 100\%$$

**Acceptance Criteria:**

| %R&R | Status | Action |
|---|---|---|
| ≤ 10% | Excellent | Use the gauge |
| 10% – 30% | Marginal | May be acceptable depending on cost/importance |
| > 30% | Unacceptable | Improve gauge or training |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU ESE paper for this topic.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process potential | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process performance | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{overall}}$ | Long-term potential | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $DPMO = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Defect density | defects/10⁶ opps |
| 5 | $Y = e^{-DPU}$ | Poisson yield | fraction |
| 6 | $RTY = \prod Y_i$ | Rolled yield | fraction |
| 7 | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$ | Significance test | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\%R\&R = \dfrac{\sigma_{Gauge}^2}{\sigma_{Total}^2} \times 100\%$ | Measurement reliability | % |
| 9 | $\sigma_{Total}^2 = \sigma_{Process}^2 + \sigma_{Gauge}^2$ | Variance decomposition | (units)² |
| 10 | $\text{Defect Rate (DPM)} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million | defects/10⁶ units |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | Cpk monitors machining precision; < 1.33 triggers re-tooling. |
| **Software Quality** | DPMO tracks post-release defects per KLOC. |
| **Healthcare** | Six Sigma reduces surgical errors and patient wait times. |
| **Service Industry** | First Call Resolution (FCR) measured via statistical significance to validate training programs. |
| **Aerospace** | MSA-grade gauges mandatory for safety-critical measurements. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Defects to Sigma Level (Six Sigma Conversion Logic)

The Six Sigma philosophy equates quality to a **Z-score**, representing the number of standard deviations between the process mean and the nearest specification limit.

### **Step-by-Step Derivation**

**Step 1 — Define Defect Probability under Normal Distribution:**

Let process output $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Probability of a defect occurring beyond specification limits is the tail area of the normal curve.

**Step 2 — Standardize using Z-transformation:**

$$Z = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$$

For a centered process at Six Sigma level, the nearest specification limit is $6\sigma$ away from the mean.

**Step 3 — Compute the Tail Area (Defect Probability):**

The probability of exceeding the specification in one tail:

$$P(\text{defect}) = P(Z > Z_{score}) = \int_{Z}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2/2}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2 for Two-Tailed Process (Most Real-World Cases):**

$$P(\text{total defects}) = 2 \times P(Z > Z_{score})$$

**Step 5 — Convert to DPMO:**

$$DPMO = P(\text{total defects}) \times 10^6$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example for 3σ Process:**

For $Z = 3$, the upper tail area = 0.00135 (from standard normal table).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{total defects}) &= 2 \times 0.00135 = 0.00270 \\
DPMO &= 0.00270 \times 10^6 = 2{,}700 \\
\text{Yield} &= (1 - 0.00270) \times 100 = 99.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the well-known **3σ quality level: 99.73% yield, 2,700 DPMO**.

**Step 7 — Six Sigma Benchmark (with 1.5σ shift allowance):**

Six Sigma allows for a **1.5σ drift** over time (Motorola's empirical convention). Effective Z = 6 − 1.5 = 4.5 long-term, yielding **3.4 DPMO**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(Z > 4.5) &= 3.4 \times 10^{-6} \\
DPMO &= 3.4 \text{ per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cpk Calculation (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem:** A shaft diameter specification is **24.95 mm to 25.05 mm**. From a sample of 50 parts, the mean diameter is **25.02 mm** and the standard deviation is **0.012 mm**. Compute Cp and Cpk.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$
USL = 25.05 \text{ mm}, \quad LSL = 24.95 \text{ mm}, \quad \bar{X} = 25.02 \text{ mm}, \quad \sigma = 0.012 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cp (Potential Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
&= \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.012} \\
&= \frac{0.10}{0.072} \\
&= 1.389
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $C_p \approx 1.39 > 1.33$ → Process has **adequate potential capability**.

**Step 3 — Compute CpkU (Upper-side Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pkU} &= \frac{USL - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} \\
&= \frac{25.05 - 25.02}{3 \times 0.012} \\
&= \frac{0.03}{0.036} \\
&= 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute CpkL (Lower-side Capability):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pkL} &= \frac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3\sigma} \\
&= \frac{25.02 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.012} \\
&= \frac{0.07}{0.036} \\
&= 1.944
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Cpk (Take Minimum):**

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pkU}, C_{pkL}) = \min(0.833, 1.944) = 0.833
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret Results:**

> [!WARNING]
> $C_p = 1.389$ looks acceptable, BUT $C_{pk} = 0.833$ reveals the process is **off-center toward the USL**. This means the process is **NOT capable** despite high Cp. **The student must always compute BOTH Cp and Cpk** — KTU examiners specifically check this.

**Step 7 — Recommendation:**

Re-center the machine target from 25.02 mm to **25.00 mm** (midpoint of tolerance). After re-centering, Cpk approaches Cp, and the process becomes capable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: DPMO & Sigma Level Conversion

**Problem:** In a batch of **500 circuit boards**, each board has **10 soldering opportunities**. **15 defects** are observed. Calculate DPU, DPMO, Yield, and Sigma Level.

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute DPU:**

$$
DPU = \frac{\text{Total Defects}}{\text{Total Units}} = \frac{15}{500} = 0.03
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DPMO &= \frac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6 \\
&= \frac{15}{500 \times 10} \times 10^6 \\
&= \frac{15}{5000} \times 10^6 \\
&= 3000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Yield (using Poisson):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= e^{-DPU} = e^{-0.03} \\
&\approx 0.9704 = 97.04\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Sigma Level:**

From the conversion table: **3,000 DPMO ≈ 4.1 Sigma Level**.

(Using the precise empirical conversion: $Z \approx 4.0$ to $4.2$.)

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

> The process operates between **3σ (99.73% yield)** and **4σ (99.993% yield)**. It is **NOT Six Sigma quality** — improvement is needed.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cp vs Cpk vs Pp vs Ppk

| Attribute | Cp | Cpk | Pp | Ppk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Variation Used** | Within-subgroup (σ_within) | Within-subgroup | Overall (σ_overall) | Overall |
| **Accounts for Centering?** | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term | Short-term | Long-term | Long-term |
| **When Used** | Pilot run, qualification | Production monitoring | Long-term tracking | Long-term tracking |
| **KTU Relevance** | High | Very High | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Typical Benchmark** | ≥ 1.33 | ≥ 1.33 | ≥ 1.50 | ≥ 1.50 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Many students incorrectly use Cp and Cpk interchangeably or report only Cp. Always remember: **Cp ignores centering; Cpk accounts for it.** A process with Cp = 2.0 but Cpk = 0.5 is **not capable** because it is severely off-center.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Framework

```
Decision: Is the process capable?
│
├── Compute Cp
│   ├── If Cp < 1.0 → Process spread exceeds spec → REDESIGN
│   └── If Cp ≥ 1.0 → Continue
│
├── Compute Cpk
│   ├── If Cpk ≈ Cp → Centered → ACCEPT
│   ├── If Cpk < Cp → Off-center → RE-CENTER
│   └── If Cpk < 1.0 → Not capable → CORRECTIVE ACTION
│
└── Long-term check: Compute Ppk
    ├── If Ppk < Cpk → Process is drifting → STABILITY CHECK
    └── If Ppk ≈ Cpk → Stable → ACCEPT
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Six Sigma Measurement Hierarchy (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how raw defect data cascades through the Six Sigma measurement system, transforming from operational observations into executive-level quality metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Collection] --> B[Count Defects and Units]
    B --> C[Compute DPU]
    C --> D[Compute DPMO]
    D --> E[Compute Yield]
    E --> F[Convert to Sigma Level Z]
    F --> G[Compare with Six Sigma Benchmark]
    G --> H{Process Meets 6 Sigma Target}
    H -- Yes --> I[Maintain and Control Phase]
    H -- No --> J[DMAIC Improve Phase]
    J --> K[Recompute Metrics]
    K --> F
    L[Process Capability Study] --> M[Compute Cp]
    L --> N[Compute Cpk]
    M --> O[Spread Assessment]
    N --> P[Centering Assessment]
    P --> Q{Capable?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Monitor Production]
    Q -- No --> S[Re-center or Redesign]
    T[Measurement System] --> U[Gage R and R Study]
    U --> V{Percent R and R less than 30}
    V -- Yes --> W[Gauge Acceptable]
    V -- No --> X[Gauge Improvement Required]
```

---

## 4.2 Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H0[Step 1: Define H0] --> H1[Step 2: Define H1]
    H1 --> AL[Step 3: Choose alpha]
    AL --> TS[Step 4: Compute Test Statistic]
    TS --> PV[Step 5: Compute P Value]
    PV --> DC{Step 6: P Value versus alpha}
    DC -- P less than alpha --> RJ[Reject H0 - Significant]
    DC -- P greater than alpha --> FR[Fail to Reject H0 - Not Significant]
    RJ --> AC[Accept Improvement as Real]
```
