- **Manufacturing:** Reducing defective parts per million in automotive, electronics, and pharmaceutical plants.
- **Healthcare:** Reducing patient wait times, medication errors, hospital-acquired infections.
- **Banking/Finance:** Reducing loan processing errors, ATM downtime, transaction failures.
- **IT/Software:** Reducing bug counts, shortening release cycles, lowering customer churn.
- **Telecom:** Reducing call drops, network outages, billing complaints.
- **Hospitality:** Reducing check-in time, food order errors, housekeeping complaints.

The **beauty** of DMAIC lies in its **industry-agnostic, data-driven discipline** — it is the universal grammar of process improvement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Walkthrough of DMAIC Case Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Note:** For your KTU ESE, you are NOT expected to memorize all cases below verbatim. Instead, study **2–3 cases in depth** and understand the **logical skeleton** so you can adapt any unseen case study to the DMAIC framework. The four canonical cases below (Manufacturing, Service, Healthcare, IT) cover ~95% of typical KTU questions.

---

## 3.1 CASE EXAMPLE 1 — Manufacturing: Reducing Defects in an Automotive Brake-Pad Production Line

### **Background**
A leading automotive component manufacturer observes a **3.2% defect rate** in its brake-pad production line (target: <0.1%). Each brake pad has **8 critical-to-quality (CTQ) features** (thickness, density, hardness, surface finish, etc.). Monthly output: **50,000 units**. Monthly defects observed: **1,600 units**.

### **D — DEFINE**

**Step 1: Capture VOC.** Customer feedback (OEMs) reveals three pain points:
1. Surface scratches on friction surface.
2. Inconsistent thickness.
3. Premature wear under high-temperature testing.

**Step 2: Build CTQ Tree.** Translating VOC into measurable CTQs yields:
- CTQ1: Surface roughness $\leq 1.6\ \mu m$ Ra.
- CTQ2: Thickness within $\pm 0.05$ mm of nominal.
- CTQ3: Hardness between $40{-}55$ HRC.

**Step 3: Project Charter.**
- **Problem Statement:** *Brake-pad line produces 3.2% defectives, causing ₹48 lakh/month warranty costs.*
- **Goal:** *Reduce defects from 3.2% to 0.05% (i.e., 4σ → 5σ) within 6 months.*
- **Scope:** Press-machining and surface-grinding stations only.

**Step 4: SIPOC.**
- **S**uppliers: Steel coil vendors, abrasive suppliers.
- **I**nputs: Raw coils, abrasive grit, coolant.
- **P**rocess: Cutting → Pressing → Sintering → Grinding → Inspection.
- **O**utputs: Brake pads meeting 8 CTQ specs.
- **C**ustomers: OEM automotive assembly plants.

### **M — MEASURE**

**Step 5: Compute Baseline DPMO.**

Given:
- $D = 1{,}600$ defects per month.
- $U = 50{,}000$ units per month.
- $O = 8$ opportunities per unit.

$$
T = U \times O = 50{,}000 \times 8 = 400{,}000\ \text{opportunities/month}
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{T} \times 10^6 = \frac{1{,}600}{400{,}000} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 0.004 \times 10^6 = 4{,}000\ \text{DPMO}
$$

$$
\text{Yield} = \frac{U - D}{U} = \frac{50{,}000 - 1{,}600}{50{,}000} = \frac{48{,}400}{50{,}000} = 0.968 = 96.8\%
$$

**Step 6: Convert DPMO to Process Sigma.**
From the standard Six Sigma conversion table, **DPMO = 4,000** corresponds to **≈ 4.25 σ** (short-term). Applying the **+1.5 shift** for long-term:
$$
\sigma_{\text{long-term}} \approx 4.25 - 0\ \text{(already long-term adjusted)} \approx 4.25\ \sigma
$$

> The process is currently operating at roughly **4.25σ**, well short of the **5σ** target.

**Step 7: Gage R&R Study.** A measurement system analysis confirms that **27%** of observed variation comes from the thickness gauge — exceeding the **30% rule-of-thumb acceptable limit** is borderline; recalibration and operator training are scheduled.

**Step 8: Pareto Chart.** Plotting defects by category reveals:
- Surface scratches: **62%**
- Thickness deviation: **24%**
- Hardness out-of-spec: **14%**

→ **Vital Few:** Surface scratches + Thickness (86% of all defects).

### **A — ANALYZE**

**Step 9: Fishbone Diagram.** The team brainstorms causes under the 6M framework:

| 6M Category | Suspected Causes |
|---|---|
| **Man** | Operator skill gap on grinder; inadequate training |
| **Machine** | Grinding wheel wear; coolant nozzle misalignment |
| **Material** | Inconsistent steel coil hardness from supplier |
| **Method** | No standardized SOP for grit-change interval |
| **Measurement** | Gauge drift; manual visual inspection of surface |
| **Mother Nature** | Workshop humidity affects coolant performance |

**Step 10: 5-Why Analysis (for surface scratches):**
1. *Why are there surface scratches?* → Improper grit change.
2. *Why no proper grit change?* → No SOP exists.
3. *Why no SOP?* → Previous supervisor left; documentation lost.
4. *Why not documented?* → No controlled-document system.
5. *Why no controlled system?* → Quality department under-resourced.

→ **Root Cause:** Absence of a **documented SOP and preventive maintenance schedule** for the grinding wheel.

**Step 11: Hypothesis Test.** A **two-sample t-test** confirms that brake pads produced *after* the morning shift change have significantly higher scratch rates ($p < 0.01$), pointing to **operator handover procedure** as a contributory root cause.

### **I — IMPROVE**

**Step 12: Solution Generation.**
- **Solution A:** Install **automated coolant nozzle alignment** (Poka-Yoke).
- **Solution B:** Implement **SOP for grit change every 200 units** + logbook.
- **Solution C:** Upgrade surface roughness to **laser profilometer** (replacing manual gauge).
- **Solution D:** Supplier quality agreement requiring **hardness certificate per batch**.

**Step 13: DOE (Design of Experiments).** A $2^{3}$ full factorial DOE is run with factors:
- Factor A: Grinding wheel speed (1500 vs 1800 RPM).
- Factor B: Feed rate (0.05 vs 0.10 mm/s).
- Factor C: Coolant flow (5 vs 8 L/min).

The optimal settings: **1800 RPM, 0.05 mm/s, 8 L/min** — reducing scratch rate from 1.98% to 0.04%.

**Step 14: Pilot.** 30-day pilot on Line 2:
- $D = 22$ defects out of $50{,}000$ units.

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{22}{400{,}000} \times 10^6 = 55\ \text{DPMO} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \approx 5.9\ \sigma
$$

> **Massive improvement:** From **4.25σ to 5.9σ**.

### **C — CONTROL**

**Step 15: Standardization.**
- SOP issued for grit-change intervals and operator handover checklist.
- **Control Plan** updated with reaction rules.
- **X-bar and R control charts** deployed on the line (with USL/LSL on thickness, Ra on roughness).
- **Mistake-proofing:** Automatic machine shutdown if coolant pressure drops below threshold.
- Monthly **management review** of SPC charts; weekly **Gage R&R** re-checks.

**Step 16: COPQ Savings.**
- Old COPQ: ₹48 lakh/month warranty + ₹6 lakh scrap = **₹54 lakh/month**.
- New COPQ: ₹2 lakh/month.
- **Annual savings: ≈ ₹6.24 crore.**

---

## 3.2 CASE EXAMPLE 2 — Service Industry: Reducing Customer Complaint Resolution Time at a Telecom

### **Background**
A telecom major receives **12,000 complaints/month** with average resolution time of **96 hours** (target: ≤ 24 hours).

### **D — DEFINE**
- VOC from CSAT surveys: *“I have to call 4 times before my issue is fixed.”*
- CTQ: **First-Contact Resolution (FCR)** ≥ 80%.
- Project Charter: Reduce resolution time from 96 h to 24 h in 4 months.

### **M — MEASURE**
- Baseline data: 12,000 complaints/month; mean resolution = 96 h; σ = 18 h.
- Pareto of complaint types: **Billing (52%), Network (28%), Service (12%), Others (8%)** → Billing is the vital few.
- DPMO equivalent (using resolution > 24 h as the defect):
$$
D = 9{,}600 \text{ complaints over 24 h},\quad U = 12{,}000,\quad O = 1
$$
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{9{,}600}{12{,}000 \times 1} \times 10^6 = 800{,}000\ \text{DPMO} \approx 1.6\ \sigma
$$

### **A — ANALYZE**
- **Fishbone** → Knowledge gap (agents lack billing-system training), lack of integrated CRM view, escalation delays.
- **5-Why:** *Why do billing complaints take > 24 h? → Because they are escalated twice. → Why escalated? → Because first agent lacks access to billing-correction tool. → Why no access? → License restrictions by IT security. → Root Cause: Siloed IT systems & permission policy.*

### **I — IMPROVE**
- Implemented **single-pane CRM** showing billing + network data.
- Provided **role-based access** to billing-correction module.
- Introduced **knowledge-base AI assistant** for agents.
- Pilot over 30 days: average resolution dropped to **18 hours** with FCR = 84%.

### **C — CONTROL**
- **Standardized call-handling SOP** in CRM.
- Real-time **SPC dashboard** on resolution time (X-chart with UCL = 24 h).
- Weekly **CSAT review** with feedback loop to training.

> **Result:** Resolution time 96 h → 18 h; CSAT rose from 6.2 to 8.7/10.

---

## 3.3 CASE EXAMPLE 3 — Healthcare: Reducing Patient Wait Time in an Emergency Department

### **Background**
A 300-bed hospital's Emergency Department (ED) averages **4.2-hour wait** before doctor consultation. Industry benchmark: **≤ 1 hour** (Level-1 triage within 60 min).

### **D — DEFINE**
- VOC: Patients complain of long waits, lack of communication.
- CTQ: Door-to-Doctor Time ≤ 60 min; Left-Without-Being-Seen (LWBS) rate ≤ 2%.
- Project Charter: Reduce door-to-doctor time from 4.2 h to ≤ 1 h within 5 months.

### **M — MEASURE**
- Data collected over 30 days: $n = 4{,}200$ ED visits.
- Mean wait = 252 min; σ = 78 min; LWBS rate = 9%.
- DPMO calculation (using "wait > 60 min" as defect):
$$
D = 3{,}800,\quad U = 4{,}200,\quad O = 1
$$
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{3{,}800}{4{,}200} \times 10^6 \approx 904{,}762\ \text{DPMO} \approx 1.4\ \sigma
$$

### **A — ANALYZE**
- **Fishbone** under 6M:
  - **Man:** Shortage of triage nurses (3 vs required 6).
  - **Method:** Sequential triage → registration → vitals → doctor (bottleneck).
  - **Machine:** Slow ECG machines (avg 12 min/patient).
  - **Material:** Missing lab forms delay treatment.
- **5-Why:** *Why wait so long? → No fast-track for low-acuity patients. → Why no fast-track? → Unclear triage protocol. → Why unclear? → No clinical decision support. → Root Cause: Lack of ESI (Emergency Severity Index) protocol.*

### **I — IMPROVE**
- Implemented **5-level ESI triage protocol**.
- Deployed **parallel processing** (vitals taken while registration).
- Added a **fast-track lane** for ESI 4 & 5 patients.
- Hired **3 additional triage nurses**.
- Pilot over 60 days: average door-to-doctor = **52 minutes**, LWBS = 1.8%.

$$
\text{New DPMO} = \frac{420}{4{,}200} \times 10^6 = 100{,}000\ \text{DPMO} \approx 3.1\ \sigma
$$

### **C — CONTROL**
- **Standardized triage SOP** posted at every station.
- **Daily huddle** review of ED KPIs.
- **SPC p-chart** tracking LWBS rate weekly.
- **Electronic medical record alerts** if ESI not assigned within 5 min of arrival.

> **Outcome:** Door-to-doctor 4.2 h → 52 min; LWBS 9% → 1.8%; patient satisfaction 5.8 → 8.9/10.

---

## 3.4 CASE EXAMPLE 4 — IT/Software: Reducing Software Defect Leakage in a Banking Application

### **Background**
A fintech firm releases monthly software updates with **185 defects** leaking into production (target: ≤ 10 defects/release).

### **D — DEFINE**
- VOC: Banking customers complain of crashes during UPI transactions.
- CTQ: Production defects ≤ 10/release; crash rate ≤ 0.001%.
- Project Charter: Reduce defect leakage from 185 to ≤ 10 within 3 releases.

### **M — MEASURE**
- Baseline: 185 defects/release, 12,000 test cases/run.
- DPMO using test-case as opportunity:
$$
D = 185,\quad U = 12{,}000,\quad O = 1
$$
$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{185}{12{,}000} \times 10^6 \approx 15{,}417\ \text{DPMO} \approx 3.7\ \sigma
$$

### **A — ANALYZE**
- **Fishbone:** 
  - Man: QA team lacks automation skills.
  - Method: Manual regression testing only.
  - Machine: Test environment ≠ production configuration.
  - Measurement: No defect-severity classification.
- **5-Why:** *Why do defects leak? → Not caught in QA. → Why not caught? → No automated regression suite. → Why none? → Never prioritized. → Root Cause: Absence of test automation + environment parity.*

### **I — IMPROVE**
- Implemented **Selenium-based automated regression suite** (covering 80% of test cases).
- Provisioned a **production-mirror test environment**.
- Introduced **code-review checklist** + static analysis (SonarQube).
- Pilot over 2 months: defects leaked dropped from 185 → **9**.
$$
\text{New DPMO} = \frac{9}{12{,}000} \times 10^6 = 750\ \text{DPMO} \approx 5.5\ \sigma
$$

### **C — CONTROL**
- **CI/CD pipeline** with automated regression gating.
- **Defect-density SPC chart** monitored per release.
- **On-call rotation** + post-release review meeting.
- **Poka-Yoke:** Deploy-blocker if critical defect count > 0.

> **Outcome:** Production defects 185 → 9; release cycle shortened by 30%; CSAT improved 22%.

---

## 3.5 Summary Comparison Table — All Four Case Examples

| Dimension | Case 1: Brake Pads | Case 2: Telecom | Case 3: Hospital ED | Case 4: Banking App |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Industry** | Manufacturing | Service | Healthcare | IT/Software |
| **Baseline DPMO** | 4,000 | 800,000 | 904,762 | 15,417 |
| **Baseline σ** | ~4.25 | ~1.6 | ~1.4 | ~3.7 |
| **Improved σ** | ~5.9 | ~3.6 | ~3.1 | ~5.5 |
| **Key Tool** | DOE, Gage R&R | Pareto, CRM | ESI Triage | Test Automation |
| **Big Savings** | ₹6.24 cr/yr | CSAT ↑ 40% | LWBS ↓ 80% | Release cycle ↓ 30% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal DMAIC Flow Diagram (Master Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram is the **single most important visual** for your KTU exam. Reproducing this in your answer script instantly earns method marks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Problem Identified]) --> phaseD
    phaseD["PHASE D : DEFINE"] --> phaseM
    phaseM["PHASE M : MEASURE"] --> phaseA
    phaseA["PHASE A : ANALYZE"] --> phaseI
    phaseI["PHASE I : IMPROVE"] --> phaseC
    phaseC["PHASE C : CONTROL"] --> phaseSustain
    phaseSustain([Sustained Improvement])
    phaseD -.-> toolD["Project Charter, SIPOC, VOC, CTQ Tree"]
    phaseM -.-> toolM["Gage R&R, Pareto, DPMO Baseline, Sigma Table"]
    phaseA -.-> toolA["Fishbone, 5-Why, Hypothesis Testing, Regression"]
    phaseI -.-> toolI["DOE, Pilot, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen, FMEA"]
    phaseC -.-> toolC["SPC Charts, SOP, Control Plan, Reaction Plan"]
    phaseC -.->|New problem found| phaseD
```

## 4.2 Manufacturing Case (Brake Pads) — Detailed DMAIC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    customerM[OEM Customers] --> vocM[VOC Capture]
    vocM --> ctqM[CTQ Tree]
    ctqM --> charterM[Project Charter]
    charterM --> sipocM[SIPOC Map]
    sipocM --> measureM
    measureM[/"MEASURE : DPMO 4000 - Sigma 4.25"/]
    measureM --> gageM[Gage R&R Study]
    gageM --> paretoM[Pareto : Surface Scratches 62 percent]
    paretoM --> analyzeM
    analyzeM[/"ANALYZE : Fishbone and 5 Why"/]
    analyzeM --> rcM1[Root Cause 1: No SOP for Grit Change]
    analyzeM --> rcM2[Root Cause 2: Operator Handover Gap]
    rcM1 --> improveM
    rcM2 --> improveM
    improveM[/"IMPROVE : DOE and Poka Yoke"/]
    improveM --> doeM[DOE 2 to the power 3]
    doeM --> pilotM[Pilot 30 days : DPMO 55]
    pilotM --> controlM
    controlM[/"CONTROL : SPC and SOP"/]
    controlM --> spcM[X-bar and R Chart]
    controlM --> sopM[Standard Operating Procedure]
    controlM --> savingsM[Annual Savings 6.24 Crore INR]
```

## 4.3 Healthcare Case (ED Wait Time) — Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    patientA[Patient Arrives at ED] --> regA[Registration]
    regA --> vitalsA[Vitals Check]
    vitalsA --> triageA[Triage Assessment]
    triageA --> decisionA{ESI Severity 4 or 5?}
    decisionA -->|Yes| fastA[Fast Track Lane]
    decisionA -->|No| standardA[Standard ED Path]
    fastA --> doctorA[Doctor Consult]
    standardA --> doctorA
    doctorA --> dischargeA[Discharge or Admit]
    subgraph BEFORE_B[/"BEFORE : Sequential, No ESI"/]
        regA
        vitalsA
        triageA
    end
    subgraph AFTER_C[/"AFTER : Parallel, ESI Protocol"/]
        fastA
        standardA
    end
    doctorA --> spcA[Control: p-chart on LWBS]
```

## 4.4 DPMO-to-Sigma Conversion Logic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputU[Units U] --> multOp[Multiply by Opportunities O]
    multOp --> totalOp[Total Opportunities T]
    inputD[Defects D] --> divOp[Divide by T]
    totalOp --> divOp
    divOp --> dpmoVal[DPMO Value]
    dpmoVal --> normal[Standard Normal CDF]
    normal --> zVal[Z Score]
    zVal --> shift[Add 1.5 sigma shift]
    shift --> sigmaVal[Final Process Sigma Level]
    dpmoVal -.-> tableVal[Or use DPMO Sigma Lookup Table]
    tableVal --> sigmaVal
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the DPMO concept with one industrial example. State the formula.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)** is the standard Six Sigma metric that expresses the number of defects normalized to one million opportunities, enabling fair comparison across processes with different complexities.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$$
>
> where $D$ = number of defects, $U$ = units produced, $O$ = opportunities per unit.
>
> **Example:** A printed circuit board (PCB) line produces **20,000 boards/month** with **15 defects/month** and **5 CTQ features per board**.
> $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{15}{20{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^6 = \frac{15}{100{,}000} \times 10^6 = 150\ \text{DPMO} \approx 5.7\ \sigma$$
> This indicates a high-performing process nearing **6σ** quality. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any four tools used in the "Analyze" phase of DMAIC with a one-line purpose of each.**

> **Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**
> 1. **Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram** — categorizes potential causes of a problem under 6M (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature).
> 2. **5-Why Analysis** — iteratively asks "why" five times to drill from a symptom down to the systemic root cause.
> 3. **Pareto Chart** — identifies the "vital few" causes contributing to ~80% of the problems.
> 4. **Hypothesis Testing (t-test, chi-square)** — statistically validates whether a suspected cause is significantly contributing to defects.
> 5. *(Optional — for extra credit)* **Regression Analysis** — quantifies the relationship between a CTQ and its suspected drivers. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

A hospital's pathology laboratory processes **8,000 blood samples/month**. Each sample is tested for **5 parameters**. Currently, **600 samples/month** fail quality checks. The hospital wants to reach **5σ** performance in 4 months.

#### **(a) Calculate the baseline DPMO and process sigma level. Comment on the gap from the goal. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:**
> - $U = 8{,}000$ samples/month
> - $D = 600$ defects/month (failed QC samples)
> - $O = 5$ parameters per sample
>
> **Step 1: Total Opportunities**
> $$T = U \times O = 8{,}000 \times 5 = 40{,}000$$
>
> **Step 2: Compute DPMO**
> $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{T} \times 10^6 = \frac{600}{40{,}000} \times 10^6 = 0.015 \times 10^6 = 15{,}000\ \text{DPMO}$$
>
> **Step 3: Convert DPMO to Process Sigma**
> From standard Six Sigma conversion table: **DPMO = 15,000 ≈ 3.65σ**.
>
> **Step 4: Compute Yield**
> $$\text{Yield} = \frac{U - D}{U} = \frac{8{,}000 - 600}{8{,}000} = \frac{7{,}400}{8{,}000} = 0.925 = 92.5\%$$
>
> **Step 5: Gap Analysis**
> - Current σ = 3.65
> - Target σ = 5.0
> - **Gap = 1.35σ** (≈ a 65× reduction in DPMO needed)
> - Equivalent yield must rise from 92.5% to ~99.977%.
>
> **Conclusion:** The lab operates at **3.65σ**, far below world-class. An improvement of **1.35σ** is achievable but requires structured DMAIC. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) Apply the Measure, Analyze, and Improve phases to recommend actions for the laboratory. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **MEASURE Phase:**
> - Verify the **analyzer calibration** via a Gage R&R study on the hematology analyzer and biochemistry analyzer.
> - Stratify defects by parameter (Pareto): typical findings might be — Glucose 40%, Hemoglobin 28%, Cholesterol 18%, others 14%. **Glucose + Hb = 68% → vital few.**
> - Re-baseline **DPMO per parameter**.
>
> **ANALYZE Phase:**
> - **Fishbone (6M):**
>   - *Man:* Lab technician training gap on new analyzer.
>   - *Machine:* Reagent probe contamination, centrifuge imbalance.
>   - *Material:* Expired reagent kits, hemolyzed samples from poor collection.
>   - *Method:* No SOP for sample-rejection criteria.
>   - *Measurement:* Manual pipetting errors.
>   - *Environment:* Temperature fluctuation in reagent storage.
> - **5-Why (for glucose errors):** Why glucose fails? → Wrong reading. Why? → Calibration drift. Why? → No weekly QC. Why? → No documented schedule. **Root Cause = absence of daily QC + calibration SOP.**
>
> **IMPROVE Phase:**
> - Implement **daily QC runs** with Westgard rules.
> - **Automate sample barcode tracking** to eliminate manual mislabeling.
> - **Poka-Yoke:** Analyzer auto-blocks reporting if QC fails.
> - **DOE** to optimize **reagent storage temperature** (2–8°C) and **incubation time**.
> - Pilot over 60 days: defects drop from 600 → 80.
> $$\text{New DPMO} = \frac{80}{40{,}000} \times 10^6 = 2{,}000\ \text{DPMO} \approx 4.4\ \sigma$$
>
> **Conclusion:** A 1.35σ gap is reduced through rigorous M-A-I actions, bringing the lab closer to 5σ. **(7 marks)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

A software company releases a mobile banking application. In the latest release, **45 critical bugs** were reported by users out of **30,000 downloads**. The company sets a target of **≤ 3 critical bugs per release** within 6 months.

#### **(a) Compute the baseline DPMO and Sigma level. State the gap to target. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **Given:**
> - $U = 30{,}000$ downloads
> - $D = 45$ critical bugs
> - $O = 1$ (one critical-defect opportunity per download)
>
> **Step 1: DPMO**
> $$\text{DPMO} = \frac{45}{30{,}000 \times 1} \times 10^6 = 1{,}500\ \text{DPMO}$$
>
> **Step 2: Sigma Level (from table)** ≈ **4.45σ**.
>
> **Step 3: Yield**
> $$\text{Yield} = \frac{30{,}000 - 45}{30{,}000} = 99.85\%$$
>
> **Step 4: Target Comparison**
> - Target DPMO = $\frac{3}{30{,}000} \times 10^6 = 100\ \text{DPMO} \approx 5.85\sigma$.
> - **Gap = 4.45σ → 5.85σ = 1.4σ improvement needed.**
>
> **Conclusion:** The app operates at world-class levels already, but improvements in critical-bug prevention are still required. **(7 marks)**

#### **(b) Outline a DMAIC roadmap for the software firm, with specific tools for each phase. (7 marks)**

> **Step-by-Step Model Solution:**
>
> **D — DEFINE:**
> - VOC via app-store reviews, support tickets.
> - CTQ: Crash-free sessions ≥ 99.95%; critical bug count ≤ 3/release.
> - Charter + SIPOC: Requirements → Coding → Code Review → QA → Release → Customer.
>
> **M — MEASURE:**
> - Track **Defect Removal Efficiency (DRE)** at each stage.
> - **Pareto** of bug categories (UI, payment, login, sync).
> - Establish **baseline DPMO per release**.
>
> **A — ANALYZE:**
> - **Fishbone** for top bug category (e.g., payment crashes).
> - **5-Why:** Why payment crashes? → Race condition in concurrent transactions. → Why race condition? → No thread-safe design. → **Root Cause = inadequate concurrency testing.**
> - **Root cause validated** via code-review audit + statistical sampling of crash logs.
>
> **I — IMPROVE:**
> - Introduce **static code analysis** (SonarQube) gates in CI/CD.
> - **Automated unit + integration tests** targeting concurrency.
> - **Beta release program** to 5% of users before full rollout.
> - Pilot: bugs drop from 45 → **4 per release**.
> $$\text{New DPMO} = \frac{4}{30{,}000} \times 10^6 \approx 133\ \text{DPMO} \approx 5.8\sigma$$
>
> **C — CONTROL:**
> - **SPC chart** on critical bug count per release (c-chart or u-chart).
> - **CI/CD gate:** Block release if static analysis > critical threshold.
> - **Post-release monitoring dashboard** with crash analytics.
> - **Reaction plan:** Roll-back trigger if crash rate > 0.05%.
>
> **Outcome:** Critical bugs 45 → 4, achieving the company's ≤ 3 target within 2 release cycles. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in DMAIC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the +1.5σ shift** when converting DPMO to Sigma. The shift is mandatory in Six Sigma; omitting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Cp and Cpk.** $C_p$ assumes the process is centered; $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-centering. Examiners specifically look for the correct formula:
> $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$
> 3. **Confusing FPY with RTY.** First Pass Yield is per step; Rolled Throughput Yield is **multiplicative** across steps.
> 4. **Skipping VOC and CTQ in the Define phase.** Students often jump straight to tools. Always begin with **VOC → CTQ → Charter → SIPOC**.
> 5. **Not mentioning Control phase actions.** Many answers stop at "Improve." Always close with **SPC + SOP + Control Plan + Reaction Plan**.
> 6. **Using "DPMO" without units.** Always write "**15,000 DPMO**" — not just "15,000."
> 7. **Wrong opportunity count.** If a part has 8 inspection points, $O = 8$, not 1.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Read it the morning of your exam.**

### 🔑 Universal DMAIC Truths
- [x] **DMAIC** = **D**efine, **M**easure, **A**nalyze, **I**mprove, **C**ontrol.
- [x] Six Sigma target = **3.4 DPMO** (≈ 99.9997% yield).
- [x] Always apply the **+1.5σ shift** when converting DPMO to sigma.
- [x] DMAIC is **data-driven**, **closed-loop**, and **industry-agnostic**.

### 🔑 Phase-Specific Anchor Tools
- [x] **Define** → VOC, CTQ Tree, Project Charter, SIPOC.
- [x] **Measure** → DPMO, Baseline Sigma, Gage R&R, Pareto.
- [x] **Analyze** → Fishbone (6M), 5-Why, Hypothesis Testing, Regression.
- [x] **Improve** → DOE, Pilot, Poka-Yoke, Kaizen, FMEA.
- [x] **Control** → SPC (X-bar, R, p, c, u charts), SOP, Control Plan.

### 🔑 Formula Anchors (Memorize Cold)
- [x] $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$
- [x] $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$
- [x] $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$
- [x] $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$
- [x] $\sigma_{LT} = \sigma_{ST} + 1.5$

### 🔑 Sigma Anchors (Must Memorize)
- [x] 3σ ≈ 66,807 DPMO
- [x] 4σ ≈ 6,210 DPMO
- [x] 5σ ≈ 233 DPMO
- [x] 6σ ≈ 3.4 DPMO

### 🔑 Case-Study Strategy
- [x] Memorize **2–3 cases in depth** (Manufacturing + Service/Healthcare is the safest KTU combo).
- [x] For unseen cases, **decompose** the scenario using the **DMAIC skeleton** rather than trying to recall a "matching example."
- [x] Always show **numerical calculations** for at least one DPMO/Sigma problem to score full marks.
- [x] Close every answer with the **Control phase** — examiners reward completeness.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls to Avoid
- [x] Don't forget the **+1.5σ** shift.
- [x] Don't confuse **Yield** types (FPY vs RTY).
- [x] Don't skip the **Control** phase.
- [x] Don't fabricate units or opportunities — always derive $O$ from the CTQ list.

---

**🎯 End of Module 4 — Case Examples of DMAIC. Best of luck for your KTU ESE!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Six Sigma in Manufacturing and Service Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET631 - TOTAL QUALITY MANAGEMENT AND SIX SIGMA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Six Sigma in Manufacturing and Service Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Six Sigma in Manufacturing and Service Industries

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

**Six Sigma** is a data-driven methodology and statistical quality management philosophy aimed at reducing process variation and eliminating defects to a level of **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**, which corresponds to a process operating at **4.5 standard deviations from the mean** in the long term (with a 1.5 sigma shift assumption).

When applied across industries, Six Sigma's deployment is fundamentally shaped by whether the organization produces **tangible goods (Manufacturing)** or delivers **intangible services (Service)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 4):**
> Six Sigma in Manufacturing focuses on **tangible process outputs**, where defects are physically countable and opportunities are well-defined engineering tolerances. Six Sigma in Service Industries focuses on **intangible process outputs**, where defects are defined relative to customer expectations, service specifications, and transaction accuracy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital vs. Factory" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students:**
> Imagine you are running a **biscuit factory** (Manufacturing). A "defect" is straightforward — a broken biscuit, an under-baked unit, or a wrongly printed wrapper. You can **count**, **weigh**, and **inspect** every unit coming off the conveyor belt.
>
> Now imagine running a **hospital emergency ward** (Service). A "defect" is much harder to define — was the patient greeted within 2 minutes? Was the diagnosis accurate? Did the billing have zero errors? Here, the customer **experiences** the service, and the defect is a **gap between expectation and delivery**.
>
> **Six Sigma is the same statistical engine**, but it is mounted on two different chassis:
> - In **Manufacturing**, the engine is bolted to a **production line** (countable defects, fixed tolerances).
> - In **Services**, the engine is bolted to a **customer journey** (perceived defects, SLA-driven tolerances).

---

### 1.3 Six Sigma — Universal Building Blocks

Regardless of industry, every Six Sigma deployment is built on five universal pillars:

> [!NOTE]
> **The 5 Pillars (DMAIC):**
> 1. **D — Define**: Identify the problem, customer (internal/external), and project scope.
> 2. **M — Measure**: Quantify the current state using DPMO, yield, and process capability.
> 3. **A — Analyze**: Identify root causes of variation using statistical and graphical tools.
> 4. **I — Improve**: Implement and validate solutions that reduce defects.
> 5. **C — Control**: Sustain the gains using SOPs, control charts, and mistake-proofing.

### 1.4 Defining "Defect" and "Opportunity" — Industry Comparison

| Industry Type | What is a "Defect"? | What is an "Opportunity"? | Tangibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manufacturing** | A non-conforming part, a wrong dimension, a missed weld | Each inspection point on the part | Tangible, countable |
| **Service** | A wrong bill, a delayed response, an inaccurate claim | Each customer touchpoint or transaction step | Intangible, perceptual |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Across Both Industries:**
> The master metric is **DPMO (Defects Per Million Opportunities)**, with the target value being **≤ 3.4 DPMO** for a true Six Sigma process.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Six Sigma Differs Between Manufacturing and Services

The underlying statistical principles are identical, but the **operational implementation** diverges sharply because of three structural differences:

### A. Tangibility of Output
- **Manufacturing**: Output is a physical artifact that can be measured against engineering drawings and tolerances.
- **Service**: Output is an experience whose quality is judged by customer perception and SLA conformance.

### B. Customer Interaction
- **Manufacturing**: Customer rarely sees the process; defects are caught at inspection or warranty stage.
- **Service**: Customer **is** part of the process; defects are felt in real time and are non-recoverable once delivered.

### C. Process Variability
- **Manufacturing**: Variability comes from machines, materials, methods, and the environment (4M).
- **Service**: Variability comes from the **5th M — the human customer**, plus employee skill, mood, and demand fluctuation.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for Module 4 numerical problems.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$ | $D$ = Defects, $U$ = Units, $O$ = Opportunities per unit | defects / million |
| 2 | **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{N_{g}}{N_{t}} \times 100$ | $N_{g}$ = Good units, $N_{t}$ = Total units | percent |
| 3 | **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_{i}$ | Product of FPY of all sub-processes | fraction (0 to 1) |
| 4 | **Defects per Unit (DPU)** | $\text{DPU} = \dfrac{D}{U}$ | Average defects per single unit | defects / unit |
| 5 | **Process Yield (Y)** | $Y = 1 - \text{DPMO} / 10^{6}$ | Fraction of defect-free outputs | fraction |
| 6 | **Sigma Level (Long-Term, Z-shift)** | $Z_{LT} = \Phi^{-1}(Y) + 1.5$ | Standard normal inverse + 1.5σ shift | sigma |
| 7 | **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** | $\text{COPQ} = C_{\text{internal}} + C_{\text{external}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{prevention}}$ | Sum of all quality-related costs | currency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember for KTU Exams:**
> - A true **Six Sigma process** has $\text{DPMO} = 3.4$ and **Yield = 99.99966%**.
> - **FPY and RTY must lie in [0, 1]** as fractions (or 0%–100% as percentages).
> - The **1.5σ shift** is a Motorola convention used to convert short-term capability to long-term performance. Examiners love this number — **memorize 1.5**.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Application Logic — Manufacturing Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Logic in a Manufacturing Plant:**
> 1. **Define** the critical-to-quality (CTQ) characteristics of the part (e.g., shaft diameter = 25 ± 0.05 mm).
> 2. **Measure** the current process by sampling parts and counting defects.
> 3. **Analyze** using Pareto, Fishbone (Ishikawa), and Regression to find root causes.
> 4. **Improve** via Design of Experiments (DOE), poka-yoke, and process re-engineering.
> 5. **Control** using SPC charts ($\bar{X}$-R, p-chart, c-chart), FMEAs, and standard work.

**Real-World Engineering Utility (Manufacturing):**
- Used by **General Electric, Ford, Toyota, Boeing** to reduce warranty costs.
- Typical applications: reducing weld defects, lowering PCB rejection rates, improving machining tolerances.

---

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Application Logic — Service Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Logic in a Service Firm:**
> 1. **Define** the customer journey map and identify CTQ service attributes (e.g., call answered in ≤ 20 seconds).
> 2. **Measure** using Voice of Customer (VoC) surveys, transaction audits, and CTQ dashboards.
> 3. **Analyze** using process mining, gap analysis, and SERVQUAL gap models.
> 4. **Improve** via service blueprint redesign, automation (RPA), and agent training.
> 5. **Control** using Service Level Agreements (SLAs), balanced scorecards, and customer feedback loops.

**Real-World Engineering Utility (Services):**
- Used by **American Express, Citibank, Amazon, Indian Railways, Apollo Hospitals** to reduce transaction errors and improve turnaround.
- Typical applications: reducing claim settlement time, lowering call abandonment, improving first-call resolution.

---

## 2.5 Cross-Industry Comparison — Engineering the Right Metrics

| Dimension | Manufacturing | Services |
|---|---|---|
| Output Type | Tangible product | Intangible experience |
| Customer Touchpoints | Few (purchase, after-sales) | Many (every interaction) |
| Defect Detection | Pre-dispatch inspection | Post-delivery (often too late) |
| Primary Tool | SPC, DOE, FMEA | VoC, SERVQUAL, CTQ Trees |
| Sample Size | Large, statistical | Often small, judgmental |
| Cycle Time | Hours to weeks | Seconds to minutes |
| Correctability | Re-work possible | Often non-recoverable |
| Lead Time | Inventory-buffered | Real-time, no buffer |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Manufacturing (PCB Assembly Line)

### Problem Statement
A printed circuit board (PCB) assembly line produces **20,000 units per week**. Each PCB has **8 inspection opportunities** (solder joints, component placement, polarity, etc.). In a sample audit, **320 defects** were observed across the week's production.

**Find:**
1. DPU (Defects per Unit)
2. DPMO
3. Process Yield
4. Long-Term Sigma Level

### Step 1 — Compute DPU (Defects per Unit)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPU} &= \frac{D}{U} \\
\text{DPU} &= \frac{320}{20{,}000} \\
\text{DPU} &= 0.016 \;\;\text{defects per unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** DPU tells us that on average, each PCB carries 0.016 defects. This is the entry-level metric before scaling.

---

### Step 2 — Compute DPMO (Defects per Million Opportunities)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{320 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{20{,}000 \times 8} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{320{,}000{,}000}{160{,}000} \\
\text{DPMO} &= 2{,}000 \;\;\text{defects per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** We scale the defects to a per-million base and divide by the total opportunity count (units × opportunities per unit).

---

### Step 3 — Compute Process Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} \\
Y &= 1 - \frac{2{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
Y &= 1 - 0.002 \\
Y &= 0.998 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 99.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** A yield of 99.8% means 99.8% of all opportunities are defect-free. This translates to **2 defects per 1,000 opportunities**.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Long-Term Sigma Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{ST} &= \Phi^{-1}(Y) \\
Z_{ST} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.998) \\
Z_{ST} &\approx 2.88 \;\;\text{sigma (short-term)} \\
Z_{LT} &= Z_{ST} + 1.5 \\
Z_{LT} &\approx 2.88 + 1.5 \\
Z_{LT} &\approx 4.38 \;\;\text{sigma (long-term)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Using the standard normal table, a cumulative probability of 0.998 corresponds to roughly 2.88σ. After applying the Motorola 1.5σ shift for long-term drift, the process is at **approximately 4.38σ**, which is below the Six Sigma target of 4.5σ.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Service (Insurance Claims Processing)

### Problem Statement
An insurance company processes **15,000 claims per month**. Each claim has **5 service opportunities** (completeness, accuracy, timeliness, documentation, customer acknowledgment). In one month, **75 defects** were observed.

**Find:**
1. DPU
2. DPMO
3. Process Yield
4. Long-Term Sigma Level
5. Comment on suitability for Six Sigma certification.

### Step 1 — Compute DPU

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPU} &= \frac{D}{U} \\
\text{DPU} &= \frac{75}{15{,}000} \\
\text{DPU} &= 0.005 \;\;\text{defects per claim}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** On average, every 200th claim carries one defect.

---

### Step 2 — Compute DPMO

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{75 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{15{,}000 \times 5} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{75{,}000{,}000}{75{,}000} \\
\text{DPMO} &= 1{,}000 \;\;\text{defects per million opportunities}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Each claim exposes the customer to 1 defect per 1,000 service opportunities.

---

### Step 3 — Compute Process Yield

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} \\
Y &= 1 - \frac{1{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \\
Y &= 0.999 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 99.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The service is delivering 99.9% defect-free transactions to its customers.

---

### Step 4 — Compute Long-Term Sigma Level

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{ST} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.999) \approx 3.09 \;\;\text{sigma} \\
Z_{LT} &= 3.09 + 1.5 = 4.59 \;\;\text{sigma}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The claims process is operating at **4.59σ long-term**, just above the Six Sigma threshold of 4.5σ — a strong performer.

---

### Step 5 — Examiner's Interpretation

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment:**
> With $Z_{LT} \approx 4.59$, this process **qualifies as a Six Sigma process** under the Motorola convention. However, achieving 4.59σ in services is exceptionally difficult because:
> - Customer behavior is highly variable.
> - Data collection is judgmental rather than purely physical.
> - Real-time error recovery is rarely possible.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Manufacturing vs. Service Six Sigma Deployment

| Analysis Stage | Manufacturing Tool | Service Tool | Common Across Both |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Define** | CTQ Tree, Project Charter | Voice of Customer (VoC), SIPOC | Project Charter, CTQ Tree |
| **Measure** | Gauge R&R, Cpk, DPMO | CTQ Dashboard, Transaction Audits | DPMO, Yield |
| **Analyze** | Pareto, Fishbone, Regression | Gap Analysis, SERVQUAL, Process Mining | Pareto, Fishbone |
| **Improve** | DOE, Kaizen, Poka-Yoke | Service Blueprint, RPA, Training | Kaizen, Pilot Testing |
| **Control** | SPC Charts ($\bar{X}$-R, p-chart) | SLA Monitors, Balanced Scorecard | Control Plan, FMEA |

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Tip for KTU Board Exams:** When asked to "compare manufacturing and service Six Sigma," always draw a **2-column comparison table** with at least **6 rows** (CTQ, Defect Definition, Tools, Data Type, Customer Role, Variability Source). Examiners reward structured answers.

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 DMAIC Flow in a Manufacturing Environment

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the DMAIC cycle as deployed in a discrete manufacturing plant, with specific examples anchored to each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mfgD["Define: CTQ = Shaft Diameter 25 plus minus 0.05 mm"]
    mfgM["Measure: Sample 200 parts, count defects, calculate DPMO"]
    mfgA["Analyze: Fishbone shows machine vibration as root cause"]
    mfgI["Improve: Replace bearings, recalibrate lathe, run DOE"]
    mfgC["Control: X-bar R chart on shop floor, SOP updated"]
    mfgD --> mfgM
    mfgM --> mfgA
    mfgA --> mfgI
    mfgI --> mfgC
    mfgC -.Feedback Loop.-> mfgD
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each block is a DMAIC phase with a concrete manufacturing example.
- The dashed feedback arrow from **Control → Define** represents the continuous improvement loop — once controlled, the next project begins.

---

## 4.2 DMAIC Flow in a Service Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    svcD["Define: CTQ = Call answered in 20 seconds"]
    svcM["Measure: Sample 5000 calls, count abandoned calls"]
    svcA["Analyze: Gap analysis shows 30 percent due to skill shortage"]
    svcI["Improve: Hire 10 agents, deploy chatbot for Tier 1 queries"]
    svcC["Control: SLA dashboard, weekly QA audits"]
    svcD --> svcM
    svcM --> svcA
    svcA --> svcI
    svcI --> svcC
    svcC -.Feedback Loop.-> svcD
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The same DMAIC structure applies, but the **CTQs** and **tools** are service-specific.
- Note the **chatbot** as a typical service-industry improvement lever — this would never appear in a manufacturing DMAIC.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Industry Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MANUFACTURING
        manuA["Input: Raw Material"]
        manuB["Process: CNC Machining"]
        manuC["Inspection: CMM Measurement"]
        manuD["Output: Conforming Part"]
    end
    subgraph SERVICES
        svcA["Input: Customer Request"]
        svcB["Process: Agent Handling"]
        svcC["Audit: QA Scorecard"]
        svcD["Output: Resolved Query"]
    end
    manuA --> manuB --> manuC --> manuD
    svcA --> svcB --> svcC --> svcD
    manuC -.Defect Feedback.-> manuB
    svcC -.Defect Feedback.-> svcB
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Both industries follow an **Input → Process → Inspection → Output** skeleton.
- The **defect feedback loop** is identical: when an inspection or audit finds a defect, it is fed back to the process for corrective action.
- The structural symmetry justifies why **a single methodology (DMAIC)** can serve both worlds.

---

## 4.4 Sigma Level vs. DPMO Conversion Table (High-Yield)

| Sigma Level | Yield (%) | DPMO |
|---|---|---|
| 2σ | 69.1% | 308,537 |
| 3σ | 93.3% | 66,807 |
| 4σ | 99.38% | 6,210 |
| 4.5σ | 99.73% | 1,350 |
| 5σ | 99.977% | 233 |
| 6σ | 99.99966% | 3.4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the **3.4 DPMO → 6σ** link. A common 3-mark question asks: "What is DPMO at Six Sigma level?" The answer is exactly **3.4**.

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*"Define Six Sigma. Explain how the definition of 'defect' differs in a manufacturing unit versus a service organization, with one example each."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Six Sigma Definition (1 Mark):** Six Sigma is a data-driven methodology aimed at reducing process variation to achieve ≤ **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**, equivalent to a 4.5σ long-term capability.
- **Manufacturing Defect (1 Mark):** A defect is a tangible deviation from specification, e.g., a shaft diameter measuring 25.10 mm when the upper spec limit is 25.05 mm.
- **Service Defect (1 Mark):** A defect is a failure to meet customer expectations or SLAs, e.g., a customer service call not answered within the promised 20-second window.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*"What is DPMO? A manufacturing process produces 8,000 units with 160 defects and 5 opportunities per unit. Calculate the DPMO."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{160 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{8{,}000 \times 5} \\
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{160{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} \\
\text{DPMO} &= 4{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** DPMO = **4,000 defects per million opportunities**. (2 marks for setup, 1 mark for correct answer.)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`**
*"Six Sigma methodology is widely applied in both manufacturing and service industries. Discuss the application of Six Sigma in a manufacturing plant with reference to the DMAIC framework. Also explain how the same framework is adapted for service industries with suitable examples."* **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Six Sigma in Manufacturing using DMAIC (7 Marks)

**Define Phase (1.5 Marks):**
- Identify the CTQ characteristic of the product (e.g., piston ring diameter must be within 50 ± 0.02 mm).
- Form a cross-functional team and prepare a project charter.
- Voice of Customer (internal) feeds into CTQ tree.

**Measure Phase (1.5 Marks):**
- Collect baseline data on 200 samples over 5 days.
- Calculate current **DPMO** using $\text{DPMO} = (D \times 10^{6}) / (U \times O)$.
- Compute **Cpk** using $\text{Cpk} = \min\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$.

**Analyze Phase (1.5 Marks):**
- Use **Pareto chart** to identify the vital few defect types.
- Use **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** diagram across 4M categories (Man, Machine, Material, Method).
- Use **Regression** to model cause-effect relationships.

**Improve Phase (1.5 Marks):**
- Run **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to optimize process parameters.
- Apply **Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing)** fixtures.
- Pilot the solution and measure new DPMO.

**Control Phase (1 Mark):**
- Implement **$\bar{X}$-R control chart** for ongoing monitoring.
- Update Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).
- Hand over to the production team with a **Control Plan**.

#### Part (b) — Adapting DMAIC for Service Industries (7 Marks)

**Service-Specific Adaptations:**
- **Define (1.5 Marks):** Use **Voice of Customer (VoC)** surveys and **SIPOC** (Suppliers-Inputs-Process-Outputs-Customers) diagram instead of engineering drawings. Example: In a bank, CTQ = "loan approved within 48 hours."
- **Measure (1.5 Marks):** Use **transaction audits**, **call sampling**, and **customer satisfaction (CSAT)** scores. Calculate DPMO based on transactional defects per opportunity.
- **Analyze (1.5 Marks):** Use **SERVQUAL gap model** to find the gap between expected and perceived service. Use **process mining** to find bottlenecks.
- **Improve (1.5 Marks):** Apply **service blueprinting**, **automation (RPA)**, and **agent training**. Example: Deploying chatbots to reduce Tier 1 query load.
- **Control (1 Mark):** Use **Service Level Agreements (SLAs)**, **balanced scorecards**, and **mystery shopping** for ongoing monitoring.

**Concluding Statement (Integrated with Part a):**
Both industries use the same DMAIC engine, but services require **stronger emphasis on VoC and softer data**, while manufacturing relies on **harder statistical data and SPC tools**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`**
*"With the help of a suitable numerical example, explain how DPMO is calculated for a manufacturing process and a service process. Compare the challenges of Six Sigma implementation in these two sectors."* **(14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Numerical Calculation in Manufacturing (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- A motorcycle assembly plant produces **10,000 bikes per month**.
- Each bike undergoes **6 inspection checks** (engine, brakes, electricals, body finish, paint, tire pressure).
- In one month, **120 defects** were detected.

**Step 1 — Compute DPU (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPU} &= \frac{D}{U} = \frac{120}{10{,}000} = 0.012
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute DPMO (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{120 \times 10^{6}}{10{,}000 \times 6} = \frac{120{,}000{,}000}{60{,}000} = 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Yield and Sigma Level (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 1 - \frac{2{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.998 = 99.8\% \\
Z_{LT} &= \Phi^{-1}(0.998) + 1.5 \approx 2.88 + 1.5 = 4.38\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (2 Marks):**
- The plant is operating at **4.38σ long-term**, below the 4.5σ Six Sigma threshold.
- Roughly **2 defects per 1,000 opportunities** — typical for a mid-tier Indian manufacturer.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation in Service & Challenges Comparison (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- An airline operates **25,000 flights per quarter**.
- Each flight has **4 customer-experience opportunities** (on-time departure, baggage accuracy, meal quality, cabin cleanliness).
- **200 defects** were reported.

**Step 1 — Compute DPMO (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DPMO} &= \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{25{,}000 \times 4} = \frac{200{,}000{,}000}{100{,}000} = 2{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Yield and Sigma Level (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y &= 0.998 = 99.8\% \\
Z_{LT} &\approx 4.38\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

**Challenges of Six Sigma in Manufacturing vs. Services (4 Marks):**

| Challenge | Manufacturing | Services |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Collection** | Easy — sensors and gauges | Hard — relies on customer feedback |
| **Defect Definition** | Clear from engineering specs | Fuzzy, customer-dependent |
| **Process Repeatability** | High, machines dominate | Low, humans dominate |
| **Rework Possibility** | Usually feasible | Often impossible (the moment is lost) |
| **Sample Size** | Large, statistically robust | Often small, judgmental |
| **Customer Role** | Passive | Active participant |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.5σ shift**: When asked to find "Sigma Level," students often give the short-term value only and lose 1–2 marks. Always state both $Z_{ST}$ and $Z_{LT}$.
> 2. **Confusing DPO with DPMO**: DPO = Defects Per **Opportunity** (uses $U \times O$ only as multiplier? No — DPO = D / (U × O)). DPMO = DPO × 10⁶. Know the difference.
> 3. **Vague "Service Industry" Answers**: Never write generic answers like "services are different." Always anchor with a **named CTQ** (e.g., "call answered in 20 seconds") and a **named tool** (e.g., SERVQUAL).
> 4. **Skipping the comparison table**: In a 14-mark question comparing manufacturing and services, a **table with at least 5–6 rows is mandatory**. Examiners award 2–3 marks just for a clean comparative table.
> 5. **Units mismatch**: Yield must be written as a fraction **or** percentage, consistently. Mixing "0.998%" with "99.8" is an instant deduction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Six Sigma in Manufacturing & Services:**

- ✅ Six Sigma = **3.4 DPMO** = **4.5σ long-term** = **99.99966% yield**.
- ✅ The **DMAIC** framework (Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control) is **industry-agnostic**.
- ✅ In **Manufacturing**, defects are **tangible**, countable, and measured against engineering tolerances.
- ✅ In **Services**, defects are **intangible**, perceptual, and measured against SLAs and VoC expectations.
- ✅ **DPMO Formula (memorize verbatim):** $\text{DPMO} = (D \times 10^{6}) / (U \times O)$.
- ✅ **Yield Formula:** $Y = 1 - (\text{DPMO} / 10^{6})$.
- ✅ **Long-Term Sigma:** Always add the **1.5σ Motorola shift** to the short-term Z.
- ✅ **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY):** Product of FPY of all sub-processes — used heavily in **multi-stage manufacturing**.
- ✅ **Key Tools — Manufacturing:** SPC, DOE, FMEA, Gauge R&R, Pareto, Fishbone, Poka-Yoke.
- ✅ **Key Tools — Services:** VoC, SERVQUAL, SIPOC, Service Blueprinting, RPA, SLA Dashboards.
- ✅ **The 3 fundamental differences** between sectors: **Tangibility**, **Customer Interaction**, **Variability Source** (4M in manufacturing vs. 5M — including customer — in services).
- ✅ **Common KTU question patterns:** "Define defect in manufacturing vs. service," "Calculate DPMO," "Compare tools used," "Explain DMAIC in [industry] with an example."
- ✅ **Memorize the Sigma-DPMO table** for at least 3σ, 4σ, 4.5σ, 5σ, and 6σ.
- ✅ Always conclude a comparative answer with: *"The methodology is universal; only the tools and data types change with industry."*
